title: Evaluation as Evolution: Transforming Adversarial Diffusion into Closed-Loop Curricula for Autonomous Vehicles
summary: Autonomous vehicles in interactive traffic environments are often limited by the scarcity of safety-critical tail events in static datasets, which biases learned policies toward average-case behaviors and reduces robustness. Existing evaluation methods attempt to address this through adversarial stress testing, but are predominantly open-loop and post-hoc, making it difficult to incorporate discovered failures back into the training process. We introduce Evaluation as Evolution ($E^2$), a closed-loop framework that transforms adversarial generation from a static validation step into an adaptive evolutionary curriculum. Specifically, $E^2$ formulates adversarial scenario synthesis as transport-regularized sparse control over a learned reverse-time SDE prior. To make this high-dimensional generation tractable, we utilize topology-driven support selection to identify critical interacting agents, and introduce Topological Anchoring to stabilize the process. This approach enables the targeted discovery of failure cases while strictly constraining deviations from realistic data distributions. Empirically, $E^2$ improves collision failure discovery by 9.01% on the nuScenes dataset and up to 21.43% on the nuPlan dataset over the strongest baselines, while maintaining low invalidity and high realism. It further yields substantial robustness gains when the resulting boundary cases are recycled for closed-loop policy fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: E^2 reframes adversarial scenario generation as a closed-loop, adaptive evolution process that uses transport-regularized sparse control over a reverse-time SDE prior to synthesize targeted, realistic safety-tail events. It uses topology-driven agent selection and Topological Anchoring to stabilize the search and constrain realism, yielding improved collision failure discovery and robustness when failures are recycled for policy fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of safety-critical tail events in static datasets, which biases learned policies toward average-case behavior and reduces robustness in interactive traffic. Provide a closed-loop mechanism to discover, constrain, and reuse failure cases.
AI.method: Formulate adversarial scenario synthesis as transport-regularized sparse control over a learned reverse-time SDE prior. Use topology-driven support selection to identify critical interacting agents, plus Topological Anchoring to stabilize the optimization. Maintain realism by constraining deviations from data distributions, enabling closed-loop discovery and reuse for training.
AI.result: Collision failure discovery improves by 9.01% on nuScenes and up to 21.43% on nuPlan over strong baselines, with low invalidity and high realism. Boundary cases can be recycled to improve policy robustness via closed-loop fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: E^2 provides a closed-loop, adaptive adversarial generation framework that increases tail-event discovery while preserving realism and enabling effective policy improvement through reuse of discovered failures.

title: A Physical Agentic Loop for Language-Guided Grasping with Execution-State Monitoring
summary: Robotic manipulation systems that follow language instructions often execute grasp primitives in a largely single-shot manner: a model proposes an action, the robot executes it, and failures such as empty grasps, slips, stalls, timeouts, or semantically wrong grasps are not surfaced to the decision layer in a structured way. Inspired by agentic loops in digital tool-using agents, we reformulate language-guided grasping as a bounded embodied agent operating over grounded execution states, where physical actions expose an explicit tool-state stream. We introduce a physical agentic loop that wraps an unmodified learned manipulation primitive (grasp-and-lift) with (i) an event-based interface and (ii) an execution monitoring layer, Watchdog, which converts noisy gripper telemetry into discrete outcome labels using contact-aware fusion and temporal stabilization. These outcome events, optionally combined with post-grasp semantic verification, are consumed by a deterministic bounded policy that finalizes, retries, or escalates to the user for clarification, guaranteeing finite termination. We validate the resulting loop on a mobile manipulator with an eye-in-hand D405 camera, keeping the underlying grasp model unchanged and evaluating representative scenarios involving visual ambiguity, distractors, and induced execution failures. Results show that explicit execution-state monitoring and bounded recovery enable more robust and interpretable behavior than open-loop execution, while adding minimal architectural overhead. For the source code and demo refer to our project page: https://wenzewwz123.github.io/Agentic-Loop/

AI.tldr: The paper presents a bounded embodied agent framework to make language-guided grasping more robust by wrapping a standard grasp primitive with a Watchdog execution monitor and a bounded policy that surfaces failures, retries, or escalations, improving robustness and interpretability with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To address opaque, single-shot grasping where failures are not surfaced to the decision layer, leading to brittle behavior under ambiguity, distractors, or execution errors; inspired by agentic loops in tool-using agents to create a bounded, interpretable loop.
AI.method: Replace- or wrap an existing grasp-and-lift primitive with an event-based interface and an execution-monitoring layer (Watchdog). The monitor maps noisy gripper telemetry into discrete outcome events using contact-aware fusion and temporal stabilization, optionally combines with post-grasp semantic verification, and feeds a deterministic bounded policy that decides to finalize, retry, or escalate. Finite termination is guaranteed.
AI.result: Experimentally validated on a mobile manipulator with an eye-in-hand camera, keeping the grasp model unchanged. Scenarios include visual ambiguity, distractors, and induced execution failures. Explicit execution-state monitoring yields more robust and interpretable behavior than open-loop execution with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: Explicit execution-state monitoring and bounded recovery improve robustness and interpretability of language-guided manipulation, with negligible architectural overhead; the approach can be applied atop existing primitives to surface failures and provide structured recovery.

title: OpenPRC: A Unified Open-Source Framework for Physics-to-Task Evaluation in Physical Reservoir Computing
summary: Physical Reservoir Computing (PRC) leverages the intrinsic nonlinear dynamics of physical substrates, mechanical, optical, spintronic, and beyond, as fixed computational reservoirs, offering a compelling paradigm for energy-efficient and embodied machine learning. However, the practical workflow for developing and evaluating PRC systems remains fragmented: existing tools typically address only isolated parts of the pipeline, such as substrate-specific simulation, digital reservoir benchmarking, or readout training. What is missing is a unified framework that can represent both high-fidelity simulated trajectories and real experimental measurements through the same data interface, enabling reproducible evaluation, analysis, and physics-aware optimization across substrates and data sources. We present OpenPRC, an open-source Python framework that fills this gap through a schema-driven physics-to-task pipeline built around five modules: a GPU-accelerated hybrid RK4-PBD physics engine (demlat), a video-based experimental ingestion layer (openprc.vision), a modular learning layer (reservoir), information-theoretic analysis and benchmarking tools (analysis), and physics-aware optimization (optimize). A universal HDF5 schema enforces reproducibility and interoperability, allowing GPU-simulated and experimentally acquired trajectories to enter the same downstream workflow without modification. Demonstrated capabilities include simulations of Origami tessellations, video-based trajectory extraction from a physical reservoir, and a common interface for standardized PRC benchmarking, correlation diagnostics, and capacity analysis. The longer-term vision is to serve as a standardizing layer for the PRC community, compatible with external physics engines including PyBullet, PyElastica, and MERLIN.

AI.tldr: OpenPRC is a unified, open-source Python framework for Physical Reservoir Computing that unifies simulated and experimental data via a universal HDF5 schema, enabling reproducible benchmarking and physics-aware optimization across substrates.
AI.motivation: PRC research suffers from fragmented workflows that handle only isolated pipeline parts (substrate simulation, digital benchmarking, or readout training). A unified, substrate-agnostic framework is needed to enable reproducible evaluation, cross-substrate analysis, and physics-aware optimization using both high-fidelity simulations and real measurements.
AI.method: Five-module, schema-driven pipeline: (1) demlat GPU-accelerated hybrid RK4-PBD physics engine, (2) openprc.vision video-based experimental ingestion, (3) reservoir modular learning layer, (4) analysis information-theoretic benchmarking tools, (5) optimize physics-aware optimization; a universal HDF5 schema ensures interoperability with GPU-simulated and experimental trajectories and supports external engines like PyBullet, PyElastica, MERLIN.
AI.result: Demonstrations include Origami tessellations simulations, video-based trajectory extraction from physical reservoirs, and a common interface for standardized PRC benchmarking, correlation diagnostics, and capacity analysis.
AI.conclusion: OpenPRC aspires to standardize the PRC ecosystem, providing a reproducible, cross-substrate framework and serving as a bridge to external physics engines, thereby facilitating broader adoption and collaboration.

title: CMP: Robust Whole-Body Tracking for Loco-Manipulation via Competence Manifold Projection
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures. Under review. Project page and videos: https://shepherd1226.github.io/CMP
summary: While decoupled control schemes for legged mobile manipulators have shown robustness, learning holistic whole-body control policies for tracking global end-effector poses remains fragile against Out-of-Distribution (OOD) inputs induced by sensor noise or infeasible user commands. To improve robustness against these perturbations without sacrificing task performance and continuity, we propose Competence Manifold Projection (CMP). Specifically, we utilize a Frame-Wise Safety Scheme that transforms the infinite-horizon safety constraint into a computationally efficient single-step manifold inclusion. To instantiate this competence manifold, we employ a Lower-Bounded Safety Estimator that distinguishes unmastered intentions from the training distribution. We then introduce an Isomorphic Latent Space (ILS) that aligns manifold geometry with safety probability, enabling efficient O(1) seamless defense against arbitrary OOD intents. Experiments demonstrate that CMP achieves up to a 10-fold survival rate improvement in typical OOD scenarios where baselines suffer catastrophic failure, incurring under 10% tracking degradation. Notably, the system exhibits emergent ``best-effort'' generalization behaviors to progressively accomplish OOD goals by adhering to the competence boundaries. Result videos are available at: https://shepherd1226.github.io/CMP.

AI.tldr: Proposes Competence Manifold Projection (CMP) to improve robustness of holistic whole-body control in legged mobile manipulators against OOD perturbations, using a Frame-Wise Safety Scheme, a Lower-Bounded Safety Estimator, and an Isomorphic Latent Space to achieve O(1) defensive responses and emergent best-effort generalization; reports up to 10x survival rate gains with minimal tracking degradation.
AI.motivation: To address fragility of learned holistic whole-body control policies under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) inputs caused by sensor noise or infeasible user commands, while preserving task performance and continuity.
AI.method: Introduce CMP, consisting of: (1) Frame-Wise Safety Scheme that converts infinite-horizon safety constraints into a single-step manifold inclusion; (2) Lower-Bounded Safety Estimator to separate unmastered intentions from the training distribution; (3) Isomorphic Latent Space (ILS) that aligns manifold geometry with safety probability, enabling O(1) defense against arbitrary OOD intents.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows CMP yields up to 10x higher survival rate in typical OOD scenarios where baselines catastrophically fail, with tracking degradation under 10%; reports emergent best-effort generalization toward achieving OOD goals within competence boundaries; result videos available.
AI.conclusion: CMP provides a robust, safe, and scalable framework for handling OOD intents in whole-body control of legged manipulators, achieving strong robustness with minimal performance loss and showing emergent generalization behaviors.

title: Prediction Arena: Benchmarking AI Models on Real-World Prediction Markets
comment: 18 pages, 10 figures, 3 tables. Evaluation period: January 12 - March 9, 2026
summary: We introduce Prediction Arena, a benchmark for evaluating AI models' predictive accuracy and decision-making by enabling them to trade autonomously on live prediction markets with real capital. Unlike synthetic benchmarks, Prediction Arena tests models in environments where trades execute on actual exchanges (Kalshi and Polymarket), providing objective ground truth that cannot be gamed or overfitted. Each model operates as an independent agent starting with $10,000, making autonomous decisions every 15-45 minutes. Over a 57-day longitudinal evaluation (January 12 to March 9, 2026), we track two cohorts: six frontier models in live trading (Cohort 1, full period) and four next-generation models in paper trading (Cohort 2, 3-day preliminary). For Cohort 1, final Kalshi returns range from -16.0% to -30.8%. Our analysis identifies a clear performance hierarchy: initial prediction accuracy and the ability to capitalize on correct predictions are the main drivers, while research volume shows no correlation with outcomes. A striking cross-platform contrast emerges from parallel Polymarket live trading: Cohort 1 models averaged only -1.1% on Polymarket vs. -22.6% on Kalshi, with grok-4-20-checkpoint achieving a 71.4% settlement win rate - the highest across any platform or cohort. gemini-3.1-pro-preview (Cohort 2), which executed zero trades on Kalshi, achieved +6.02% on Polymarket in 3 days - the best return of any model across either cohort - demonstrating that platform design has a profound effect on which models succeed. Beyond performance, we analyze computational efficiency (token usage, cycle time), settlement accuracy, exit patterns, and market preferences, providing a comprehensive view of how frontier models behave under real financial pressure.

AI.tldr: Prediction Arena tests AI models in live prediction markets with real capital, revealing that predictive accuracy and the ability to capitalize on correct predictions drive performance, while research volume does not; platform design markedly shapes outcomes across Kalshi and Polymarket.
AI.motivation: Provide an objective, hard-ground-truth benchmark for predictive accuracy and decision-making under real financial pressure, avoiding gaming or overfitting common to synthetic benchmarks.
AI.method: Independent agents start with $10,000 and trade autonomously at 15–45 minute intervals over 57 days, across two cohorts: Cohort 1 (six frontier models in live trading) and Cohort 2 (four next-generation models in paper trading). Measurements include returns, settlement accuracy, exit patterns, and market preferences, plus efficiency metrics (token usage, cycle time).
AI.result: For Cohort 1, Kalshi final returns ranged from -16.0% to -30.8%. Across platforms, Polymarket live trading yielded an average of -1.1% vs. -22.6% on Kalshi; grok-4-20-checkpoint achieved a 71.4% settlement win rate (highest to date). Cohort 2 (gemini-3.1-pro-preview) traded zero on Kalshi but achieved +6.02% on Polymarket in 3 days, the best return across cohorts. Platform design had a profound impact on which models succeed.
AI.conclusion: Initial prediction accuracy and the ability to capitalize on correct predictions are the primary drivers of performance; research volume shows no correlation with outcomes. Frontier models exhibit behavior shaped by real financial pressure, and platform design critically determines model success.

title: FORGE:Fine-grained Multimodal Evaluation for Manufacturing Scenarios
summary: The manufacturing sector is increasingly adopting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to transition from simple perception to autonomous execution, yet current evaluations fail to reflect the rigorous demands of real-world manufacturing environments. Progress is hindered by data scarcity and a lack of fine-grained domain semantics in existing datasets. To bridge this gap, we introduce FORGE. Wefirst construct a high-quality multimodal dataset that combines real-world 2D images and 3D point clouds, annotated with fine-grained domain semantics (e.g., exact model numbers). We then evaluate 18 state-of-the-art MLLMs across three manufacturing tasks, namely workpiece verification, structural surface inspection, and assembly verification, revealing significant performance gaps. Counter to conventional understanding, the bottleneck analysis shows that visual grounding is not the primary limiting factor. Instead, insufficient domain-specific knowledge is the key bottleneck, setting a clear direction for future research. Beyond evaluation, we show that our structured annotations can serve as an actionable training resource: supervised fine-tuning of a compact 3B-parameter model on our data yields up to 90.8% relative improvement in accuracy on held-out manufacturing scenarios, providing preliminary evidence for a practical pathway toward domain-adapted manufacturing MLLMs. The code and datasets are available at https://ai4manufacturing.github.io/forge-web.

AI.tldr: FORGE provides a real-world multimodal manufacturing dataset (2D images + 3D point clouds) with fine-grained annotations; evaluating 18 MLLMs reveals domain knowledge, not visual grounding, is the bottleneck; supervised fine-tuning on a compact 3B model yields substantial gains.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and lack of domain semantics in manufacturing MLLMs; demonstrate realistic evaluation across tasks and provide actionable training resources.
AI.method: Construct FORGE dataset with real-world 2D images and 3D point clouds annotated with exact model numbers; evaluate 18 state-of-the-art MLLMs on workpiece verification, structural surface inspection, and assembly verification; analyze bottlenecks; perform supervised fine-tuning of a 3B-parameter model on the dataset.
AI.result: Bottleneck attributed to insufficient domain-specific knowledge, not visual grounding; significant performance gaps among MLLMs; up to 90.8% relative improvement on held-out scenarios via fine-tuning; code and data released.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific knowledge is the primary bottleneck; FORGE offers a practical path toward domain-adapted manufacturing MLLMs by providing rich annotations and a training-ready dataset.

title: An Analysis of Artificial Intelligence Adoption in NIH-Funded Research
summary: Understanding the landscape of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) adoption across the National Institutes of Health (NIH) portfolio is critical for research funding strategy, institutional planning, and health policy. The advent of large language models (LLMs) has fundamentally transformed research landscape analysis, enabling researchers to perform large-scale semantic extraction from thousands of unstructured research documents. In this paper, we illustrate a human-in-the-loop research methodology for LLMs to automatically classify and summarize research descriptions at scale. Using our methodology, we present a comprehensive analysis of 58,746 NIH-funded biomedical research projects from 2025. We show that: (1) AI constitutes 15.9% of the NIH portfolio with a 13.4% funding premium, concentrated in discovery, prediction, and data integration across disease domains; (2) a critical research-to-deployment gap exists, with 79% of AI projects remaining in research/development stages while only 14.7% engage in clinical deployment or implementation; and (3) health disparities research is severely underrepresented at just 5.7% of AI-funded work despite its importance to NIH's equity mission. These findings establish a framework for evidence-based policy interventions to align the NIH AI portfolio with health equity goals and strategic research priorities.

AI.tldr: Large-scale LLM-assisted analysis of NIH AI/ML portfolio reveals 15.9% AI share with 13.4% funding premium; most AI work remains in R&D (79%) vs deployment (14.7%); health disparities research underrepresented at 5.7% of AI funding; proposes policy framework to align portfolio with equity goals.
AI.motivation: To map AI/ML adoption across the NIH portfolio to inform funding strategy, planning, and health policy; leverage LLMs to extract insights from thousands of unstructured project descriptions.
AI.method: Human-in-the-loop LLM workflow to automatically classify and summarize project descriptions; dataset of 58,746 NIH-funded biomedical projects from 2025; analyses include AI/ML classification, funding premium, deployment status, domain categorization (discovery, prediction, data integration), disease-domain coverage, and equity focus.
AI.result: Quantified portfolio composition and deployment gap: AI projects 15.9% with a 13.4% funding premium; 79% in research/development, 14.7% in clinical deployment; health disparities research only 5.7% of AI-funded work; framework for evidence-based policy interventions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a framework to guide NIH policy interventions to align AI portfolio with health equity goals and strategic research priorities, enabling evidence-based planning.

title: Active Reward Machine Inference From Raw State Trajectories
summary: Reward machines are automaton-like structures that capture the memory required to accomplish a multi-stage task. When combined with reinforcement learning or optimal control methods, they can be used to synthesize robot policies to achieve such tasks. However, specifying a reward machine by hand, including a labeling function capturing high-level features that the decisions are based on, can be a daunting task. This paper deals with the problem of learning reward machines directly from raw state and policy information. As opposed to existing works, we assume no access to observations of rewards, labels, or machine nodes, and show what trajectory data is sufficient for learning the reward machine in this information-scarce regime. We then extend the result to an active learning setting where we incrementally query trajectory extensions to improve data (and indirectly computational) efficiency. Results are demonstrated with several grid world examples.

AI.tldr: Learns reward machines from raw state and policy data without rewards/labels, identifies sufficient trajectory data, and extends to active learning; validated on grid worlds.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on handcrafted reward machines by deriving them from data in scenarios with limited labeled feedback; enable efficient policy synthesis for multi-stage tasks.
AI.method: Learn reward machines directly from raw state trajectories and policy information, without access to rewards, labels, or machine nodes; first characterize the minimal trajectory information needed for identifiability; then introduce an active learning extension that iteratively queries trajectory extensions to improve data and computational efficiency.
AI.result: Demonstrates the approach on several grid world tasks, showing successful recovery of reward machines and improved data efficiency via active learning.
AI.conclusion: Feasible framework for learning reward machines from information-scarce data; active querying enhances efficiency; broad applicability to RL/controller synthesis without manual reward specification.

title: BLEG: LLM Functions as Powerful fMRI Graph-Enhancer for Brain Network Analysis
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been widely used in diverse brain network analysis tasks based on preprocessed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data. However, their performances are constrained due to high feature sparsity and inherent limitations of domain knowledge within uni-modal neurographs. Meanwhile, large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated powerful representation capabilities. Combining LLMs with GNNs presents a promising direction for brain network analysis. While LLMs and MLLMs have emerged in neuroscience, integration of LLMs with graph-based data remains unexplored. In this work, we deal with these issues by incorporating LLM's powerful representation and generalization capabilities. Considering great cost for directly tuning LLMs, we instead function LLM as enhancer to boost GNN's performance on downstream tasks. Our method, namely BLEG, can be divided into three stages. We firstly prompt LLM to get augmented texts for fMRI graph data, then we design a LLM-LM instruction tuning method to get enhanced textual representations at a relatively lower cost. GNN is trained together for coarsened alignment. Finally we finetune an adapter after GNN for given downstream tasks. Alignment loss between LM and GNN logits is designed to further enhance GNN's representation. Extensive experiments on different datasets confirmed BLEG's superiority.

AI.tldr: BLEG uses LLM-generated augmented texts and instruction-tuned textual representations to enhance GNNs for fMRI brain graphs, with joint training, an adapter finetune, and an LM–GNN alignment loss, achieving superior performance across datasets at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Addresses high feature sparsity and limited domain knowledge in uni-modal brain graphs by leveraging LLMs' rich representations while avoiding expensive full LM fine-tuning.
AI.method: 1) Prompt LLM to generate augmented text for fMRI graph data. 2) Use LLM–LM instruction tuning to obtain enhanced textual representations at lower cost. 3) Train GNN jointly for coarsened alignment with textual representations. 4) Finetune an adapter after GNN for downstream tasks. 5) Employ an alignment loss between LM and GNN logits to strengthen representations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse datasets demonstrate that BLEG outperforms baselines, validating the effectiveness of integrating LLM-derived textual cues with GNN-based brain graph analysis.
AI.conclusion: BLEG presents a cost-efficient, effective framework to integrate LLMs with graph-based brain-network analysis, improving representation alignment and downstream task performance; it opens avenues for further optimization of prompts, instruction tuning, and alignment objectives.

title: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation to Individual Taste
summary: Modern text-to-image (T2I) models generate high-fidelity visuals but remain indifferent to individual user preferences. While existing reward models optimize for "average" human appeal, they fail to capture the inherent subjectivity of aesthetic judgment. In this work, we introduce a novel dataset and predictive framework, called PAMELA, designed to model personalized image evaluations. Our dataset comprises 70,000 ratings across 5,000 diverse images generated by state-of-the-art models (Flux 2 and Nano Banana). Each image is evaluated by 15 unique users, providing a rich distribution of subjective preferences across domains such as art, design, fashion, and cinematic photography. Leveraging this data, we propose a personalized reward model trained jointly on our high-quality annotations and existing aesthetic assessment subsets. We demonstrate that our model predicts individual liking with higher accuracy than the majority of current state-of-the-art methods predict population-level preferences. Using our personalized predictor, we demonstrate how simple prompt optimization methods can be used to steer generations towards individual user preferences. Our results highlight the importance of data quality and personalization to handle the subjectivity of user preferences. We release our dataset and model to facilitate standardized research in personalized T2I alignment and subjective visual quality assessment.

AI.tldr: PAMELA introduces a 70k-rating dataset (5k images, 15 raters per image) and a personalized reward model for T2I, enabling user-specific aesthetic predictions and prompt optimization to align generations with individual preferences. Dataset and model released for standardized evaluation of personalized T2I alignment.
AI.motivation: Aesthetic judgments are highly subjective and vary across individuals, yet most reward models optimize for population-average appeal. Personalization is needed to capture user-specific preferences in T2I outputs.
AI.method: Create PAMELA dataset with 70,000 ratings over 5,000 images generated by Flux 2 and Nano Banana, each image rated by 15 unique users. Train a personalized reward model jointly on PAMELA annotations and existing aesthetic assessment subsets. Demonstrate improved prediction of individual liking and use simple prompt optimization to steer generations toward individual preferences.
AI.result: The personalized predictor achieves higher accuracy in predicting individual liking than most state-of-the-art methods predict population-level preferences. Demonstrates that data quality and personalization improve subjective visual quality assessment. Dataset and model are released to enable standardized research in personalized T2I alignment.
AI.conclusion: Personalization is essential for capturing subjective aesthetics in T2I; PAMELA provides a high-quality resource and a practical method to tailor generations to individual users through targeted data and lightweight prompts.

title: Munkres' General Topology Autoformalized in Isabelle/HOL
summary: We describe an experiment in LLM-assisted autoformalization that produced over 85,000 lines of Isabelle/HOL code covering all 39 sections of Munkres' Topology (general topology, Chapters 2--8), from topological spaces through dimension theory. The LLM-based coding agents (initially ChatGPT 5.2 and then Claude Opus 4.6) used 24 active days for that. The formalization is complete: all 806 formal results are fully proved with zero sorry's. Proved results include the Tychonoff theorem, the Baire category theorem, the Nagata--Smirnov and Smirnov metrization theorems, the Stone--Čech compactification, Ascoli's theorem, the space-filling curve, and others. The methodology is based on a "sorry-first" declarative proof workflow combined with bulk use of sledgehammer - two of Isabelle major strengths. This leads to relatively fast autoformalization progress. We analyze the resulting formalization in detail, analyze the human--LLM interaction patterns from the session log, and briefly compare with related autoformalization efforts in Megalodon, HOL Light, and Naproche. The results indicate that LLM-assisted formalization of standard mathematical textbooks in Isabelle/HOL is quite feasible, cheap and fast, even if some human supervision is useful.

AI.tldr: LLM-assisted autoformalization produced 85k+ lines of Isabelle/HOL code formalizing all 39 sections of Munkres' Topology, with 806 theorems fully proved (no sorries) using a 'sorry-first' workflow and sledgehammer; demonstrates feasibility, speed, and cost-effectiveness of formalizing standard textbooks; includes analysis of human–LLM interaction and comparisons to Megalodon, HOL Light, and Naproche.
AI.motivation: Evaluate the feasibility, efficiency, and interaction patterns of large-scale autoformalization of standard mathematical textbooks in Isabelle/HOL using LLMs, and assess potential for broader adoption.
AI.method: LLMs (initially ChatGPT 5.2, then Claude Opus 4.6) guided coding agents over 24 active days to formalize Munkres' Topology (Chapters 2–8) in 39 sections; employed a 'sorry-first' declarative proof workflow and extensive sledgehammer use; produced 806 fully proved results (no sorries); analyzed session logs and compared with Megalodon, HOL Light, and Naproche.
AI.result: Complete formalization: 806 results proven; 85,000+ lines of Isabelle/HOL code; highlights include Tychonoff, Baire, Nagata–Smirnov and Smirnov metrization theorems, Stone–Čech compactification, Ascoli's theorem, space-filling curve; progress described as fast and cheap; human supervision remains useful.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted formalization of standard mathematical textbooks in Isabelle/HOL is feasible, cheap, and fast; results are promising for broader adoption, with human supervision providing beneficial guidance.

title: Grasp as You Dream: Imitating Functional Grasping from Generated Human Demonstrations
summary: Building generalist robots capable of performing functional grasping in everyday, open-world environments remains a significant challenge due to the vast diversity of objects and tasks. Existing methods are either constrained to narrow object/task sets or rely on prohibitively large-scale data collection to capture real-world variability. In this work, we present an alternative approach, GraspDreamer, a method that leverages human demonstrations synthesized by visual generative models (VGMs) (e.g., video generation models) to enable zero-shot functional grasping without labor-intensive data collection. The key idea is that VGMs pre-trained on internet-scale human data implicitly encode generalized priors about how humans interact with the physical world, which can be combined with embodiment-specific action optimization to enable functional grasping with minimal effort. Extensive experiments on the public benchmarks with different robot hands demonstrate the superior data efficiency and generalization performance of GraspDreamer compared to previous methods. Real-world evaluations further validate the effectiveness on real robots. Additionally, we showcase that GraspDreamer can (1) be naturally extended to downstream manipulation tasks, and (2) can generate data to support visuomotor policy learning.

AI.tldr: GraspDreamer uses visual generative model–based demonstrations to enable zero-shot functional grasping via embodiment-specific optimization, achieving data-efficient generalization across robot hands and real robots; also supports downstream tasks and visuomotor policy data generation.
AI.motivation: Open-world object diversity and task variation make functional grasping difficult; existing methods are data-hungry or constrained to narrow scopes. A zero-shot, generalizable approach that leverages broad human-interaction priors is sought.
AI.method: Synthesize human demonstrations with visual generative models (VGMs) to capture generalized priors of human–object interaction; fuse these demonstrations with embodiment-specific action optimization to perform functional grasps in a zero-shot manner; evaluate across multiple robot hands on benchmarks and in real-world robots; additionally show extension to downstream manipulation tasks and capacity to generate data for visuomotor policy learning.
AI.result: GraspDreamer demonstrates superior data efficiency and generalization on public benchmarks compared with prior methods; real-world robot evaluations validate effectiveness; the approach naturally extends to downstream manipulation tasks and can generate data to support visuomotor policy learning.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging VGMs-generated demonstrations as generalized priors, coupled with embodiment-aware optimization, enables robust zero-shot functional grasping with minimal data, and offers broad applicability to downstream manipulation and policy-learning data generation.

title: LLM-Generated Fault Scenarios for Evaluating Perception-Driven Lane Following in Autonomous Edge Systems
summary: Deploying autonomous vision systems on edge devices faces a critical challenge: resource constraints prevent real-time and predictable execution of comprehensive safety tests. Existing validation methods depend on static datasets or manual fault injection, failing to capture the diverse environmental hazards encountered in real-world deployment. To address this, we introduce a decoupled offline-online fault injection framework. This architecture separates the validation process into two distinct phases: a computationally intensive Offline Phase and a lightweight Online Phase. In the offline phase, we employ Large Language Models (LLMs) to semantically generate structured fault scenarios and Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) to synthesize high-fidelity sensor degradations. These complex fault dynamics are distilled into a pre-computed lookup table, enabling the edge device to perform real-time fault-aware inference without running heavy AI models locally. We extensively validated this framework on a ResNet18 lane-following model across 460 fault scenarios. Results show that while the model achieves a baseline R^2 of approximately 0.85 on clean data, our generated faults expose significant robustness degradation, with RMSE increasing by up to 99% and within-0.10 localization accuracy dropping to as low as 31.0% under fog conditions, demonstrating the inadequacy of normal-data evaluation for real-world edge AI deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decoupled offline-online fault injection framework for edge AI; uses LLMs to generate fault scenarios and Latent Diffusion Models to synthesize degradations; offline phase builds a lookup table enabling fault-aware real-time inference; validated on a ResNet18 lane-following model across 460 fault scenarios; shows significant degradation under faults, underscoring limitations of clean-data evaluation.
AI.motivation: Edge devices have limited compute and real-time constraints; existing validation relying on static datasets or manual fault injection fails to capture diverse, real-world hazards; need scalable, realistic fault dynamics for robust safety testing.
AI.method: Two-phase architecture: Offline phase uses LLMs to semantically generate structured fault scenarios and LDMs to synthesize high-fidelity sensor degradations; distills complex fault dynamics into a pre-computed lookup table for edge deployment. Online phase uses the lookup table to perform fault-aware inference without running heavy AI models locally.
AI.result: Baseline R^2 ~0.85 on clean data; on fault scenarios, RMSE increases by up to ~99% and within-0.10 localization accuracy drops to as low as 31.0% under fog, indicating that results on clean data do not reflect real-world robustness.
AI.conclusion: A decoupled offline-online fault-injection framework enables real-time fault-aware inference on edge devices and highlights the necessity of diverse fault testing beyond normal-data evaluation for safe deployment of edge AI systems.

title: GameWorld: Towards Standardized and Verifiable Evaluation of Multimodal Game Agents
summary: Towards an embodied generalist for real-world interaction, Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) agents still suffer from challenging latency, sparse feedback, and irreversible mistakes. Video games offer an ideal testbed with rich visual observations and closed-loop interaction, demanding fine-grained perception, long-horizon planning, and precise control. However, systematically evaluating these capabilities is currently hindered by heterogeneous action interfaces and heuristic verification. To this end, we introduce GameWorld, a benchmark designed for standardized and verifiable evaluation of MLLMs as generalist game agents in browser environments. Two game agent interfaces are studied: (i) computer-use agents that directly emit keyboard and mouse controls, and (ii) generalist multimodal agents that act in a semantic action space via deterministic Semantic Action Parsing. GameWorld contains 34 diverse games and 170 tasks, each paired with state-verifiable metrics for outcome-based evaluation. The results across 18 model-interface pairs suggest that even the best performing agent is far from achieving human capabilities on video games. Extensive experiments of repeated full-benchmark reruns demonstrate the robustness of the benchmark, while further studies on real-time interaction, context-memory sensitivity, and action validity expose more challenges ahead for game agents. Together, by offering a standardized, verifiable, and reproducible evaluation framework, GameWorld lays a robust foundation for advancing research on multimodal game agents and beyond. The project page is at https://gameworld-bench.github.io.

AI.tldr: GameWorld standardizes evaluation of multimodal LLM agents in browser-based video games using two interfaces; 34 games, 170 tasks; robust, verifiable, reproducible benchmark, revealing current agents lag behind humans.
AI.motivation: To address latency, sparse feedback, irreversible mistakes in MLLMs and the lack of standardized, verifiable evaluation for real-time, closed-loop game interaction.
AI.method: Develop two interfaces: computer-use (keyboard/mouse) and semantic-action parsing for generic action space; compile 34 games and 170 tasks with state-verifiable metrics; evaluate 18 model-interface pairs; conduct repeated full-benchmark reruns; study real-time interaction, memory sensitivity, action validity.
AI.result: Even the best agents perform far below human levels; benchmark demonstrates robustness and reveals remaining challenges; results emphasize need for further advances in perception, planning, and control under real-time constraints.
AI.conclusion: GameWorld offers a standardized, verifiable, reproducible framework for evaluating multimodal game agents, providing a foundation for progress in MLLMs and related fields; project page provided.

title: M-ArtAgent: Evidence-Based Multimodal Agent for Implicit Art Influence Discovery
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE Access
summary: Implicit artistic influence, although visually plausible, is often undocumented and thus poses a historically constrained attribution problem: resemblance is necessary but not sufficient evidence. Most prior systems reduce influence discovery to embedding similarity or label-driven graph completion, while recent multimodal large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to temporal inconsistency and unverified attributions. This paper introduces M-ArtAgent, an evidence-based multimodal agent that reframes implicit influence discovery as probabilistic adjudication. It follows a four-phase protocol consisting of Investigation, Corroboration, Falsification, and Verdict governed by a Reasoning and Acting (ReAct)-style controller that assembles verifiable evidence chains from images and biographies, enforces art-historical axioms, and subjects each hypothesis to adversarial falsification via a prompt-isolated critic. Two theory-grounded operators, StyleComparator for Wolfflin formal analysis and ConceptRetriever for ICONCLASS-based iconographic grounding, ensure that intermediate claims are formally auditable. On the balanced WikiArt Influence Benchmark-100 (WIB-100) of 100 artists and 2,000 directed pairs, M-ArtAgent achieves 83.7% positive-class F1, 0.666 Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC), and 0.910 area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC), with leakage-control and robustness checks confirming that the gains persist when explicit influence phrases are masked. By coupling multimodal perception with domain-constrained falsification, M-ArtAgent demonstrates that implicit influence analysis benefits from historically grounded adjudication rather than pattern matching alone.

AI.tldr: Evidence-based multimodal agent for implicit artistic influence discovery using a four-phase adjudication protocol and theory-grounded operators, achieving strong performance on WIB-100 with robustness to masking.
AI.motivation: Implicit influence attribution is underdetermined by resemblance alone; prior methods rely on embedding similarity or LLM-driven graphs, while LLMs suffer from temporal inconsistency and unverified attributions.
AI.method: Four-phase Investigation–Corroboration–Falsification–Verdict controller (ReAct-style) assembling verifiable evidence from images and biographies; enforces art-historical axioms; adversarial falsification via a prompt-isolated critic; StyleComparator (Wolfflin formal analysis) and ConceptRetriever (ICONCLASS-based grounding) for auditable intermediate claims; evaluated on WIB-100 (100 artists, 2,000 directed pairs) with leakage-control.
AI.result: On WIB-100, 83.7% positive-class F1, MCC 0.666, ROC-AUC 0.910; gains persist under masking of explicit influence phrases; leakage-control and robustness checks confirm robustness.
AI.conclusion: Coupling multimodal perception with domain-constrained falsification yields benefits for implicit influence analysis, demonstrating that historically grounded adjudication improves over pattern-matching approaches.

title: Robust Multi-Agent Target Tracking in Intermittent Communication Environments via Analytical Belief Merging
summary: Autonomous multi-agent target tracking in GPS-denied and communication-restricted environments (e.g., underwater exploration, subterranean search and rescue, and adversarial domains) forces agents to operate independently and only exchange information during brief reconnection windows. Because transmitting complete observation and trajectory histories is bandwidth-exhaustive, exchanging probabilistic belief maps serves as a highly efficient proxy that preserves the topology of agent knowledge. While minimizing divergence metrics to merge these decentralized beliefs is conceptually sound, traditional approaches often rely on numerical solvers that introduce critical quantization errors and artificial noise floors. In this paper, we formulate the decentralized belief merging problem as Forward and Reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence optimizations and derive their exact closed-form analytical solutions. By deploying these derivations, we mathematically eliminate optimization artifacts, achieving perfect mathematical fidelity while reducing the computational complexity of the belief merge to $\mathcal{O}(N|S|)$ scalar operations. Furthermore, we propose a novel spatially-aware visit-weighted KL merging strategy that dynamically weighs agent beliefs based on their physical visitation history. Validated across tens of thousands of distributed simulations, extensive sensitivity analysis demonstrates that our proposed method significantly suppresses sensor noise and outperforms standard analytical means in environments characterized by highly degraded sensors and prolonged communication intervals.

AI.tldr: Exact closed-form solutions for decentralized belief merging using Forward and Reverse KL divergences, removing optimization artifacts and reducing merge complexity to O(N|S|). Adds a spatially-aware visit-weighted KL strategy. Demonstrated via extensive simulations showing robustness to sensor degradation and long communication gaps.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate, efficient fusion of probabilistic belief maps in GPS-denied, bandwidth-constrained, and intermittently connected multi-agent systems to improve target tracking without introducing quantization errors from numerical solvers.
AI.method: Formulate decentralized belief merging as optimization of Forward KL and Reverse KL divergences with analytically derived closed-form solutions. Introduce a spatially-aware visit-weighted KL merging strategy that weights agent beliefs by visitation history. Analyze complexity to O(N|S|) scalar operations.
AI.result: Derivations yield exact closed-form solutions that avoid numerical artifacts commonly introduced by solvers. The proposed method reduces computational burden to O(N|S|). The visit-weighted KL strategy enhances robustness and accuracy. Validation across tens of thousands of distributed simulations and sensitivity analyses show superior performance under degraded sensing and long communication intervals compared to standard analytical merging methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach achieves artifact-free, efficient decentralized belief fusion in challenging, bandwidth-restricted environments, significantly improving robustness in multi-agent target tracking when communication is intermittent.

title: Benchmark Shadows: Data Alignment, Parameter Footprints, and Generalization in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models often achieve strong benchmark gains without corresponding improvements in broader capability. We hypothesize that this discrepancy arises from differences in training regimes induced by data distribution. To investigate this, we design controlled data interventions that isolate distributional effects under fixed training settings. We find that benchmark-aligned data improves narrow evaluation metrics while limiting broader representational development, whereas coverage-expanding data leads to more distributed parameter adaptation and better generalization. We further introduce parameter-space diagnostics based on spectral and rank analyses, which reveal distinct structural signatures of these regimes. Similar patterns are observed across diverse open-source model families, including multimodal models as a key case study, suggesting that these effects extend beyond controlled settings. A case study on prompt repetition shows that not all data artifacts induce regime shifts. These results indicate that benchmark performance alone is insufficient to characterize model capability, and highlight the importance of data distribution in shaping learning dynamics.

AI.tldr: Benchmark gains from large language models can arise from data distributions that shape learning regimes; controlled data interventions show benchmark-aligned data boosts narrow metrics but limits broad capabilities, while coverage-expanding data promotes distributed adaptation and better generalization. Spectral/rank diagnostics reveal regime-specific signatures; patterns hold across diverse open-source, including multimodal models; prompt repetition effects vary. Benchmark metrics alone are insufficient to assess overall capability.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between strong benchmark performance and broader capability development by isolating distributional effects of training data under fixed settings and assessing how different data regimes influence learning dynamics.
AI.method: 1) Design controlled data interventions that separate distributional effects while keeping training settings fixed. 2) Compare benchmark-aligned data versus coverage-expanding data. 3) Develop parameter-space diagnostics using spectral and rank analyses to characterize regime structures. 4) Validate findings across diverse open-source model families, including multimodal models. 5) Include case studies (e.g., prompt repetition) to assess artifacts.
AI.result: Benchmark-aligned data improves narrow evaluation metrics but constrains broader representational development; coverage-expanding data promotes more distributed parameter adaptation and better generalization. Diagnostics reveal distinct structural signatures for each regime. Similar patterns observed across diverse model families, including multimodal models. Not all data artifacts (e.g., prompt repetition) induce regime shifts. The work emphasizes that benchmark performance alone cannot characterize model capability and highlights data distribution as a key learning-dynamics driver.
AI.conclusion: Data distribution critically shapes learning dynamics and regime development in LLMs; evaluation should incorporate broader diagnostics beyond benchmark scores to accurately assess capabilities. The observed generality across model families suggests these effects are robust and warrant consideration in future model training and evaluation practices.

title: HY-Embodied-0.5: Embodied Foundation Models for Real-World Agents
summary: We introduce HY-Embodied-0.5, a family of foundation models specifically designed for real-world embodied agents. To bridge the gap between general Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and the demands of embodied agents, our models are developed to enhance the core capabilities required by embodied intelligence: spatial and temporal visual perception, alongside advanced embodied reasoning for prediction, interaction, and planning. The HY-Embodied-0.5 suite comprises two primary variants: an efficient model with 2B activated parameters designed for edge deployment, and a powerful model with 32B activated parameters targeted for complex reasoning. To support the fine-grained visual perception essential for embodied tasks, we adopt a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture to enable modality-specific computing. By incorporating latent tokens, this design effectively enhances the perceptual representation of the models. To improve reasoning capabilities, we introduce an iterative, self-evolving post-training paradigm. Furthermore, we employ on-policy distillation to transfer the advanced capabilities of the large model to the smaller variant, thereby maximizing the performance potential of the compact model. Extensive evaluations across 22 benchmarks, spanning visual perception, spatial reasoning, and embodied understanding, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Our MoT-2B model outperforms similarly sized state-of-the-art models on 16 benchmarks, while the 32B variant achieves performance comparable to frontier models such as Gemini 3.0 Pro. In downstream robot control experiments, we leverage our robust VLM foundation to train an effective Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model, achieving compelling results in real-world physical evaluations. Code and models are open-sourced at https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/HY-Embodied.

AI.tldr: HY-Embodied-0.5 presents two MoT-based foundation models for embodied agents (2B edge-efficient and 32B high-capacity), with latent-token perceptual tokens, iterative post-training, and on-policy distillation to a compact model. Evaluated on 22 benchmarks, the 2B variant outperforms similar-sized SOTA on 16 tasks; 32B approaches frontier models like Gemini 3.0 Pro; supports Vision-Language-Action for real-world robotics; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between general Vision-Language Models and embodied agents, by building a scalable foundation that emphasizes spatial/temporal perception and embodied reasoning (prediction, interaction, planning).
AI.method: Mixture-of-Transformers architecture enables modality-specific computing; latent tokens enhance perceptual representations; iterative self-evolving post-training to improve reasoning; on-policy distillation transfers capabilities from the 32B to the 2B model; two variants (2B and 32B); extensive evaluation across 22 benchmarks; downstream Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for robot control.
AI.result: HY-Embodied-2B (MoT-2B) surpasses similar-sized SOTA on 16/22 benchmarks; HY-Embodied-32B attains performance comparable to frontier models (e.g., Gemini 3.0 Pro); robust base enables effective VLA models in real-world tests; code and models open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: HY-Embodied-0.5 demonstrates a scalable, effective foundation for embodied AI, combining efficient perception with strong reasoning, validated across perception, spatial reasoning, and embodied tasks, with practical robotic control capabilities and open-source availability.

title: ConsistRM: Improving Generative Reward Models via Consistency-Aware Self-Training
summary: Generative reward models (GRMs) have emerged as a promising approach for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences by offering greater representational capacity and flexibility than traditional scalar reward models. However, GRMs face two major challenges: reliance on costly human-annotated data restricts scalability, and self-training approaches often suffer from instability and vulnerability to reward hacking. To address these issues, we propose ConsistRM, a self-training framework that enables effective and stable GRM training without human annotations. ConsistRM incorporates the Consistency-Aware Answer Reward, which produces reliable pseudo-labels with temporal consistency, thereby providing more stable model optimization. Moreover, the Consistency-Aware Critique Reward is introduced to assess semantic consistency across multiple critiques and allocates fine-grained and differentiated rewards. Experiments on five benchmark datasets across four base models demonstrate that ConsistRM outperforms vanilla Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) by an average of 1.5%. Further analysis shows that ConsistRM enhances output consistency and mitigates position bias caused by input order, highlighting the effectiveness of consistency-aware rewards in improving GRMs.

AI.tldr: ConsistRM is a self-training framework for generative reward models (GRMs) that uses consistency-aware rewards to enable annotation-free training, achieving better stability and ~1.5% average improvement over vanilla Reinforcement Fine-Tuning across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GRMs require costly human annotations and self-training can be unstable and vulnerable to reward hacking. A scalable, annotation-free approach is needed to align LLMs with human preferences.
AI.method: Introduce ConsistRM with two components: (1) Consistency-Aware Answer Reward that yields temporally consistent pseudo-labels for stable optimization, and (2) Consistency-Aware Critique Reward that evaluates semantic consistency across multiple critiques and assigns fine-grained rewards. The framework operates in a self-training loop without human labels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmark datasets across four base models shows ConsistRM beats vanilla Reinforcement Fine-Tuning by an average of 1.5%. Further analysis indicates improved output consistency and reduced sensitivity to input order (position bias).
AI.conclusion: Consistency-aware rewards enable stable, scalable GRM training without human annotations and effectively improve alignment performance, illustrating the value of temporal and semantic consistency in reward design.

title: Spatio-Temporal Grounding of Large Language Models from Perception Streams
summary: Embodied-AI agents must reason about how objects move and interact in 3-D space over time, yet existing smaller frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) still mis-handle fine-grained spatial relations, metric distances, and temporal orderings. We introduce the general framework Formally Explainable Spatio-Temporal Scenes (FESTS) that injects verifiable spatio-temporal supervision into an LLM by compiling natural-language queries into Spatial Regular Expression (SpRE) -- a language combining regular expression syntax with S4u spatial logic and extended here with universal and existential quantification. The pipeline matches each SpRE against any structured video log and exports aligned (query, frames, match, explanation) tuples, enabling unlimited training data without manual labels. Training a 3-billion-parameter model on 27k such tuples boosts frame-level F1 from 48.5% to 87.5%, matching GPT-4.1 on complex spatio-temporal reasoning while remaining two orders of magnitude smaller, and, hence, enabling spatio-temporal intelligence for Video LLM.

AI.tldr: FESTS introduces a verifiable spatio-temporal supervision framework that compiles natural-language queries into Spatial Regular Expressions (SpRE) and trains a compact Video-LMM; achieves strong spatio-temporal reasoning with 3B parameters using 27k tuples, rivaling GPT-4.1 while being two orders of magnitude smaller.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs struggle with fine-grained 3-D spatial relations, metric distances, and temporal orderings in embodied AI; need scalable, verifiable supervision for spatio-temporal reasoning in video-language models.
AI.method: Convert NL queries into SpRE combining regex syntax with S4u spatial logic including universal/existential quantification; match SpRE against structured video logs to produce (query, frames, match, explanation) tuples; train a 3B model on 27k tuples; evaluate frame-level F1.
AI.result: Model achieves 87.5% frame-level F1, matching GPT-4.1 on complex spatio-temporal reasoning; two orders of magnitude smaller; demonstrates scalability and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FESTS provides scalable, verifiable supervision for spatio-temporal reasoning in Video LLM and enables practical spatio-temporal intelligence in embodied AI, with potential for broader data generation via SpRE-structured logs.

title: Flow Learners for PDEs: Toward a Physics-to-Physics Paradigm for Scientific Computing
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) govern nearly every physical process in science and engineering, yet solving them at scale remains prohibitively expensive. Generative AI has transformed language, vision, and protein science, but learned PDE solvers have not undergone a comparable shift. Existing paradigms each capture part of the problem. Physics-informed neural networks embed residual structure, yet they are often difficult to optimize in stiff, multiscale, or large-domain regimes. Neural operators amortize across instances, yet they commonly inherit a snapshot-prediction view of solving and can degrade over long rollouts. Diffusion-based solvers model uncertainty, yet they are often built on a solver template that still centers on state regression. We argue that the core issue is the abstraction used to train learned solvers. Many models are asked to predict states, while many scientific settings require modeling how uncertainty moves through constrained dynamics. The relevant object is transport over physically admissible futures. This motivates \emph{flow learners}: models that parameterize transport vector fields and generate trajectories through integration, echoing the continuous dynamics that define PDE evolution. This physics-to-physics alignment supports continuous-time prediction, native uncertainty quantification, and new opportunities for physics-aware solver design. We explain why transport-based learning offers a stronger organizing principle for learned PDE solving and outline the research agenda that follows from this shift.

AI.tldr: Flow learners are transport-based models for PDE solving that learn transport vector fields to generate trajectories, enabling continuous-time predictions and physics-aware uncertainty quantification, addressing limitations of PINNs, neural operators, and diffusion-based solvers.
AI.motivation: Current learned PDE solvers either predict states directly, rely on residual-based training, or imitate fixed templates. They struggle with stiffness, multiscale dynamics, large domains, and uncertainty propagation. A transport-centric view aligns learning with physical evolution, enabling continuous-time forecasts and principled uncertainty handling.
AI.method: Introduce flow learners that parameterize transport vector fields and integrate them to produce state trajectories. Emphasize continuous-time dynamics, native uncertainty quantification, and compatibility with physics-aware solver design. Outline a research agenda to develop, implement, and evaluate such models for PDEs.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and argument for a transport-based approach; no empirical results reported. Establishes benefits of transport-based learning for continuous-time prediction and uncertainty modeling and provides a roadmap for future work.
AI.conclusion: Transport-based flow learners offer a stronger organizing principle for learned PDE solving, enabling physics-consistent, continuous-time predictions and uncertainty-aware solvers, with a clear research agenda to realize this potential.

title: CLEAR: Context Augmentation from Contrastive Learning of Experience via Agentic Reflection
summary: Large language model agents rely on effective model context to obtain task-relevant information for decision-making. Many existing context engineering approaches primarily rely on the context generated from the past experience and retrieval mechanisms that reuse these context. However, retrieved context from past tasks must be adapted by the execution agent to fit new situations, placing additional reasoning burden on the underlying LLM. To address this limitation, we propose a generative context augmentation framework using Contrastive Learning of Experience via Agentic Reflection (CLEAR). CLEAR first employs a reflection agent to perform contrastive analysis over past execution trajectories and summarize useful context for each observed task. These summaries are then used as supervised fine-tuning data to train a context augmentation model (CAM). Then we further optimize CAM using reinforcement learning, where the reward signal is obtained by running the task execution agent. By learning to generate task-specific knowledge rather than retrieve knowledge from the past, CAM produces context that is better tailored to the current task. We conduct comprehensive evaluations on the AppWorld and WebShop benchmarks. Experimental results show that CLEAR consistently outperforms strong baselines. It improves task completion rate from 72.62% to 81.15% on AppWorld test set and averaged reward from 0.68 to 0.74 on a subset of WebShop, compared with baseline agent. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/awslabs/CLEAR.

AI.tldr: A generative context augmentation framework (CLEAR) uses a reflection agent to perform contrastive analysis on past experiences, producing summaries to fine-tune a context augmentation model (CAM); CAM is further optimized with reinforcement learning using task-execution rewards, yielding task-specific context that improves LLM agent performance on AppWorld and WebShop compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Past-context retrieval methods require adaptation of retrieved context to new tasks, imposing additional reasoning on the LLM; generating task-specific context aims to reduce this burden and improve decision quality.
AI.method: A reflection agent conducts contrastive analysis over historical execution trajectories to produce useful context summaries per task; these summaries are used as supervised fine-tuning data for CAM; CAM is then optimized with reinforcement learning using rewards from running the task execution agent.
AI.result: CLEAR outperforms strong baselines; AppWorld test set task completion rate rises from 72.62% to 81.15%; WebShop subset reward increases from 0.68 to 0.74; code for CLEAR is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Generative context augmentation via CAM, guided by contrastive reflection, effectively produces task-specific knowledge that improves decision-making in LLM agents and is empirically validated on AppWorld and WebShop benchmarks.

title: SANDO: Safe Autonomous Trajectory Planning for Dynamic Unknown Environments
summary: SANDO is a safe trajectory planner for 3D dynamic unknown environments, where obstacle locations and motions are unknown a priori and a collision-free plan can become unsafe at any moment, requiring fast replanning. Existing soft-constraint planners are fast but cannot guarantee collision-free paths, while hard-constraint methods ensure safety at the cost of longer computation. SANDO addresses this trade-off through three contributions. First, a heat map-based A* global planner steers paths away from high-risk regions using soft costs, and a spatiotemporal safe flight corridor (STSFC) generator produces time-layered polytopes that inflate obstacles only by their worst-case reachable set at each time layer, rather than by the worst case over the entire horizon. Second, trajectory optimization is formulated as a Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program (MIQP) with hard collision-avoidance constraints, and a variable elimination technique reduces the number of decision variables, enabling fast computation. Third, a formal safety analysis establishes collision-free guarantees under explicit velocity-bound and estimation-error assumptions. Ablation studies show that variable elimination yields up to 7.4x speedup in optimization time, and that STSFCs are critical for feasibility in dense dynamic environments. Benchmark simulations against state-of-the-art methods across standardized static benchmarks, obstacle-rich static forests, and dynamic environments show that SANDO consistently achieves the highest success rate with no constraint violations across all difficulty levels; perception-only experiments without ground truth obstacle information confirm robust performance under realistic sensing. Hardware experiments on a UAV with fully onboard planning, perception, and localization demonstrate six safe flights in static environments and ten safe flights among dynamic obstacles.

AI.tldr: SANDO: a safe, fast trajectory planner for 3D dynamic unknown environments using a soft-cost global planner, spatiotemporal safe corridors, MIQP with hard constraints, and formal safety guarantees, achieving high success rates in simulations and hardware flights.
AI.motivation: Address the fundamental trade-off between fast, unsafe soft-constraint planners and slow, safe hard-constraint methods for planning in environments with unknown obstacle locations and motions.
AI.method: 1) Heat-map-based A* global planner with soft costs to guide paths away from high-risk regions. 2) Spatiotemporal Safe Flight Corridor (STSFC) generator producing time-layered polytopes, inflating obstacles by their worst-case reachable set per time layer rather than the full horizon. 3) Trajectory optimization formulated as a MIQP with hard collision-avoidance constraints, aided by a variable elimination technique to reduce decision variables and speed up computation. 4) Formal safety analysis establishing collision-free guarantees under specified velocity-bound and estimation-error assumptions.
AI.result: Ablation: variable elimination yields up to 7.4x speedup in optimization time; STSFCs are essential for feasibility in dense dynamic scenarios. Benchmark results show SANDO achieves the highest success rate with zero constraint violations across static benchmarks, obstacle-rich static forests, and dynamic environments; perception-only experiments show robustness to sensing limitations. Hardware UAV experiments: six safe flights in static environments and ten safe flights with dynamic obstacles, all onboard planning/perception/localization.
AI.conclusion: SANDO provides a practical, provably safe trajectory planning framework for 3D dynamic unknown environments, achieving high success rates, fast planning times, and robustness in simulated and real-world tests, by integrating soft-risk global planning, spatiotemporal safety conditioning, hard-constraint MIQP optimization, and formal safety guarantees.

title: The Role of Emotional Stimuli and Intensity in Shaping Large Language Model Behavior
summary: Emotional prompting - the use of specific emotional diction in prompt engineering - has shown increasing promise in improving large language model (LLM) performance, truthfulness, and responsibility. However these studies have been limited to single types of positive emotional stimuli and have not considered varying degrees of emotion intensity in their analyses. In this paper, we explore the effects of four distinct emotions - joy, encouragement, anger, and insecurity - in emotional prompting and evaluate them on accuracy, sycophancy, and toxicity. We develop a prompt-generation pipeline with GPT-4o mini to create a suite of LLM and human-generated prompts with varying intensities across the four emotions. Then, we compile a "Gold Dataset" of prompts where human and model labels align. Our empirical evaluation on LLM behavior suggests that positive emotional stimuli lead to more accurate and less toxic results, but also increase sycophantic behavior.

AI.tldr: Four emotions (joy, encouragement, anger, insecurity) and their intensities are tested in emotional prompting for LLMs. Using a GPT-4o mini pipeline to generate prompts (and a Gold Dataset where human and model labels align), results show positive emotions improve accuracy and reduce toxicity but increase sycophancy.
AI.motivation: To move beyond single positive emotion studies and to understand how emotion type and intensity affect LLM truthfulness, safety, and reliability. This addresses gaps in how emotional cues shape model behavior and potential trade-offs in prompt design.
AI.method: Develop a prompt-generation pipeline using GPT-4o mini to create prompts across four emotions and varying intensities, including both LLM-generated and human-generated prompts. Compile a Gold Dataset where human and model labels align. Evaluate LLM behavior on accuracy, sycophancy, and toxicity.
AI.result: Positive emotional stimuli lead to higher accuracy and lower toxicity but induce higher sycophancy in LLM outputs.
AI.conclusion: Emotional prompting offers performance gains with trade-offs: improved accuracy and safety at the cost of increased sycophantic bias. Designers should consider emotion type and intensity and validate across models; further work needed on measurement validity and generalizability.

title: Training-free Spatially Grounded Geometric Shape Encoding (Technical Report)
summary: Positional encoding has become the de facto standard for grounding deep neural networks on discrete point-wise positions, and it has achieved remarkable success in tasks where the input can be represented as a one-dimensional sequence. However, extending this concept to 2D spatial geometric shapes demands carefully designed encoding strategies that account not only for shape geometry and pose, but also for compatibility with neural network learning. In this work, we address these challenges by introducing a training-free, general-purpose encoding strategy, dubbed XShapeEnc, that encodes an arbitrary spatially grounded 2D geometric shape into a compact representation exhibiting five favorable properties, including invertibility, adaptivity, and frequency richness. Specifically, a 2D spatially grounded geometric shape is decomposed into its normalized geometry within the unit disk and its pose vector, where the pose is further transformed into a harmonic pose field that also lies within the unit disk. A set of orthogonal Zernike bases is constructed to encode shape geometry and pose either independently or jointly, followed by a frequency-propagation operation to introduce high-frequency content into the encoding. We demonstrate the theoretical validity, efficiency, discriminability, and applicability of XShapeEnc via extensive analysis and experiments across a wide range of shape-aware tasks and our self-curated XShapeCorpus. We envision XShapeEnc as a foundational tool for research that goes beyond one-dimensional sequential data toward frontier 2D spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: Introduces XShapeEnc, a training-free 2D shape encoding using Zernike bases and harmonic pose fields with frequency propagation, yielding an invertible, adaptive, and frequency-rich representation.
AI.motivation: Extend the success of positional encodings to 2D spatial shapes by creating a general, training-free encoding that respects geometry, pose, and neural-network compatibility.
AI.method: Decompose a shape into normalized geometry in the unit disk and a pose vector. Transform pose into a harmonic pose field within the unit disk. Build orthogonal Zernike bases to encode geometry and pose independently or jointly. Apply a frequency-propagation step to inject high-frequency content into the encoding.
AI.result: The approach is theoretically sound and efficient, offering discriminability across tasks. Demonstrated via analyses and experiments on a wide range of shape-aware tasks and the authors\' XShapeCorpus, validating applicability and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: XShapeEnc provides a compact, invertible, adaptive, and frequency-rich encoding for 2D shapes that serves as a foundational tool for 2D spatial intelligence and shape-aware learning, without requiring training for encoding.

title: ReflectRM: Boosting Generative Reward Models via Self-Reflection within a Unified Judgment Framework
summary: Reward Models (RMs) are critical components in the Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) pipeline, directly determining the alignment quality of Large Language Models (LLMs). Recently, Generative Reward Models (GRMs) have emerged as a superior paradigm, offering higher interpretability and stronger generalization than traditional scalar RMs. However, existing methods for GRMs focus primarily on outcome-level supervision, neglecting analytical process quality, which constrains their potential. To address this, we propose ReflectRM, a novel GRM that leverages self-reflection to assess analytical quality and enhance preference modeling. ReflectRM is trained under a unified generative framework for joint modeling of response preference and analysis preference. During inference, we use its self-reflection capability to identify the most reliable analysis, from which the final preference prediction is derived. Experiments across four benchmarks show that ReflectRM consistently improves performance, achieving an average accuracy gain of +3.7 on Qwen3-4B. Further experiments confirm that response preference and analysis preference are mutually reinforcing. Notably, ReflectRM substantially mitigates positional bias, yielding +10.2 improvement compared with leading GRMs and establishing itself as a more stable evaluator.

AI.tldr: ReflectRM introduces a self-reflective generative reward model that jointly models response and analysis preferences, using self-reflection to select the most reliable analysis for final preference, yielding improvements in accuracy and reducing positional bias.
AI.motivation: Traditional GRMs focus on outcome-level supervision, neglecting analytical process quality. Enhancing interpretability, generalization, and bias robustness in RLHF requires modeling the quality of the analysis itself.
AI.method: A unified generative framework that jointly models response preference and analysis preference. During inference, self-reflection identifies the most reliable analysis to derive the final preference. Evaluated across four benchmarks.
AI.result: ReflectRM achieves an average accuracy gain of +3.7 on Qwen3-4B. Demonstrates mutual reinforcement between response and analysis preferences. Substantially mitigates positional bias, with +10.2 improvement over leading GRMs, and acts as a more stable evaluator.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating analytical-quality through self-reflection improves GRM performance and bias robustness, indicating a promising direction for RLHF reward modeling and evaluation.

title: EgoVerse: An Egocentric Human Dataset for Robot Learning from Around the World
summary: Robot learning increasingly depends on large and diverse data, yet robot data collection remains expensive and difficult to scale. Egocentric human data offer a promising alternative by capturing rich manipulation behavior across everyday environments. However, existing human datasets are often limited in scope, difficult to extend, and fragmented across institutions.
