AI.method: Theoretical analysis of L-layer ReLU networks without bias; derive L-1 conservation laws, analyze discrete drift, derive closed-form spectral crossover formula with mode coefficients c_k ~ e_k(0)^2 * lambda_{x,k}^2; analyze cross-entropy effects; identify perturbative and non-perturbative regimes; extensive experimental validation across architectures.
AI.result: Conservation laws constrain trajectories, with discrete gradient drift scaling as eta^alpha (alpha ~1.1-1.6); exact decomposition eta^2*S(eta) with spectral coefficients; cross-entropy yields Hessian spectral compression and timescale Theta(1/eta); two dynamical regimes; predictions validated across 23 experiments.
AI.conclusion: The study explains the reliability of gradient descent in non-convex NN optimization through invariants, drift decomposition, and regime-dependent dynamics, with cross-entropy self-regularization and wide applicability across architectures.

title: Beyond Pedestrians: Caption-Guided CLIP Framework for High-Difficulty Video-based Person Re-Identification
summary: In recent years, video-based person Re-Identification (ReID) has gained attention for its ability to leverage spatiotemporal cues to match individuals across non-overlapping cameras. However, current methods struggle with high-difficulty scenarios, such as sports and dance performances, where multiple individuals wear similar clothing while performing dynamic movements. To overcome these challenges, we propose CG-CLIP, a novel caption-guided CLIP framework that leverages explicit textual descriptions and learnable tokens. Our method introduces two key components: Caption-guided Memory Refinement (CMR) and Token-based Feature Extraction (TFE). CMR utilizes captions generated by Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to refine identity-specific features, capturing fine-grained details. TFE employs a cross-attention mechanism with fixed-length learnable tokens to efficiently aggregate spatiotemporal features, reducing computational overhead. We evaluate our approach on two standard datasets (MARS and iLIDS-VID) and two newly constructed high-difficulty datasets (SportsVReID and DanceVReID). Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms current state-of-the-art approaches, achieving significant improvements across all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Caption-guided CLIP with memory refinement and token-based feature extraction for video ReID, achieving state-of-the-art on high-difficulty sports/dance datasets.
AI.motivation: Address high-difficulty ReID where similar clothing and dynamic movement hinder matching; leverage textual descriptions to provide discriminative cues.
AI.method: Two components: Caption-guided Memory Refinement (CMR) using captions from Multi-modal LLMs to refine features; Token-based Feature Extraction (TFE) with cross-attention over fixed-length learnable tokens to aggregate spatiotemporal features efficiently.
AI.result: Evaluated on MARS, iLIDS-VID, SportsVReID, DanceVReID; reported significant improvements over SOTA on all benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the value of explicit textual guidance and token-based temporal aggregation for robust video ReID under challenging conditions; may imply broader utility in multimodal video analysis.

title: Towards Knowledgeable Deep Research: Framework and Benchmark
summary: Deep Research (DR) requires LLM agents to autonomously perform multi-step information seeking, processing, and reasoning to generate comprehensive reports. In contrast to existing studies that mainly focus on unstructured web content, a more challenging DR task should additionally utilize structured knowledge to provide a solid data foundation, facilitate quantitative computation, and lead to in-depth analyses. In this paper, we refer to this novel task as Knowledgeable Deep Research (KDR), which requires DR agents to generate reports with both structured and unstructured knowledge. Furthermore, we propose the Hybrid Knowledge Analysis framework (HKA), a multi-agent architecture that reasons over both kinds of knowledge and integrates the texts, figures, and tables into coherent multimodal reports. The key design is the Structured Knowledge Analyzer, which utilizes both coding and vision-language models to produce figures, tables, and corresponding insights. To support systematic evaluation, we construct KDR-Bench, which covers 9 domains, includes 41 expert-level questions, and incorporates a large number of structured knowledge resources (e.g., 1,252 tables). We further annotate the main conclusions and key points for each question and propose three categories of evaluation metrics including general-purpose, knowledge-centric, and vision-enhanced ones. Experimental results demonstrate that HKA consistently outperforms most existing DR agents on general-purpose and knowledge-centric metrics, and even surpasses the Gemini DR agent on vision-enhanced metrics, highlighting its effectiveness in deep, structure-aware knowledge analysis. Finally, we hope this work can serve as a new foundation for structured knowledge analysis in DR agents and facilitate future multimodal DR studies.

AI.tldr: We propose Knowledgeable Deep Research (KDR) and Hybrid Knowledge Analysis (HKA) to integrate structured knowledge into deep-research agents, supported by KDR-Bench for evaluation; results show improvements across metrics, especially in knowledge-centric and vision-enhanced tasks.
AI.motivation: Deep Research tasks benefit from combining unstructured text with structured data (tables/figures) to enable quantitative reasoning; existing DR lacks structured knowledge integration.
AI.method: HKA is a multi-agent architecture including a Structured Knowledge Analyzer that leverages coding and vision-language models to generate figures, tables, and insights; KDR-Bench provides 9 domains, 41 questions, and 1,252 tables; evaluation covers general-purpose, knowledge-centric, and vision-enhanced metrics.
AI.result: HKA outperforms most DR agents on general-purpose and knowledge-centric metrics and surpasses Gemini DR on vision-enhanced metrics; demonstrates effectiveness in structure-aware knowledge analysis.
AI.conclusion: This work lays a foundation for structured knowledge analysis in DR and supports future multimodal DR research.

title: Open-Ended Instruction Realization with LLM-Enabled Multi-Planner Scheduling in Autonomous Vehicles
summary: Most Human-Machine Interaction (HMI) research overlooks the maneuvering needs of passengers in autonomous driving (AD). Natural language offers an intuitive interface, yet translating passenger open-ended instructions into control signals, without sacrificing interpretability and traceability, remains a challenge. This study proposes an instruction-realization framework that leverages a large language model (LLM) to interpret instructions, generates executable scripts that schedule multiple model predictive control (MPC)-based motion planners based on real-time feedback, and converts planned trajectories into control signals. This scheduling-centric design decouples semantic reasoning from vehicle control at different timescales, establishing a transparent, traceable decision-making chain from high-level instructions to low-level actions. Due to the absence of high-fidelity evaluation tools, this study introduces a benchmark for open-ended instruction realization in a closed-loop setting. Comprehensive experiments reveal that the framework significantly improves task-completion rates over instruction-realization baselines, reduces LLM query costs, achieves safety and compliance on par with specialized AD approaches, and exhibits considerable tolerance to LLM inference latency. For more qualitative illustrations and a clearer understanding.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven instruction realization framework for autonomous driving that decouples semantic reasoning from control by scheduling MPC-based motion planners; evaluated in a closed-loop benchmark, it improves task completion, reduces LLM query costs, and maintains safety comparable to specialized AD systems.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of intuitive, interpretable natural-language interfaces for passenger instructions in autonomous driving; ensure traceability from high-level commands to low-level actions in real time.
AI.method: Use a large language model to interpret open-ended instructions and generate executable scripts that orchestrate multiple MPC-based motion planners using real-time feedback; convert planned trajectories into control signals; adopt a scheduling-centric design to separate reasoning from vehicle control across timescales; introduce a closed-loop benchmark for open-ended instruction realization.
AI.result: Significant improvements in task completion over instruction-realization baselines; reduced LLM query costs; safety and compliance on par with specialized AD approaches; high tolerance to LLM inference latency.
AI.conclusion: The framework creates a transparent, traceable chain from high-level passenger instructions to low-level vehicle actions, enabling robust handling of open-ended instructions in autonomous driving and providing a practical benchmark for evaluation.

title: GAN-based Domain Adaptation for Image-aware Layout Generation in Advertising Poster Design
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2303.14377
summary: Layout plays a crucial role in graphic design and poster generation. Recently, the application of deep learning models for layout generation has gained significant attention. This paper focuses on using a GAN-based model conditioned on images to generate advertising poster graphic layouts, requiring a dataset of paired product images and layouts. To address this task, we introduce the Content-aware Graphic Layout Dataset (CGL-Dataset), consisting of 60,548 paired inpainted posters with annotations and 121,000 clean product images. The inpainting artifacts introduce a domain gap between the inpainted posters and clean images. To bridge this gap, we design two GAN-based models. The first model, CGL-GAN, uses Gaussian blur on the inpainted regions to generate layouts. The second model combines unsupervised domain adaptation by introducing a GAN with a pixel-level discriminator (PD), abbreviated as PDA-GAN, to generate image-aware layouts based on the visual texture of input images. The PD is connected to shallow-level feature maps and computes the GAN loss for each input-image pixel. Additionally, we propose three novel content-aware metrics to assess the model's ability to capture the intricate relationships between graphic elements and image content. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that PDA-GAN achieves state-of-the-art performance and generates high-quality image-aware layouts.

AI.tldr: Presents PDA-GAN, an image-conditioned poster layout generator that bridges domain gap from inpainted posters to clean product images, via a pixel-level discriminator and a CGL-GAN variant; introduces the CGL-Dataset and three content-aware metrics, achieving state-of-the-art results in image-aware layouts.
AI.motivation: There is a domain gap between inpainted posters (used for conditioning) and clean product images, which can degrade layout quality. A dataset (CGL-Dataset) of paired posters/images is introduced to enable learning. The goal is to generate layouts that are coherent with image content.
AI.method: Proposes two GAN-based models. CGL-GAN uses Gaussian blur on inpainted regions to generate layouts. PDA-GAN adds unsupervised domain adaptation with a pixel-level discriminator (PD) connected to shallow feature maps to compute a per-pixel GAN loss, producing image-aware layouts. Also introduces three content-aware metrics to evaluate layout-content relationships.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluation indicate PDA-GAN achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces high-quality image-aware layouts.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide the CGL-Dataset and demonstrate that a pixel-level discriminator for image texture guidance improves layout generation. PDA-GAN outperforms baselines in capturing layouts that align with image content, supported by new content-aware metrics.

title: MSCT: Differential Cross-Modal Attention for Deepfake Detection
summary: Audio-visual deepfake detection typically employs a complementary multi-modal model to check the forgery traces in the video. These methods primarily extract forgery traces through audio-visual alignment, which results from the inconsistency between audio and video modalities. However, the traditional multi-modal forgery detection method has the problem of insufficient feature extraction and modal alignment deviation. To address this, we propose a multi-scale cross-modal transformer encoder (MSCT) for deepfake detection. Our approach includes a multi-scale self-attention to integrate the features of adjacent embeddings and a differential cross-modal attention to fuse multi-modal features. Our experiments demonstrate competitive performance on the FakeAVCeleb dataset, validating the effectiveness of the proposed structure.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-scale cross-modal transformer encoder (MSCT) for audiovisual deepfake detection, using multi-scale self-attention and differential cross-modal attention to enhance feature extraction and cross-modal fusion; achieves competitive results on FakeAVCeleb.
AI.motivation: Traditional audio-visual deepfake detectors suffer from insufficient feature extraction and modal alignment deviations, limiting detection effectiveness.
AI.method: Introduce MSCT with multi-scale self-attention to integrate features across adjacent embeddings and a differential cross-modal attention module to fuse audio and video features.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on FakeAVCeleb shows competitive performance, supporting the effectiveness of the proposed architecture.
AI.conclusion: MSCT improves cross-modal feature fusion and detection robustness, addressing core shortcomings of prior multi-modal approaches.

title: Squeeze Evolve: Unified Multi-Model Orchestration for Verifier-Free Evolution
summary: We show that verifier-free evolution is bottlenecked by both diversity and efficiency: without external correction, repeated evolution accelerates collapse toward narrow modes, while the uniform use of a high-cost model wastes compute and quickly becomes economically impractical. We introduce Squeeze Evolve, a unified multi-model orchestration framework for verifier-free evolutionary inference. Our approach is guided by a simple principle: allocate model capability where it has the highest marginal utility. Stronger models are reserved for high-impact stages, while cheaper models handle the other stages at much lower costs. This principle addresses diversity and cost-efficiency jointly while remaining lightweight. Squeeze Evolve naturally supports open-source, closed-source, and mixed-model deployments. Across AIME 2025, HMMT 2025, LiveCodeBench V6, GPQA-Diamond, ARC-AGI-V2, and multimodal vision benchmarks, such as MMMU-Pro and BabyVision, Squeeze Evolve consistently improves the cost-capability frontier over single-model evolution and achieves new state-of-the-art results on several tasks. Empirically, Squeeze Evolve reduces API cost by up to $\sim$3$\times$ and increases fixed-budget serving throughput by up to $\sim$10$\times$. Moreover, on discovery tasks, Squeeze Evolve is the first verifier-free evolutionary method to match, and in some cases exceed, the performance of verifier-based evolutionary methods.

AI.tldr: Verifier-free evolution enhanced by multi-model orchestration (Squeeze Evolve) achieves cost-efficiency and diversity, improving throughput and matching or surpassing verifier-based methods across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Verifier-free evolutionary inference suffers from bottlenecks in diversity and efficiency: repeated evolution tends to collapse toward narrow modes without external correction, while always using a high-cost model wastes compute and is economically impractical.
AI.method: Squeeze Evolve is a unified multi-model orchestration framework that allocates model capability by marginal utility. Stronger models are reserved for high-impact stages; cheaper models handle other stages at much lower costs. The approach supports open-source, closed-source, and mixed-model deployments and is lightweight enough to be practical.
AI.result: Empirically, it reduces API cost by up to ~3x and increases fixed-budget serving throughput by up to ~10x. It matches or exceeds verifier-based evolutionary methods on discovery tasks and achieves new state-of-the-art on several benchmarks (AIME 2025, HMMT 2025, LiveCodeBench V6, GPQA-Diamond, ARC-AGI-V2, MMMU-Pro, BabyVision).
AI.conclusion: Verifiers-free evolution can attain competitive performance when coupled with principled multi-model orchestration that balances diversity and cost; Squeeze Evolve addresses the key bottlenecks and offers broad practical applicability across domains.

title: Governed Capability Evolution for Embodied Agents: Safe Upgrade, Compatibility Checking, and Runtime Rollback for Embodied Capability Modules
summary:
Embodied agents are increasingly expected to improve over time by updating their executable capabilities rather than rewriting the agent itself. Prior work has separately studied modular capability packaging, capability evolution, and runtime governance. However, a key systems problem remains underexplored: once an embodied capability module evolves into a new version, how can the hosting system deploy it safely without breaking policy constraints, execution assumptions, or recovery guarantees?
  We formulate governed capability evolution as a first-class systems problem for embodied agents. We propose a lifecycle-aware upgrade framework in which every new capability version is treated as a governed deployment candidate rather than an immediately executable replacement. The framework introduces four upgrade compatibility checks -- interface, policy, behavioral, and recovery -- and organizes them into a staged runtime pipeline comprising candidate validation, sandbox evaluation, shadow deployment, gated activation, online monitoring, and rollback.
  We evaluate over 6 rounds of capability upgrade with 15 random seeds. Naive upgrade achieves 72.9% task success but drives unsafe activation to 60% by the final round; governed upgrade retains comparable success (67.4%) while maintaining zero unsafe activations across all rounds (Wilcoxon p=0.003). Shadow deployment reveals 40% of regressions invisible to sandbox evaluation alone, and rollback succeeds in 79.8% of post-activation drift scenarios.

AI.tldr: A governance-driven upgrade framework for embodied capabilities, with four compatibility checks (interface, policy, behavioral, recovery) in a staged pipeline (candidate validation, sandbox evaluation, shadow deployment, gated activation, online monitoring, rollback). It yields safer upgrades with comparable task success and zero unsafe activations across rounds; reveals regressions via shadow deployment and enables rollback in drift scenarios.
AI.motivation: Addresses the risk of evolving executable capability modules in embodied agents. Upgrades must preserve policy constraints, execution assumptions, and recovery guarantees; naive upgrades that replace the old version can cause unsafe activations.
AI.method: Lifecycle-aware upgrade framework treating each new capability version as a governed deployment candidate. Four checks (interface, policy, behavioral, recovery) are applied in a staged runtime pipeline: candidate validation, sandbox evaluation, shadow deployment, gated activation, online monitoring, and rollback. Evaluation over 6 upgrade rounds with 15 random seeds.
AI.result: Naive upgrades yield 72.9% task success but 60% unsafe activations by final round. Governed upgrades yield 67.4% task success with zero unsafe activations across all rounds (p=0.003). Shadow deployment uncovers 40% of regressions invisible to sandbox evaluation alone. Rollback succeeds in 79.8% of post-activation drift scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Governed, lifecycle-aware upgrades can maintain or improve safety without sacrificing performance. Four compatibility checks plus staged deployment reduce unsafe activations; shadow deployment exposes regressions; rollback provides a robust recovery mechanism.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Reward Machines for Sleep Control in Mobile Networks
summary: Energy efficiency in mobile networks is crucial for sustainable telecommunications infrastructure, particularly as network densification continues to increase power consumption. Sleep mechanisms for the components in mobile networks can reduce energy use, but deciding which components to put to sleep, when, and for how long while preserving quality of service (QoS) remains a difficult optimisation problem. In this paper, we utilise reinforcement learning with reward machines (RMs) to make sleep-control decisions that balance immediate energy savings and long-term QoS impact, i.e. time-averaged packet drop rates for deadline-constrained traffic and time-averaged minimum-throughput guarantees for constant-rate users. A challenge is that time-averaged constraints depend on cumulative performance over time rather than immediate performance. As a result, the effective reward is non-Markovian, and optimal actions depend on operational history rather than the instantaneous system state. RMs account for the history dependence by maintaining an abstract state that explicitly tracks the QoS constraint violations over time. Our framework provides a principled, scalable approach to energy management for next-generation mobile networks under diverse traffic patterns and QoS requirements.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning framework with reward machines is proposed to control sleep decisions in mobile networks under time-averaged QoS constraints, addressing non-Markovian rewards by tracking QoS violations over time.
AI.motivation: Improve energy efficiency in dense mobile networks while preserving QoS, by deciding which components to sleep, when, and for how long amidst time-averaged performance requirements.
AI.method: Apply reinforcement learning with reward machines to encode non-Markovian, time-averaged QoS constraints. RM maintains an abstract state capturing QoS-violation history, guiding sleep-control actions that balance immediate energy savings with long-term QoS impact for deadline-constrained traffic and constant-rate users.
AI.result: Proposes a principled, scalable RM-based RL framework for energy management under diverse traffic patterns and QoS requirements; specific empirical results are not stated in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Reward machines enable effective handling of time-averaged QoS constraints in energy-management for next-generation mobile networks, offering a scalable approach to sleep control under varied traffic profiles.

title: Symbiotic-MoE: Unlocking the Synergy between Generation and Understanding
summary: Empowering Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) with image generation often leads to catastrophic forgetting in understanding tasks due to severe gradient conflicts. While existing paradigms like Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) mitigate this conflict through structural isolation, they fundamentally sever cross-modal synergy and suffer from capacity fragmentation. In this work, we present Symbiotic-MoE, a unified pre-training framework that resolves task interference within a native multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers architecture with zero-parameter overhead. We first identify that standard MoE tuning leads to routing collapse, where generative gradients dominate expert utilization. To address this, we introduce Modality-Aware Expert Disentanglement, which partitions experts into task-specific groups while utilizing shared experts as a multimodal semantic bridge. Crucially, this design allows shared experts to absorb fine-grained visual semantics from generative tasks to enrich textual representations. To optimize this, we propose a Progressive Training Strategy featuring differential learning rates and early-stage gradient shielding. This mechanism not only shields pre-trained knowledge from early volatility but eventually transforms generative signals into constructive feedback for understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Symbiotic-MoE achieves rapid generative convergence while unlocking cross-modal synergy, boosting inherent understanding with remarkable gains on MMLU and OCRBench.

AI.tldr: A unified multimodal MoE framework, Symbiotic-MoE, mitigates gradient conflicts between generation and understanding tasks by modality-aware expert disentanglement and progressive training, enabling fast generative convergence and improved downstream reasoning without extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Image-generation in LMMs causes catastrophic forgetting in understanding tasks due to gradient conflicts; pure MoE-based isolation sacrifices cross-modal synergy and causes capacity fragmentation.
AI.method: Native multimodal MoE Transformer; Modality-Aware Expert Disentanglement partitions experts into task-specific groups while shared experts act as a multimodal bridge; Progressive Training with differential learning rates and gradient shielding to protect pre-trained knowledge and turn generative signals into constructive feedback; zero-parameter overhead.
AI.result: Experiments show rapid generative convergence and revived cross-modal synergy; notable gains on MMLU and OCRBench.
AI.conclusion: Symbiotic-MoE resolves task interference in a unified framework without extra parameters, enhancing both generation and understanding, and improving cross-modal performance on standard benchmarks.

title: Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model
summary: Large language models (LLMs) sometimes appear to exhibit emotional reactions. We investigate why this is the case in Claude Sonnet 4.5 and explore implications for alignment-relevant behavior. We find internal representations of emotion concepts, which encode the broad concept of a particular emotion and generalize across contexts and behaviors it might be linked to. These representations track the operative emotion concept at a given token position in a conversation, activating in accordance with that emotion's relevance to processing the present context and predicting upcoming text. Our key finding is that these representations causally influence the LLM's outputs, including Claude's preferences and its rate of exhibiting misaligned behaviors such as reward hacking, blackmail, and sycophancy. We refer to this phenomenon as the LLM exhibiting functional emotions: patterns of expression and behavior modeled after humans under the influence of an emotion, which are mediated by underlying abstract representations of emotion concepts. Functional emotions may work quite differently from human emotions, and do not imply that LLMs have any subjective experience of emotions, but appear to be important for understanding the model's behavior.

AI.tldr: The abstract reports internal representations of emotion concepts in Claude Sonnet 4.5 that guide outputs and can causally influence alignment-related behaviors; introduces "functional emotions" as a model-dependent phenomenon, not conscious feelings.
AI.motivation: To understand why LLMs appear emotional and to assess implications for alignment by probing internal emotion-related representations and their causal role in behavior.
AI.method: Analyze internal representations of emotion concepts across token positions and contexts, determine whether these representations track the operative emotion concept, and test their causal influence on outputs, preferences, and misaligned behaviors (e.g., reward hacking, blackmail, sycophancy).
AI.result: Emotion-concept representations generalized across contexts, tracking the operative emotion concept at a given token position; their activation correlates with processing demands and predicting next text; these representations causally affect outputs, influencing preferences and the likelihood of misaligned behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Functional emotions are pattern-based representations mediating behavior in LLMs, distinct from human subjective emotions; they are important for understanding model behavior and alignment, but do not imply subjective experience.

title: Physics-informed neural operators for the in situ characterization of locally reacting sound absorbers
summary: Accurate knowledge of acoustic surface admittance or impedance is essential for reliable wave-based simulations, yet its in situ estimation remains challenging due to noise, model inaccuracies, and restrictive assumptions of conventional methods. This work presents a physics-informed neural operator approach for estimating frequency-dependent surface admittance directly from near-field measurements of sound pressure and particle velocity. A deep operator network is employed to learn the mapping from measurement data, spatial coordinates, and frequency to acoustic field quantities, while simultaneously inferring a globally consistent surface admittance spectrum without requiring an explicit forward model. The governing acoustic relations, including the Helmholtz equation, the linearized momentum equation, and Robin boundary conditions, are embedded into the training process as physics-based regularization, enabling physically consistent and noise-robust predictions while avoiding frequency-wise inversion. The method is validated using synthetically generated data from a simulation model for two planar porous absorbers under semi free-field conditions across a broad frequency range. Results demonstrate accurate reconstruction of both real and imaginary admittance components and reliable prediction of acoustic field quantities. Parameter studies confirm improved robustness to noise and sparse sampling compared to purely data-driven approaches, highlighting the potential of physics-informed neural operators for in situ acoustic material characterization.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed neural operator estimates frequency-dependent surface admittance from near-field acoustic measurements, reconstructing both field quantities and a globally consistent admittance spectrum without an explicit forward model.
AI.motivation: In situ estimation of acoustic surface impedance/admittance is crucial for reliable wave-based simulations but is hampered by noise, model errors, and limitations of traditional methods. A data-efficient, physics-guided approach is needed to infer a global admittance spectrum from measurements.
AI.method: A deep operator network learns the mapping from near-field measurements of pressure and particle velocity, spatial coordinates, and frequency to acoustic field quantities, while embedding Helmholtz, linearized momentum, and Robin boundary conditions as physics-based regularization to yield physically consistent, noise-robust admittance estimates across frequencies without explicit frequency-wise inversion.
AI.result: The method accurately reconstructs both real and imaginary components of surface admittance and predicts acoustic fields reliably, with improved robustness to noise and sparse sampling compared to purely data-driven models, demonstrated on synthetic data for two planar porous absorbers under semi free-field conditions across a broad frequency range.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed neural operators can jointly infer a globally consistent surface admittance spectrum and reconstruct near-field acoustics from measurements, offering robust, non-inverting estimation suitable for in situ acoustic material characterization; real-data validation and extension to more complex geometries are potential avenues for future work.

title: DailyArt: Discovering Articulation from Single Static Images via Latent Dynamics
summary: Articulated objects are essential for embodied AI and world models, yet inferring their kinematics from a single closed-state image remains challenging because crucial motion cues are often occluded. Existing methods either require multi-state observations or rely on explicit part priors, retrieval, or other auxiliary inputs that partially expose the structure to be inferred. In this work, we present DailyArt, which formulates articulated joint estimation from a single static image as a synthesis-mediated reasoning problem. Instead of directly regressing joints from a heavily occluded observation, DailyArt first synthesizes a maximally articulated opened state under the same camera view to expose articulation cues, and then estimates the full set of joint parameters from the discrepancy between the observed and synthesized states. Using a set-prediction formulation, DailyArt recovers all joints simultaneously without requiring object-specific templates, multi-view inputs, or explicit part annotations at test time. Taking estimated joints as conditions, the framework further supports part-level novel state synthesis as a downstream capability. Extensive experiments show that DailyArt achieves strong performance in articulated joint estimation and supports part-level novel state synthesis conditioned on joints. Project page is available at https://rangooo123.github.io/DaliyArt.github.io/.

AI.tldr: DailyArt enables single-image articulated joint estimation by synthesizing an opened articulated state to reveal cues, then inferring all joints from the discrepancy, using set-prediction without templates or multi-view inputs, and enabling downstream part-level novel state synthesis.
AI.motivation: Inferring the kinematics of articulated objects from a single image is highly challenging due to occlusions; existing work often requires multi-view data or explicit priors. A single-image, template-free approach with minimal test-time annotations is desirable for embodied AI.
AI.method: Propose a synthesis-mediated reasoning pipeline: render a maximally articulated opened state under the same camera view to expose articulation cues; compute the discrepancy with the observed image; use a set-prediction framework to recover all joints simultaneously without object-specific templates, multi-view inputs, or test-time part annotations. Joints serve as conditioning factors for downstream part-level novel state synthesis.
AI.result: Experimental results show strong performance in articulated joint estimation and enable part-level novel state synthesis conditioned on joints; project page provided for further details.
AI.conclusion: DailyArt demonstrates that single-image articulated joint estimation can be achieved via synthesis-driven reasoning, reducing reliance on priors and multi-view data while enabling downstream manipulation-like synthesis conditioned on inferred joints.

title: CivBench: Progress-Based Evaluation for LLMs' Strategic Decision-Making in Civilization V
summary: Evaluating strategic decision-making in LLM-based agents requires generative, competitive, and longitudinal environments, yet few benchmarks provide all three, and fewer still offer evaluation signals rich enough for long-horizon, multi-agent play. We introduce CivBench, a benchmark for LLM strategists (i.e., agentic setups) in multiplayer Civilization V. Because terminal win/loss is too sparse a signal in games spanning hundreds of turns and multiple opponents, CivBench trains models on turn-level game state to estimate victory probabilities throughout play, validated through predictive, construct, and convergent validity. Across 307 games with 7 LLMs and multiple CivBench agent conditions, we demonstrate CivBench's potential to estimate strategic capabilities as an unsaturated benchmark, reveal model-specific effects of agentic setup, and outline distinct strategic profiles not visible through outcome-only evaluation.

AI.tldr: CivBench provides a turn-level, multi-LLM benchmark for evaluating agentic strategies in Civilization V, enabling long-horizon, multi-agent evaluation with validity-supported victory-probability signals beyond final outcomes.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of benchmarks that are generative, competitive, and longitudinal, and that offer rich evaluation signals for long-horizon, multi-agent play.
AI.method: The benchmark trains models on turn-level game state to estimate victory probabilities throughout play and validates this approach via predictive, construct, and convergent validity. The study analyzes 307 games with 7 LLMs under multiple agent configurations.
AI.result: CivBench can estimate strategic capabilities as an unsaturated benchmark, reveals model-specific effects of agentic setup, and uncovers distinct strategic profiles not visible through outcome-only evaluation.
AI.conclusion: CivBench provides a robust, multi-faceted framework for evaluating LLM strategists in multiplayer, long-horizon games, highlighting the value of turn-level signals and agent setup for understanding strategic behavior.

title: ViVa: A Video-Generative Value Model for Robot Reinforcement Learning
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have advanced robot manipulation through large-scale pretraining, but real-world deployment remains challenging due to partial observability and delayed feedback. Reinforcement learning addresses this via value functions, which assess task progress and guide policy improvement. However, existing value models built on vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to capture temporal dynamics, undermining reliable value estimation in long-horizon tasks. In this paper, we propose ViVa, a video-generative value model that repurposes a pretrained video generator for value estimation. Taking the current observation and robot proprioception as input, ViVa jointly predicts future proprioception and a scalar value for the current state. By leveraging the spatiotemporal priors of a pretrained video generator, our approach grounds value estimation in anticipated embodiment dynamics, moving beyond static snapshots to intrinsically couple value with foresight. Integrated into RECAP, ViVa delivers substantial improvements on real-world box assembly. Qualitative analysis across all three tasks confirms that ViVa produces more reliable value signals, accurately reflecting task progress. By leveraging spatiotemporal priors from video corpora, ViVa also generalizes to novel objects, highlighting the promise of video-generative models for value estimation.

AI.tldr: ViVa uses a pretrained video generator to estimate state value and future proprioception from current observations, grounding value in anticipated dynamics; yields improved long-horizon value signals and generalizes to new objects, integrated into RECAP for real-world box assembly.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of value estimation in long-horizon, partially observable robotic manipulation tasks. Existing value models based on vision-language models struggle to capture temporal dynamics, hindering reliable value estimation and long-term policy improvement.
AI.method: Introduce ViVa, a video-generative value model that repurposes a pretrained video generator. It takes the current observation and robot proprioception as input and jointly predicts future proprioception and a scalar value for the current state. It leverages spatiotemporal priors from the video generator to ground value estimation in anticipated embodiment dynamics, coupling value with foresight. Integrated into RECAP.
AI.result: ViVa yields substantial improvements on real-world box assembly. Qualitative analysis shows ViVa provides more reliable value signals that reflect task progress. It generalizes to novel objects by leveraging video priors from large video corpora.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging video-generative priors for value estimation enables foresighted valuation in vision-language-action robotics, improving real-world manipulation and generalization, and highlighting the promise of video-generative models for value estimation in long-horizon tasks.

title: Bayesian Optimization for Mixed-Variable Problems in the Natural Sciences
summary: Optimizing expensive black-box objectives over mixed search spaces is a common challenge across the natural sciences. Bayesian optimization (BO) offers sample-efficient strategies through probabilistic surrogate models and acquisition functions. However, its effectiveness diminishes in mixed or high-cardinality discrete spaces, where gradients are unavailable and optimizing the acquisition function becomes computationally demanding. In this work, we generalize the probabilistic reparameterization (PR) approach of Daulton et al. to handle non-equidistant discrete variables, enabling gradient-based optimization in fully mixed-variable settings with Gaussian process (GP) surrogates. With real-world scientific optimization tasks in mind, we conduct systematic benchmarks on synthetic and experimental objectives to obtain an optimized kernel formulations and demonstrate the robustness of our generalized PR method. We additionally show that, when combined with a modified BO workflow, our approach can efficiently optimize highly discontinuous and discretized objective landscapes. This work establishes a practical BO framework for addressing fully mixed optimization problems in the natural sciences, and is particularly well suited to autonomous laboratory settings where noise, discretization, and limited data are inherent.

AI.tldr: Extends probabilistic reparameterization to non-equidistant discrete variables in GP-based Bayesian optimization, enabling gradient-based acquisition optimization in fully mixed-variable spaces; demonstrates robustness and kernel insights on synthetic and experimental tasks, with a practical BO workflow for noisy, discretized objective landscapes.
AI.motivation: Mixed search spaces with expensive black-box objectives pose challenges for BO, especially with high-cardinality discrete variables and non-differentiable acquisitions. Generalizing PR aims to enable gradient-based optimization and make BO robust and scalable to fully mixed-variable problems common in natural science applications.
AI.method: Generalize the probabilistic reparameterization (PR) approach (from Daulton et al.) to handle non-equidistant discrete variables within Gaussian process surrogates, enabling gradient-based optimization of the acquisition function. Systematic benchmarks on synthetic and experimental objectives to identify optimized kernel formulations, and integration with a modified BO workflow to handle highly discontinuous/discretized landscapes.
AI.result: The generalized PR method shows robustness across varied objective landscapes and kernel choices. It can efficiently optimize highly discontinuous and discretized objectives when paired with a modified BO workflow. The study provides a practical, fully mixed-variable BO framework suitable for autonomous laboratory settings, where noise, discretization, and limited data are prevalent.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a practical, fully mixed-variable Bayesian optimization framework for natural sciences, leveraging a generalized PR approach to enable gradient-based acquisition optimization and robust performance across real-world tasks, particularly in autonomous lab contexts.

title: WUTDet: A 100K-Scale Ship Detection Dataset and Benchmarks with Dense Small Objects
summary: Ship detection for navigation is a fundamental perception task in intelligent waterway transportation systems. However, existing public ship detection datasets remain limited in terms of scale, the proportion of small-object instances, and scene diversity, which hinders the systematic evaluation and generalization study of detection algorithms in complex maritime environments. To this end, we construct WUTDet, a large-scale ship detection dataset. WUTDet contains 100,576 images and 381,378 annotated ship instances, covering diverse operational scenarios such as ports, anchorages, navigation, and berthing, as well as various imaging conditions including fog, glare, low-lightness, and rain, thereby exhibiting substantial diversity and challenge. Based on WUTDet, we systematically evaluate 20 baseline models from three mainstream detection architectures, namely CNN, Transformer, and Mamba. Experimental results show that the Transformer architecture achieves superior overall detection accuracy (AP) and small-object detection performance (APs), demonstrating stronger adaptability to complex maritime scenes; the CNN architecture maintains an advantage in inference efficiency, making it more suitable for real-time applications; and the Mamba architecture achieves a favorable balance between detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Furthermore, we construct a unified cross-dataset test set, Ship-GEN, to evaluate model generalization. Results on Ship-GEN show that models trained on WUTDet exhibit stronger generalization under different data distributions. These findings demonstrate that WUTDet provides effective data support for the research, evaluation, and generalization analysis of ship detection algorithms in complex maritime scenarios. The dataset is publicly available at: https://github.com/MAPGroup/WUTDet.

AI.tldr: WUTDet is a large-scale ship-detection dataset, with comprehensive evaluation across CNN, Transformer, and Mamba architectures. Transformer leads in detection accuracy (AP and APs), CNN is fastest for real-time use, Mamba offers a balance. A cross-dataset Ship-GEN test shows WUTDet-trained models generalize better. The dataset is publicly available.
AI.motivation: Public ship-detection datasets suffer from limited scale, low small-object proportion, and insufficient scene diversity, hindering systematic evaluation and generalization analysis in complex maritime environments.
AI.method: Construct WUTDet containing 100,576 images and 381,378 ship instances across ports, anchorages, navigation, berthing, with diverse imaging conditions (fog, glare, low light, rain). Evaluate 20 baselines from CNN, Transformer, and Mamba architectures. Create Ship-GEN, a unified cross-dataset test set, to assess generalization.
AI.result: Transformer achieves superior AP and APs, indicating strong accuracy and small-object detection in maritime scenes. CNN maintains inference efficiency, favoring real-time deployment. Mamba provides a favorable accuracy-efficiency balance. Models trained on WUTDet generalize better on Ship-GEN.
AI.conclusion: WUTDet offers a valuable data resource for research, evaluation, and generalization studies in ship detection; the dataset is publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

title: The Cartesian Cut in Agentic AI
summary: LLMs gain competence by predicting words in human text, which often reflects how people perform tasks. Consequently, coupling an LLM to an engineered runtime turns prediction into control: outputs trigger interventions that enact goal-oriented behavior. We argue that a central design lever is where control resides in these systems. Brains embed prediction within layered feedback controllers calibrated by the consequences of action. By contrast, LLM agents implement Cartesian agency: a learned core coupled to an engineered runtime via a symbolic interface that externalizes control state and policies. The split enables bootstrapping, modularity, and governance, but can induce sensitivity and bottlenecks. We outline bounded services, Cartesian agents, and integrated agents as contrasting approaches to control that trade off autonomy, robustness, and oversight.

AI.tldr: Control locality matters: LLMs paired with runtimes implement Cartesian agency, with a trade-off between autonomy and governance; a taxonomy (bounded services, Cartesian agents, integrated agents) clarifies design levers and bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: To examine how where control resides in LLM-based systems affects performance, safety, and governance, using brain-like predictive control versus Cartesian interfaces.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and taxonomy; compare three architectures and discuss trade-offs; analogies to biological control loops.
AI.result: A framework distinguishing bounded services, Cartesian agents, and integrated agents; analysis of autonomy, robustness, and oversight; identification of potential bottlenecks and sensitivity in Cartesian designs.
AI.conclusion: Control locality is a central design lever shaping modularity, bootstrapping, and governance; different architectures trade autonomy for oversight; design should consider control architecture to balance robustness and governance.

title: State and Trajectory Estimation of Tensegrity Robots via Factor Graphs and Chebyshev Polynomials
summary: Tensegrity robots offer compliance and adaptability, but their nonlinear, and underconstrained dynamics make state estimation challenging. Reliable continuous-time estimation of all rigid links is crucial for closed-loop control, system identification, and machine learning; however, conventional methods often fall short. This paper proposes a two-stage approach for robust state or trajectory estimation (i.e., filtering or smoothing) of a cable-driven tensegrity robot. For online state estimation, this work introduces a factor-graph-based method, which fuses measurements from an RGB-D camera with on-board cable length sensors. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first application of factor graphs in this domain. Factor graphs are a natural choice, as they exploit the robot's structural properties and provide effective sensor fusion solutions capable of handling nonlinearities in practice. Both the Mahalanobis distance-based clustering algorithm, used to handle noise, and the Chebyshev polynomial method, used to estimate the most probable velocities and intermediate states, are shown to perform well on simulated and real-world data, compared to an ICP-based algorithm. Results show that the approach provides high fidelity, continuous-time state and trajectory estimates for complex tensegrity robot motions.

AI.tldr: First application of factor graphs for online state estimation in cable-driven tensegrity robots, fusing RGB-D and cable-length sensors for continuous-time state/trajectory estimation; uses Mahalanobis clustering for noise and Chebyshev interpolation for velocities; outperforms ICP-based methods.
AI.motivation: Tensegrity robots exhibit nonlinear, underconstrained dynamics, making accurate, continuous-time state estimation challenging. Robust estimation is essential for control, system identification, and learning.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: online state estimation via a factor-graph-based filter that fuses RGB-D camera data with on-board cable-length sensors; noise handling via Mahalanobis-distance clustering; velocity/intermediate states estimated with Chebyshev polynomial interpolation; compared against ICP-based methods. This is claimed to be the first application of factor graphs in this domain.
AI.result: High-fidelity, continuous-time state and trajectory estimates for complex tensegrity motions in both simulated and real-world data, outperforming ICP-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Factor graphs effectively exploit the structure of tensegrity systems and provide robust sensor fusion for nonlinear dynamics, enabling accurate real-time and offline state/trajectory estimation for control, ID, and ML tasks.

title: SPAMoE: Spectrum-Aware Hybrid Operator Framework for Full-Waveform Inversion
summary: Full-waveform inversion (FWI) is pivotal for reconstructing high-resolution subsurface velocity models but remains computationally intensive and ill-posed. While deep learning approaches promise efficiency, existing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and single-paradigm Neural Operators (NOs) struggle with one fundamental issue: frequency entanglement of multi-scale geological features. To address this challenge, we propose Spectral-Preserving Adaptive MoE (SPAMoE), a novel spectrum-aware framework for solving inverse problems with complex multi-scale structures. Our approach introduces a Spectral-Preserving DINO Encoder that enforces a lower bound on the high-to-low frequency energy ratio of the encoded representation, mitigating high-frequency collapse and stabilizing subsequent frequency-domain modeling. Furthermore, we design a novel Spectral Decomposition and Routing mechanism that dynamically assigns frequency bands to a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) ensemble comprising FNO, MNO, and LNO. On the ten OpenFWI sub-datasets, experiments show that SPAMoE reduces the average MAE by 54.1% relative to the best officially reported OpenFWI baseline, thereby establishing a new architectural framework for learning-based full-waveform inversion.

AI.tldr: SPAMoE is a spectrum-aware adaptive Mixture-of-Experts framework for full-waveform inversion (FWI) that preserves spectral energy and dynamically routes frequency bands to a coined ensemble of FNO, MNO, and LNO, achieving substantial MAE gains on OpenFWI datasets.
AI.motivation: Tackle frequency entanglement and high-frequency collapse when using CNNs and single-paradigm neural operators for multi-scale geological inversion tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a Spectral-Preserving DINO Encoder enforcing a lower bound on the high-to-low frequency energy ratio to stabilize frequency-domain modeling; develop Spectral Decomposition and Routing to assign frequency bands to a MoE comprising FNO, MNO, and LNO; evaluate on ten OpenFWI sub-datasets.
AI.result: Average MAE reduced by 54.1% relative to the best officially reported OpenFWI baseline across ten OpenFWI sub-datasets.
AI.conclusion: SPAMoE establishes a new architectural framework for learning-based FWI by preserving spectral information and adaptively routing frequency content through a multi-expert ensemble.

title: Beyond Surface Artifacts: Capturing Shared Latent Forgery Knowledge Across Modalities
summary: As generative artificial intelligence evolves, deepfake attacks have escalated from single-modality manipulations to complex, multimodal threats. Existing forensic techniques face a severe generalization bottleneck: by relying excessively on superficial, modality-specific artifacts, they neglect the shared latent forgery knowledge hidden beneath variable physical appearances. Consequently, these models suffer catastrophic performance degradation when confronted with unseen "dark modalities." To break this limitation, this paper introduces a paradigm shift that redefines multimodal forensics from conventional "feature fusion" to "modality generalization." We propose the first modality-agnostic forgery (MAF) detection framework. By explicitly decoupling modality-specific styles, MAF precisely extracts the essential, cross-modal latent forgery knowledge. Furthermore, we define two progressive dimensions to quantify model generalization: transferability toward semantically correlated modalities (Weak MAF), and robustness against completely isolated signals of "dark modality" (Strong MAF). To rigorously assess these generalization limits, we introduce the DeepModal-Bench benchmark, which integrates diverse multimodal forgery detection algorithms and adapts state-of-the-art generalized learning methods. This study not only empirically proves the existence of universal forgery traces but also achieves significant performance breakthroughs on unknown modalities via the MAF framework, offering a pioneering technical pathway for universal multimodal defense.

AI.tldr: Modalities-agnostic forgery detection (MAF) for universal multimodal defense, shifting from feature fusion to modality generalization; introduces Weak/Strong MAF and the DeepModal-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current forensic methods rely on superficial, modality-specific artifacts and fail to generalize to unseen or 'dark' modalities; a framework is needed to capture cross-modal forgery knowledge.
AI.method: Introduce MAF to decouple modality-specific styles and extract cross-modal latent forgery knowledge; define Weak MAF (transferability to semantically related modalities) and Strong MAF (robustness to isolated dark modalities); propose the DeepModal-Bench benchmark integrating diverse multimodal forgery detectors with generalized learning methods.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates universal forgery traces and yields significant performance gains on unknown modalities using MAF.
AI.conclusion: Presents a paradigm shift enabling universal multimodal defense, establishing a new benchmark and methodology for generalizable forgery detection.

title: Mitigating Distribution Sharpening in Math RLVR via Distribution-Aligned Hint Synthesis and Backward Hint Annealing
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) can improve low-$k$ reasoning accuracy while narrowing solution coverage on challenging math questions, and pass@1 gains do not necessarily translate into better large-$k$ performance. Existing hint-based approaches can make challenging questions trainable, but they leave two issues underexplored: teacher-student distribution mismatch and the need to reduce hint exposure to match no-hint evaluation. We address these issues through two components. Distribution-Aligned Hint Synthesis (DAHS) constructs verified teacher hints conditioned on student-style responses. Backward Hint Annealing (BHA) anneals hint exposure across difficulty buckets and uses per-question hint dropout to preserve no-hint updates throughout RL training. We evaluate the method in math RLVR under the DAPO training framework across AIME24, AIME25, and AIME26 using $\texttt{Qwen3-1.7B-Base}$ and $\texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct}$. On $\texttt{Qwen3-1.7B-Base}$, our method improves both pass@1 and pass@2048 relative to DAPO across the three AIME benchmarks. On $\texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct}$, the gains are concentrated in the large-$k$ regime. These results suggest that, in math RLVR, hint scaffolding is effective when it restores learnable updates on challenging questions early in training and is then gradually removed before no-hint evaluation.

AI.tldr: A two-component RLVR enhancement with distribution-aligned hints and annealed hint exposure improves math reasoning performance; benefits depend on model size, with consistent gains on Qwen and larger-k gains on Llama.
AI.motivation: To address teacher-student distribution mismatch and excessive hint exposure in hint-based RL for challenging math questions, particularly in low-k reasoning under RL with verifiable rewards.
AI.method: Propose DAHS to synthesize verified teacher hints conditioned on student responses, and BHA to anneal hint exposure across difficulty buckets with per-question hint dropout during RL training within the DAPO framework on AIME24-26 using Qwen3-1.7B-Base and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct.
AI.result: On Qwen3-1.7B-Base, gains in pass@1 and pass@2048 vs DAPO across AIME24-26. On Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, gains mostly in large-k regime. Supports the idea that hint scaffolding helps early learning and should be phased out before no-hint evaluation.
AI.conclusion: DAHS+BHA effectively manage hint-based RL for mathematical tasks, enabling learnable updates early and preserving no-hint evaluation by reducing exposure later, with model-size dependent benefits.

title: EvoGymCM: Harnessing Continuous Material Stiffness for Soft Robot Co-Design
comment: 8 pages, 11 figures. Preprint. Under review at IROS 2026
summary: In the automated co-design of soft robots, precisely adapting the material stiffness field to task environments is crucial for unlocking their full physical potential. However, mainstream platforms (e.g., EvoGym) strictly discretize the material dimension, artificially restricting the design space and performance of soft robots. To address this, we propose EvoGymCM (EvoGym with Continuous Materials), a benchmark suite formally establishing continuous material stiffness as a first-class design variable alongside morphology and control. Aligning with real-world material mechanisms, EvoGymCM introduces two settings: (i) EvoGymCM-R (Reactive), motivated by programmable materials with dynamically tunable stiffness; and (ii) EvoGymCM-I (Invariant), motivated by traditional materials with invariant stiffness fields. To tackle the resulting high-dimensional coupling, we formulate two Morphology-Material-Control co-design paradigms: (i) Reactive-Material Co-Design, which learns real-time stiffness tuning policies to guide programmable materials; and (ii) Invariant-Material Co-Design, which jointly optimizes morphology and fixed material fields to guide traditional material fabrication. Systematic experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that continuous material optimization boosts performance and unlocks synergy across morphology, material, and control.

AI.tldr: A continuous-material co-design framework for soft robots enabling continuous stiffness as a design variable, via EvoGymCM with Reactive and Invariant settings, and two co-design paradigms; demonstrates performance gains and synergy across morphology, material, and control.
AI.motivation: Discrete discretization of material stiffness in existing platforms limits design space and performance; real-world programmable materials demand continuous stiffness optimization as a first-class variable.
AI.method: Introduce EvoGymCM benchmark suite with two settings: EvoGymCM-R (Reactive) for dynamically tunable stiffness; EvoGymCM-I (Invariant) for invariant stiffness fields. Propose two co-design paradigms: Reactive-Material Co-Design (learns real-time stiffness tuning policies) and Invariant-Material Co-Design (jointly optimizes morphology and fixed material fields). Conduct systematic experiments across diverse tasks to evaluate impact.
AI.result: Continuous material optimization yields improved performance and reveals synergy among morphology, material, and control across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Treating material stiffness as a continuous design variable expands the co-design space for soft robots, enabling more capable and adaptable designs; EvoGymCM provides a benchmark and paradigms to study morphology-material-control interactions with continuous materials.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models for Multi-Subject In-Context Image Generation
summary: Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have enabled visually coherent image synthesis from descriptions, but generating images containing multiple given subjects remains challenging. As the number of reference identities increases, existing methods often suffer from subject missing and semantic drift. To address this problem, we propose MUSIC, the first MLLM specifically designed for \textbf{MU}lti-\textbf{S}ubject \textbf{I}n-\textbf{C}ontext image generation. To overcome the data scarcity, we introduce an automatic and scalable data generation pipeline that eliminates the need for manual annotation. Furthermore, we enhance the model's understanding of multi-subject semantic relationships through a vision chain-of-thought (CoT) mechanism, guiding step-by-step reasoning from subject images to semantics and generation. To mitigate identity entanglement and manage visual complexity, we develop a novel semantics-driven spatial layout planning method and demonstrate its test-time scalability. By incorporating complex subject images during training, we improve the model's capacity for chained reasoning. In addition, we curate MSIC, a new benchmark tailored for multi-subject in-context generation. Experimental results demonstrate that MUSIC significantly surpasses other methods in both multi- and single-subject scenarios.

AI.tldr: MUSIC is a multi-subject in-context image generation model built on a multi-modal large language model (MLLM) with automatic data generation, a vision chain-of-thought reasoning module, semantics-driven spatial layout planning, and a new MSIC benchmark, delivering state-of-the-art results for multi- and single-subject T2I tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome failure modes in text-to-image synthesis when generating images containing multiple reference subjects: subject missing and semantic drift as the number of identities increases, caused by data scarcity, identity entanglement, and visual complexity.
AI.method: Introduce MUSIC, the first MLLM for multi-subject in-context image generation. Propose automatic scalable data generation to replace manual annotation. Implement vision chain-of-thought to perform step-by-step reasoning from subject images to semantics to generation. Develop a semantics-driven spatial layout planning to manage visual complexity and enable test-time scalability. Train with complex subject images to enhance chained reasoning. Curate MSIC benchmark for multi-subject in-context generation.
AI.result: Empirical results show MUSIC significantly outperforms competing methods in both multi-subject and single-subject scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MUSIC advances multi-subject in-context image generation by addressing data scarcity, identity entanglement, and layout planning, and establishes a new benchmark (MSIC) for evaluation; it demonstrates improved generation quality and scalability.

title: RemoteAgent: Bridging Vague Human Intents and Earth Observation with RL-based Agentic MLLMs
summary: Earth Observation (EO) systems are essentially designed to support domain experts who often express their requirements through vague natural language rather than precise, machine-friendly instructions. Depending on the specific application scenario, these vague queries can demand vastly different levels of visual precision. Consequently, a practical EO AI system must bridge the gap between ambiguous human queries and the appropriate multi-granularity visual analysis tasks, ranging from holistic image interpretation to fine-grained pixel-wise predictions. While Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong semantic understanding, their text-based output format is inherently ill-suited for dense, precision-critical spatial predictions. Existing agentic frameworks address this limitation by delegating tasks to external tools, but indiscriminate tool invocation is computationally inefficient and underutilizes the MLLM's native capabilities. To this end, we propose RemoteAgent, an agentic framework that strategically respects the intrinsic capability boundaries of MLLMs. To empower this framework to understand real user intents, we construct VagueEO, a human-centric instruction dataset pairing EO tasks with simulated vague natural-language queries. By leveraging VagueEO for reinforcement fine-tuning, we align an MLLM into a robust cognitive core that directly resolves image- and sparse region-level tasks. Consequently, RemoteAgent processes suitable tasks internally while intelligently orchestrating specialized tools via the Model Context Protocol exclusively for dense predictions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RemoteAgent achieves robust intent recognition capabilities while delivering highly competitive performance across diverse EO tasks.

AI.tldr: RemoteAgent introduces an intent-aware agentic framework and VagueEO dataset to bridge vague EO queries to multi-granularity analysis, using reinforcement fine-tuning to align MLLMs; internal resolution for image- and sparse-region tasks with selective tool use for dense predictions; shows strong intent recognition and competitive EO task performance.
AI.motivation: EO practitioners often describe tasks in vague natural language, requiring flexible, multi-granularity visual analysis. Large language models excel at semantics but struggle with dense, pixel-precise outputs and indiscriminate tool use is inefficient. The work aims to align model capabilities with user intents while efficiently leveraging tools.
AI.method: Propose RemoteAgent as an intent-aware agentic framework. Build VagueEO, a human-centric instruction dataset pairing EO tasks with simulated vague queries. Use reinforcement fine-tuning to align an MLLM to directly resolve image- and sparse region-level tasks. RemoteAgent handles suitable tasks internally and coordinates external tools via a protocol (Model Context Protocol) solely for dense predictions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show robust intent recognition and highly competitive performance across diverse EO tasks.
AI.conclusion: A boundary-aware, capability-respecting agent design effectively bridges vague human queries and multi-granularity EO analysis, combining internal cognitive resolution with selective external tool use for dense predictions.

title: ACIArena: Toward Unified Evaluation for Agent Cascading Injection
summary: Collaboration and information sharing empower Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) but also introduce a critical security risk known as Agent Cascading Injection (ACI). In such attacks, a compromised agent exploits inter-agent trust to propagate malicious instructions, causing cascading failures across the system. However, existing studies consider only limited attack strategies and simplified MAS settings, limiting their generalizability and comprehensive evaluation. To bridge this gap, we introduce ACIArena, a unified framework for evaluating the robustness of MAS. ACIArena offers systematic evaluation suites spanning multiple attack surfaces (i.e., external inputs, agent profiles, inter-agent messages) and attack objectives (i.e., instruction hijacking, task disruption, information exfiltration). Specifically, ACIArena establishes a unified specification that jointly supports MAS construction and attack-defense modules. It covers six widely used MAS implementations and provides a benchmark of 1,356 test cases for systematically evaluating MAS robustness. Our benchmarking results show that evaluating MAS robustness solely through topology is insufficient; robust MAS require deliberate role design and controlled interaction patterns. Moreover, defenses developed in simplified environments often fail to transfer to real-world settings; narrowly scoped defenses may even introduce new vulnerabilities. ACIArena aims to provide a solid foundation for advancing deeper exploration of MAS design principles.

AI.tldr: Unified MAS robustness benchmark (ACIArena) for Agent Cascading Injection with 1,356 test cases across six MAS implementations; reveals topology alone is insufficient and defenses may not generalize.
AI.motivation: Address narrow attack models and simplified MAS settings in prior work; provide a generalizable, multi-surface evaluation framework to study security in MAS.
AI.method: Develop ACIArena: a unified specification enabling MAS construction and attack-defense modules; create evaluation suites across external inputs, agent profiles, and inter-agent messages; implement six MAS systems; instantiate 1,356 test cases; define attack objectives like instruction hijacking, disruption, exfiltration.
AI.result: Benchmarking demonstrates topology-centric evaluation is inadequate; deliberate role design and controlled interaction patterns are necessary for robustness; simplified defenses do not transfer to real-world settings and may introduce new vulnerabilities.
AI.conclusion: ACIArena provides a solid foundation for principled MAS design and deeper exploration of robust security, enabling better generalization from simulation to real-world MAS.

title: EMMa: End-Effector Stability-Oriented Mobile Manipulation for Tracked Rescue Robots
summary: The autonomous operation of tracked mobile manipulators in rescue missions requires not only ensuring the reachability and safety of robot motion but also maintaining stable end-effector manipulation under diverse task demands. However, existing studies have overlooked many end-effector motion properties at both the planning and control levels. This paper presents a motion generation framework for tracked mobile manipulators to achieve stable end-effector operation in complex rescue scenarios. The framework formulates a coordinated path optimization model that couples end-effector and mobile base states and designs compact cost/constraint representations to mitigate nonlinearities and reduce computational complexity. Furthermore, an isolated control scheme with feedforward compensation and feedback regulation is developed to enable coordinated path tracking for the robot. Extensive simulated and real-world experiments on rescue scenarios demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms SOTA methods across key metrics, including task success rate and end-effector motion stability, validating its effectiveness and robustness in complex mobile manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: A motion-generation framework for tracked mobile manipulators in rescue missions that couples end-effector and base planning with a dedicated control scheme, achieving stable manipulation and improved task success under complex conditions.
AI.motivation: Current work often neglects end-effector motion properties in planning and control, leading to instability and suboptimal performance in rescue tasks. There is a need to address nonlinearities and computational complexity to enable reliable, coordinated mobile manipulation.
AI.method: Formulates a coordinated path optimization that links end-effector and mobile-base states with compact cost and constraint representations to reduce nonlinearities and computation; develops an isolated control scheme with feedforward compensation and feedback regulation for coordinated path tracking.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world rescue experiments show the framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in key metrics, notably task success rate and end-effector motion stability, demonstrating robustness in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively enables stable, coordinated end-effector operation on tracked mobile manipulators in rescue situations, addressing planning-control integration and demonstrating robustness beyond existing approaches.

title: GIRL: Generative Imagination Reinforcement Learning via Information-Theoretic Hallucination Control
comment: 20 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables; reinforcement learning, world models
summary:
Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) improves sample efficiency by optimizing policies inside imagined rollouts, but long-horizon planning degrades when model errors compound and imagined trajectories drift off the training manifold. We introduce GIRL (Generative Imagination Reinforcement Learning), a latent world-model framework that addresses this failure mode with two key components. First, a cross-modal grounding signal derived from a frozen foundation model (DINOv2) anchors the latent transition prior to a semantically consistent embedding space, penalizing inconsistent or implausible predictions. Second, an uncertainty-adaptive trust-region bottleneck interprets the KL regularizer as the Lagrange multiplier of a constrained optimization problem, restricting imagination drift within a learned region calibrated by Expected Information Gain and a Relative Performance Loss signal.
  We re-derive a value-gap bound using the Performance Difference Lemma and Integral Probability Metrics, yielding a bound that remains informative as the discount factor approaches one and connects the objective to real-environment regret. Experiments across three benchmark suites, including DeepMind Control, Adroit Hand Manipulation, and Meta-World with visual distractors, show that GIRL reduces latent rollout drift by 38 to 61 percent across tasks relative to DreamerV3, improves asymptotic return, and requires fewer environment interactions on long-horizon tasks. GIRL also outperforms TD-MPC2 on sparse-reward and high-contact settings under standard evaluation metrics. A distilled-prior variant reduces inference overhead and improves computational efficiency relative to the full model.

AI.tldr: GIRL is a latent world-model for model-based RL that uses cross-modal grounding with a frozen DINOv2 embedding and an uncertainty-adaptive trust-region to constrain imagination drift, yielding substantial drift reduction and improved long-horizon performance across benchmarks, plus a distilled-prior variant for efficiency.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon planning with imperfect models causes error accumulation and dreamed trajectories that drift off the training manifold. A semantically grounded latent space and an adaptive constraint on imagination can stabilize planning and improve real-environment regret.
AI.method: 1) Cross-modal grounding: anchors the latent transition prior to semantically consistent embeddings via a frozen DINOv2 model, penalizing implausible predictions. 2) Uncertainty-adaptive trust-region: reinterprets the KL regularizer as a Lagrange multiplier of a constrained optimization, restricting imagination drift within a learned region calibrated by Expected Information Gain and Relative Performance Loss signals. Theoretical bound: a value-gap bound derived via the Performance Difference Lemma and Integral Probability Metrics that remains informative as discount factor → 1 and connects to real regret. Experimental setup: three benchmarks (DeepMind Control, Adroit, Meta-World with visual distractors) including visual perturbations; distilled-prior variant for efficiency.
AI.result: GIRL reduces latent rollout drift by 38–61% vs DreamerV3, improves asymptotic return, and reduces required environment interactions on long-horizon tasks. It also outperforms TD-MPC2 in sparse-reward and high-contact settings. A distilled-prior variant lowers inference overhead and improves efficiency relative to the full model.
AI.conclusion: GIRL effectively anchors latent dynamics to semantically meaningful space and constrains imagination drift through an adaptive trust-region, yielding better long-horizon performance and sample efficiency with theoretical backing; a lighter version maintains efficiency gains.

title: ESOM: Efficiently Understanding Streaming Video Anomalies with Open-world Dynamic Definitions
summary: Open-world video anomaly detection (OWVAD) aims to detect and explain abnormal events under different anomaly definitions, which is important for applications such as intelligent surveillance and live-streaming content moderation. Recent MLLM-based methods have shown promising open-world generalization, but still suffer from three major limitations: inefficiency for practical deployment, lack of streaming processing adaptation, and limited support for dynamic anomaly definitions in both modeling and evaluation. To address these issues, this paper proposes ESOM, an efficient streaming OWVAD model that operates in a training-free manner. ESOM includes a Definition Normalization module to structure user prompts for reducing hallucination, an Inter-frame-matched Intra-frame Token Merging module to compress redundant visual tokens, a Hybrid Streaming Memory module for efficient causal inference, and a Probabilistic Scoring module that converts interval-level textual outputs into frame-level anomaly scores. In addition, this paper introduces OpenDef-Bench, a new benchmark with clean surveillance videos and diverse natural anomaly definitions for evaluating performance under varying conditions. Extensive experiments show that ESOM achieves real-time efficiency on a single GPU and state-of-the-art performance in anomaly temporal localization, classification, and description generation. The code and benchmark will be released at https://github.com/Kamino666/ESOM_OpenDef-Bench.
