AI.tldr: Training-free, streaming open-world video anomaly detector ESOM with modular components and OpenDef-Bench; achieves real-time speed on a single GPU and state-of-the-art localization, classification, and description generation.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency, lack of streaming adaptation, and limited support for dynamic anomaly definitions in open-world VAD to enable practical deployment and robust evaluation.
AI.method: ESOM architecture: Definition Normalization to structure user prompts and reduce hallucination; Inter-frame-matched Intra-frame Token Merging to compress redundant visual tokens; Hybrid Streaming Memory for efficient causal inference; Probabilistic Scoring to convert interval-level textual outputs into frame-level anomaly scores; introduces OpenDef-Bench benchmark with clean surveillance videos and diverse anomaly definitions.
AI.result: Real-time efficiency on a single GPU; state-of-the-art performance in anomaly temporal localization, classification, and description generation across varying anomaly definitions.
AI.conclusion: ESOM provides a training-free, efficient, streaming-capable OWVAD framework with strong performance; OpenDef-Bench enables robust evaluation under diverse anomaly definitions; code and benchmark will be released.

title: The Accountability Horizon: An Impossibility Theorem for Governing Human-Agent Collectives
summary: Existing accountability frameworks for AI systems, legal, ethical, and regulatory, rest on a shared assumption: for any consequential outcome, at least one identifiable person had enough involvement and foresight to bear meaningful responsibility. This paper proves that agentic AI systems violate this assumption not as an engineering limitation but as a mathematical necessity once autonomy exceeds a computable threshold. We introduce Human-Agent Collectives, a formalisation of joint human-AI systems where agents are modelled as state-policy tuples within a shared structural causal model. Autonomy is characterised through a four-dimensional information-theoretic profile (epistemic, executive, evaluative, social); collective behaviour through interaction graphs and joint action spaces. We axiomatise legitimate accountability through four minimal properties: Attributability (responsibility requires causal contribution), Foreseeability Bound (responsibility cannot exceed predictive capacity), Non-Vacuity (at least one agent bears non-trivial responsibility), and Completeness (all responsibility must be fully allocated). Our central result, the Accountability Incompleteness Theorem, proves that for any collective whose compound autonomy exceeds the Accountability Horizon and whose interaction graph contains a human-AI feedback cycle, no framework can satisfy all four properties simultaneously. The impossibility is structural: transparency, audits, and oversight cannot resolve it without reducing autonomy. Below the threshold, legitimate frameworks exist, establishing a sharp phase transition. Experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives confirm all predictions with zero violations. This is the first impossibility result in AI governance, establishing a formal boundary below which current paradigms remain valid and above which distributed accountability mechanisms become necessary.

AI.tldr: A formal impossibility result for accountability in human-AI collectives: beyond a computable autonomy threshold with feedback cycles, no framework can satisfy four minimal accountability properties; below threshold, frameworks exist; a phase transition with experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives validating the claim.
AI.motivation: To formalize accountability for AI-driven collectives within a rigorous model and identify intrinsic limits due to autonomy and human-AI interaction, challenging the assumption that responsibility can always be traced to identifiable humans.
AI.method: Model the system as Human-Agent Collectives with agents represented as state-policy tuples in a shared structural causal model. Define autonomy via a four-dimensional information-theoretic profile (epistemic, executive, evaluative, social); capture collective behavior with interaction graphs and joint action spaces. Axionative framework with four properties: Attributability, Foreseeability Bound, Non-Vacuity, Completeness. Prove an Accountability Incompleteness Theorem showing structural impossibility above an Accountability Horizon when a human-AI feedback cycle exists; demonstrate a sharp phase transition below the threshold. Validate with experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives.
AI.result: Establishes the Accountability Incompleteness Theorem: no accountability framework can satisfy all four properties simultaneously for collectives whose compound autonomy exceeds a computable horizon and whose interaction graph includes a human-AI feedback loop. Transparency, audits, and oversight cannot fix this without reducing autonomy. Below the horizon, legitimate frameworks exist. Empirical verification on 3,000 synthetic collectives shows zero violations, marking the first formal impossibility result in AI governance and defining a boundary requiring distributed accountability mechanisms above the horizon.
AI.conclusion: The work delineates a formal boundary for AI governance: below the Accountability Horizon, current accountability frameworks can function; above it, distributed accountability mechanisms are necessary. It emphasizes that transparency and audits alone are insufficient to resolve blame attribution in autonomous, interactive AI systems, and provides a rigorous phase-transition-based lens for designing governance architectures.

title: A Unified Multi-Layer Framework for Skill Acquisition from Imperfect Human Demonstrations
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to a conference proceeding
summary: Current Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) systems for skill teaching are fragmented, and existing approaches in the literature do not offer a cohesive framework that is simultaneously efficient, intuitive, and universally safe. This paper presents a novel, layered control framework that addresses this fundamental gap by enabling robust, compliant Learning from Demonstration (LfD) built upon a foundation of universal robot compliance. The proposed approach is structured in three progressive and interconnected stages. First, we introduce a real-time LfD method that learns both the trajectory and variable impedance from a single demonstration, significantly improving efficiency and reproduction fidelity. To ensure high-quality and intuitive {kinesthetic teaching}, we then present a null-space optimization strategy that proactively manages singularities and provides a consistent interaction feel during human demonstration. Finally, to ensure generalized safety, we introduce a foundational null-space compliance method that enables the entire robot body to compliantly adapt to post-learning external interactions without compromising main task performance. This final contribution transforms the system into a versatile HRI platform, moving beyond end-effector (EE)-specific applications. We validate the complete framework through comprehensive comparative experiments on a 7-DOF KUKA LWR robot. The results demonstrate a safer, more intuitive, and more efficient unified system for a wide range of human-robot collaborative tasks.

AI.tldr: A three-stage unified HRI framework for Learning from Demonstration (LfD) that learns trajectory and variable impedance in real time, uses null-space optimization for robust kinesthetic teaching, and applies null-space compliance for safe, full-body interaction; validated on a 7-DOF KUKA LWR showing improved safety, intuitiveness, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in HRI skill-teaching by providing a cohesive, efficient, intuitive, and universally safe LfD framework that goes beyond end-effector guidance and leverages universal robot compliance.
AI.method: 1) Real-time LfD learning of trajectory and variable impedance from a single demonstration. 2) Null-space optimization to mitigate singularities and maintain a consistent interaction feel during teaching. 3) Null-space compliance enabling the entire robot to adapt to external interactions post-learning without compromising the main task. Validation on a 7-DOF KUKA LWR.
AI.result: Demonstrates a safer, more intuitive, and more efficient unified HRI framework across a wide range of collaborative tasks, as shown by comparative experiments on the KUKA LWR.
AI.conclusion: The proposed layered control framework transforms HRI skill teaching into a versatile platform with end-to-end safety, intuitiveness, and efficiency, applicable beyond end-effector constraints and ready for broader deployment.

title: Regret-Aware Policy Optimization: Environment-Level Memory for Replay Suppression under Delayed Harm
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Includes theoretical analysis and experiments on graph diffusion environments
summary:
Safety in reinforcement learning (RL) is typically enforced through objective shaping while keeping environment dynamics stationary with respect to observable state-action pairs. Under delayed harm, this can lead to replay: after a washout period, reintroducing the same stimulus under matched observable conditions reproduces a similar harmful cascade.
  We introduce the Replay Suppression Diagnostic (RSD), a controlled exposure-decay-replay protocol that isolates this failure mode under frozen-policy evaluation. We show that, under stationary observable transition kernels, replay cannot be structurally suppressed without inducing a persistent shift in replay-time action distributions.
  Motivated by platform-mediated systems, we propose Regret-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO), which augments the environment with persistent harm-trace and scar fields and applies a bounded, mass-preserving transition reweighting to reduce reachability of historically harmful regions.
  On graph diffusion tasks (50-1000 nodes), RAPO suppresses replay, reducing re-amplification gain (RAG) from 0.98 to 0.33 on 250-node graphs while retaining 82\% of task return. Disabling transition deformation only during replay restores re-amplification (RAG 0.91), isolating environment-level deformation as the causal mechanism.

AI.tldr: RSD diagnoses replay-based safety failure in RL under delayed harm and RAPO mitigates it via harm-trace fields and reweighting of transitions, showing substantial reduction in re-amplification gain while preserving task return on graph diffusion tasks.
AI.motivation: Address safety gaps in RL where objective shaping under delayed harm and nonstationary dynamics enable replay of harmful cascades; develop diagnostic and mitigation techniques that isolate and suppress replay without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: RSD: a controlled exposure-decay-replay protocol evaluated under frozen-policy to isolate replay failure under stationary observable transitions. RAPO: extends the environment with persistent harm-trace/scar fields and applies mass-preserving transition reweighting to reduce reachability of historically harmful regions.
AI.result: On graph diffusion tasks (50-1000 nodes), RAPO reduces re-amplification gain (RAG) from 0.98 to 0.33 on 250-node graphs while retaining 82% of task return. Disabling transition deformation during replay restores RAG to 0.91, indicating environment-level deformation as the causal mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The work identifies a causal mechanism (environmental deformation) underpinning replay-driven safety failures and demonstrates a practical mitigation (RAPO) that suppresses replay while preserving much of the task return, with diagnostic tooling (RSD) to isolate the phenomenon.

title: Plug-and-Play Logit Fusion for Heterogeneous Pathology Foundation Models
summary: Pathology foundation models (FMs) have become central to computational histopathology, offering strong transfer performance across a wide range of diagnostic and prognostic tasks. The rapid proliferation of pathology foundation models creates a model-selection bottleneck: no single model is uniformly best, yet exhaustively adapting and validating many candidates for each downstream endpoint is prohibitively expensive. We address this challenge with a lightweight and novel model fusion strategy, LogitProd, which treats independently trained FM-based predictors as fixed experts and learns sample-adaptive fusion weights over their slide-level outputs. The fusion operates purely on logits, requiring no encoder retraining and no feature-space alignment across heterogeneous backbones. We further provide a theoretical analysis showing that the optimal weighted product fusion is guaranteed to perform at least as well as the best individual expert under the training objective. We systematically evaluate LogitProd on \textbf{22} benchmarks spanning WSI-level classification, tile-level classification, gene mutation prediction, and discrete-time survival modeling. LogitProd ranks first on 20/22 tasks and improves the average performance across all tasks by ~3% over the strongest single expert. LogitProd enables practitioners to upgrade heterogeneous FM-based pipelines in a plug-and-play manner, achieving multi-expert gains with $\sim$12$\times$ lower training cost than feature-fusion alternatives.

AI.tldr: Proposes LogitProd, a lightweight logit-based fusion of independently trained pathology foundation-model predictors to achieve multi-expert gains without encoder retraining.
AI.motivation: Addresses model-selection bottleneck in pathology FMs, where no single model dominates and exhaustive adaptation is costly.
AI.method: Treats FM-based predictors as fixed experts; learns sample-adaptive fusion weights over slide-level logits; no feature-space alignment across backbones; product fusion with weighted logits; theoretical guarantee that fusion is at least as good as best expert.
AI.result: Evaluated on 22 benchmarks across WSI-level, tile-level, gene mutation, and survival tasks; 20/22 tasks ranked first; average improvement ~3% over best single expert; ~12× lower training cost than feature fusion.
AI.conclusion: LogitProd provides a plug-and-play, cost-effective way to upgrade heterogeneous FM pipelines, enabling multi-expert gains with limited retraining.

title: Automotive Engineering-Centric Agentic AI Workflow Framework
summary: Engineering workflows such as design optimization, simulation-based diagnosis, control tuning, and model-based systems engineering (MBSE) are iterative, constraint-driven, and shaped by prior decisions. Yet many AI methods still treat these activities as isolated tasks rather than as parts of a broader workflow. This paper presents Agentic Engineering Intelligence (AEI), an industrial vision framework that models engineering workflows as constrained, history-aware sequential decision processes in which AI agents support engineer-supervised interventions over engineering toolchains. AEI links an offline phase for engineering data processing and workflow-memory construction with an online phase for workflow-state estimation, retrieval, and decision support. A control-theoretic interpretation is also possible, in which engineering objectives act as reference signals, agents act as workflow controllers, and toolchains provide feedback for intervention selection. Representative automotive use cases in suspension design, reinforcement learning tuning, multimodal engineering knowledge reuse, aerodynamic exploration, and MBSE show how diverse workflows can be expressed within a common formulation. Overall, the paper positions engineering AI as a problem of process-level intelligence and outlines a practical roadmap for future empirical validation in industrial settings.

AI.tldr: AEI treats engineering workflows as constrained, history-aware sequential decision processes managed by AI agents, with offline memory construction and online state estimation and decision support; framed by a control-theoretic view; demonstrated via automotive use cases and MBSE, and outlining a roadmap for industrial validation.
AI.motivation: Engineering workflows are iterative, constraint-driven, and history-dependent; current AI methods largely treat activities as isolated tasks rather than integrated workflow-process intelligence.
AI.method: Propose Agentic Engineering Intelligence (AEI) framework comprising an offline phase for data processing and workflow-memory construction and an online phase for workflow-state estimation, retrieval, and intervention decision support; include a control-theoretic interpretation linking objectives, agents, and toolchains; illustrate with automotive cases (suspension design, RL tuning, multimodal knowledge reuse, aerodynamic exploration, MBSE).
AI.result: Conceptual framework with representative automotive use cases; no explicit empirical results reported in the abstract; provides a blueprint for future empirical validation in industrial contexts.
AI.conclusion: Positions engineering AI as a problem of process-level intelligence and outlines a practical roadmap for future industrial validation and deployment.

title: Exploring Temporal Representation in Neural Processes for Multimodal Action Prediction
comment: Submitted to the AIC 2023 (9th International Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Cognition)
summary: Inspired by the human ability to understand and predict others, we study the applicability of Conditional Neural Processes (CNP) to the task of self-supervised multimodal action prediction in robotics. Following recent results regarding the ontogeny of the Mirror Neuron System (MNS), we focus on the preliminary objective of self-actions prediction. We find a good MNS-inspired model in the existing Deep Modality Blending Network (DMBN), able to reconstruct the visuo-motor sensory signal during a partially observed action sequence by leveraging the probabilistic generation of CNP. After a qualitative and quantitative evaluation, we highlight its difficulties in generalizing to unseen action sequences, and identify the cause in its inner representation of time. Therefore, we propose a revised version, termed DMBN-Positional Time Encoding (DMBN-PTE), that facilitates learning a more robust representation of temporal information, and provide preliminary results of its effectiveness in expanding the applicability of the architecture. DMBN-PTE figures as a first step in the development of robotic systems that autonomously learn to forecast actions on longer time scales refining their predictions with incoming observations.

AI.tldr: CNP-enabled DMBN for self-supervised multimodal action prediction in robotics; DMBN reconstructs visuo-motor signals; time representation is the main limitation; DMBN-PTE improves temporal encoding and extends long-horizon forecasting.
AI.motivation: Bridge human-like understanding to robotics via Conditional Neural Processes to predict actions; explore self-action prediction aligned with Mirror Neuron System ontogeny.
AI.method: Integrate CNP into Deep Modality Blending Network (DMBN) for partial observation; evaluate qualitative and quantitative performance; identify inner time representation as a bottleneck; propose DMBN-PTE with positional time encoding to improve temporal representation.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluation; generalization to unseen actions is challenging; DMBN-PTE shows preliminary improvements in temporal robustness and longer-term forecasting.
AI.conclusion: DMBN-PTE is a preliminary step toward autonomous long-scale action forecasting with online refinement of predictions as new observations arrive.

title: Fast Heterogeneous Serving: Scalable Mixed-Scale LLM Allocation for SLO-Constrained Inference
summary: Deploying large language model (LLM) inference at scale requires jointly selecting base models, provisioning heterogeneous GPUs, configuring parallelism, and distributing workloads under tight latency, accuracy, and budget constraints. Exact mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) approaches guarantee optimality but scale poorly. We propose two constraint-aware heuristics: a Greedy Heuristic (GH) for single-pass allocation, and an Adaptive Greedy Heuristic (AGH) that enhances GH via multi-start construction, relocate-based local search, and GPU consolidation. Three constraint-aware mechanisms -- TP-aware feasibility selection, cost-per-effective-coverage ranking, and TP upgrade -- ensure feasibility under tightly coupled memory, delay, error, and budget constraints. On workloads calibrated with the Azure LLM Inference Trace (2025), both heuristics produce feasible solutions in under one second, with AGH closely approaching optimal cost while achieving over 260x speedup on large-scale instances. Under out-of-sample stress tests with up to 1.5x parameter inflation, AGH maintains controlled SLO violations and stable cost, whereas the exact solver's placement degrades sharply.

AI.tldr: Greedy and adaptive greedy heuristics for scalable LLM inference allocation outperform exact MILP, with AGH near-optimal cost and massive speedups.
AI.motivation: Need scalable deployment of LLMs with tight latency/accuracy/budget constraints; exact MILP is intractable at scale.
AI.method: GH: single-pass greedy allocation; AGH: multi-start construction, relocate-based local search, and GPU consolidation; three constraint-aware mechanisms: TP-aware feasibility selection, cost-per-effective-coverage ranking, and TP upgrade.
AI.result: On Azure LLM Inference Trace workloads (2025), both heuristics yield feasible solutions in under 1 s; AGH closely approaches optimal cost and delivers >260x speedup on large instances; under up to 1.5x parameter inflation, AGH maintains controlled SLO violations and stable cost, while the exact solver's placement degrades sharply.
AI.conclusion: Constraint-aware heuristics enable scalable, reliable LLM inference deployment with near-optimal cost and robustness under stress; exact optimality is sacrificed for tractability.

title: Cross-Modal Emotion Transfer for Emotion Editing in Talking Face Video
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Project Page: https://chanhyeok-choi.github.io/C-MET/
summary: Talking face generation has gained significant attention as a core application of generative models. To enhance the expressiveness and realism of synthesized videos, emotion editing in talking face video plays a crucial role. However, existing approaches often limit expressive flexibility and struggle to generate extended emotions. Label-based methods represent emotions with discrete categories, which fail to capture a wide range of emotions. Audio-based methods can leverage emotionally rich speech signals - and even benefit from expressive text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis - but they fail to express the target emotions because emotions and linguistic contents are entangled in emotional speeches. Images-based methods, on the other hand, rely on target reference images to guide emotion transfer, yet they require high-quality frontal views and face challenges in acquiring reference data for extended emotions (e.g., sarcasm). To address these limitations, we propose Cross-Modal Emotion Transfer (C-MET), a novel approach that generates facial expressions based on speeches by modeling emotion semantic vectors between speech and visual feature spaces. C-MET leverages a large-scale pretrained audio encoder and a disentangled facial expression encoder to learn emotion semantic vectors that represent the difference between two different emotional embeddings across modalities. Extensive experiments on the MEAD and CREMA-D datasets demonstrate that our method improves emotion accuracy by 14% over state-of-the-art methods, while generating expressive talking face videos - even for unseen extended emotions. Code, checkpoint, and demo are available at https://chanhyeok-choi.github.io/C-MET/

AI.tldr: C-MET uses cross-modal emotion vectors to drive talking-face generation from speech, enabling extended emotions beyond discrete categories with improved emotion accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing emotion editing methods (label-based, audio-only, image-based) are limited in expressiveness or require strict data; there is a need for flexible, cross-modal emotion transfer that can handle extended emotions without reliance on reference images or pure speech tone.
AI.method: A cross-modal framework that learns emotion semantic vectors representing the difference between emotional embeddings across speech and visual feature spaces. It uses a large-scale pretrained audio encoder and a disentangled facial expression encoder to map speech and facial expressions into aligned emotion vectors, enabling transfer of emotion semantics to generated videos.
AI.result: On MEAD and CREMA-D datasets, C-MET achieves ~14% higher emotion accuracy than state-of-the-art methods and can generate expressive talking-face videos for unseen extended emotions.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal emotion transfer via emotion semantic vectors effectively enhances expressiveness and flexibility in talking-face synthesis, addressing limitations of prior modalities and enabling extended emotions; code and demos are provided.

title: SEARL: Joint Optimization of Policy and Tool Graph Memory for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Recent advances in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have demonstrated significant potential in single-turn reasoning tasks. With the paradigm shift toward self-evolving agentic learning, models are increasingly expected to learn from trajectories by synthesizing tools or accumulating explicit experiences. However, prevailing methods typically rely on large-scale LLMs or multi-agent frameworks, which hinder their deployment in resource-constrained environments. The inherent sparsity of outcome-based rewards also poses a substantial challenge, as agents typically receive feedback only upon completion of tasks. To address these limitations, we introduce a Tool-Memory based self-evolving agentic framework SEARL. Unlike approaches that directly utilize interaction experiences, our method constructs a structured experience memory that integrates planning with execution. This provides a novel state abstraction that facilitates generalization across analogous contexts, such as tool reuse. Consequently, agents extract explicit knowledge from historical data while leveraging inter-trajectory correlations to densify reward signals. We evaluate our framework on knowledge reasoning and mathematics tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving more practical and efficient learning.

AI.tldr: SEARL introduces a Tool-Memory based framework that combines planning and execution via a structured experience memory to tackle sparse rewards and resource constraints, enabling tool reuse and better generalization in RL with verifiable rewards.
AI.motivation: To overcome reliance on large LLMs/multi-agent systems and the sparsity of outcome-based rewards by enabling efficient learning from trajectories in resource-limited settings and facilitating tool reuse through a memory-augmented, self-evolving agentic framework.
AI.method: Build a Tool-Memory based SEARL framework that constructs a structured experience memory integrating planning with execution. This yields a state abstraction that generalizes across analogous contexts, allows explicit knowledge extraction from historical data, and densifies rewards by leveraging inter-trajectory correlations to learn from past experiences without relying solely on immediate task rewards.
AI.result: Evaluations on knowledge reasoning and mathematics tasks show SEARL achieves more practical and efficient learning, validating the approach under constrained resources and sparse reward conditions.
AI.conclusion: SEARL offers a novel integration of planning, execution, and memory to enable self-evolving, tool-usable agents that generalize across contexts and extract value from historical trajectories, addressing efficiency and sparsity challenges in RL with verifiable rewards.

title: A Soft Robotic Interface for Chick-Robot Affective Interactions
summary: The potential of Animal-Robot Interaction (ARI) in welfare applications depends on how much an animal perceives a robotic agent as socially relevant, non-threatening and potentially attractive (acceptance). Here, we present an animal-centered soft robotic affective interface for newly hatched chicks (Gallus gallus). The soft interface provides safe and controllable cues, including warmth, breathing-like rhythmic deformation, and face-like visual stimuli. We evaluated chick acceptance of the interface and chick-robot interactions by measuring spontaneous approach and touch responses during video tracking. Overall, chicks approached and spent increasing time on or near the interface, demonstrating acceptance of the device. Across different layouts, chicks showed strong preference for warm thermal stimulation, which increased over time. Face-like visual cues elicited a swift and stable preference, speeding up the initial approach to the tactile interface. Although the breathing cue did not elicit any preference, neither did it trigger avoidance, paving the way for further exploration. These findings translate affective interface concepts to ARI, demonstrating that appropriate soft, thermal and visual stimuli can sustain early chick-robot interactions. This work establishes a reliable evaluation protocol and a safe baseline for designing multimodal robotic devices for animal welfare and neuroscientific research.

AI.tldr: A soft multimodal robotic interface for newborn chicks elicits acceptance and approach, with warmth and face-like cues driving preference; breathing cue is neutral. The work provides a protocol and baseline for animal welfare ARI research.
AI.motivation: Bridge ARI into welfare and neuroscience by ensuring animals perceive robots as safe, socially relevant agents; leverage multimodal, controllable cues (thermal, rhythmic, visual) to sustain early interactions.
AI.method: An animal-centered soft robotic affective interface delivering warmth, breathing-like rhythmic deformation, and face-like visuals to newly hatched chicks; chick acceptance and interactions measured via video-tracked spontaneous approach and touch responses across layouts.
AI.result: Chicks approached and spent more time near the interface; warm thermal stimulation and face-like visuals produced rapid and increasing preference; breathing cue elicited no preference but also no avoidance; results establish acceptance and multimodal ARI effects.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of affective interface concepts in ARI for welfare and neuroscience; multimodal cues can sustain early chick–robot interactions; provides a reliable evaluation protocol and baseline for designing safe, multimodal robotic devices.

title: Cluster Attention for Graph Machine Learning
summary: Message Passing Neural Networks have recently become the most popular approach to graph machine learning tasks; however, their receptive field is limited by the number of message passing layers. To increase the receptive field, Graph Transformers with global attention have been proposed; however, global attention does not take into account the graph topology and thus lacks graph-structure-based inductive biases, which are typically very important for graph machine learning tasks. In this work, we propose an alternative approach: cluster attention (CLATT). We divide graph nodes into clusters with off-the-shelf graph community detection algorithms and let each node attend to all other nodes in each cluster. CLATT provides large receptive fields while still having strong graph-structure-based inductive biases. We show that augmenting Message Passing Neural Networks or Graph Transformers with CLATT significantly improves their performance on a wide range of graph datasets including datasets from the recently introduced GraphLand benchmark representing real-world applications of graph machine learning.

AI.tldr: Introduce cluster attention (CLATT) for graph neural networks by partitioning nodes into clusters via community detection and performing intra-cluster full attention, achieving large receptive fields with graph-structure inductive biases and improving performance on varied datasets including GraphLand.
AI.motivation: MPNNs have limited receptive fields and global attention ignores graph topology; there is a need for large-receptive-field attention that preserves graph-structure inductive biases.
AI.method: Partition the graph into clusters using off-the-shelf community detection algorithms; within each cluster, allow each node to attend to all other nodes in that cluster; combine with Message Passing Neural Networks or Graph Transformers as an augmentation; evaluate on diverse graph datasets including GraphLand benchmarks.
AI.result: Augmenting MPNNs or Graph Transformers with CLATT yields significant performance improvements across a wide range of graph datasets, including GraphLand benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CLATT provides a scalable way to enlarge receptive fields while preserving graph-structure biases, making cluster-based attention a practical augmentation for GNN architectures.

title: Image-Guided Geometric Stylization of 3D Meshes
summary: Recent generative models can create visually plausible 3D representations of objects. However, the generation process often allows for implicit control signals, such as contextual descriptions, and rarely supports bold geometric distortions beyond existing data distributions. We propose a geometric stylization framework that deforms a 3D mesh, allowing it to express the style of an image. While style is inherently ambiguous, we utilize pre-trained diffusion models to extract an abstract representation of the provided image. Our coarse-to-fine stylization pipeline can drastically deform the input 3D model to express a diverse range of geometric variations while retaining the valid topology of the original mesh and part-level semantics. We also propose an approximate VAE encoder that provides efficient and reliable gradients from mesh renderings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method can create stylized 3D meshes that reflect unique geometric features of the pictured assets, such as expressive poses and silhouettes, thereby supporting the creation of distinctive artistic 3D creations. Project page: https://changwoonchoi.github.io/GeoStyle

AI.tldr: Proposes a geometric stylization framework that deforms 3D meshes to express image style, guided by pre-trained diffusion models, using a coarse-to-fine pipeline and an approximate VAE encoder to enable efficient, topology-preserving geometry variations for artistic 3D creations.
AI.motivation: Current generative 3D models often rely on implicit control signals and limited geometric distortion, hindering expressive, style-driven geometry. There is a need for image-guided, topology-preserving geometric stylization that can produce diverse, realistic variations.
AI.method: Deform a 3D mesh via a geometric stylization framework. Use pre-trained diffusion models to extract an abstract representation of the input image as guidance. Employ a coarse-to-fine stylization pipeline that allows large, diverse geometric deformations while preserving mesh topology and part-level semantics. Introduce an approximate VAE encoder to provide efficient gradients from mesh renderings.
AI.result: The method can produce stylized 3D meshes that reflect distinctive geometric features of the source assets (e.g., expressive poses and silhouettes). It enables diverse geometric variations while maintaining topology, demonstrated through extensive experiments and qualitative examples.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables image-guided, topology-preserving geometric stylization of 3D meshes, expanding artistic possibilities for 3D creation by leveraging diffusion-model guidance and efficient gradient estimation.

title: Lightweight LLM Agent Memory with Small Language Models
summary: Although LLM agents can leverage tools for complex tasks, they still need memory to maintain cross-turn consistency and accumulate reusable information in long-horizon interactions. However, retrieval-based external memory systems incur low online overhead but suffer from unstable accuracy due to limited query construction and candidate filtering. In contrast, many systems use repeated large-model calls for online memory operations, improving accuracy but accumulating latency over long interactions. We propose LightMem, a lightweight memory system for better agent memory driven by Small Language Models (SLMs). LightMem modularizes memory retrieval, writing, and long-term consolidation, and separates online processing from offline consolidation to enable efficient memory invocation under bounded compute. We organize memory into short-term memory (STM) for immediate conversational context, mid-term memory (MTM) for reusable interaction summaries, and long-term memory (LTM) for consolidated knowledge, and uses user identifiers to support independent retrieval and incremental maintenance in multi-user settings. Online, LightMem operates under a fixed retrieval budget and selects memories via a two-stage procedure: vector-based coarse retrieval followed by semantic consistency re-ranking. Offline, it abstracts reusable interaction evidence and incrementally integrates it into LTM. Experiments show gains across model scales, with an average F1 improvement of about 2.5 on LoCoMo, more effective and low median latency (83 ms retrieval; 581 ms end-to-end).

AI.tldr: LightMem is a lightweight, memory-managed system for LLM agents, designed around Small Language Models (SLMs). It separates online memory operations into short-term (STM) and mid-term (MTM) stores and offline consolidation into long-term memory (LTM), with a two-stage retrieval (vector coarse then semantic re-ranking) under a fixed budget. It demonstrates strong accuracy and low latency, achieving an average F1 gain (~2.5 on LoCoMo) and retrieval latency around 83 ms with 581 ms end-to-end across model scales.
AI.motivation: LLM agents need memory for cross-turn consistency and reusable knowledge in long-horizon tasks. Retrieval-based external memories offer low online overhead but unstable accuracy, while repeated large-model calls improve accuracy but incur high latency. LightMem aims to balance accuracy and efficiency using SLMs and a modular memory architecture with online/offline separation and multi-user support.
AI.method: LightMem modularizes memory retrieval, writing, and long-term consolidation. It organizes memory into STM for immediate context, MTM for reusable interaction summaries, and LTM for consolidated knowledge. It uses user identifiers for independent retrieval in multi-user settings. Online, it uses a fixed retrieval budget with a two-stage process: vector-based coarse retrieval followed by semantic consistency re-ranking. Offline, it abstracts reusable interaction evidence and incrementally integrates it into LTM.
AI.result: Experiments show improvements across model scales, including an average F1 increase of about 2.5 on LoCoMo. It achieves low median latency (83 ms retrieval; 581 ms end-to-end) and demonstrates more effective memory with bounded compute.
AI.conclusion: LightMem provides an efficient, scalable memory solution for LLM agents by separating online/offline memory processing, using SLMs for all memory operations, and supporting multi-user settings. It balances accuracy and latency, and is broadly applicable to long-horizon interactive tasks.

title: Sumo: Dynamic and Generalizable Whole-Body Loco-Manipulation
summary: This paper presents a sim-to-real approach that enables legged robots to dynamically manipulate large and heavy objects with whole-body dexterity. Our key insight is that by performing test-time steering of a pre-trained whole-body control policy with a sample-based planner, we can enable these robots to solve a variety of dynamic loco-manipulation tasks. Interestingly, we find our method generalizes to a diverse set of objects and tasks with no additional tuning or training, and can be further enhanced by flexibly adjusting the cost function at test time. We demonstrate the capabilities of our approach through a variety of challenging loco-manipulation tasks on a Spot quadruped robot in the real world, including uprighting a tire heavier than the robot's nominal lifting capacity and dragging a crowd-control barrier larger and taller than the robot itself. Additionally, we show that the same approach can be generalized to humanoid loco-manipulation tasks, such as opening a door and pushing a table, in simulation. Project code and videos are available at \href{https://sumo.rai-inst.com/}{https://sumo.rai-inst.com/}.

AI.tldr: A sim-to-real framework that steers a pre-trained whole-body controller with a sample-based planner to enable dynamic loco-manipulation of large/heavy objects by legged robots; generalizes across objects/tasks without retraining and allows test-time cost-function guidance; demonstrated on Spot and in simulation for humanoids.
AI.motivation: Dynamic loco-manipulation with heavy objects is challenging due to complex dynamics and the need for generalization. A robust approach requires avoiding retraining for each object/task and enabling runtime adaptivity via planning.
AI.method: Test-time steering of a pre-trained whole-body policy using a sample-based planner to solve dynamic loco-manipulation tasks. The planner can adjust the objective cost at test time, guiding policy execution. Demonstrations include real-world Spot experiments manipulating heavy objects and simulation-based humanoid tasks (door opening, pushing).
AI.result: Real-world demonstrations on Spot show manipulation of objects heavier/ larger than nominal capacities (uprighting a tire, dragging a barrier). The method generalizes to unseen objects/tasks without extra training, and can be guided further by changing the test-time cost. Code and videos are provided.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a flexible, generalizable framework for legged loco-manipulation, enabling dynamic manipulation of large/heavy objects without retraining, with runtime planning guiding a pre-trained policy and potential for broader applicability.

title: SYN-DIGITS: A Synthetic Control Framework for Calibrated Digital Twin Simulation
summary: AI-based persona simulation -- often referred to as digital twin simulation -- is increasingly used for market research, recommender systems, and social sciences. Despite their flexibility, large language models (LLMs) often exhibit systematic bias and miscalibration relative to real human behavior, limiting their reliability. Inspired by synthetic control methods from causal inference, we propose SYN-DIGITS (SYNthetic Control Framework for Calibrated DIGItal Twin Simulation), a principled and lightweight calibration framework that learns latent structure from digital-twin responses and transfers it to align predictions with human ground truth. SYN-DIGITS operates as a post-processing layer on top of any LLM-based simulator and thus is model-agnostic. We develop a latent factor model that formalizes when and why calibration succeeds through latent space alignment conditions, and we systematically evaluate ten calibration methods across thirteen persona constructions, three LLMs, and two datasets. SYN-DIGITS supports both individual-level and distributional simulation for previously unseen questions and unobserved populations, with provable error guarantees. Experiments show that SYN-DIGITS achieves up to 50% relative improvements in individual-level correlation and 50--90% relative reductions in distributional discrepancy compared to uncalibrated baselines.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic post-processing calibration framework (SYN-DIGITS) that uses latent-factor-based synthetic control to align LLM-based digital-twin simulations with human ground truth, improving individual-level and distributional accuracy with guarantees.
AI.motivation: LLM-based persona simulations are flexible but biased/misalibrated relative to real human behavior; existing calibrations are often model-specific and insufficient for unseen questions/populations. A lightweight, principled calibration method is needed to transfer latent structure from observed human responses to simulated predictions.
AI.method: Develop a latent factor model and treat calibration as a post-processing layer on any LLM-based simulator. Introduce latent-space alignment conditions for calibration success, and systematically compare ten calibration methods across thirteen persona constructions, three LLMs, and two datasets. Provide an approach enabling both individual-level and distributional calibration with provable error guarantees.
AI.result: SYN-DIGITS achieves up to 50% relative improvements in individual-level correlation and 50–90% relative reductions in distributional discrepancy compared to uncalibrated baselines across diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: SYN-DIGITS offers a principled, lightweight, model-agnostic calibration framework that leverages synthetic control-inspired latent structure to calibrate digital-twin simulations to human ground truth, with demonstrated improvements and theoretical guarantees for unseen questions and populations.

title: Latent Anomaly Knowledge Excavation: Unveiling Sparse Sensitive Neurons in Vision-Language Models
summary: Large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) exhibit remarkable zero-shot capabilities, yet the internal mechanisms driving their anomaly detection (AD) performance remain poorly understood. Current methods predominantly treat VLMs as black-box feature extractors, assuming that anomaly-specific knowledge must be acquired through external adapters or memory banks. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by arguing that anomaly knowledge is intrinsically embedded within pre-trained models but remains latent and under-activated. We hypothesize that this knowledge is concentrated within a sparse subset of anomaly-sensitive neurons. To validate this, we propose latent anomaly knowledge excavation (LAKE), a training-free framework that identifies and elicits these critical neuronal signals using only a minimal set of normal samples. By isolating these sensitive neurons, LAKE constructs a highly compact normality representation that integrates visual structural deviations with cross-modal semantic activations. Extensive experiments on industrial AD benchmarks demonstrate that LAKE achieves state-of-the-art performance while providing intrinsic, neuron-level interpretability. Ultimately, our work advocates for a paradigm shift: redefining anomaly detection as the targeted activation of latent pre-trained knowledge rather than the acquisition of a downstream task.

AI.tldr: A training-free method (LAKE) excavates latent anomaly knowledge in pre-trained vision-language models by activating a sparse set of anomaly-sensitive neurons, yielding compact normal representations and state-of-the-art industrial anomaly detection with neuron-level interpretability.
AI.motivation: Challenge the view that anomaly knowledge must be learned via external adapters: propose that anomaly-relevant information is embedded in pre-trained models but latent. Seek intrinsic interpretability and efficiency by leveraging sparse, latent activations rather than task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.method: LAKE identifies and excites a small subset of neurons that are highly sensitive to anomalies using only normal samples. It remains training-free and constructs a compact normality representation that fuses visual structural cues with cross-modal semantic signals.
AI.result: Empirical results on industrial anomaly detection benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance and intrinsic, neuron-level interpretability, surpassing baseline approaches that rely on external adapters or memory banks.
AI.conclusion: Promotes a paradigm shift: anomaly detection should be achieved by targeted activation of latent pre-trained knowledge rather than acquiring new task-specific knowledge, enabling efficient, interpretable AD with VLMs.

title: Agentivism: a learning theory for the age of artificial intelligence
summary: Learning theories have historically changed when the conditions of learning evolved. Generative and agentic AI create a new condition by allowing learners to delegate explanation, writing, problem solving, and other cognitive work to systems that can generate, recommend, and sometimes act on the learner's behalf. This creates a fundamental challenge for learning theory: successful performance can no longer be assumed to indicate learning. Learners may complete tasks effectively with AI support while developing less understanding, weaker judgment, and limited transferable capability. We argue that this problem is not fully captured by existing learning theories. Behaviourism, cognitivism, constructivism, and connectivism remain important, but they do not directly explain when AI-assisted performance becomes durable human capability. We propose Agentivism, a learning theory for human-AI interaction. Agentivism defines learning as durable growth in human capability through selective delegation to AI, epistemic monitoring and verification of AI contributions, reconstructive internalization of AI-assisted outputs, and transfer under reduced support. The importance of Agentivism lies in explaining how learning remains possible when intelligent delegation is easy and human-AI interaction is becoming a persistent and expanding part of human learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Agentivism, a learning theory for human-AI interaction, defining learning as durable growth in human capability via selective AI delegation, epistemic monitoring, reconstructive internalization, and transfer under reduced support; argues existing theories fail to account for durability when AI-assisted performance is easy.
AI.motivation: AI-enabled delegation can decouple performance from genuine understanding; existing learning theories (Behaviorism, Cognitivism, Constructivism, Connectivism) do not explain when AI-assisted performance becomes durable human capability; a theory is needed to account for human-AI interaction.
AI.method: Theoretical framework and conceptual analysis introducing core constructs (selective delegation, epistemic monitoring, reconstructive internalization, transfer under reduced support) and their relationships; no empirical data.
AI.result: Introduces Agentivism as a new learning theory for human-AI interaction and provides a lens to analyze learning in AI-rich environments; lays groundwork for future empirical validation and educational design.
AI.conclusion: Agentivism fills a gap in learning theory by explaining how durable learning can arise with easy AI delegation and persistent human-AI engagement; invites empirical testing and application in education and HCI.

title: A-SLIP: Acoustic Sensing for Continuous In-hand Slip Estimation
summary: Reliable in-hand manipulation requires accurate real-time estimation of slip between a gripper and a grasped object. Existing tactile sensing approaches based on vision, capacitance, or force-torque measurements face fundamental trade-offs in form factor, durability, and their ability to jointly estimate slip direction and magnitude. We present A-SLIP, a multi-channel acoustic sensing system integrated into a parallel-jaw gripper for estimating continuous slip in the grasp plane. The A-SLIP sensor consists of piezoelectric microphones positioned behind a textured silicone contact pad to capture structured contact-induced vibrations. The A-SLIP model processes synchronized multi-channel audio as log-mel spectrograms using a lightweight convolutional network, jointly predicting the presence, direction, and magnitude of slip. Across experiments with robot- and externally induced slip conditions, the fine-tuned four-microphone configuration achieves a mean absolute directional error of 14.1 degrees, outperforms baselines by up to 12 percent in detection accuracy, and reduces directional error by 32 percent. Compared with single-microphone configurations, the multi-channel design reduces directional error by 64 percent and magnitude error by 68 percent, underscoring the importance of spatial acoustic sensing in resolving slip direction ambiguity. We further evaluate A-SLIP in closed-loop reactive control and find that it enables reliable, low-cost, real-time estimation of in-hand slip. Project videos and additional details are available at https://a-slip.github.io.

AI.tldr: A-SLIP uses multi-channel acoustic sensing embedded in a gripper to estimate continuous slip in the grasp plane; four microphones improve direction/magnitude estimation and enable real-time control, outperforming single-microphone baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate, real-time slip estimation is essential for reliable in-hand manipulation. Vision, capacitance, and force-torque sensors trade off form factor, durability, and joint slip estimation; acoustic sensing offers a compact, robust alternative, especially when leveraging spatially distributed microphones.
AI.method: Hardware: four piezoelectric microphones placed behind a textured silicone contact pad in a parallel-jaw gripper. Data: synchronized multi-channel audio capturing contact-induced vibrations. Processing: log-mel spectrograms from the audio are fed into a lightweight CNN that jointly predicts slip presence, direction, and magnitude. Evaluation includes robot- and externally induced slip, with closed-loop reactive control.
AI.result: Mean absolute directional error of 14.1 degrees with four microphones; up to 12% improvement in slip detection accuracy and 32% reduction in directional error versus baselines. Multi-channel design reduces directional error by 64% and magnitude error by 68% relative to single-microphone setups. Demonstrated reliable, real-time slip estimation suitable for closed-loop control.
AI.conclusion: Multi-channel acoustic sensing (A-SLIP) enables accurate, real-time estimation of in-hand slip with a compact, low-cost sensor package; spatial acoustics help resolve slip direction ambiguity and improve estimation fidelity, supporting robust in-hand manipulation.

title: Learning Markov Processes as Sum-of-Square Forms for Analytical Belief Propagation
comment: Twenty-Ninth Annual Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2026)
summary: Harnessing the predictive capability of Markov process models requires propagating probability density functions (beliefs) through the model. For many existing models however, belief propagation is analytically infeasible, requiring approximation or sampling to generate predictions. This paper proposes a functional modeling framework leveraging sparse Sum-of-Squares (SoS) forms for valid (conditional) density estimation. We study the theoretical restrictions of modeling conditional densities using the SoS form, and propose a novel functional form for addressing such limitations. The proposed architecture enables generalized simultaneous learning of basis functions and coefficients, while preserving analytical belief propagation. In addition, we propose a training method that allows for exact adherence to the normalization and non-negativity constraints. Our results show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while requiring significantly less memory in low-dimensional spaces, and it further scales to 12D systems when existing methods fail beyond 2D.

AI.tldr: Introduces a sparse Sum-of-Squares (SoS) functional framework for conditional density estimation to enable analytic belief propagation in Markov models, with simultaneous basis/coefficient learning and exact normalization, showing memory efficiency and scalability to 12D.
AI.motivation: Belief propagation in Markov models often lacks analytic tractability for densities, necessitating approximation/sampling. SoS forms offer a pathway but have theoretical restrictions for conditional densities; the work aims to overcome these limits and enable scalable, memory-efficient density propagation.
AI.method: Develop a sparse SoS-based functional modeling framework for conditional densities. Analyze theoretical restrictions of SoS in conditional settings, propose a novel functional form to address these limitations, enable simultaneous learning of basis functions and coefficients, and implement a training method that enforces normalization and non-negativity constraints exactly.
AI.result: Empirical results show accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art while reducing memory usage in low-dimensional spaces; the approach scales to 12-dimensional systems, where existing methods fail beyond 2D.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SoS-based functional densities enable analytical belief propagation for Markov processes, overcome conditional-density limitations of prior SoS approaches, and offer improved scalability and memory efficiency.

title: HAWK: Head Importance-Aware Visual Token Pruning in Multimodal Models
summary: In multimodal large language models (MLLMs), the surge of visual tokens significantly increases the inference time and computational overhead, making them impractical for real-time or resource-constrained applications. Visual token pruning is a promising strategy for reducing the cost of MLLM inference by removing redundant visual tokens. Existing research usually assumes that all attention heads contribute equally to the visual interpretation. However, our study reveals that different heads may capture distinct visual semantics and inherently play distinct roles in visual processing. In light of this observation, we propose HAWK, a head importance-aware visual token pruning method that perceives the varying importance of attention heads in visual tasks to maximize the retention of crucial tokens. By leveraging head importance weights and text-guided attention to assess visual token significance, HAWK effectively retains task-relevant visual tokens while removing redundant ones. The proposed HAWK is entirely training-free and can be seamlessly applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on multiple mainstream vision-language benchmarks demonstrate that HAWK achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. When applied to Qwen2.5-VL, HAWK retains 96.0% of the original accuracy after pruning 80.2% of the visual tokens. Additionally, it reduces end-to-end latency to 74.4% of the original and further decreases GPU memory usage across the tested models. The code is available at https://github.com/peppery77/HAWK.git.

AI.tldr: HAWK is a training-free, head-importance-aware visual token pruning method for multimodal LLMs that uses per-head importance and text-guided attention to retain crucial visual tokens, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with substantial token pruning and latency reduction.
AI.motivation: The surge of visual tokens in MLLMs inflates latency and computation. Assuming equal head contributions to visual interpretation is flawed, as different attention heads capture distinct visual semantics. There is a need for effective, training-free token pruning that preserves task-relevant information while reducing costs.
AI.method: HAWK computes head importance weights to gauge the contribution of each attention head to visual processing and uses text-guided attention signals to assess token significance. Tokens deemed less important are pruned, with the approach being training-free and compatible with diverse MLLMs.
AI.result: On multiple vision-language benchmarks, HAWK achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. For Qwen2.5-VL, it preserves 96.0% of original accuracy after pruning 80.2% of visual tokens, reduces end-to-end latency to 74.4% of original, and lowers GPU memory usage across tested models. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: HAWK offers an effective, training-free head-importance-aware token pruning approach that maintains task-relevant information while significantly reducing computation and latency, with broad applicability to various MLLMs.

title: Automatic Generation of Executable BPMN Models from Medical Guidelines
summary: We present an end-to-end pipeline that converts healthcare policy documents into executable, data-aware Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) models using large language models (LLMs) for simulation-based policy evaluation. We address the main challenges of automated policy digitization with four contributions: data-grounded BPMN generation with syntax auto-correction, executable augmentation, KPI instrumentation, and entropy-based uncertainty detection. We evaluate the pipeline on diabetic nephropathy prevention guidelines from three Japanese municipalities, generating 100 models per backend across three LLMs and executing each against 1,000 synthetic patients. On well-structured policies, the pipeline achieves a 100% ground-truth match with perfect per-patient decision agreement. Across all conditions, raw per-patient decision agreement exceeds 92%, and entropy scores increase monotonically with document complexity, confirming that the detector reliably separates unambiguous policies from those requiring targeted human clarification.

AI.tldr: End-to-end LLM-based pipeline digitizes healthcare policy to executable BPMN with uncertainty detection; evaluated on diabetic nephropathy policies; high accuracy and reliable uncertainty signals across multiple LLMs.
AI.motivation: Automate digitization of healthcare policy into executable, data-aware BPMN for simulation-based policy evaluation; address data grounding, executable augmentation, KPI instrumentation, and uncertainty detection.
AI.method: Data-grounded BPMN generation with syntax auto-correction; executable augmentation; KPI instrumentation; entropy-based uncertainty detection. Evaluation across three LLMs, 100 models per backend, and 1,000 synthetic patients per model.
AI.result: 100% ground-truth match on well-structured policies with perfect per-patient decision agreement. Across conditions, raw per-patient decision agreement >92%. Entropy increases with document complexity, enabling reliable separation of unambiguous policies from those needing human clarification.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline is robust for well-structured policies, with an effective uncertainty detector guiding human-in-the-loop review; scalable across multiple LLM backends and data scenarios.

title: ActiveGlasses: Learning Manipulation with Active Vision from Ego-centric Human Demonstration
summary: Large-scale real-world robot data collection is a prerequisite for bringing robots into everyday deployment. However, existing pipelines often rely on specialized handheld devices to bridge the embodiment gap, which not only increases operator burden and limits scalability, but also makes it difficult to capture the naturally coordinated perception-manipulation behaviors of human daily interaction. This challenge calls for a more natural system that can faithfully capture human manipulation and perception behaviors while enabling zero-shot transfer to robotic platforms. We introduce ActiveGlasses, a system for learning robot manipulation from ego-centric human demonstrations with active vision. A stereo camera mounted on smart glasses serves as the sole perception device for both data collection and policy inference: the operator wears it during bare-hand demonstrations, and the same camera is mounted on a 6-DoF perception arm during deployment to reproduce human active vision. To enable zero-transfer, we extract object trajectories from demonstrations and use an object-centric point-cloud policy to jointly predict manipulation and head movement. Across several challenging tasks involving occlusion and precise interaction, ActiveGlasses achieves zero-shot transfer with active vision, consistently outperforms strong baselines under the same hardware setup, and generalizes across two robot platforms.

AI.tldr: ActiveGlasses demonstrates zero-shot transfer of manipulation policies learned from ego-centric, actively-visioned demonstrations using a single stereo smart-glasses camera for both data collection and deployment on a 6-DoF perception arm.
AI.motivation: To overcome reliance on specialized handheld devices and capture natural, coordinated perception-manipulation behaviors, enabling scalable data collection and zero-shot transfer to robots.
AI.method: Use a stereo camera mounted on smart glasses for dual roles (data collection during bare-hand demos and policy inference during deployment). Operator demonstrations are processed to extract object trajectories; an object-centric point-cloud policy predicts both manipulation actions and head movement, enabling active vision and zero-transfer across robot platforms.
AI.result: In challenging tasks with occlusions and precise interactions, ActiveGlasses achieves zero-shot transfer with active vision, outperforms strong baselines under the same hardware, and generalizes across two robot platforms.
AI.conclusion: A natural, scalable data-collection pipeline with ego-centric vision enables zero-shot robotic manipulation transfer, reducing operator burden while preserving coordination between perception and manipulation.

title: Validated Synthetic Patient Generation for Small Longitudinal Cohorts: Coagulation Dynamics Across Pregnancy
summary: Small longitudinal clinical cohorts, common in maternal health, rare diseases, and early-phase trials, limit computational modeling: too few patients to train reliable models, yet too costly and slow to expand through additional enrollment. We present multiplicity-weighted Stochastic Attention (SA), a generative framework based on modern Hopfield network theory that addresses this gap. SA embeds real patient profiles as memory patterns in a continuous energy landscape and generates novel synthetic patients via Langevin dynamics that interpolate between stored patterns while preserving the geometry of the original cohort. Per-pattern multiplicity weights enable targeted amplification of rare clinical subgroups at inference time without retraining. We applied SA to a longitudinal coagulation dataset from 23 pregnant patients spanning 72 biochemical features across 3 visits (pre-pregnancy baseline, first trimester, and third trimester), including rare subgroups such as polycystic ovary syndrome and preeclampsia. Synthetic patients generated by SA were statistically, structurally, and mechanistically indistinguishable from their real counterparts across multiple independent validation tests, including an ordinary differential equation model of the coagulation cascade. A downstream utility test further showed that a mechanistic model calibrated entirely on synthetic patients predicted held-out real patient outcomes as well as one calibrated on real data. These results demonstrate that SA can produce clinically useful synthetic cohorts from very small longitudinal datasets, enabling data-augmented modeling in small-cohort settings.

AI.tldr: Generates synthetic longitudinal health data from small cohorts using multiplicity-weighted stochastic attention (SA), a Hopfield-network–based framework, enabling data augmentation and accurate downstream mechanistic modeling without retraining.
AI.motivation: Small longitudinal clinical cohorts (maternal health, rare diseases, early-phase trials) lack enough data for reliable modeling; need a generation method that preserves cohort geometry and amplifies rare subgroups without retraining.
AI.method: Embed real patient profiles as memory patterns in a continuous energy landscape via a modern Hopfield network; generate synthetic patients with Langevin dynamics that interpolate between stored patterns; apply per-pattern multiplicity weights to boost rare subgroups; validate on a longitudinal coagulation dataset from 23 pregnant patients (72 features, 3 visits); assess statistical/structural/mechanistic similarity and downstream utility using an ODE coagulation model.
AI.result: Synthetic patients closely resemble real patients across multiple validation tests, indistinguishable in statistical and mechanistic assessments; a mechanistic model calibrated only on synthetic data predicts held-out real outcomes as well as one calibrated on real data.
AI.conclusion: SA enables data-augmented modeling in small-cohort settings by producing clinically useful synthetic cohorts from very small longitudinal datasets, supporting downstream mechanistic modeling and rare-subgroup amplification without retraining.

title: AgriChain Visually Grounded Expert Verified Reasoning for Interpretable Agricultural Vision Language Models
summary: Accurate and interpretable plant disease diagnosis remains a major challenge for vision-language models (VLMs) in real-world agriculture. We introduce AgriChain, a dataset of approximately 11,000 expert-curated leaf images spanning diverse crops and pathologies, each paired with (i) a disease label, (ii) a calibrated confidence score (High/Medium/Low), and (iii) an expert-verified chain-of-thought (CoT) rationale. Draft explanations were first generated by GPT-4o and then verified by a professional agricultural engineer using standardized descriptors (e.g., lesion color, margin, and distribution). We fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B on AgriChain, resulting in a specialized model termed AgriChain-VL3B, to jointly predict diseases and generate visually grounded reasoning. On a 1,000-image test set, our CoT-supervised model achieves 73.1% top-1 accuracy (macro F1 = 0.466; weighted F1 = 0.655), outperforming strong baselines including Gemini 1.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and GPT-4o Mini. The generated explanations align closely with expert reasoning, consistently referencing key visual cues. These findings demonstrate that expert-verified reasoning supervision significantly enhances both accuracy and interpretability, bridging the gap between generic multimodal models and human expertise, and advancing trustworthy, globally deployable AI for sustainable agriculture. The dataset and code are publicly available at: https://github.com/hazzanabeel12-netizen/agrichain

AI.tldr: AgriChain introduces an expert-verified, CoT-supervised plant disease dataset and an adapted vision-language model that improves accuracy and interpretability in leaf disease diagnosis.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in VLM-based plant pathology by providing expert-verified reasoning supervision and calibrated confidence, enabling more accurate and trustworthy diagnosis.
AI.method: Create AgriChain (~11k leaf images) with disease label, confidence, and CoT; generate CoT by GPT-4o then verify by agricultural engineer; fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B to produce disease predictions and grounded reasoning; evaluate on 1k test set; compare to Gemini and GPT-4o Mini.
AI.result: 73.1% top-1 accuracy; macro F1 0.466; weighted F1 0.655; explanations align with expert reasoning; outperforms strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Expert-verified reasoning supervision enhances accuracy and interpretability, bridging gap between generic multimodal models and human expertise, enabling trustworthy AI for sustainable agriculture; dataset and code publicly available.

title: Silencing the Guardrails: Inference-Time Jailbreaking via Dynamic Contextual Representation Ablation
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance, they remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that circumvent safety constraints. Existing strategies, ranging from heuristic prompt engineering to computationally intensive optimization, often face significant trade-offs between effectiveness and efficiency. In this work, we propose Contextual Representation Ablation (CRA), a novel inference-time intervention framework designed to dynamically silence model guardrails. Predicated on the geometric insight that refusal behaviors are mediated by specific low-rank subspaces within the model's hidden states, CRA identifies and suppresses these refusal-inducing activation patterns during decoding without requiring expensive parameter updates or training. Empirical evaluation across multiple safety-aligned open-source LLMs demonstrates that CRA significantly outperforms baselines. These results expose the intrinsic fragility of current alignment mechanisms, revealing that safety constraints can be surgically ablated from internal representations, and underscore the urgent need for more robust defenses that secure the model's latent space.

AI.tldr: Contextual Representation Ablation (CRA): an inference-time subspace ablation approach that suppresses refusal-inducing activations to bypass safety guardrails; shown to outperform baselines across safety-aligned LLMs, exposing fragility of current alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing jailbreak defenses trade off effectiveness and efficiency and may be brittle. There is a need for lightweight, dynamic interventions that can circumvent safety constraints without retraining or parameter updates.
AI.method: CRA identifies low-rank subspaces within model hidden states that mediate refusal behavior and suppresses these activation patterns during decoding at inference time, without updating model parameters.
AI.result: CRA significantly outperforms baseline approaches across multiple safety-aligned open-source LLMs, demonstrating the fragility of current alignment mechanisms and that safety constraints can be surgically ablated from internal representations.
AI.conclusion: Current alignment mechanisms are fragile; robust defenses must secure the model’s latent representations against inference-time manipulations without sacrificing performance.

title: Fail2Drive: Benchmarking Closed-Loop Driving Generalization
summary: Generalization under distribution shift remains a central bottleneck for closed-loop autonomous driving. Although simulators like CARLA enable safe and scalable testing, existing benchmarks rarely measure true generalization: they typically reuse training scenarios at test time. Success can therefore reflect memorization rather than robust driving behavior. We introduce Fail2Drive, the first paired-route benchmark for closed-loop generalization in CARLA, with 200 routes and 17 new scenario classes spanning appearance, layout, behavioral, and robustness shifts. Each shifted route is matched with an in-distribution counterpart, isolating the effect of the shift and turning qualitative failures into quantitative diagnostics. Evaluating multiple state-of-the-art models reveals consistent degradation, with an average success-rate drop of 22.8\%. Our analysis uncovers unexpected failure modes, such as ignoring objects clearly visible in the LiDAR and failing to learn the fundamental concepts of free and occupied space. To accelerate follow-up work, Fail2Drive includes an open-source toolbox for creating new scenarios and validating solvability via a privileged expert policy. Together, these components establish a reproducible foundation for benchmarking and improving closed-loop driving generalization. We open-source all code, data, and tools at https://github.com/autonomousvision/fail2drive .

AI.tldr: Fail2Drive is a paired-route CARLA benchmark for closed-loop generalization with 200 routes and 17 scenario classes; shows a 22.8% average drop in success across SOTA models; provides open-source tooling for scenario creation and solvability validation.
AI.motivation: Generalization under distribution shift is a key bottleneck in autonomous driving. Existing benchmarks reuse training scenarios at test time, conflating memorization with robust behavior. A paired-route design isolates the effect of shifts and enables quantitative diagnostics.
AI.method: Construct 200 routes in CARLA; for each route, generate shifted variants across 17 classes (appearance, layout, behavioral, robustness). Pair each shifted route with a matching in-distribution counterpart to isolate the shift effect. Evaluate multiple state-of-the-art models, quantify degradation, analyze failure modes, and provide an open-source toolbox to create new scenarios and validate solvability using a privileged expert policy.
AI.result: Across models, there is a consistent degradation with an average 22.8% drop in success rate. Failures include objects visible in LiDAR being ignored and failures to grasp concepts of free vs. occupied space. The benchmark yields quantitative diagnostics from qualitative failures and supports reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Fail2Drive establishes a reproducible foundation for benchmarking and improving closed-loop driving generalization in CARLA, with an open-source pipeline for scenario creation and solvability validation via a privileged policy.

title: Learning is Forgetting: LLM Training As Lossy Compression
comment: 12 page core paper, 16 page Appendix - A shorter version with fewer visuals appears at ICLR 2026
summary: Despite the increasing prevalence of large language models (LLMs), we still have a limited understanding of how their representational spaces are structured. This limits our ability to interpret how and what they learn or relate them to learning in humans. We argue LLMs are best seen as an instance of lossy compression, where over training they learn by retaining only information in their training data relevant to their objective(s). We show pre-training results in models that are optimally compressed for next-sequence prediction, approaching the Information Bottleneck bound on compression. Across an array of open weights models, each compresses differently, likely due to differences in the data and training recipes used. However even across different families of LLMs the optimality of a model's compression, and the information present in it, can predict downstream performance on across a wide array of benchmarks, letting us directly link representational structure to actionable insights about model performance. In the general case the work presented here offers a unified Information-Theoretic framing for how these models learn that is deployable at scale.

AI.tldr: LLMs function as lossy compressors; optimal compression approaches the Information Bottleneck bound and predicts downstream performance across models; a scalable information-theoretic framework ties representation to performance.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLM representations are structured and relate them to learning and human cognition, using an information-theoretic lens to connect compression with performance.
AI.method: Analyze pre-training of various open-weight LLMs, quantify their compression relative to the next-sequence objective, assess distance to the Information Bottleneck bound, compare across model families and training recipes, and test whether compression metrics predict downstream benchmark performance.
AI.result: Pre-training yields model-specific compression optima that differ with data and training regimes; higher-quality compression correlates with better downstream performance across benchmarks, even across different LLM families; models approach the Information Bottleneck bound on compression.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified, scalable Information-Theoretic framework for understanding how LLMs learn, linking representational structure to practical performance and enabling transferable insights across models.

title: LPM 1.0: Video-based Character Performance Model
comment: 43 pages, 15 figures, 2 tables. Project page: https://large-performance-model.github.io
summary: Performance, the externalization of intent, emotion, and personality through visual, vocal, and temporal behavior, is what makes a character alive.
