Learning such performance from video is a promising alternative to traditional 3D pipelines. However, existing video models struggle to jointly achieve high expressiveness, real-time inference, and long-horizon identity stability, a tension we call the performance trilemma. Conversation is the most comprehensive performance scenario, as characters simultaneously speak, listen, react, and emote while maintaining identity over time. To address this, we present LPM 1.0 (Large Performance Model), focusing on single-person full-duplex audio-visual conversational performance. Concretely, we build a multimodal human-centric dataset through strict filtering, speaking-listening audio-video pairing, performance understanding, and identity-aware multi-reference extraction; train a 17B-parameter Diffusion Transformer (Base LPM) for highly controllable, identity-consistent performance through multimodal conditioning; and distill it into a causal streaming generator (Online LPM) for low-latency, infinite-length interaction. At inference, given a character image with identity-aware references, LPM 1.0 generates listening videos from user audio and speaking videos from synthesized audio, with text prompts for motion control, all at real-time speed with identity-stable, infinite-length generation. LPM 1.0 thus serves as a visual engine for conversational agents, live streaming characters, and game NPCs. To systematically evaluate this setting, we propose LPM-Bench, the first benchmark for interactive character performance. LPM 1.0 achieves state-of-the-art results across all evaluated dimensions while maintaining real-time inference.

AI.tldr: A 17B diffusion-based Large Performance Model (LPM 1.0) enables real-time, identity-stable, long-horizon, single-person audio-visual conversation generation from limited references, with a dedicated benchmark (LPM-Bench); achieves state-of-the-art results and is distilled into a streaming generator for infinite-length interaction.
AI.motivation: Address the performance trilemma in video-based character performance: expressiveness, real-time inference, and long-horizon identity stability; conversation is a comprehensive scenario requiring synchronized speaking, listening, reacting, and emoting while maintaining identity over time.
AI.method: Create a filtered, multimodal dataset with speaking-listening audio-video pairs and identity-aware references; train a 17B Diffusion Transformer with multimodal conditioning for controllable, identity-consistent performance; distill into a causal streaming generator for low-latency, infinite-length interaction; evaluate with LPM-Bench.
AI.result: LPM 1.0 achieves state-of-the-art results across evaluated dimensions with real-time inference; enables listening-generated videos from user audio and speaking videos from synthesized audio, controlled by text prompts, maintaining identity and long-horizon interaction; introduces LPM-Bench as a benchmark for interactive character performance.
AI.conclusion: LPM 1.0 provides a practical visual engine for conversational agents, live-streamed characters, and game NPCs, enabling identity-stable, infinite-length generation at real-time speeds; sets a benchmark for interactive character performance.

title: SPARD: Self-Paced Curriculum for RL Alignment via Integrating Reward Dynamics and Data Utility
summary: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) is shifting the focus from single, verifiable tasks toward complex, open-ended real-world scenarios, imposing significant challenges on the post-training phase. In these settings, the scale and complexity of reward systems have grown significantly, transitioning toward multi-objective formulations that encompass a comprehensive spectrum of model capabilities and application contexts. However, traditional methods typically rely on fixed reward weights, ignoring non-stationary learning dynamics and struggling with data heterogeneity across dimensions. To address these issues, we propose SPARD, a framework that establishes an automated, self-paced curriculum by perceiving learning progress to dynamically adjust multi-objective reward weights and data importance, thereby synchronizing learning intent with data utility for optimal performance. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SPARD significantly enhances model capabilities across all domains.

AI.tldr: SPARD proposes an automated self-paced curriculum for LLM post-training, dynamically adjusting multi-objective reward weights and data importance based on learning progress to align training with data utility, improving performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In real-world, multi-objective post-training with non-stationary data and heterogeneous samples, fixed reward weights and static curricula fail to exploit data utility and adapt to learning dynamics.
AI.method: SPARD induces a self-paced curriculum by monitoring learning progress to automatically adjust the weights of multiple rewards and the importance of data samples, synchronizing learning intent with data utility.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks show that SPARD substantially improves model capabilities across diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, data-aware curricula for multi-objective post-training can significantly enhance LLM performance, validating the effectiveness of SPARD across tasks.

title: SIM1: Physics-Aligned Simulator as Zero-Shot Data Scaler in Deformable Worlds
summary: Robotic manipulation with deformable objects represents a data-intensive regime in embodied learning, where shape, contact, and topology co-evolve in ways that far exceed the variability of rigids. Although simulation promises relief from the cost of real-world data acquisition, prevailing sim-to-real pipelines remain rooted in rigid-body abstractions, producing mismatched geometry, fragile soft dynamics, and motion primitives poorly suited for cloth interaction. We posit that simulation fails not for being synthetic, but for being ungrounded. To address this, we introduce SIM1, a physics-aligned real-to-sim-to-real data engine that grounds simulation in the physical world. Given limited demonstrations, the system digitizes scenes into metric-consistent twins, calibrates deformable dynamics through elastic modeling, and expands behaviors via diffusion-based trajectory generation with quality filtering. This pipeline transforms sparse observations into scaled synthetic supervision with near-demonstration fidelity. Experiments show that policies trained on purely synthetic data achieve parity with real-data baselines at a 1:15 equivalence ratio, while delivering 90% zero-shot success and 50% generalization gains in real-world deployment. These results validate physics-aligned simulation as scalable supervision for deformable manipulation and a practical pathway for data-efficient policy learning.

AI.tldr: SIM1 grounds deformable object simulation in physical reality to enable data-efficient manipulation learning by converting sparse demonstrations into scalable, metric-consistent synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Deformable manipulation is highly data-intensive and standard rigid-body simulations fail to capture deformation physics, leading to poor sim-to-real transfer. Grounding sim in real physics can yield better fidelity and data efficiency.
AI.method: 1) digitize real scenes into metric-consistent twins; 2) calibrate deformable dynamics with elastic modeling; 3) expand behaviors via diffusion-based trajectory generation with quality filtering; 4) use synthetic supervision to train policies.
AI.result: Synthetic-data–trained policies achieve parity with real-data baselines at a 1:15 equivalence ratio, 90% zero-shot success, and 50% improvement in real-world generalization.
AI.conclusion: Physics-aligned simulation is a scalable supervision source for deformable manipulation and enables data-efficient policy learning, bridging the sim-to-real gap for deformable objects.

title: Implicit Regularization and Generalization in Overparameterized Neural Networks
summary:
Classical statistical learning theory predicts that overparameterized models should exhibit severe overfitting, yet modern deep neural networks with far more parameters than training samples consistently generalize well. This contradiction has become a central theoretical question in machine learning.
  This study investigates the role of optimization dynamics and implicit regularization in enabling generalization in overparameterized neural networks through controlled experiments. We examine stochastic gradient descent (SGD) across batch sizes, the geometry of flat versus sharp minima via Hessian eigenvalue estimation and weight perturbation analysis, the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regime through wide-network experiments, double descent across model scales, and the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis through iterative magnitude pruning. All experiments use PyTorch on CIFAR-10 and MNIST with multiple random seeds.
  Our findings demonstrate that generalization is strongly influenced by the interaction between network architecture, optimization algorithms, and loss landscape geometry. Smaller batch sizes consistently produced lower test error and flatter minima, with an 11.8x difference in top Hessian eigenvalue between small-batch and large-batch solutions corresponding to 1.61 percentage points higher test accuracy. Sparse subnetworks retaining only 10% of parameters achieved within 1.15 percentage points of full model performance when retrained from their original initialization. These results highlight the need for revised learning-theoretic frameworks capable of explaining generalization in high-dimensional model regimes.

AI.tldr: Optimization dynamics and loss landscape shape generalization in overparameterized nets; smaller batch SGD yields flatter minima and better test accuracy; highly sparse subnetworks can nearly match full models, challenging classical theories.
AI.motivation: Classical statistical learning theory predicts overfitting for overparameterized models, yet deep nets generalize well; study investigates implicit regularization via optimization dynamics, Hessian geometry, NTK, double descent, and lottery ticket phenomena.
AI.method: Controlled PyTorch experiments on CIFAR-10 and MNIST; vary batch size in SGD; estimate Hessian eigenvalues, perform weight perturbation; explore NTK regime with wide networks; analyze double descent across model scales; apply iterative magnitude pruning to find lottery tickets; multiple seeds.
AI.result: Generalization depends on interaction of architecture, optimization, and loss landscape; smaller batch sizes yield flatter minima with an 11.8x difference in top Hessian eigenvalue compared to large-batch; this correlates with 1.61 percentage point improvement in test accuracy; 10% sparse subnetworks can reach within 1.15 percentage points of full model when retrained from initialization.
AI.conclusion: Need revised learning-theoretic frameworks capable of explaining generalization in high-dimensional regimes beyond classical theory.

title: FlowGuard: Towards Lightweight In-Generation Safety Detection for Diffusion Models via Linear Latent Decoding
summary: Diffusion-based image generation models have advanced rapidly but pose a safety risk due to their potential to generate Not-Safe-For-Work (NSFW) content. Existing NSFW detection methods mainly operate either before or after image generation. Pre-generation methods rely on text prompts and struggle with the gap between prompt safety and image safety. Post-generation methods apply classifiers to final outputs, but they are poorly suited to intermediate noisy images. To address this, we introduce FlowGuard, a cross-model in-generation detection framework that inspects intermediate denoising steps. This is particularly challenging in latent diffusion, where early-stage noise obscures visual signals. FlowGuard employs a novel linear approximation for latent decoding and leverages a curriculum learning approach to stabilize training. By detecting unsafe content early, FlowGuard reduces unnecessary diffusion steps to cut computational costs. Our cross-model benchmark spanning nine diffusion-based backbones shows the effectiveness of FlowGuard for in-generation NSFW detection in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, outperforming existing methods by over 30% in F1 score while delivering transformative efficiency gains, including slashing peak GPU memory demand by over 97% and projection time from 8.1 seconds to 0.2 seconds compared to standard VAE decoding.

AI.tldr: FlowGuard is a cross-model in-generation NSFW detector for diffusion models that inspects intermediate denoising steps. It uses a linear latent-decoding approximation and curriculum learning to stabilize training, achieving >30% F1 improvement over prior methods and dramatic efficiency gains (GPU memory down ~97%, projection time down from 8.1s to 0.2s) across nine backbones.
AI.motivation: Pre- and post-generation NSFW detectors have limitations: prompts do not always guarantee image safety; post-hoc classifiers struggle with noisy intermediate images. There is a need to detect unsafe content during denoising to save computation.
AI.method: FlowGuard inspects intermediate denoising steps in latent diffusion, employing a novel linear approximation for latent decoding and curriculum learning to stabilize training. It operates cross-model (across backbones) and evaluates NSFW signals early to reduce diffusion steps, reporting results across nine diffusion backbones.
AI.result: FlowGuard outperforms existing methods by over 30% in F1 score in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, and achieves substantial efficiency gains: peak GPU memory reduced by over 97% and projection time reduced from 8.1 seconds to 0.2 seconds compared to standard VAE decoding.
AI.conclusion: FlowGuard enables effective in-generation NSFW detection with major computational savings and broad compatibility across diffusion backbones, addressing the gap between prompt safety and image safety while reducing unnecessary diffusion steps.

title: Hidden Biases in Conditioning Autoregressive Models
summary: Large language and music models are increasingly used for constrained generation: rhyming lines, fixed meter, inpainting or infilling, positional endings, and other global form requirements. These systems often perform strikingly well, but the induced procedures are usually not exact conditioning of the underlying autoregressive model. This creates a hidden inferential bias, distinct from the better-known notion of bias inherited from the training set: samples are distorted relative to the true constrained distribution, with no generic guarantee of complete coverage of the admissible solution space or of correct conditional probabilities over valid completions. We formalize several exact inference tasks for autoregressive models and prove corresponding hardness results. For succinctly represented autoregressive models whose next-token probabilities are computable in polynomial time, exact sentence-level maximum a posteriori (MAP) decoding is NP-hard. This hardness persists under unary and metrical constraints. On the sampling side, exact conditioned normalization is \#P-hard even for regular constraints such as fixed-length terminal events. Unlike finite-state Markov models, general autoregressive models do not admit a bounded-state dynamic program for these tasks. These results formalize a standard claim in the neural decoding literature: local autoregressive sampling is easy, whereas exact decoding and exact conditioning under global form constraints are computationally intractable in general.

AI.tldr: Exact decoding and conditioning for autoregressive models under global form constraints are computationally intractable in general: MAP decoding is NP-hard and exact conditioned normalization is #P-hard, with no bounded-state dynamic program, explaining why practical constrained generation relies on approximate methods.
AI.motivation: Address the tension between empirically effective constrained generation (e.g., rhyme, meter, inpainting) and the lack of guarantees from the underlying autoregressive models; formalize the computational limits of exact inference to explain biases and coverage gaps in constrained sampling.
AI.method: Define exact inference tasks for autoregressive models with efficiently computable next-token probabilities. Prove computational hardness results using reductions: sentence-level MAP decoding is NP-hard under regular and unary/metrical constraints; exact conditioned normalization is #P-hard even for fixed-length events; show no bounded-state DP exists for these tasks in general autoregressive models.
AI.result: Establishes formal hardness landscape: exact decoding and exact conditioning under global constraints are intractable for general autoregressive models, unlike local sampling. This aligns with the empirical claim that exact global constraints are difficult to guarantee, whereas local sampling is tractable.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical justification for the prevalence of approximate decoding methods in constrained generation and highlights the limits of exact inference under global form constraints. It suggests focusing on tractable constraint subclasses or efficient approximation algorithms to achieve practical performance.

title: Auto-Configured Networks for Multi-Scale Multi-Output Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Industrial forecasting often involves multi-source asynchronous signals and multi-output targets, while deployment requires explicit trade-offs between prediction error and model complexity. Current practices typically fix alignment strategies or network designs, making it difficult to systematically co-design preprocessing, architecture, and hyperparameters in budget-limited training-based evaluations. To address this issue, we propose an auto-configuration framework that outputs a deployable Pareto set of forecasting models balancing error and complexity. At the model level, a Multi-Scale Bi-Branch Convolutional Neural Network (MS--BCNN) is developed, where short- and long-kernel branches capture local fluctuations and long-term trends, respectively, for multi-output regression. At the search level, we unify alignment operators, architectural choices, and training hyperparameters into a hierarchical-conditional mixed configuration space, and apply Player-based Hybrid Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithm (PHMOEA) to approximate the error--complexity Pareto frontier within a limited computational budget. Experiments on hierarchical synthetic benchmarks and a real-world sintering dataset demonstrate that our framework outperforms competitive baselines under the same budget and offers flexible deployment choices.

AI.tldr: Auto-config framework yielding a deployable Pareto set of forecasting models balancing error and model complexity; uses MS-BCNN and PHMOEA within a hierarchical configuration space to co-design preprocessing, architecture, and hyperparameters under budget constraints.
AI.motivation: Forecasting with multi-source asynchronous signals and multiple outputs requires explicit trade-offs between accuracy and model complexity, but current practices fix alignment or architecture, hindering systematic budget-aware co-design.
AI.method: Introduce a Multi-Scale Bi-Branch CNN (MS-BCNN) with short- and long-kernel branches for local and long-term patterns in multi-output regression. Unify alignment, architecture, and hyperparameters into a hierarchical-conditional mixed configuration space. Apply a Player-based Hybrid Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithm (PHMOEA) to approximate the error–complexity Pareto frontier within a fixed budget.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on hierarchical synthetic benchmarks and a real-world sintering dataset shows the proposed framework outperforms baselines under the same budget and provides a deployable Pareto set for flexible deployment.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables systematic co-design of forecasting systems by balancing error and complexity, delivering a practical, budget-aware Pareto frontier and adaptable deployment options for multi-source, multi-output forecasting tasks.

title: ReconPhys: Reconstruct Appearance and Physical Attributes from Single Video
summary: Reconstructing non-rigid objects with physical plausibility remains a significant challenge. Existing approaches leverage differentiable rendering for per-scene optimization, recovering geometry and dynamics but requiring expensive tuning or manual annotation, which limits practicality and generalizability. To address this, we propose ReconPhys, the first feedforward framework that jointly learns physical attribute estimation and 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction from a single monocular video. Our method employs a dual-branch architecture trained via a self-supervised strategy, eliminating the need for ground-truth physics labels. Given a video sequence, ReconPhys simultaneously infers geometry, appearance, and physical attributes. Experiments on a large-scale synthetic dataset demonstrate superior performance: our method achieves 21.64 PSNR in future prediction compared to 13.27 by state-of-the-art optimization baselines, while reducing Chamfer Distance from 0.349 to 0.004. Crucially, ReconPhys enables fast inference (<1 second) versus hours required by existing methods, facilitating rapid generation of simulation-ready assets for robotics and graphics.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised, feedforward framework ReconPhys reconstructs non-rigid objects and estimates physical attributes from a single monocular video using 3D Gaussian splatting; it achieves faster, more accurate future-prediction and geometry than optimization baselines on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Overcome the high cost, manual annotation, and limited generalization of differentiable rendering-based per-scene optimization by providing a fast, self-supervised, end-to-end solution for physically plausible non-rigid reconstruction.
AI.method: Dual-branch, self-supervised architecture that jointly learns physical attribute estimation and 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction from monocular video; infers geometry, appearance, and physical attributes without ground-truth physics labels; fast inference (<1s).
AI.result: On a large synthetic dataset, 21.64 PSNR in future prediction vs 13.27 for optimization baselines; Chamfer Distance reduced from 0.349 to 0.004; inference time <1 second vs hours for prior methods.
AI.conclusion: ReconPhys is the first feedforward framework to jointly estimate physical attributes and 3D geometry from monocular video using Gaussian splatting, delivering substantial accuracy and speed gains and enabling rapid generation of simulation-ready assets, though real-world generalization remains to be thoroughly validated.

title: An Agentic Evaluation Architecture for Historical Bias Detection in Educational Textbooks
comment: Accepted for ITS(Intelligent Tutoring Systems) 2026 Full Paper
summary:
History textbooks often contain implicit biases, nationalist framing, and selective omissions that are difficult to audit at scale. We propose an agentic evaluation architecture comprising a multimodal screening agent, a heterogeneous jury of five evaluative agents, and a meta-agent for verdict synthesis and human escalation. A central contribution is a Source Attribution Protocol that distinguishes textbook narrative from quoted historical sources, preventing the misattribution that causes systematic false positives in single-model evaluators.
  In an empirical study on Romanian upper-secondary history textbooks, 83.3\% of 270 screened excerpts were classified as pedagogically acceptable (mean severity 2.9/7), versus 5.4/7 under a zero-shot baseline, demonstrating that agentic deliberation mitigates over-penalization. In a blind human evaluation (18 evaluators, 54 comparisons), the Independent Deliberation configuration was preferred in 64.8\% of cases over both a heuristic variant and the zero-shot baseline. At approximately \$2 per textbook, these results position agentic evaluation architectures as economically viable decision-support tools for educational governance.

AI.tldr: An agentic, multi-agent evaluation system with a source-attribution protocol improves auditing of history textbooks and reduces misclassification, demonstrated in Romanian texts at low cost.
AI.motivation: To address implicit biases, nationalist framing, and selective omissions in history textbooks and to scale auditing beyond manual review by reducing false positives from single-model evaluators.
AI.method: An agentic evaluation architecture: a multimodal screening agent, a heterogeneous jury of five evaluative agents, and a meta-agent for verdict synthesis and human escalation, plus a Source Attribution Protocol to separate narrative from quoted sources. Empirical study on Romanian upper-secondary history textbooks (n=270 excerpts); blind human evaluation comparing configurations and baselines; cost analysis (~$2 per textbook).
AI.result: 83.3% of excerpts classified as pedagogically acceptable (n=270); mean severity 2.9/7 vs 5.4/7 under zero-shot baseline. Independent Deliberation preferred in 64.8% of cases over heuristic variant and zero-shot baseline. Economically viable at ~ $2 per textbook.
AI.conclusion: Agentic deliberation reduces over-penalization and offers a viable, scalable decision-support tool for educational governance, with the Source Attribution Protocol addressing misattribution that plagues single-model evaluators.

title: Sheaf-Laplacian Obstruction and Projection Hardness for Cross-Modal Compatibility on a Modality-Independent Site
comment: 21 pages, 4 figures, submitted to Annals of Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence of Springer Nature
summary: We develop a unified framework for analyzing cross-modal compatibility in learned representations. The core object is a modality-independent neighborhood site on sample indices, equipped with a cellular sheaf of finite-dimensional real inner-product spaces. For a directed modality pair $(a\to b)$, we formalize two complementary incompatibility mechanisms: projection hardness, the minimal complexity within a nested Lipschitz-controlled projection family needed for a single global map to align whitened embeddings; and sheaf-Laplacian obstruction, the minimal spatial variation required by a locally fit field of projection parameters to achieve a target alignment error. The obstruction invariant is implemented via a projection-parameter sheaf whose 0-Laplacian energy exactly matches the smoothness penalty used in sheaf-regularized regression, making the theory directly operational. This separates two distinct failure modes: hardness failure, where no low-complexity global projection exists, and obstruction failure, where local projections exist but cannot be made globally consistent over the semantic neighborhood graph without large parameter variation. We link the sheaf spectral gap to stability of global alignment, derive bounds relating obstruction energy to excess global-map error under mild Lipschitz assumptions, and give explicit constructions showing that compatibility is generally non-transitive. We further define bridging via composed projection families and show, in a concrete ReLU setting, that an intermediate modality can strictly reduce effective hardness even when direct alignment remains infeasible.

AI.tldr: A mathematically rigorous, unified sheaf-theoretic framework for cross-modal representation alignment that separates two core failure modes—projection hardness and sheaf-Laplacian obstruction—and analyzes stability via spectral gaps, with results on non-transitivity and bridging through intermediate modalities.
AI.motivation: Quantify and diagnose cross-modal compatibility of learned representations using a principled, modular mathematical formalism that can operationalize alignment penalties and stability bounds.
AI.method: Introduce a directed modality pair with a modality-agnostic neighborhood site and a cellular sheaf of finite-dimensional inner-product spaces. Define two incompatibility mechanisms: (i) projection hardness (minimal complexity for a global map to align whitened embeddings within a Lipschitz-controlled projection family) and (ii) sheaf-Laplacian obstruction (minimal spatial variation in projection parameters to achieve target alignment). Implement obstruction via a projection-parameter sheaf; equate its 0-Laplacian energy with the smoothness penalty in sheaf-regularized regression. Analyze failure modes (hardness vs obstruction), link sheaf spectral gap to alignment stability, derive bounds relating obstruction energy to excess global-map error under Lipschitz assumptions, and give constructions showing non-transitivity of compatibility. Propose bridging via composed projection families and show that in a ReLU setting an intermediate modality can reduce hardness even if direct alignment fails.
AI.result: Defines invariants (hardness, obstruction energy, spectral gap) and quantitative bounds connecting them to global-map error; demonstrates non-transitivity of compatibility; provides mechanisms to bridge modalities and a concrete ReLU example illustrating reduced hardness through intermediates; enables direct operationalization via 0-Laplacian energy matching standard regularization penalties.
AI.conclusion: The framework clarifies when cross-modal alignment is feasible and identifies two distinct obstruction sources, while revealing that added modalities can improve compatibility even when direct alignment is infeasible; offers a trackable path for designing robust, multi-modal representations and for empirical validation of the proposed invariants.

title: Reinforcement-Guided Synthetic Data Generation for Privacy-Sensitive Identity Recognition
summary: High-fidelity generative models are increasingly needed in privacy-sensitive scenarios, where access to data is severely restricted due to regulatory and copyright constraints. This scarcity hampers model development--ironically, in settings where generative models are most needed to compensate for the lack of data. This creates a self-reinforcing challenge: limited data leads to poor generative models, which in turn fail to mitigate data scarcity. To break this cycle, we propose a reinforcement-guided synthetic data generation framework that adapts general-domain generative priors to privacy-sensitive identity recognition tasks. We first perform a cold-start adaptation to align a pretrained generator with the target domain, establishing semantic relevance and initial fidelity. Building on this foundation, we introduce a multi-objective reward that jointly optimizes semantic consistency, coverage diversity, and expression richness, guiding the generator to produce both realistic and task-effective samples. During downstream training, a dynamic sample selection mechanism further prioritizes high-utility synthetic samples, enabling adaptive data scaling and improved domain alignment. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our framework significantly improves both generation fidelity and classification accuracy, while also exhibiting strong generalization to novel categories in small-data regimes.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement-guided synthetic data generation to adapt general-domain priors for privacy-sensitive identity recognition, addressing data scarcity via cold-start domain adaptation, multi-objective rewards (semantic consistency, coverage diversity, expression richness), and dynamic sample selection to boost fidelity and downstream accuracy; shows improved fidelity, accuracy, and generalization in small-data regimes.
AI.motivation: Privacy and copyright constraints create severe data scarcity, hindering high-fidelity generative modeling; the cycle of limited data leading to poor models that cannot alleviate scarcity needs breaking.
AI.method: 1) Cold-start adaptation to align a pretrained generator with the target privacy-sensitive domain; 2) Multi-objective reinforcement learning reward combining semantic consistency, coverage diversity, and expression richness to produce task-effective samples; 3) Downstream training with a dynamic sample selection mechanism prioritizing high-utility synthetic data for adaptive data scaling and improved domain alignment.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant gains in generation fidelity and classification accuracy and strong generalization to novel categories in small-data regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reinforcement-guided synthetic data framework effectively leverages general-domain priors and adapts to privacy-sensitive tasks, mitigating data scarcity and improving both generative and discriminative performance, with good generalization to unseen categories.

title: DialBGM: A Benchmark for Background Music Recommendation from Everyday Multi-Turn Dialogues
summary: Selecting an appropriate background music (BGM) that supports natural human conversation is a common production step in media and interactive systems. In this paper, we introduce dialogue-conditioned BGM recommendation, where a model should select non-intrusive, fitting music for a multi-turn conversation that often contains no music descriptors. To study this novel problem, we present DialBGM, a benchmark of 1,200 open-domain daily dialogues, each paired with four candidate music clips and annotated with human preference rankings. Rankings are determined by background suitability criteria, including contextual relevance, non-intrusiveness, and consistency. We evaluate a wide range of open-source and proprietary models, including audio-language models and multimodal LLMs, and show that current models fall far short of human judgments; no model exceeds 35% Hit@1 when selecting the top-ranked clip. DialBGM provides a standardized benchmark for developing discourse-aware methods for BGM selection and for evaluating both retrieval-based and generative models.

AI.tldr: DialBGM introduces a benchmark for dialogue-conditioned background music (BGM) recommendation, revealing that current systems struggle to match human judgments in selecting non-intrusive, contextually appropriate music for multi-turn conversations; best Hit@1 remains below 35%.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of selecting suitable BGM that supports natural, discourse-focused conversations when dialogue lacks explicit music descriptors, requiring discourse-aware recommendations.
AI.method: Constructed DialBGM dataset with 1,200 open-domain dialogues. For each dialogue, provided four candidate music clips and human-annotated preference rankings based on contextual relevance, non-intrusiveness, and consistency. Evaluated a broad set of models (audio–language models and multimodal LLMs) in retrieval and generative settings.
AI.result: Current models fall far short of human judgments; no model achieves Hit@1 above 35% for top-ranked clip, indicating a substantial gap.
AI.conclusion: DialBGM offers a standardized benchmark for developing and evaluating discourse-aware BGM selection methods, enabling fair comparison across retrieval-based and generative approaches, and highlighting the need for improved dialogue-aware BGM systems.

title: ParkSense: Where Should a Delivery Driver Park? Leveraging Idle AV Compute and Vision-Language Models
comment: 7 pages, 3 tables. No university resources were used for this work
summary: Finding parking consumes a disproportionate share of food delivery time, yet no system addresses precise parking-spot selection relative to merchant entrances. We propose ParkSense, a framework that repurposes idle compute during low-risk AV states -- queuing at red lights, traffic congestion, parking-lot crawl -- to run a Vision-Language Model (VLM) on pre-cached satellite and street view imagery, identifying entrances and legal parking zones. We formalize the Delivery-Aware Precision Parking (DAPP) problem, show that a quantized 7B VLM completes inference in 4-8 seconds on HW4-class hardware, and estimate annual per-driver income gains of 3,000-8,000 USD in the U.S. Five open research directions are identified at this unexplored intersection of autonomous driving, computer vision, and last-mile logistics.

AI.tldr: ParkSense repurposes idle autonomous vehicle compute to run a Vision-Language Model on pre-cached satellite and street-view imagery to identify entrances and legal parking zones, enabling precise parking for last-mile food delivery. It formalizes the Delivery-Aware Precision Parking (DAPP) problem, demonstrates 7B VLM inference in 4–8 seconds on HW4-class hardware, and estimates substantial per-driver income gains (US$3k–8k annually). Five open research directions are identified at the intersection of autonomous driving, computer vision, and last-mile logistics.
AI.motivation: To reduce the substantial time that parking consumes in food delivery by enabling precise, legally compliant parking spot selection near merchant entrances, addressing a gap in existing systems.
AI.method: Repurpose idle compute during low-risk autonomous-vehicle states (e.g., queuing at red lights, traffic, parking-lot crawling) to run a 7B Vision-Language Model on pre-cached satellite and street-view imagery, identify entrances and legal parking zones, and formalize the Delivery-Aware Precision Parking (DAPP) problem.
AI.result: A quantized 7B VLM completes inference in 4–8 seconds on HW4-class hardware. Estimated annual per-driver income gains in the U.S. range from $3,000 to $8,000.
AI.conclusion: The work identifies five open research directions at the confluence of autonomous driving, computer vision, and last-mile logistics.

title: Cognitive-Causal Multi-Task Learning with Psychological State Conditioning for Assistive Driving Perception
summary: Multi-task learning for advanced driver assistance systems requires modeling the complex interplay between driver internal states and external traffic environments. However, existing methods treat recognition tasks as flat and independent objectives, failing to exploit the cognitive causal structure underlying driving behavior. In this paper, we propose CauPsi, a cognitive science-grounded causal multi-task learning framework that explicitly models the hierarchical dependencies among Traffic Context Recognition (TCR), Vehicle Context Recognition (VCR), Driver Emotion Recognition (DER), and Driver Behavior Recognition (DBR). The proposed framework introduces two key mechanisms. First, a Causal Task Chain propagates upstream task predictions to downstream tasks via learnable prototype embeddings, realizing the cognitive cascade from environmental perception to behavioral regulation in a differentiable manner. Second, Cross-Task Psychological Conditioning (CTPC) estimates a psychological state signal from driver facial expressions and body posture and injects it as a conditioning input to all tasks including environmental recognition, thereby modeling the modulatory effect of driver internal states on cognitive and decision-making processes. Evaluated on the AIDE dataset, CauPsi achieves a mean accuracy of 82.71% with only 5.05M parameters, surpassing prior work by +1.0% overall, with notable improvements on DER (+3.65%) and DBR (+7.53%). Ablation studies validate the independent contribution of each component, and analysis of the psychological state signal confirms that it acquires systematic task-label-dependent patterns in a self-supervised manner without explicit psychological annotations.

AI.tldr: CauPsi is a cognitive science-grounded multi-task framework for driving-related recognition tasks that uses a Causal Task Chain and Cross-Task Psychological Conditioning to model hierarchical dependencies and internal-state modulation, achieving state-of-the-art results on AIDE with 5.05M parameters.
AI.motivation: Current multi-task learning for driver assistance treats tasks as flat and independent, missing the cognitive causal structure linking environmental perception, vehicle context, driver state, and behavior. There are hierarchical dependencies among Traffic Context Recognition (TCR), Vehicle Context Recognition (VCR), Driver Emotion Recognition (DER), and Driver Behavior Recognition (DBR) that could be exploited.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) Causal Task Chain propagates upstream task predictions to downstream tasks via learnable prototype embeddings, creating a differentiable cognitive cascade from perception to behavioral regulation. (2) Cross-Task Psychological Conditioning (CTPC) derives a psychological state signal from driver facial expressions and body posture and injects it as conditioning input to all tasks, modeling how internal states modulate cognitive and decision processes.
AI.result: Evaluated on the AIDE dataset, CauPsi achieves a mean accuracy of 82.71% with 5.05M parameters, surpassing prior work by 1.0% overall, with notable gains on DER (+3.65%) and DBR (+7.53%). Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component, and the psychological state signal shows task-label-dependent patterns in a self-supervised manner without explicit psychological annotations.
AI.conclusion: CauPsi effectively encodes cognitive causal structure in a multi-task driving framework, offering parameter efficiency and improved performance across tasks. The two proposed components—Causal Task Chain and CTPC—play complementary roles in capturing hierarchical dependencies and internal-state modulation.

title: Sampling-Aware 3D Spatial Analysis in Multiplexed Imaging
comment: Accepted to The 11th IEEE Workshop on Computer Vision for Multimodal Microscopy Image Analysis (CVMI), a CVPR 2026 workshop
summary: Highly multiplexed microscopy enables rich spatial characterization of tissues at single-cell resolution, yet most analyses rely on two-dimensional sections despite inherently three-dimensional tissue organization. Acquiring dense volumetric data in spatial proteomics remains costly and technically challenging, leaving practitioners to choose between 2D sections or 3D serial sections under limited imaging budgets. In this work, we study how sampling geometry impacts the stability of commonly used spatial statistics, and we introduce a geometry-aware reconstruction module that enables sparse yet consistent 3D analysis from serial sections. Using controlled simulations, we show that planar sampling reliably recovers global cell-type abundance but exhibits high variance for local statistics such as cell clustering and cell-cell interactions, particularly for rare or spatially localized populations. We observe consistent behavior in real multiplexed datasets, where interaction metrics and neighborhood relationships fluctuate substantially across individual sections. To support sparse 3D analysis in practice, we present a reconstruction approach that links cell projections across adjacent sections using phenotype and proximity constraints and recovers single-cell 3D centroids using cell-type-specific shape priors. We further analyze the trade-off between section spacing, coverage, and redundancy, identifying acquisition regimes that maximize reconstruction utility under fixed imaging budgets. We validate the reconstruction module on a public imaging mass cytometry dataset with dense axial sampling and demonstrate its downstream utility on an in-house CODEX dataset by enabling structure-level 3D analyses that are unreliable in 2D. Together, our results provide diagnostic tools and practical guidance for deciding when 2D sampling suffices and when sparse 3D reconstruction is warranted.

AI.tldr: Proposes geometry-aware 3D reconstruction for sparse sampling in spatial proteomics; planar 2D sampling preserves global cell-type abundance but poorly captures local statistics; introduces a reconstruction module that links cell projections across sections using phenotype/proximity constraints and shape priors; analyzes trade-offs between section spacing and budget; validates on public IMC data and CODEX, providing guidance on when 2D suffices vs sparse 3D.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between costly dense 3D spatial proteomics and cheaper 2D sampling by enabling stable, interpretable 3D analyses from sparse serial sections.
AI.method: Controlled simulations to study sampling geometry effects; develop a geometry-aware reconstruction module that links cell projections across adjacent sections via phenotype and proximity constraints; use cell-type-specific shape priors to recover 3D centroids; analyze section spacing, coverage, and redundancy under fixed imaging budgets; validate on a publicly available dense-axial IMC dataset and apply to an in-house CODEX dataset.
AI.result: Planar sampling reliably recovers global cell-type abundance but shows high variance for local statistics (e.g., clustering, cell-cell interactions), especially for rare/localized populations. Real datasets show similar fluctuations across sections. The reconstruction module improves 3D consistency, enabling structure-level analyses that 2D cannot support. Identified acquisition regimes that maximize reconstruction utility under budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: Provides diagnostic tools and practical guidance for deciding when 2D sampling suffices and when sparse 3D reconstruction is warranted, thereby enabling more reliable 3D analyses in spatial proteomics with limited imaging budgets.

title: Visual Perceptual to Conceptual First-Order Rule Learning Networks
summary: Learning rules plays a crucial role in deep learning, particularly in explainable artificial intelligence and enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models. While existing rule learning methods are primarily designed for symbolic data, learning rules from image data without supporting image labels and automatically inventing predicates remains a challenge. In this paper, we tackle these inductive rule learning problems from images with a framework called γILP, which provides a fully differentiable pipeline from image constant substitution to rule structure induction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that γILP achieves strong performance not only on classical symbolic relational datasets but also on relational image data and pure image datasets, such as Kandinsky patterns.

AI.tldr: γILP is a differentiable inductive logic programming framework that learns rules directly from images, enabling predicate invention and image-label-free learning, with strong results on symbolic and image datasets.
AI.motivation: Address inductive rule learning from image data without labels and the automatic invention of predicates, bridging symbolic ILP and perception for explainable AI and enhanced reasoning in LLMs.
AI.method: A fully differentiable pipeline that goes from image constant substitution to rule structure induction, forming an end-to-end differentiable ILP framework (γILP).
AI.result: Experiments show strong performance on classical symbolic relational datasets, relational image data, and purely image datasets such as Kandinsky patterns.
AI.conclusion: γILP provides a viable approach to learning rules from images and inventing predicates in a differentiable setting, enabling scalable reasoning across perceptual and symbolic domains.

title: WorldMAP: Bootstrapping Vision-Language Navigation Trajectory Prediction with Generative World Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) and generative world models are opening new opportunities for embodied navigation. VLMs are increasingly used as direct planners or trajectory predictors, while world models support look-ahead reasoning by imagining future views. Yet predicting a reliable trajectory from a single egocentric observation remains challenging. Current VLMs often generate unstable trajectories, and world models, though able to synthesize plausible futures, do not directly provide the grounded signals needed for navigation learning. This raises a central question: how can generated futures be turned into supervision for grounded trajectory prediction? We present WorldMAP, a teacher--student framework that converts world-model-generated futures into persistent semantic-spatial structure and planning-derived supervision. Its world-model-driven teacher builds semantic-spatial memory from generated videos, grounds task-relevant targets and obstacles, and produces trajectory pseudo-labels through explicit planning. A lightweight student with a multi-hypothesis trajectory head is then trained to predict navigation trajectories directly from vision-language inputs. On Target-Bench, WorldMAP achieves the best ADE and FDE among compared methods, reducing ADE by 18.0% and FDE by 42.1% relative to the best competing baseline, while lifting a small open-source VLM to DTW performance competitive with proprietary models. More broadly, the results suggest that, in embodied navigation, the value of world models may lie less in supplying action-ready imagined evidence than in synthesizing structured supervision for navigation learning.

AI.tldr: WorldMAP turns world-model-generated futures into supervision for grounded trajectory prediction via a teacher–student framework, yielding superior ADE/FDE on Target-Bench and enabling small VLMs to approach DTW-level performance.
AI.motivation: Predicting reliable egocentric navigation trajectories from a single observation is highly challenging. VLMs often produce unstable trajectories, and world models, while capable of imagining futures, do not provide grounded supervisory signals for learning. A way to turn imagined futures into actionable supervision is needed.
AI.method: WorldMAP introduces a teacher–student framework: the teacher uses world-model-generated videos to build a semantic–spatial memory, ground task-relevant targets/obstacles, and generate trajectory pseudo-labels via explicit planning; the student is a lightweight model with a multi-hypothesis trajectory head trained to predict navigation trajectories directly from vision–language inputs.
AI.result: On Target-Bench, WorldMAP achieves the best ADE and FDE among compared methods, reducing ADE by 18.0% and FDE by 42.1% relative to the best baseline; also lifts a small open-source VLM to Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) performance competitive with proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: World-models may derive their value not from action-ready imagined evidence but from synthesizing structured supervision for navigation learning, enabling grounded trajectory prediction and robust performance improvements in embodied navigation.

title: Guardian-as-an-Advisor: Advancing Next-Generation Guardian Models for Trustworthy LLMs
summary: Hard-gated safety checkers often over-refuse and misalign with a vendor's model spec; prevailing taxonomies also neglect robustness and honesty, yielding safer-on-paper yet less useful systems. This work introduces Guardian-as-an-Advisor (GaaA), a soft-gating pipeline where a guardian predicts a binary risk label plus a concise explanation and prepends this advice to the original query for re-inference, keeping the base model operating under its original spec. To support training and evaluation, GuardSet is constructed, a 208k+ multi-domain dataset unifying harmful and harmless cases with targeted robustness and honesty slices. GuardAdvisor is trained via SFT followed by RL to enforce label-explanation consistency. GuardAdvisor attains competitive detection accuracy while enabling the advisory workflow; when used to augment inputs, responses improve over unaugmented prompts. A latency study shows advisor inference uses below 5% of base-model compute and adds only 2-10% end-to-end overhead under realistic harmful-input rates. Overall, GaaA steers models to comply with the model spec, maintaining safety while reducing over-refusal.

AI.tldr: Guardian-as-an-Advisor (GaaA) is a soft-gating advisory framework that appends a binary risk label and concise explanation to user queries for re-inference, preserving the base model’s original specification while improving safety and usefulness. It is supported by GuardSet (208k+ multi-domain data) and GuardAdvisor (SFT+RL-trained) which delivers competitive detection accuracy and low latency overhead.
AI.motivation: Hard-gated safety checkers tend to over-refuse and misalign with vendor model specs, and current taxonomies neglect robustness and honesty. This leads to safer-on-paper but less useful systems. There is a need to align safety mechanisms with model behavior while maintaining usability and compliance with the model’s spec.
AI.method: Introduce a soft-gating guardian that predicts a binary risk label plus a concise explanation and prepends this advice to the original query for re-inference, leaving the base model unchanged. Build GuardSet (208k+ multi-domain) to cover harmful/harmless cases with targeted robustness and honesty slices. Train GuardAdvisor via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) to enforce label-explanation consistency. Evaluate detection accuracy, advisory usefulness, and latency (guardian inference uses <5% of base-model compute; 2–10% end-to-end overhead).
AI.result: GuardAdvisor achieves competitive detection accuracy and enables the advisory workflow; augmented inputs yield improved responses compared to unaugmented prompts. The latency is low, preserving efficiency while increasing adherence to the model spec.
AI.conclusion: GaaA steers models toward compliance with the model spec, preserving safety while reducing over-refusal.

title: AnomalyAgent: Agentic Industrial Anomaly Synthesis via Tool-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
summary: Industrial anomaly generation is a crucial method for alleviating the data scarcity problem in anomaly detection tasks. Most existing anomaly synthesis methods rely on single-step generation mechanisms, lacking complex reasoning and iterative optimization capabilities, making it difficult to generate anomaly samples with high semantic realism. We propose AnomalyAgent, an anomaly synthesis agent with self-reflection, knowledge retrieval, and iterative refinement capabilities, aiming to generate realistic and diverse anomalies. Specifically, AnomalyAgent is equipped with five tools: Prompt Generation (PG), Image Generation (IG), Quality Evaluation (QE), Knowledge Retrieval (KR), and Mask Generation (MG), enabling closed-loop optimization. To improve decision-making and self-reflection, we construct structured trajectories from real anomaly images and design a two-stage training framework: supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning. This process is driven by a three-part reward mechanism: (1) task rewards to supervise the quality and location rationality of generated anomalies; (2) reflection rewards to train the model's ability to improve anomaly synthesis prompt; (3) behavioral rewards to ensure adherence to the trajectory. On the MVTec-AD dataset, AnomalyAgent achieves IS/IC-L of 2.10/0.33 for anomaly generation, 57.0% classification accuracy using ResNet34, and 99.3%/74.2% AP at the image/pixel level using a simple UNet, surpassing all zero-shot SOTA methods. The code and data will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: AnomalyAgent uses a multi-tool, self-reflective anomaly synthesis agent with iterative refinement and closed-loop optimization to generate realistic industrial anomalies, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot results on MVTec-AD.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity in anomaly detection by enabling high-semantic-realism anomaly synthesis through iterative reasoning, self-reflection, and knowledge-guided prompts.
AI.method: AnomalyAgent employs five tools—Prompt Generation (PG), Image Generation (IG), Quality Evaluation (QE), Knowledge Retrieval (KR), and Mask Generation (MG)—to construct closed-loop trajectories from real anomaly images. Training comprises supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning, guided by a three-part reward: task rewards (quality/location rationality), reflection rewards (prompt improvement), and behavioral rewards (trajectory adherence).
AI.result: On MVTec-AD: anomaly generation metrics IS/IC-L = 2.10/0.33; classification accuracy 57.0% with ResNet34; image/pixel AP = 99.3% / 74.2% using a simple UNet. Claims to surpass all zero-shot SOTA; code and data will be released.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a multi-tool, self-reflective approach can significantly enhance anomaly synthesis realism and diversity, with strong empirical results on MVTec-AD and potential applicability to broader industrial inspection tasks.

title: Capture-Quiet Decomposition: A Verification Theorem for Chess Endgame Tablebases
comment: 9 pages, 3 tables. Validated on 517 endgames covering 6.5 billion positions
summary: We present the Capture-Quiet Decomposition (CQD), a structural theorem for verifying Win-Draw-Loss (WDL) labelings of chess endgame tablebases. The theorem decomposes every legal position into exactly one of three categories -- terminal, capture, or quiet -- and shows that a WDL labeling is correct if and only if: (1) terminal positions are labeled correctly, (2) capture positions are consistent with verified sub-models of smaller piece count, and (3) quiet positions satisfy retrograde consistency within the same endgame. The key insight is that capture positions anchor the labeling to externally verified sub-models, breaking the circularity that allows trivial fixpoints (such as the all-draw labeling) to satisfy self-consistency alone. We validate CQD exhaustively on all 35 three- and four-piece endgames (42 million positions), all 110 five-piece endgames, and all 372 six-piece endgames -- 517 endgames in total -- with the decomposed verifier producing identical violation counts to a full retrograde baseline in every case.

AI.tldr: Capture-Quiet Decomposition (CQD) provides a formal, verifiable decomposition for Win-Draw-Loss endgame labeling by partitioning positions into terminal, capture, and quiet, and proving correctness via three conditions; validated exhaustively on 517 endgames, matching full retrograde baselines.
AI.motivation: Address circularity and trivial fixpoints in retrograde WDL verification by anchoring labeling to externally verified sub-models via capture positions; ensure soundness and scalability across endgames.
AI.method: Decompose each legal position into terminal, capture, or quiet. Require: (a) terminal labels are correct; (b) capture labels are consistent with verified sub-models of smaller piece count; (c) quiet labels satisfy retrograde consistency within the same endgame. Validate exhaustively against all positions in 35 three- and four-piece endgames, 110 five-piece endgames, and 372 six-piece endgames (517 total), comparing to a full retrograde baseline.
AI.result: The decomposed verifier produced identical violation counts to the full retrograde baseline in every tested case.
AI.conclusion: CQD provides a correct, scalable framework for validating WDL endgame labels, mitigating circularity by anchoring through capture positions and matching full baselines up to six pieces; it offers a practical verification path for larger endgames.

title: "Why This Avoidance Maneuver?" Contrastive Explanations in Human-Supervised Maritime Autonomous Navigation
comment: Submitted to IEEE Intelligent Transportation Systems Conference (ITSC) 2026
summary: Automated maritime collision avoidance will rely on human supervision for the foreseeable future. This necessitates transparency into how the system perceives a scenario and plans a maneuver. However, the causal logic behind avoidance maneuvers is often complex and difficult to convey to a navigator. This paper explores how to explain these factors in a selective, understandable manner for supervisors with a nautical background. We propose a method for generating contrastive explanations, which provide human-centric insights by comparing a system's proposed solution against relevant alternatives. To evaluate this, we developed a framework that uses visual and textual cues to highlight key objectives from a state-of-the-art collision avoidance system. An exploratory user study with four experienced marine officers suggests that contrastive explanations support the understanding of the system's objectives. However, our findings also reveal that while these explanations are highly valuable in complex multi-vessel encounters, they can increase cognitive workload, suggesting that future maritime interfaces may benefit most from demand-driven or scenario-specific explanation strategies.

AI.tldr: Study on contrastive, human-centric explanations for automated maritime collision avoidance; shows usefulness for understanding in complex scenarios but potential cognitive load, advocating adaptive, scenario-specific explanations.
AI.motivation: To improve transparency and trust in autonomous maritime perception/planning by explaining decisions to navigators with nautical background using contrastive explanations.
AI.method: Developed a framework generating contrastive explanations with visual/textual cues highlighting objectives from a state-of-the-art system; evaluated via exploratory user study with four experienced marine officers.
AI.result: Contrastive explanations improved understanding of the system's objectives; especially helpful in multi-vessel encounters but may increase cognitive workload; imply demand-driven or scenario-specific explanation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive explanations are promising for transparency but should be adaptive to complexity and user workload; future work to tailor explanations to scenarios and possibly reduce cognitive load.

title: Optimal Decay Spectra for Linear Recurrences
summary: Linear recurrent models offer linear-time sequence processing but often suffer from suboptimal long-range memory. We trace this to the decay spectrum: for $N$ channels, random initialization collapses the minimum spectral gap to $O(N^{-2})$, yielding sub-exponential error $\exp(-Ω(N/\log N))$; linear spacing avoids collapse but degrades to $\exp(-O(N/\sqrt{T}))$, practically algebraic over long contexts. We introduce Position-Adaptive Spectral Tapering (PoST), an architecture-agnostic framework combining two mechanisms: (1) Spectral Reparameterization, which structurally enforces geometrically spaced log-decay rates, proven minimax optimal at rate $O(\exp(-cN/\log T))$; and (2) Position-Adaptive Scaling, the provably unique mechanism that eliminates the scale mismatch of static spectra (where only $N\log t/\log T$ of $N$ channels are effective at position $t$) by stretching the spectrum to the actual dependency range, sharpening the rate to $O(\exp(-cN/\log t))$. This scaling natively induces fractional invariance: the impulse response becomes scale-free, with channels interpolating between relative and absolute temporal coordinates. PoST integrates into any diagonal linear recurrence without overhead. We instantiate it across Mamba-2, RWKV-7, Gated DeltaNet, Gated Linear Attention, and RetNet. Pre-training at 180M-440M scales shows consistent zero-shot language modeling improvements, significant long-context retrieval gains for Mamba-2 (MQAR and NIAH), and competitive or improved performance across other architectures. Code: https://github.com/SiLifen/PoST.

AI.tldr: PoST (Position-Adaptive Spectral Tapering) is a framework to improve long-range memory in linear recurrent models by spectral reparameterization and position-adaptive scaling, achieving minimax-optimal decay rates and scale-free impulse responses; it is architecture-agnostic and shows empirical gains across several models.
AI.motivation: Long-range dependencies in linear recurrent models are limited by the decay spectrum; random init collapses spectral gap; existing spacing causes suboptimal decay; need architecture-agnostic, provably optimal methods to extend context length.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: Spectral Reparameterization (enforces geometrically spaced log-decay rates; minimax optimal at rate exp(-c N / log T)); Position-Adaptive Scaling (stretches spectrum to actual dependency range, makes impulse response scale-free; rate exp(-c N / log t); integrates into diagonal linear recurrence).
AI.result: Pre-training 180M–440M shows consistent zero-shot language modeling improvements; substantial long-context retrieval gains for Mamba-2 (MQAR and NIAH); competitive/improved performance for other architectures.
AI.conclusion: PoST provides an architecture-agnostic spectral-tapering framework with provable long-range memory advantages, scalable to large contexts; code available.

title: PanoSAM2: Lightweight Distortion- and Memory-aware Adaptions of SAM2 for 360 Video Object Segmentation
summary: 360 video object segmentation (360VOS) aims to predict temporally-consistent masks in 360 videos, offering full-scene coverage, benefiting applications, such as VR/AR and embodied AI. Learning 360VOS model is nontrivial due to the lack of high-quality labeled dataset. Recently, Segment Anything Models (SAMs), especially SAM2 -- with its design of memory module -- shows strong, promptable VOS capability. However, directly using SAM2 for 360VOS yields implausible results as 360 videos suffer from the projection distortion, semantic inconsistency of left-right sides, and sparse object mask information in SAM2's memory. To this end, we propose PanoSAM2, a novel 360VOS framework based on our lightweight distortion- and memory-aware adaptation strategies of SAM2 to achieve reliable 360VOS while retaining SAM2's user-friendly prompting design. Concretely, to tackle the projection distortion and semantic inconsistency issues, we propose a Pano-Aware Decoder with seam-consistent receptive fields and iterative distortion refinement to maintain continuity across the 0/360 degree boundary. Meanwhile, a Distortion-Guided Mask Loss is introduced to weight pixels by distortion magnitude, stressing stretched regions and boundaries. To address the object sparsity issue, we propose a Long-Short Memory Module to maintain a compact long-term object pointer to re-instantiate and align short-term memories, thereby enhancing temporal coherence. Extensive experiments show that PanoSAM2 yields substantial gains over SAM2: +5.6 on 360VOTS and +6.7 on PanoVOS, showing the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: PanoSAM2 adapts SAM2 for 360VOS by adding distortion-aware decoding, distortion-guided loss, and long-short memory to address projection distortion, boundary seam, and sparse masks, achieving stateful improvements on 360VOS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: 360VOS lacks large labeled datasets; SAM2-like memory-based VOS shows promise but struggles with 360-specific issues (projection distortion, left-right semantic inconsistency, sparse object masks). A 360-aware, memory-efficient adaptation can leverage SAM2’s promptable VOS while addressing 360 distortions.
AI.method: Introduce Pano-SAM2 with (1) Pano-Aware Decoder featuring seam-consistent receptive fields and iterative distortion refinement to maintain 0/360 continuity; (2) Distortion-Guided Mask Loss that weights pixels by distortion magnitude to emphasize boundaries; (3) Long-Short Memory Module to maintain a compact long-term object pointer and align with short-term memories to improve temporal coherence.
AI.result: Empirical gains over SAM2: +5.6 on 360VOTS and +6.7 on PanoVOS, demonstrating effectiveness of the proposed adaptation strategies.
AI.conclusion: PanoSAM2 effectively tailors SAM2 for 360VOS, preserving promptability while mitigating 360-specific challenges; the approach yields notable performance boosts on standard 360VOS benchmarks. Further work could explore broader generalization and ablations.

title: PriPG-RL: Privileged Planner-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Partially Observable Systems with Anytime-Feasible MPC
summary: This paper addresses the problem of training a reinforcement learning (RL) policy under partial observability by exploiting a privileged, anytime-feasible planner agent available exclusively during training. We formalize this as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) in which a planner agent with access to an approximate dynamical model and privileged state information guides a learning agent that observes only a lossy projection of the true state. To realize this framework, we introduce an anytime-feasible Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm that serves as the planner agent. For the learning agent, we propose Planner-to-Policy Soft Actor-Critic (P2P-SAC), a method that distills the planner agent's privileged knowledge to mitigate partial observability and thereby improve both sample efficiency and final policy performance. We support this framework with rigorous theoretical analysis. Finally, we validate our approach in simulation using NVIDIA Isaac Lab and successfully deploy it on a real-world Unitree Go2 quadruped navigating complex, obstacle-rich environments.

AI.tldr: A training-time planner-guided RL framework for partially observable tasks using an anytime-feasible MPC planner and Planner-to-Policy SAC (P2P-SAC) to distill privileged knowledge, improving sample efficiency and final policy performance, validated in simulation and on a real quadruped.
AI.motivation: Partial observability in RL hinders learning and data efficiency. Leveraging a privileged, approximate-model planner available during training can provide informative guidance to the learning agent, enabling better handling of lossy observations.
AI.method: Formulate the problem as a POMDP with a planner that has access to privileged information and an approximate dynamics model. Develop an anytime-feasible MPC planner as the planner agent. Propose Planner-to-Policy Soft Actor-Critic (P2P-SAC) to distill planner knowledge into the learning agent, improving sample efficiency and final performance. Provide theoretical analysis and validate via simulation (NVIDIA Isaac Lab) and real-world deployment (Unitree Go2 quadruped) in obstacle-rich environments.
AI.result: The framework yields improved sample efficiency and superior final policy performance in simulation, and achieves successful real-world deployment on a quadruped navigating complex environments.
AI.conclusion: Privileged planner guidance during training can meaningfully mitigate partial observability in RL. P2P-SAC effectively distills planner-derived information into the policy, with demonstrated benefits in both simulated and real-world settings.

title: SAGE: Sign-Adaptive Gradient for Memory-Efficient LLM Optimization
comment: Accepted to Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2026. 13 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables
summary: The AdamW optimizer, while standard for LLM pretraining, is a critical memory bottleneck, consuming optimizer states equivalent to twice the model's size. Although light-state optimizers like SinkGD attempt to address this issue, we identify the embedding layer dilemma: these methods fail to handle the sparse, high-variance gradients inherent to embeddings, forcing a hybrid design that reverts to AdamW and partially negates the memory gains. We propose SAGE (Sign Adaptive GradiEnt), a novel optimizer that resolves this dilemma by replacing AdamW in this hybrid structure. SAGE combines a Lion-style update direction with a new, memory-efficient $O(d)$ adaptive scale. This scale acts as a "safe damper," provably bounded by 1.0, which tames high-variance dimensions more effectively than existing methods. This superior stability allows SAGE to achieve better convergence. On Llama models up to 1.3B parameters, our SAGE-based hybrid achieves new state-of-the-art perplexity, outperforming all baselines, including SinkGD hybrid, while significantly reducing optimizer state memory.

AI.tldr: SAGE is a memory-efficient optimizer that replaces AdamW in a hybrid training setup, combining Lion-style updates with an O(d) adaptive scale to stabilize high-variance embedding gradients, achieving state-of-the-art perplexity on Llama up to 1.3B while reducing optimizer state memory.
AI.motivation: Tackle the AdamW memory bottleneck during LLM pretraining, especially the large optimizer states (≈2x model size) and the embedding-layer gradient instability that light-state optimizers struggle with, by developing a stable, memory-efficient alternative suited for hybrid training structures.
AI.method: Introduce SAGE (Sign Adaptive GradiEnt). It uses a Lion-style update direction and a novel, memory-efficient adaptive scale with O(d) complexity acting as a bounded 'safe damper' (provably ≤1.0) to tame high-variance gradient components, replacing AdamW within a hybrid training design.
AI.result: On Llama models up to 1.3B parameters, the SAGE-based hybrid achieves new state-of-the-art perplexity, outperforming all baselines including SinkGD hybrids, while substantially reducing optimizer-state memory consumption.
AI.conclusion: SAGE provides superior stability and convergence in a memory-efficient hybrid optimization setting, addressing embedding-gradient variance and reducing memory overhead, with strong empirical performance on medium-scale LLMs.

title: EigentSearch-Q+: Enhancing Deep Research Agents with Structured Reasoning Tools
summary: Deep research requires reasoning over web evidence to answer open-ended questions, and it is a core capability for AI agents. Yet many deep research agents still rely on implicit, unstructured search behavior that causes redundant exploration and brittle evidence aggregation. Motivated by Anthropic's "think" tool paradigm and insights from the information-retrieval literature, we introduce Q+, a set of query and evidence processing tools that make web search more deliberate by guiding query planning, monitoring search progress, and extracting evidence from long web snapshots. We integrate Q+ into the browser sub-agent of Eigent, an open-source, production-ready multi-agent workforce for computer use, yielding EigentSearch-Q+. Across four benchmarks (SimpleQA-Verified, FRAMES, WebWalkerQA, and X-Bench DeepSearch), Q+ improves Eigent's browser agent benchmark-size-weighted average accuracy by 3.0, 3.8, and 0.6 percentage points (pp) for GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, and Minimax M2.5 model backends, respectively. Case studies further suggest that EigentSearch-Q+ produces more coherent tool-calling trajectories by making search progress and evidence handling explicit.

AI.tldr: Introduces Q+, a set of query and evidence processing tools to guide web search and evidence extraction, integrated into EigentSearch-Q+ within a browser sub-agent; yields higher benchmark accuracy across four tests for multiple backends; case studies show more coherent tool-calling trajectories.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency and brittleness of implicit, unstructured search in deep research agents. Inspired by Anthropic's think tool and IR literature to enable deliberate query planning, progress monitoring, and explicit evidence extraction from long web snapshots.
AI.method: Develop Q+ tools for query planning, search progress monitoring, and explicit evidence extraction; integrate them into Eigent's browser sub-agent to form EigentSearch-Q+; evaluate on four benchmarks (SimpleQA-Verified, FRAMES, WebWalkerQA, X-Bench DeepSearch) using GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, and Minimax M2.5 backends.
AI.result: Across four benchmarks, EigentSearch-Q+ improves the browser agent benchmark-size-weighted average accuracy by 3.0 pp (GPT-4.1), 3.8 pp (GPT-5.1), and 0.6 pp (Minimax M2.5). The integration yields more coherent tool-calling trajectories by making search progress and evidence handling explicit.
AI.conclusion: Q+ enables more deliberate search and explicit evidence handling, leading to clearer tool-calling behavior and improved end-to-end reasoning in web-based, open-source agent systems.

title: BLaDA: Bridging Language to Functional Dexterous Actions within 3DGS Fields
comment: Code will be publicly available at https://github.com/PopeyePxx/BLaDA
summary: In unstructured environments, functional dexterous grasping calls for the tight integration of semantic understanding, precise 3D functional localization, and physically interpretable execution. Modular hierarchical methods are more controllable and interpretable than end-to-end VLA approaches, but existing ones still rely on predefined affordance labels and lack the tight semantic--pose coupling needed for functional dexterous manipulation. To address this, we propose BLaDA (Bridging Language to Dexterous Actions in 3DGS fields), an interpretable zero-shot framework that grounds open-vocabulary instructions as perceptual and control constraints for functional dexterous manipulation. BLaDA establishes an interpretable reasoning chain by first parsing natural language into a structured sextuple of manipulation constraints via a Knowledge-guided Language Parsing (KLP) module. To achieve pose-consistent spatial reasoning, we introduce the Triangular Functional Point Localization (TriLocation) module, which utilizes 3D Gaussian Splatting as a continuous scene representation and identifies functional regions under triangular geometric constraints. Finally, the 3D Keypoint Grasp Matrix Transformation Execution (KGT3D+) module decodes these semantic-geometric constraints into physically plausible wrist poses and finger-level commands. Extensive experiments on complex benchmarks demonstrate that BLaDA significantly outperforms existing methods in both affordance grounding precision and the success rate of functional manipulation across diverse categories and tasks. Code will be publicly available at https://github.com/PopeyePxx/BLaDA.

AI.tldr: BLaDA is an interpretable zero-shot framework that grounds open-vocabulary instructions for functional dexterous manipulation by parsing language into constraints, localizing functional regions with TriLocation, and decoding into wrist/finger commands; shows superior grounding precision and success; code release.
AI.motivation: In unstructured environments, functional dexterous grasping requires tight semantic understanding, precise 3D functional localization, and interpretable execution. Existing modular approaches either rely on predefined affordances or lack tight semantic–pose coupling, limiting dexterous manipulation.
AI.method: A Knowledge-guided Language Parsing (KLP) module converts natural language into a structured sextuple of manipulation constraints. Triangular Functional Point Localization (TriLocation) uses 3D Gaussian Splatting for continuous scene representation to identify functional regions under triangular geometric constraints. The 3D Keypoint Grasp Matrix Transformation Execution (KGT3D+) decodes these semantic–geometric constraints into wrist poses and finger-level commands in a physically plausible manner. The framework operates in zero-shot, grounding open-vocabulary instructions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on complex benchmarks show that BLaDA significantly improves affordance grounding precision and the success rate of functional manipulation across diverse categories and tasks.
AI.conclusion: BLaDA provides an interpretable reasoning chain bridging language to dexterous actions in 3DGS fields, enabling pose-consistent spatial reasoning and physically plausible manipulation; code will be publicly available.

title: An Imperfect Verifier is Good Enough: Learning with Noisy Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a prominent method for post-training Large Language Models (LLMs).
