However, verifiers are rarely error-free; even deterministic checks can be inaccurate, and the growing dependence on model-based judges exacerbates the issue. The extent to which RLVR is robust to such noise and the verifier accuracy required for effective training remain unresolved questions. We investigate these questions in the domains of code generation and scientific reasoning by introducing noise into RL training. Noise rates up to 15% yield peak validation accuracy within 2 percentage points of the clean baseline. These findings are consistent across controlled and model-based noise types, three model families (Qwen3, GLM4, Llama 3.1), and model sizes from 4B to 9B. Overall, the results indicate that imperfect verification does not constitute a fundamental barrier to RLVR. Furthermore, our findings suggest that practitioners should prioritize moderate accuracy with high precision over perfect verification.

AI.tldr: RLVR remains robust to noisy verifiers; up to 15% noise keeps peak validation accuracy within 2 points of the clean baseline, across models/domains/sizes. This suggests imperfect verification is not a fundamental barrier and moderate accuracy with high precision is preferable.
AI.motivation: Assess the robustness of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for post-training LLMs when verifiers are noisy or imperfect, particularly in code generation and scientific reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce both controlled and model-based noise into the verification signals during RLVR training across three model families (Qwen3, GLM4, Llama 3.1) and sizes from 4B to 9B; evaluate on code generation and scientific reasoning tasks; compare noisy RLVR to a clean-baseline RLVR.
AI.result: Noise up to 15% yields peak validation accuracy within 2 percentage points of the clean baseline; this holds across different noise types and model families; imperfect verification does not pose a fundamental barrier to RLVR.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners can tolerate imperfect verification and should prioritize moderate accuracy with high precision over aiming for perfect verification, as RLVR remains effective under noisy verification.

title: Mitigating Entangled Steering in Large Vision-Language Models for Hallucination Reduction
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable success across cross-modal tasks but remain hindered by hallucinations, producing textual outputs inconsistent with visual content. Existing methods mitigate hallucinations but often alter generation behavior, resulting in shorter outputs and shifted token distributions, especially in latent space steering approaches. We identify that this issue stems from entangled steering signals, where suppressing hallucinations inadvertently disrupts the model's intrinsic generation behavior. To address this, we propose MESA, an effective plug-and-play framework that performs controlled and selective latent intervention for hallucination mitigation. Specifically, MESA targets hallucination-relevant responses while preserving the model's original token distribution, enabling effective hallucination reduction without compromising generation behavior. Extensive experiments across diverse generative and discriminative benchmarks demonstrate that MESA consistently reduces hallucinations while better preserving generation behavior, outperforming prior methods across multiple LVLM families.

AI.tldr: MESA is a plug-and-play framework for selective latent intervention that reduces hallucinations in large vision-language models while preserving the original token distribution and generation behavior.
AI.motivation: LVLMs frequently hallucinate, and existing mitigation methods often alter generation behavior (shorter outputs, shifted token distributions), especially when using latent-space steering. The observed entanglement between hallucination suppression and intrinsic generation signals motivates a decoupled, controlled approach.
AI.method: MESA performs controlled, selective latent interventions aimed at hallucination-relevant responses. It suppresses hallucinations while preserving the model’s original token distribution, effectively decoupling hallucination mitigation from generation behavior in a plug-and-play fashion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse generative and discriminative benchmarks show that MESA consistently reduces hallucinations and better preserves generation behavior, outperforming prior methods across multiple LVLM families.
AI.conclusion: MESA provides an effective, plug-and-play solution for hallucination mitigation in LVLMs that maintains generation behavior and token distribution, offering improved reliability across LVLMs.

title: MONETA: Multimodal Industry Classification through Geographic Information with Multi Agent Systems
summary: Industry classification schemes are integral parts of public and corporate databases as they classify businesses based on economic activity. Due to the size of the company registers, manual annotation is costly, and fine-tuning models with every update in industry classification schemes requires significant data collection. We replicate the manual expert verification by using existing or easily retrievable multimodal resources for industry classification. We present MONETA, the first multimodal industry classification benchmark with text (Website, Wikipedia, Wikidata) and geospatial sources (OpenStreetMap and satellite imagery). Our dataset enlists 1,000 businesses in Europe with 20 economic activity labels according to EU guidelines (NACE). Our training-free baseline reaches 62.10% and 74.10% with open and closed-source Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM). We observe an increase of up to 22.80% with the combination of multi-turn design, context enrichment, and classification explanations. We will release our dataset and the enhanced guidelines.

AI.tldr: MONETA introduces a multimodal benchmark for EU NACE industry classification using text and geospatial data. It consists of 1,000 European businesses across 20 activity labels, with training-free baselines reaching ~62% (open MLLMs) and ~74% (closed MLLMs); enhancements via multi-turn prompts, context enrichment, and explanations yield up to ~22.8% gains. The dataset and guidelines will be released.
AI.motivation: Address the high cost of manual expert verification in industry classification by leveraging existing multimodal resources to enable scalable, data-efficient labeling aligned with NACE.
AI.method: Construct MONETA by aggregating text sources (Website, Wikipedia, Wikidata) and geospatial sources (OpenStreetMap, satellite imagery) for 1,000 European businesses annotated with 20 NACE labels. Evaluate training-free baselines using open and closed-source Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Experiment with multi-turn design, context enrichment, and classification explanations to improve performance.
AI.result: Baseline performances: 62.10% (open-source MLLMs) and 74.10% (closed-source MLLMs). Contextual enhancements and explanations yield up to 22.80% improvement. Dataset and enhanced guidelines will be released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of training-free multimodal industry classification and provides a resource (MONETA) and guidelines to support scalable annotation; promising direction for reducing manual annotation costs in EU NACE classification.

title: CrashSight: A Phase-Aware, Infrastructure-Centric Video Benchmark for Traffic Crash Scene Understanding and Reasoning
summary: Cooperative autonomous driving requires traffic scene understanding from both vehicle and infrastructure perspectives. While vision-language models (VLMs) show strong general reasoning capabilities, their performance in safety-critical traffic scenarios remains insufficiently evaluated due to the ego-vehicle focus of existing benchmarks. To bridge this gap, we present \textbf{CrashSight}, a large-scale vision-language benchmark for roadway crash understanding using real-world roadside camera data. The dataset comprises 250 crash videos, annotated with 13K multiple-choice question-answer pairs organized under a two-tier taxonomy. Tier 1 evaluates the visual grounding of scene context and involved parties, while Tier 2 probes higher-level reasoning, including crash mechanics, causal attribution, temporal progression, and post-crash outcomes. We benchmark 8 state-of-the-art VLMs and show that, despite strong scene description capabilities, current models struggle with temporal and causal reasoning in safety-critical scenarios. We provide a detailed analysis of failure scenarios and discuss directions for improving VLM crash understanding. The benchmark provides a standardized evaluation framework for infrastructure-assisted perception in cooperative autonomous driving. The CrashSight benchmark, including the full dataset and code, is accessible at https://mcgrche.github.io/crashsight.

AI.tldr: A large-scale vision-language benchmark (CrashSight) for roadway crash understanding using roadside video data, assessing VLMs on visual grounding and temporal-causal reasoning in safety-critical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of infrastructure-perspective evaluation in cooperative autonomous driving; ego-vehicle benchmarks under-evaluate temporal, causal reasoning and crash dynamics in safety-critical contexts.
AI.method: Construct CrashSight with 250 real-world crash videos annotated with 13k multiple-choice QA pairs organized into a two-tier taxonomy: Tier 1 for visual grounding of scene context and involved parties; Tier 2 for higher-level reasoning on crash mechanics, causal attribution, temporal progression, and post-crash outcomes. Benchmark 8 state-of-the-art VLMs, analyze failures, and provide directions. Dataset and code are released.
AI.result: VLMs show strong scene description but limited temporal and causal reasoning in crash scenarios; comprehensive failure analysis and guidance; establishes a standardized evaluation framework for infrastructure-assisted perception in cooperative driving.
AI.conclusion: CrashSight enables infrastructure-aware crash understanding in cooperative autonomous driving and provides publicly available data and tooling to benchmark and improve VLM-based crash understanding.

title: Tarot-SAM3: Training-free SAM3 for Any Referring Expression Segmentation
summary: Referring Expression Segmentation (RES) aims to segment image regions described by natural-language expressions, serving as a bridge between vision and language understanding. Existing RES methods, however, rely heavily on large annotated datasets and are limited to either explicit or implicit expressions, hindering their ability to generalize to any referring expression. Recently, the Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) has shown impressive robustness in Promptable Concept Segmentation. Nonetheless, applying it to RES remains challenging: (1) SAM3 struggles with longer or implicit expressions; (2) naive coupling of SAM3 with a multimodal large language model (MLLM) makes the final results overly dependent on the MLLM's reasoning capability, without enabling refinement of SAM3's segmentation outputs. To this end, we present Tarot-SAM3, a novel training-free framework that can accurately segment from any referring expression. Specifically, Tarot-SAM3 consists of two key phases. First, the Expression Reasoning Interpreter (ERI) phase introduces reasoning-assisted prompt options to support structured expression parsing and evaluation-aware rephrasing. This transforms arbitrary queries into robust heterogeneous prompts for generating reliable masks with SAM3. Second, the Mask Self-Refining (MSR) phase selects the best mask across prompt types and performs self-refinement by leveraging rich feature relationships from DINOv3 to compare discriminative regions among ERI outputs. It then infers region affiliation to the target, thereby correcting over- and under-segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Tarot-SAM3 achieves strong performance on both explicit and implicit RES benchmarks, as well as open-world scenarios. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of each phase.

AI.tldr: Tarot-SAM3 enables training-free segmentation from any referring expression by (1) Reasoning-Assisted Prompting (ERI) to craft robust prompts for SAM3 and (2) Mask Self-Refining (MSR) to select/refine masks using DINOv3 features, achieving strong results on explicit/implicit RES and open-world data.
AI.motivation: To generalize Referring Expression Segmentation beyond annotated data and across explicit/implicit expressions, addressing SAM3's difficulty with long/implicit queries and over-reliance on MLLMs.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: 1) Expression Reasoning Interpreter (ERI) generates reasoning-informed, heterogeneous prompts to convert arbitrary queries into robust prompts for SAM3; 2) Mask Self-Refining (MSR) selects the best mask across prompt types and refines it by exploiting rich feature relations from DINOv3 to compare discriminative regions and infer target region, correcting over- and under-segmentation.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate strong performance on explicit and implicit RES benchmarks and open-world scenarios. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each phase and the training-free framework.
AI.conclusion: Tarot-SAM3 offers a training-free solution to RES from any expression, mitigating SAM3's limitations with long/implicit queries and reducing reliance on MLLMs, with demonstrated effectiveness across standard and open-world benchmarks.

title: Visually-grounded Humanoid Agents
summary: Digital human generation has been studied for decades and supports a wide range of real-world applications. However, most existing systems are passively animated, relying on privileged state or scripted control, which limits scalability to novel environments. We instead ask: how can digital humans actively behave using only visual observations and specified goals in novel scenes? Achieving this would enable populating any 3D environments with digital humans at scale that exhibit spontaneous, natural, goal-directed behaviors. To this end, we introduce Visually-grounded Humanoid Agents, a coupled two-layer (world-agent) paradigm that replicates humans at multiple levels: they look, perceive, reason, and behave like real people in real-world 3D scenes. The World Layer reconstructs semantically rich 3D Gaussian scenes from real-world videos via an occlusion-aware pipeline and accommodates animatable Gaussian-based human avatars. The Agent Layer transforms these avatars into autonomous humanoid agents, equipping them with first-person RGB-D perception and enabling them to perform accurate, embodied planning with spatial awareness and iterative reasoning, which is then executed at the low level as full-body actions to drive their behaviors in the scene. We further introduce a benchmark to evaluate humanoid-scene interaction in diverse reconstructed environments. Experiments show our agents achieve robust autonomous behavior, yielding higher task success rates and fewer collisions than ablations and state-of-the-art planning methods. This work enables active digital human population and advances human-centric embodied AI. Data, code, and models will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: Proposes Visually-grounded Humanoid Agents with a two-layer world-agent system to enable autonomous, goal-directed humanoid behavior in novel 3D scenes via visual observations, RGB-D perception, and Gaussian-based avatars; introduces a benchmark and demonstrates improved task success and safety; opensource.
AI.motivation: Existing digital humans are largely passively animated and rely on privileged state or scripts, hindering scalability to new environments. There is a need for active, controllable digital humans that can operate in arbitrary 3D scenes.
AI.method: A coupled two-layer paradigm: (1) World Layer reconstructs semantically rich 3D Gaussian scenes from real-world videos with an occlusion-aware pipeline and supports animatable Gaussian avatars; (2) Agent Layer turns these avatars into autonomous humanoid agents with first-person RGB-D perception, embodied planning with spatial awareness and iterative reasoning, executing full-body actions at the low level.
AI.result: Agents exhibit robust autonomous behavior, achieving higher task success rates and fewer collisions compared to ablations and state-of-the-art planning methods in diverse reconstructed environments.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables active digital human population and advances human-centric embodied AI; data, code, and models will be open-sourced to facilitate further research.

title: Tensor-based computation of the Koopman generator via operator logarithm
summary: Identifying governing equations of nonlinear dynamical systems from data is challenging. While sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) and its extensions are widely used for system identification, operator-logarithm approaches use the logarithm to avoid time differentiation, enabling larger sampling intervals. However, they still suffer from the curse of dimensionality. Then, we propose a data-driven method to compute the Koopman generator in a low-rank tensor train (TT) format by taking logarithms of Koopman eigenvalues while preserving the TT format. Experiments on 4-dimensional Lotka-Volterra and 10-dimensional Lorenz-96 systems show accurate recovery of vector field coefficients and scalability to higher-dimensional systems.

AI.tldr: A data-driven method computes the Koopman generator in a low-rank tensor-train (TT) format by applying logarithms to Koopman eigenvalues, enabling scalable identification of nonlinear dynamical systems without heavy time differentiation; validated on 4D Lotka–Volterra and 10D Lorenz-96.
AI.motivation: Identifying governing equations from data is challenging for high-dimensional nonlinear systems. Sparse methods like SINDy require derivative evaluations and can struggle with dimensionality. Operator-logarithm approaches avoid explicit time differentiation but still face the curse of dimensionality. A TT representation promises scalable, structured handling of high-dimensional operators.
AI.method: Compute the Koopman generator in a TT format by taking logarithms of Koopman eigenvalues, thereby preserving the TT structure while enabling low-rank representation. Use data-driven spectral information to infer the vector-field coefficients within the TT framework, avoiding explicit high-dimensional regression.
AI.result: The approach yields accurate recovery of vector-field coefficients for 4D Lotka–Volterra and 10D Lorenz-96 systems and demonstrates scalability to higher dimensions thanks to the TT representation and logarithmic operator treatment.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, data-driven framework for nonlinear dynamical system identification that leverages a logarithmic Koopman generator in a tensor-train representation, balancing accuracy and computational efficiency in moderately high-dimensional settings.

title: Stitch4D: Sparse Multi-Location 4D Urban Reconstruction via Spatio-Temporal Interpolation
summary: Dynamic urban environments are often captured by cameras placed at spatially separated locations with little or no view overlap. However, most existing 4D reconstruction methods assume densely overlapping views. When applied to such sparse observations, these methods fail to reconstruct intermediate regions and often introduce temporal artifacts. To address this practical yet underexplored sparse multi-location setting, we propose Stitch4D, a unified 4D reconstruction framework that explicitly compensates for missing spatial coverage in sparse observations. Stitch4D (i) synthesizes intermediate bridge views to densify spatial constraints and improve spatial coverage, and (ii) jointly optimizes real and synthesized observations within a unified coordinate frame under explicit inter-location consistency constraints. By restoring intermediate coverage before optimization, Stitch4D prevents geometric collapse and reconstructs coherent geometry and smooth scene dynamics even in sparsely observed environments. To evaluate this setting, we introduce Urban Sparse 4D (U-S4D), a CARLA-based benchmark designed to assess spatiotemporal alignment under sparse multi-location configurations. Experimental results on U-S4D show that Stitch4D surpasses representative 4D reconstruction baselines and achieves superior visual quality. These results indicate that recovering intermediate spatial coverage is essential for stable 4D reconstruction in sparse urban environments.

AI.tldr: Stitch4D densifies sparse multi-location 4D data by synthesizing bridge views and jointly optimizing real and synthetic observations in a unified frame, introducing U-S4D benchmark; it achieves better visual quality than baselines in sparse urban scenes.
AI.motivation: Sparse, non-overlapping camera setups break existing dense-coverage 4D methods, causing intermediate regions to be unreconstructed and introducing temporal artifacts. A framework that explicitly compensates missing spatial coverage is needed for stable 4D reconstruction in sparse multi-location settings.
AI.method: Two-part approach: (i) synthesize intermediate bridge views to densify spatial constraints; (ii) jointly optimize real and synthesized observations within a unified coordinate frame under explicit inter-location consistency constraints, enabling restoration of intermediate coverage prior to optimization.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on the Urban Sparse 4D (U-S4D) CARLA-based benchmark shows Stitch4D outperforms representative 4D reconstruction baselines and yields superior visual quality.
AI.conclusion: Restoring intermediate spatial coverage is essential for stable 4D reconstruction in sparse urban environments; Stitch4D provides a practical solution for coherent geometry and smooth scene dynamics under sparse observations.

title: Are we still able to recognize pearls? Machine-driven peer review and the risk to creativity: An explainable RAG-XAI detection framework with markers extraction
summary: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into peer review raises a concern beyond authorship and detection: the potential cascading automation of the entire editorial process. As reviews become partially or fully machine-generated, it becomes plausible that editorial decisions may also be delegated to algorithmic systems, leading to a fully automated evaluation pipeline. They risk reshaping the criteria by which scientific work is assessed. This paper argues that machine-driven assessment may systematically favor standardized, pattern-conforming research while penalizing unconventional and paradigm-shifting ideas that require contextual human judgment. We consider that this shift could lead to epistemic homogenization, where researchers are implicitly incentivized to optimize their work for algorithmic approval rather than genuine discovery. To address this risk, we introduce an explainable framework (RAG-XAI) for assessing review quality and detecting automated patterns using markers LLM extractor, aiming to preserve transparency, accountability and creativity in science. The proposed framework achieves near-perfect detection performance, with XGBoost, Random Forest and LightGBM reaching 99.61% accuracy, AUC-ROC above 0.999 and F1-scores of 0.9925 on the test set, while maintaining extremely low false positive rates (<0.23%) and false negative rates (~0.8%). In contrast, the logistic regression baseline performs substantially worse (89.97% accuracy, F1-score 0.8314). Feature importance and SHAP analyses identify absence of personal signals and repetition patterns as the dominant predictors. Additionally, the RAG component achieves 90.5% top-1 retrieval accuracy, with strong same-class clustering in the embedding space, further supporting the reliability of the framework's outputs.

AI.tldr: Explainable RAG-XAI framework to detect machine-generated reviews and preserve human-centric scientific assessment, showing near-perfect detection and robust retrieval.
AI.motivation: Rising use of large language models in peer review risks cascading automation of the editorial process and epistemic homogenization; need for transparency, accountability, and preservation of genuine discovery.
AI.method: Proposes RAG-XAI framework with an LLM extractor for signals, and deploys ML classifiers (XGBoost, Random Forest, LightGBM) to detect automated patterns in reviews; includes SHAP/feature-importance analyses; RAG component for retrieval with 90.5% top-1 accuracy and embedding-space clustering to support reliability.
AI.result: Classification accuracy ~99.61% across XGBoost, RF, and LightGBM; AUC-ROC > 0.999; F1 ~0.9925; FP rate <0.23%; FN rate ~0.8%; logistic regression baseline~90% accuracy; SHAP indicates key predictors are absence of personal signals and repetition patterns; RAG retrieval accuracy 90.5%; strong same-class clustering.
AI.conclusion: The framework aims to preserve transparency, accountability, and creativity in science by enabling explainable detection of automated review patterns; results indicate highly effective discrimination between human and machine-generated reviews and reliable retrieval, suggesting a viable path to mitigates risks of editorial automation.

title: Joint Task Offloading, Inference Optimization and UAV Trajectory Planning for Generative AI Empowered Intelligent Transportation Digital Twin
summary: To implement the intelligent transportation digital twin (ITDT), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are scheduled to process the sensing data from the roadside sensors. At this time, generative artificial intelligence (GAI) technologies such as diffusion models are deployed on the UAVs to transform the raw sensing data into the high-quality and valuable. Therefore, we propose the GAI-empowered ITDT. The dynamic processing of a set of diffusion model inference (DMI) tasks on the UAVs with dynamic mobility simultaneously influences the DT updating fidelity and delay. In this paper, we investigate a joint optimization problem of DMI task offloading, inference optimization and UAV trajectory planning as the system utility maximization (SUM) problem to address the fidelity-delay tradeoff for the GAI-empowered ITDT. To seek a solution to the problem under the network dynamics, we model the SUM problem as the heterogeneous-agent Markov decision process, and propose the sequential update-based heterogeneous-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (SU-HATD3) algorithm, which can quickly learn a near-optimal solution. Numerical results demonstrate that compared with several baseline algorithms, the proposed algorithm has great advantages in improving the system utility and convergence rate.

AI.tldr: Proposes a diffusion-model-based inference framework on UAVs for intelligent transportation digital twins (ITDT), jointly optimizing diffusion-model inference offloading, inference configuration, and UAV trajectory to balance fidelity and delay, using a heterogeneous-agent MDP and the SU-HATD3 algorithm to learn near-optimal policies and improve convergence over baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the fidelity-delay tradeoff in ITDT under dynamic network conditions and UAV mobility by enabling on-board generative AI (diffusion models) processing of sensed data to reduce backhaul transmission and latency.
AI.method: Formulate the joint optimization as a heterogeneous-agent Markov decision process (MDP) for DMI task offloading, inference optimization, and UAV trajectory planning. Propose the sequential update-based heterogeneous-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (SU-HATD3) algorithm to learn near-optimal policies that maximize the system utility.
AI.result: Numerical results show the proposed SU-HATD3-based solution achieves higher system utility and faster convergence compared with several baseline algorithms in the dynamic ITDT setting.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating GAI-powered on-board inference with UAV-enabled ITDT, providing a scalable learning-based approach to jointly optimize offloading, inference configuration, and mobility to enhance fidelity-delay tradeoffs.

title: Generative 3D Gaussian Splatting for Arbitrary-ResolutionAtmospheric Downscaling and Forecasting
summary: While AI-based numerical weather prediction (NWP) enables rapid forecasting, generating high-resolution outputs remains computationally demanding due to limited multi-scale adaptability and inefficient data representations. We propose the 3D Gaussian splatting-based scale-aware vision transformer (GSSA-ViT), a novel framework for arbitrary-resolution forecasting and flexible downscaling of high-dimensional atmospheric fields. Specifically, latitude-longitude grid points are treated as centers of 3D Gaussians. A generative 3D Gaussian prediction scheme is introduced to estimate key parameters, including covariance, attributes, and opacity, for unseen samples, improving generalization and mitigating overfitting. In addition, a scale-aware attention module is designed to capture cross-scale dependencies, enabling the model to effectively integrate information across varying downscaling ratios and support continuous resolution adaptation. To our knowledge, this is the first NWP approach that combines generative 3D Gaussian modeling with scale-aware attention for unified multi-scale prediction. Experiments on ERA5 show that the proposed method accurately forecasts 87 atmospheric variables at arbitrary resolutions, while evaluations on ERA5 and CMIP6 demonstrate its superior performance in downscaling tasks. The proposed framework provides an efficient and scalable solution for high-resolution, multi-scale atmospheric prediction and downscaling. Code is available at: https://github.com/binbin2xs/weather-GS.

AI.tldr: A 3D Gaussian splatting-based scale-aware ViT (GSSA-ViT) enables arbitrary-resolution forecasting and cross-scale downscaling for atmospheric fields by treating grid points as 3D Gaussian centers, using a generative 3D Gaussian predictor and scale-aware attention for unified multi-scale prediction; validated on ERA5/CMIP6 with 87 variables and downscaling performance; code released.
AI.motivation: High-resolution NWP is computationally expensive due to limited multi-scale adaptability and inefficient representations. There is a need for a framework that can forecast at arbitrary resolutions and perform flexible downscaling while maintaining cross-scale consistency.
AI.method: Represent latitude-longitude grid points as centers of 3D Gaussians. Develop a generative 3D Gaussian predictor to estimate covariance, attributes, and opacity for unseen samples. Introduce a scale-aware attention module to capture cross-scale dependencies and enable continuous resolution adaptation, enabling unified multi-scale prediction.
AI.result: On ERA5, the method accurately forecasts 87 atmospheric variables at arbitrary resolutions. Evaluations on ERA5 and CMIP6 indicate superior downscaling performance compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: GSSA-ViT provides an efficient, scalable solution for high-resolution, multi-scale atmospheric prediction and downscaling, and is the first to combine generative 3D Gaussian modeling with scale-aware attention for unified multi-scale NWP. Code available at the provided repository.

title: How Far Are Large Multimodal Models from Human-Level Spatial Action? A Benchmark for Goal-Oriented Embodied Navigation in Urban Airspace
summary: Large multimodal models (LMMs) show strong visual-linguistic reasoning but their capacity for spatial decision-making and action remains unclear. In this work, we investigate whether LMMs can achieve embodied spatial action like human through a challenging scenario: goal-oriented navigation in urban 3D spaces. We first spend over 500 hours constructing a dataset comprising 5,037 high-quality goal-oriented navigation samples, with an emphasis on 3D vertical actions and rich urban semantic information. Then, we comprehensively assess 17 representative models, including non-reasoning LMMs, reasoning LMMs, agent-based methods, and vision-language-action models. Experiments show that current LMMs exhibit emerging action capabilities, yet remain far from human-level performance. Furthermore, we reveal an intriguing phenomenon: navigation errors do not accumulate linearly but instead diverge rapidly from the destination after a critical decision bifurcation. The limitations of LMMs are investigated by analyzing their behavior at these critical decision bifurcations. Finally, we experimentally explore four promising directions for improvement: geometric perception, cross-view understanding, spatial imagination, and long-term memory. The project is available at: https://github.com/serenditipy-AC/Embodied-Navigation-Bench.

AI.tldr: LMMs show nascent embodied navigation ability but lag behind humans; errors diverge after a bifurcation; a 5,037-sample dataset and benchmark are introduced; four improvement directions identified.
AI.motivation: Assess whether large multimodal models can perform embodied spatial action in urban 3D navigation, characterize failure modes, and propose directions for improvement.
AI.method: Constructed a dataset of 5,037 goal-oriented navigation samples (over 500 hours) emphasizing 3D vertical actions and rich urban semantics; evaluated 17 models spanning non-reasoning, reasoning, agent-based, and vision-language-action categories; analyzed error patterns, especially at critical decision bifurcations; investigated four improvement directions.
AI.result: LMMs exhibit emerging action capabilities but are far from human-level performance; navigation errors diverge rapidly after a critical decision bifurcation; identified key limitations and proposed four avenues for improvement; project code/data available via GitHub.
AI.conclusion: LMMs have potential for embodied navigation but require substantial advances in geometric perception, cross-view understanding, spatial imagination, and long-term memory to approach human-level spatial decision-making.

title: Tree-of-Evidence: Efficient "System 2" Search for Faithful Multimodal Grounding
summary: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) achieve state-of-the-art performance in high-stakes domains like healthcare, yet their reasoning remains opaque. Current interpretability methods, such as attention mechanisms or post-hoc saliency, often fail to faithfully represent the model's decision-making process, particularly when integrating heterogeneous modalities like time-series and text. We introduce Tree-of-Evidence (ToE), an inference-time search algorithm that frames interpretability as a discrete optimization problem. Rather than relying on soft attention weights, ToE employs lightweight Evidence Bottlenecks that score coarse groups or units of data (e.g., vital-sign windows, report sentences) and performs a beam search to identify the compact evidence set required to reproduce the model's prediction. We evaluate ToE across six tasks spanning three datasets and two domains: four clinical prediction tasks on MIMIC-IV, cross-center validation on eICU, and non-clinical fault detection on LEMMA-RCA. ToE produces auditable evidence traces while maintaining predictive performance, retaining over 0.98 of full-model AUROC with as few as five evidence units across all settings. Under sparse evidence budgets, ToE achieves higher decision agreement and lower probability fidelity error than other approaches. Qualitative analyses show that ToE adapts its search strategy: it often resolves straightforward cases using only vitals, while selectively incorporating text when physiological signals are ambiguous. ToE therefore provides a practical mechanism for auditing multimodal models by revealing which discrete evidence units support each prediction.

AI.tldr: Tree-of-Evidence (ToE) is an inference-time, discrete optimization interpretability method for large multimodal models that identifies a small set of evidence units via beam search over Evidence Bottlenecks to reproduce predictions with high fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability methods struggle to faithfully reflect decision-making in LMMs with mixed modalities; need auditable, data-driven evidence traces that are robust at inference time.
AI.method: Score coarse groups of data (e.g., vital-sign windows, report sentences) with lightweight Evidence Bottlenecks; perform beam search to select a compact evidence set that preserves model output; evaluate across six tasks on MIMIC-IV, eICU, LEMMA-RCA; compare with other methods.
AI.result: Maintains >0.98 of full-model AUROC with as few as five evidence units; better decision agreement and lower fidelity error under sparse budgets; qualitative analysis shows adaptive strategy: uses vitals for straightforward cases, adds text when ambiguous.
AI.conclusion: ToE provides auditable evidence traces and a practical auditing mechanism for multimodal models, balancing interpretability and predictive performance.

title: Shortcut Learning in Glomerular AI: Adversarial Penalties Hurt, Entropy Helps
comment: Accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026. Hien Nguyen and Chandra Mohan jointly supervised this work
summary: Stain variability is a pervasive source of distribution shift and potential shortcut learning in renal pathology AI. We ask whether lupus nephritis glomerular lesion classifiers exploit stain as a shortcut, and how to mitigate such bias without stain or site labels. We curate a multi-center, multi-stain dataset of 9{,}674 glomerular patches (224$\times$224) from 365 WSIs across three centers and four stains (PAS, H\&E, Jones, Trichrome), labeled as proliferative vs.\ non-proliferative. We evaluate Bayesian CNN and ViT backbones with Monte Carlo dropout in three settings: (1) stain-only classification; (2) a dual-head model jointly predicting lesion and stain with supervised stain loss; and (3) a dual-head model with label-free stain regularization via entropy maximization on the stain head. In (1), stain identity is trivially learnable, confirming a strong candidate shortcut. In (2), varying the strength and sign of stain supervision strongly modulates stain performance but leaves lesion metrics essentially unchanged, indicating no measurable stain-driven shortcut learning on this multi-stain, multi-center dataset, while overly adversarial stain penalties inflate predictive uncertainty. In (3), entropy-based regularization holds stain predictions near chance without degrading lesion accuracy or calibration. Overall, a carefully curated multi-stain dataset can be inherently robust to stain shortcuts, and a Bayesian dual-head architecture with label-free entropy regularization offers a simple, deployment-friendly safeguard against potential stain-related drift in glomerular AI.

AI.tldr: Stain variation can act as a shortcut in lupus nephritis glomerular classifiers, but a Bayesian dual-head with entropy-based stain regularization on a multi-stain dataset can mitigate drift while preserving lesion performance.
AI.motivation: Assess whether stain variability enables shortcut learning in renal pathology AI and evaluate mitigation strategies without relying on stain or site labels; use a large, multi-center, multi-stain dataset to test robustness.
AI.method: Bayesian CNN and ViT backbones with Monte Carlo dropout evaluated on 9,674 glomerular patches (224x224) from 365 WSIs across 3 centers and 4 stains (PAS, H&E, Jones, Trichrome) for proliferative vs non-proliferative classification. Three settings: (1) stain-only classification; (2) dual-head with supervised stain loss; (3) dual-head with label-free stain regularization via entropy maximization on stain head.
AI.result: (1) Stain identity is learnable, indicating a strong shortcut candidate. (2) Stain supervision strength/sign modulates stain performance but leaves lesion metrics unchanged; no measurable stain-driven shortcut on this dataset; overly adversarial penalties inflate predictive uncertainty. (3) Entropy regularization keeps stain predictions near chance without degrading lesion accuracy or calibration.
AI.conclusion: A carefully curated multi-stain dataset can be inherently robust to stain shortcuts; a Bayesian dual-head architecture with label-free entropy regularization provides a simple, deployment-friendly safeguard against potential stain-related drift in glomerular AI.

title: PASK: Toward Intent-Aware Proactive Agents with Long-Term Memory
summary: Proactivity is a core expectation for AGI. Prior work remains largely confined to laboratory settings, leaving a clear gap in real-world proactive agent: depth, complexity, ambiguity, precision and real-time constraints. We study this setting, where useful intervention requires inferring latent needs from ongoing context and grounding actions in evolving user memory under latency and long-horizon constraints. We first propose DD-MM-PAS (Demand Detection, Memory Modeling, Proactive Agent System) as a general paradigm for streaming proactive AI agent. We instantiate this paradigm in Pask, with streaming IntentFlow model for DD, a hybrid memory (workspace, user, global) for long-term MM, PAS infra framework and introduce how these components form a closed loop. We also introduce LatentNeeds-Bench, a real-world benchmark built from user-consented data and refined through thousands of rounds of human editing. Experiments show that IntentFlow matches leading Gemini3-Flash models under latency constraints, while identifying deeper user intent.

AI.tldr: Proposes a streaming proactive AI agent paradigm (DD-MM-PAS) instantiated as Pask with IntentFlow and LatentNeeds-Bench; demonstrates real-time proactive interventions under latency and long-horizon constraints, achieving competitive performance and deeper intent detection.
AI.motivation: Real-world proactive agents must operate under latency, ambiguity, and long-horizon constraints, inferring latent user needs from ongoing context and grounding actions in evolving user memory—a gap not fully addressed by lab studies.
AI.method: Introduce DD-MM-PAS (Demand Detection, Memory Modeling, Proactive Agent System) as a general paradigm for streaming proactive AI. Implement in Pask with streaming IntentFlow for Demand Detection, a hybrid memory (workspace, user, global) for long-term memory modeling, and a PAS infrastructure. Build LatentNeeds-Bench from real user data with extensive human refinement. Establish a closed-loop system linking perception, memory update, and action.
AI.result: Experiments show IntentFlow matches leading Gemini3-Flash models under latency constraints while uncovering deeper user intent, indicating effective real-time perception-grounded proactive intervention.
AI.conclusion: A generalizable streaming proactive AI agent paradigm paired with a real-world benchmark demonstrates effective latent-need inference and real-time proactive capability, suggesting practical viability for deploying proactive agents in real-world settings.

title: CausalVAE as a Plug-in for World Models: Towards Reliable Counterfactual Dynamics
summary: In this work, CausalVAE is introduced as a plug-in structural module for latent world models and is attached to diverse encoder-transition backbones. Across the reported benchmarks, competitive factual prediction is preserved and intervention-aware counterfactual retrieval is improved after the plug-in is added, suggesting stronger robustness under distribution shift and interventions. The largest gains are observed on the Physics benchmark: when averaged over 8 paired baselines, CF-H@1 is improved by +102.5%. In a representative GNN-NLL setting on Physics, CF-H@1 is increased from 11.0 to 41.0 (+272.7%). Through causal analysis, learned structural dependencies are shown to recover meaningful first-order physical interaction trends, supporting the interpretability of the learned latent causal structure.

AI.tldr: Plug-in CausalVAE enhances latent world models with causal structure, improving counterfactual retrieval and robustness; large gains on Physics benchmark; interpretability via recovered physical interactions.
AI.motivation: Need to bolster robustness and intervention-aware counterfactual reasoning in latent world models under distribution shift by integrating causal structure.
AI.method: Introduce CausalVAE as a plug-in structural module attached to varied encoder-transition backbones; evaluate on multiple benchmarks including Physics; analyze learned causal dependencies.
AI.result: Maintains competitive factual prediction; improves intervention-aware counterfactual retrieval; Physics shows largest gains (CF-H@1 +102.5% over 8 baselines; GNN-NLL CF-H@1 11.0→41.0, +272.7%).
AI.conclusion: Structural latent dependencies align with first-order physical interactions, offering interpretable and robust latent causal structure.

title: ImVideoEdit: Image-learning Video Editing via 2D Spatial Difference Attention Blocks
summary: Current video editing models often rely on expensive paired video data, which limits their practical scalability. In essence, most video editing tasks can be formulated as a decoupled spatiotemporal process, where the temporal dynamics of the pretrained model are preserved while spatial content is selectively and precisely modified. Based on this insight, we propose ImVideoEdit, an efficient framework that learns video editing capabilities entirely from image pairs. By freezing the pre-trained 3D attention modules and treating images as single-frame videos, we decouple the 2D spatial learning process to help preserve the original temporal dynamics. The core of our approach is a Predict-Update Spatial Difference Attention module that progressively extracts and injects spatial differences. Rather than relying on rigid external masks, we incorporate a Text-Guided Dynamic Semantic Gating mechanism for adaptive and implicit text-driven modifications. Despite training on only 13K image pairs for 5 epochs with exceptionally low computational overhead, ImVideoEdit achieves editing fidelity and temporal consistency comparable to larger models trained on extensive video datasets.

AI.tldr: ImVideoEdit enables high-quality, temporally coherent video edits trained solely on image pairs by freezing 3D attention and using a spatial-difference attention mechanism with text-guided gating; achieves strong editing fidelity with minimal data and compute.
AI.motivation: Video editing models typically require expensive paired video data, hindering scalability. A decoupled spatiotemporal approach aims to preserve temporal dynamics while modifying spatial content using image supervision.
AI.method: Freeze pretrained 3D attention modules and treat images as single-frame videos to decouple spatial learning from temporal dynamics. Introduce a Predict-Update Spatial Difference Attention module to progressively extract and inject spatial differences. Use a Text-Guided Dynamic Semantic Gating mechanism for adaptive, implicit text-driven modifications, avoiding rigid external masks. Train on 13k image pairs for 5 epochs with low computational overhead.
AI.result: Editing fidelity and temporal consistency are comparable to larger models trained on extensive video datasets, despite training on far less data and with lower compute.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, efficient pathway for video editing under image supervision by decoupling spatiotemporal learning and employing progressive spatial difference attention with dynamic, text-guided gating.

title: Evaluating Counterfactual Explanation Methods on Incomplete Inputs
summary: Existing algorithms for generating Counterfactual Explanations (CXs) for Machine Learning (ML) typically assume fully specified inputs. However, real-world data often contains missing values, and the impact of these incomplete inputs on the performance of existing CX methods remains unexplored. To address this gap, we systematically evaluate recent CX generation methods on their ability to provide valid and plausible counterfactuals when inputs are incomplete. As part of this investigation, we hypothesize that robust CX generation methods will be better suited to address the challenge of providing valid and plausible counterfactuals when inputs are incomplete. Our findings reveal that while robust CX methods achieve higher validity than non-robust ones, all methods struggle to find valid counterfactuals. These results motivate the need for new CX methods capable of handling incomplete inputs.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation shows robustness helps validity of counterfactual explanations under missing inputs, but all methods struggle to produce valid counterfactuals, indicating a need for methods that handle incomplete data.
AI.motivation: Real-world data often contain missing values; the behavior and reliability of counterfactual explanation methods under incomplete inputs is underexplored.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate recent counterfactual generation methods on incomplete inputs, comparing robust versus non-robust approaches; assess validity and plausibility of the produced counterfactuals across datasets.
AI.result: Robust CX methods yield higher validity than non-robust ones, but overall all methods struggle to find valid counterfactuals when inputs are incomplete.
AI.conclusion: There is a need to develop new CX methods explicitly designed to handle incomplete inputs and to better characterize how missing data affects counterfactual validity and plausibility.

title: Mathematical analysis of one-layer neural network with fixed biases, a new activation function and other observations
summary:
We analyze a simple one-hidden-layer neural network with ReLU activation functions and fixed biases, with one-dimensional input and output. We study both continuous and discrete versions of the model, and we rigorously prove the convergence of the learning process with the $L^2$ squared loss function and the gradient descent procedure. We also prove the spectral bias property for this learning process.
  Several conclusions of this analysis are discussed; in particular, regarding the structure and properties that activation functions should possess, as well as the relationships between the spectrum of certain operators and the learning process. Based on this, we also propose an alternative activation function, the full-wave rectified exponential function (FReX), and we discuss the convergence of the gradient descent with this alternative activation function.

AI.tldr: A shallow, one-hidden-layer ReLU network with fixed biases is analyzed for both continuous and discrete 1D settings under L2 loss. The study proves gradient-descent convergence and demonstrates spectral bias, discusses activation-function design via operator spectra, and introduces a new activation, FReX, with convergence analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand how activation properties and spectral characteristics of the training dynamics influence learnability in simple neural nets, and to guide activation design through spectral insights.
AI.method: Rigorous theoretical analysis of both continuous and discrete network dynamics under gradient descent with L2 loss. Spectral analysis of associated operators. Proposal of an alternative activation, FReX, followed by convergence discussion for gradient descent with this activation.
AI.result: Proves convergence of the learning process under gradient descent for both model variants. Establishes the spectral bias property. Provides insights into how activation-function properties relate to the spectrum of training operators. Introduces FReX and discusses its convergence under gradient descent.
AI.conclusion: Activation-function structure matters for learning dynamics; spectral properties of the training operators inform design choices. FReX offers a viable alternative with provable convergence, suggesting activation design guided by operator spectra could enhance learnability in simple networks and potentially beyond.

title: TOOLCAD: Exploring Tool-Using Large Language Models in Text-to-CAD Generation with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) is an expert-level task that relies on long-horizon reasoning and coherent modeling actions. Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable advancements in enabling language agents to tackle real-world tasks. Notably, there has been no investigation into how tool-using LLMs optimally interact with CAD engines, hindering the emergence of LLM-based agentic text-to-CAD modeling systems. We propose ToolCAD, a novel agentic CAD framework deploying LLMs as tool-using agents for text-to-CAD generation. Furthermore, we introduce an interactive CAD modeling gym to rollout reasoning and tool-augmented interaction trajectories with the CAD engine, incorporating hybrid feedback and human supervision. Meanwhile, an end-to-end post-training strategy is presented to enable the LLM agent to elicit refined CAD Modeling Chain of Thought (CAD-CoT) and evolve into proficient CAD tool-using agents via online curriculum reinforcement learning. Our findings demonstrate ToolCAD fills the gap in adopting and training open-source LLMs for CAD tool-using agents, enabling them to perform comparably to proprietary models, paving the way for more accessible and robust autonomous text-to-CAD modeling systems.

AI.tldr: ToolCAD introduces an agentic framework that uses open-source LLMs as tool-using agents to generate CAD models from text, supported by an interactive CAD modeling gym and curriculum RL to improve CAD-CoT (chain-of-thought) reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of understanding on how LLM-based agents should optimally interact with CAD engines to enable autonomous text-to-CAD generation, democratizing access to CAD tool usage.
AI.method: Integrates LLMs as tool-using agents within a Text-to-CAD pipeline, develops an interactive CAD modeling gym for reasoning and tool-driven CAD interactions (with hybrid feedback and human supervision), and proposes an end-to-end post-training strategy with online curriculum reinforcement learning to refine CAD-CoT in agents.
AI.result: Demonstrates that open-source LLMs can reach performance comparable to proprietary models in CAD tool-using tasks, and provides a pathway to more accessible autonomous text-to-CAD systems.
AI.conclusion: ToolCAD fills a gap in tool-using CAD systems, enabling accessible, robust autonomous text-to-CAD modeling by combining LLM-based tool use, interactive training environments, and curriculum RL-driven refinement of CAD reasoning.

title: Wiring the 'Why': A Unified Taxonomy and Survey of Abductive Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Regardless of its foundational role in human discovery and sense-making, abductive reasoning--the inference of the most plausible explanation for an observation--has been relatively underexplored in Large Language Models (LLMs). Despite the rapid advancement of LLMs, the exploration of abductive reasoning and its diverse facets has thus far been disjointed rather than cohesive. This paper presents the first survey of abductive reasoning in LLMs, tracing its trajectory from philosophical foundations to contemporary AI implementations. To address the widespread conceptual confusion and disjointed task definitions prevalent in the field, we establish a unified two-stage definition that formally categorizes prior work. This definition disentangles abduction into \textit{Hypothesis Generation}, where models bridge epistemic gaps to produce candidate explanations, and \textit{Hypothesis Selection}, where the generated candidates are evaluated and the most plausible explanation is chosen. Building upon this foundation, we present a comprehensive taxonomy of the literature, categorizing prior work based on their abductive tasks, datasets, underlying methodologies, and evaluation strategies. In order to ground our framework empirically, we conduct a compact benchmark study of current LLMs on abductive tasks, together with targeted comparative analyses across model sizes, model families, evaluation styles, and the distinct generation-versus-selection task typologies. Moreover, by synthesizing recent empirical results, we examine how LLM performance on abductive reasoning relates to deductive and inductive tasks, providing insights into their broader reasoning capabilities. Our analysis reveals critical gaps in current approaches--from static benchmark design and narrow domain coverage to narrow training frameworks and limited mechanistic understanding of abductive processes...

AI.tldr: This paper surveys abductive reasoning in LLMs, proposing a unified two-stage framework (Hypothesis Generation and Hypothesis Selection), develops a taxonomy, conducts a compact benchmark, and highlights gaps and directions for future work.
AI.motivation: Abductive reasoning is foundational to discovery but underexplored in LLMs; inconsistent task definitions hinder progress, necessitating a cohesive framework and empirical grounding.
AI.method: Proposes a unified two-stage definition of abduction, builds a taxonomy across abductive tasks, datasets, methodologies, and evaluations, and conducts a compact benchmark of current LLMs to compare model sizes, families, and generation-vs-selection task types; analyzes relation to deductive and inductive reasoning.
AI.result: Establishes a formal two-stage framework and a comprehensive taxonomy; provides empirical benchmark results and cross-model analyses; identifies gaps such as static benchmarks, narrow domain coverage, limited training frameworks, and insufficient mechanistic understanding of abductive processes.
AI.conclusion: Calls for broader benchmarks with richer domain coverage, improved training paradigms, and deeper investigations into the mechanisms of abductive reasoning in LLMs, outlining directions for future research.

title: DSCA: Dynamic Subspace Concept Alignment for Lifelong VLM Editing
summary: Model editing aims to update knowledge to add new concepts and change relevant information without retraining. Lifelong editing is a challenging task, prone to disrupting previously learned concepts, especially for Vision Language Models (VLMs), because sequential edits can lead to degraded reasoning and cross modal misalignment. Existing VLM knowledge editing methods based on gated adapters, activation edits, and parameter merging techniques address catastrophic forgetting seen in full fine tuning; however, they still operate in the shared representation space of the VLM, where concepts are entangled, so edits interfere with other non relevant concepts. We hypothesize that this instability persists because current methods algorithmically control edits via optimization rather than structurally separating knowledge. We introduce Dynamic Subspace Concept Alignment (DSCA) which by design mitigates this limitation by decomposing the representation space into a set of orthogonal semantic subspaces and proposing edits only in those transformed spaces. These subspaces are obtained through incremental clustering and PCA on joint vision language representations. This process structurally isolates concepts, enabling precise, non interfering edits by turning isolation from a soft training objective into an architectural property. The surgical edits are guided by a multi term loss function for maintaining task fidelity, edit locality, and cross modal alignment. With the base model frozen, our method achieves 98 percent single edit success, remains over 95 percent after 1000 sequential edits, lowers hallucination by 3 to 5 percent, and achieves the best backward transfer (BWT) scores on continual instruction tuning benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate DSCA state of the art stability and knowledge retention capability in continual lifelong editing across various datasets and benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DSCA introduces Dynamic Subspace Concept Alignment to edit VLMs by isolating knowledge in orthogonal subspaces via incremental clustering and PCA, enabling high-fidelity, non-interfering lifelong edits with strong stability and retention.
AI.motivation: To overcome catastrophic forgetting and entanglement in the shared VLM representation during sequential edits, which can degrade reasoning and cross-modal alignment.
AI.method: Decompose the joint vision-language representation into orthogonal semantic subspaces using incremental clustering and PCA; perform edits only in these transformed subspaces while keeping the base model frozen; optimize a multi-term loss (task fidelity, edit locality, cross-modal alignment) to guide surgical edits and maintain performance.
AI.result: Achieves 98% single-edit success; >95% retention after 1000 sequential edits; 3–5% reduction in hallucination; best backward transfer (BWT) on continual instruction-tuning benchmarks; state-of-the-art stability and knowledge retention across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Architectural isolation of concepts via dynamically learned subspaces yields more stable lifelong editing for VLMs, outperforming previous gating/activation/merge approaches by preventing interference between edited and non-edited concepts.

title: Towards Rapid Constitutive Model Discovery from Multi-Modal Data: Physics Augmented Finite Element Model Updating (paFEMU)
summary: Recent progress in AI-enabled constitutive modeling has concentrated on moving from a purely data-driven paradigm to the enforcement of physical constraints and mechanistic principles, a concept referred to as physics augmentation. Classical phenomenological approaches rely on selecting a pre-defined model and calibrating its parameters, while machine learning methods often focus on discovery of the model itself. Sparse regression approaches lie in between, where large libraries of pre-defined models are probed during calibration. Sparsification in the aforementioned paradigm, but also in the context of neural network architecture, has been shown to enable interpretability, uncertainty quantification, but also heterogeneous software integration due to the low-dimensional nature of the resulting models. Most works in AI-enabled constitutive modeling have also focused on data from a single source, but in reality, materials modeling workflows can contain data from many different sources (multi-modal data), and also from testing other materials within the same materials class (multi-fidelity data). In this work, we introduce physics augmented finite element model updating (paFEMU), as a transfer learning approach that combines AI-enabled constitutive modeling, sparsification for interpretable model discovery, and finite element-based adjoint optimization utilizing multi-modal data. This is achieved by combining simple mechanical testing data, potentially from a distinct material, with digital image correlation-type full-field data acquisition to ultimately enable rapid constitutive modeling discovery. The simplicity of the sparse representation enables easy integration of neural constitutive models in existing finite element workflows, and also enables low-dimensional updating during transfer learning.

AI.tldr: Physics-augmented finite element model updating (paFEMU) enables transfer-learning-based, sparse, interpretable constitutive modeling using multi-modal data (simple tests + full-field DIC data) within FE workflows.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of purely data-driven or purely phenomenological approaches by enforcing physics, enabling interpretability via sparsification, and leveraging multi-source/multi-fidelity data for rapid, transfer-learning-based model discovery.
AI.method: Couple AI-enabled constitutive modeling with sparsification to create interpretable models; integrate neural constitutive models into FE workflows; use finite element adjoint optimization; perform updating in a low-dimensional space; utilize multi-modal data from disparate sources and materials to enable transfer learning.
AI.result: Proposes paFEMU framework and demonstrates integration and potential benefits; provides a path for rapid constitutive model discovery and easier FE integration.
AI.conclusion: paFEMU combines physics-informed AI, sparse model discovery, and multi-modal transfer learning to enhance constitutive modeling across materials classes within FE contexts, enabling rapid, interpretable updates and broader data fusion.

title: Lighting-grounded Video Generation with Renderer-based Agent Reasoning
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable progress in video generation, but their controllability remains a major limitation. Key scene factors such as layout, lighting, and camera trajectory are often entangled or only weakly modeled, restricting their applicability in domains like filmmaking and virtual production where explicit scene control is essential. We present LiVER, a diffusion-based framework for scene-controllable video generation. To achieve this, we introduce a novel framework that conditions video synthesis on explicit 3D scene properties, supported by a new large-scale dataset with dense annotations of object layout, lighting, and camera parameters. Our method disentangles these properties by rendering control signals from a unified 3D representation. We propose a lightweight conditioning module and a progressive training strategy to integrate these signals into a foundational video diffusion model, ensuring stable convergence and high fidelity. Our framework enables a wide range of applications, including image-to-video and video-to-video synthesis where the underlying 3D scene is fully editable. To further enhance usability, we develop a scene agent that automatically translates high-level user instructions into the required 3D control signals. Experiments show that LiVER achieves state-of-the-art photorealism and temporal consistency while enabling precise, disentangled control over scene factors, setting a new standard for controllable video generation.

AI.tldr: LiVER offers a diffusion-based video generator conditioned on explicit 3D scene properties, disentangling layout, lighting, and camera factors via a unified 3D representation, backed by a large annotated dataset; enables editable 3D scene control and achieves state-of-the-art photorealism and temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Address the limited controllability of diffusion-based video models; explicit, disentangled control over scene factors (layout, lighting, camera) is essential for filmmaking and virtual production.
AI.method: Introduce LiVER: a diffusion-based framework conditioned on explicit 3D scene properties; render control signals from a unified 3D representation; use a lightweight conditioning module; adopt a progressive training strategy; build a large-scale dataset with dense annotations; deploy a scene agent to translate high-level instructions into 3D controls.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art photorealism and temporal consistency; enables precise disentangled control over scene factors; supports image-to-video and video-to-video generation where the 3D scene is fully editable.
AI.conclusion: LiVER sets a new standard for controllable video generation, broadening applications and usability by enabling automatic translation of user instructions into 3D controls, with stable convergence and high fidelity.

title: IoT-Brain: Grounding LLMs for Semantic-Spatial Sensor Scheduling
comment: To appear in ACM MobiCom 2026; 13 pages, 12 figures
summary: Intelligent systems powered by large-scale sensor networks are shifting from predefined monitoring to intent-driven operation, revealing a critical Semantic-to-Physical Mapping Gap. While large language models (LLMs) excel at semantic understanding, existing perception-centric pipelines operate retrospectively, overlooking the fundamental decision of what to sense and when. We formalize this proactive decision as Semantic-Spatial Sensor Scheduling (S3) and demonstrate that direct LLM planning is unreliable due to inherent gaps in representation, reasoning, and optimization. To bridge these gaps, we introduce the Spatial Trajectory Graph (STG), a neuro-symbolic paradigm governed by a verify-before-commit discipline that transforms open-ended planning into a verifiable graph optimization problem. Based on STG, we implement IoT-Brain, a concrete system embodiment, and construct TopoSense-Bench, a campus-scale benchmark with 5,250 natural-language queries across 2,510 cameras. Evaluations show that IoT-Brain boosts task success rate by 37.6% over the strongest search-intensive methods while running nearly 2 times faster and using 6.6 times fewer prompt tokens. In real-world deployment, it approaches the reliability upper bound while reducing 4.1 times network bandwidth, providing a foundational framework for LLMs to interact with the physical world with unprecedented reliability and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Bridges semantic planning and physical sensing with a neuro-symbolic STG framework via S3, enabling reliable, efficient LLM-guided sensor scheduling (IoT-Brain) and a campus-scale NL benchmark (TopoSense-Bench).
AI.motivation: LLMs excel in semantic understanding but perception pipelines are retrospective and neglect proactive decisions about what to sense and when, creating a Semantic-to-Physical Mapping Gap in sensor networks.
AI.method: Formalize Semantic-Spatial Sensor Scheduling (S3) and introduce Spatial Trajectory Graph (STG) with a verify-before-commit discipline that converts open-ended planning into verifiable graph optimization. Implement IoT-Brain and construct TopoSense-Bench (5,250 NL queries over 2,510 cameras).
AI.result: IoT-Brain achieves a 37.6% higher task success rate than the strongest search-intensive baselines, runs ~2x faster, and uses 6.6x fewer prompt tokens; in real deployment it nears the reliability upper bound and reduces network bandwidth by 4.1x.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a foundational framework enabling reliable, efficient LLM-enabled interaction with the physical world through a verifiable, graph-based planning paradigm that overcomes gaps in representation, reasoning, and optimization.

title: Structured Distillation of Web Agent Capabilities Enables Generalization
summary: Frontier LLMs can navigate complex websites, but their cost and reliance on third-party APIs make local deployment impractical. We introduce Agent-as-Annotators, a framework that structures synthetic trajectory generation for web agents by analogy to human annotation roles, replacing the Task Designer, Annotator, and Supervisor with modular LLM components. Using Gemini 3 Pro as teacher, we generate 3,000 trajectories across six web environments and fine-tune a 9B-parameter student with pure supervised learning on the 2,322 that pass quality filtering. The resulting model achieves 41.5% on WebArena, surpassing closed-source models such as Claude 3.5 Sonnet (36.0%) and GPT-4o (31.5%) under the same evaluation protocol, and nearly doubling the previous best open-weight result (Go-Browse, 21.7%). Capabilities transfer to unseen environments, with an 18.2 percentage point gain on WorkArena L1 (an enterprise platform never seen during training) and consistent improvements across three additional benchmarks. Ablations confirm that each pipeline component contributes meaningfully, with Judge filtering, evaluation hints, and reasoning traces each accounting for measurable gains. These results demonstrate that structured trajectory synthesis from a single frontier teacher is sufficient to produce competitive, locally deployable web agents. Project page: https://agent-as-annotators.github.io

AI.tldr: Agent-as-Annotators uses a frontier teacher to synthesize web-navigation trajectories; a 9B student trained on filtered synthetic data achieves strong, locally deployable performance, surpassing multiple closed/open models and generalizing to unseen environments.
AI.motivation: Reduce cost and eliminate dependence on third-party APIs by enabling local training of capable web agents through structured synthetic data generated by LLM components acting as annotation roles.
AI.method: Generate 3,000 synthetic trajectories across six web environments using Gemini 3 Pro as teacher; replace Task Designer/Annotator/Supervisor with modular LLM components; filter to 2,322 high-quality trajectories; train a 9B-parameter model with pure supervised learning; evaluate on WebArena, WorkArena L1, and three additional benchmarks; perform ablations on Judge filtering, evaluation hints, and reasoning traces.
AI.result: 9B model achieves 41.5% on WebArena, beating Claude 3.5 Sonnet (36.0%) and GPT-4o (31.5%); previously best open-weight (Go-Browse) was 21.7%. Demonstrates transfer with an 18.2 percentage-point gain on WorkArena L1 (unseen in training) and improvements across three more benchmarks. Ablations show Judge filtering, evaluation hints, and reasoning traces each contribute measurable gains.
AI.conclusion: Structured trajectory synthesis from a single frontier teacher suffices to produce competitive, locally deployable web agents.

title: Object-Centric Stereo Ranging for Autonomous Driving: From Dense Disparity to Census-Based Template Matching
summary: Accurate depth estimation is critical for autonomous driving perception systems, particularly for long range vehicle detection on highways. Traditional dense stereo matching methods such as Block Matching (BM) and Semi Global Matching (SGM) produce per pixel disparity maps but suffer from high computational cost, sensitivity to radiometric differences between stereo cameras, and poor accuracy at long range where disparity values are small. In this report, we present a comprehensive stereo ranging system that integrates three complementary depth estimation approaches: dense BM/SGM disparity, object centric Census based template matching, and monocular geometric priors, within a unified detection ranging tracking pipeline. Our key contribution is a novel object centric Census based template matching algorithm that performs GPU accelerated sparse stereo matching directly within detected bounding boxes, employing a far close divide and conquer strategy, forward backward verification, occlusion aware sampling, and robust multi block aggregation. We further describe an online calibration refinement framework that combines auto rectification offset search, radar stereo voting based disparity correction, and object level radar stereo association for continuous extrinsic drift compensation. The complete system achieves real time performance through asynchronous GPU pipeline design and delivers robust ranging across diverse driving conditions including nighttime, rain, and varying illumination.

AI.tldr: A stereo ranging system combining dense BM/SGM disparities, object-centric Census-based template matching, and monocular priors into a unified pipeline with online calibration, enabling real-time, robust long-range depth for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Accurate depth is critical for long-range highway perception; traditional dense stereo methods are costly and degrade at long range and under radiometric differences.
AI.method: Integrates three depth estimation approaches within a unified detection-ranging-tracking pipeline: (i) dense BM/SGM disparity, (ii) object-centric Census-based template matching GPU-accelerated within detected bounding boxes with far-close divide-and-conquer, forward-backward verification, occlusion-aware sampling, and multi-block aggregation, (iii) monocular geometric priors. Adds online calibration refinement with auto rectification offset search, radar-stereo voting for disparity correction, and object-level radar-stereo association; uses asynchronous GPU pipeline for real-time performance.
AI.result: Real-time performance achieved; robust ranging across diverse driving conditions (nighttime, rain, varying illumination).
AI.conclusion: Fusion of complementary depth estimation modalities with online calibration yields robust, long-range depth perception suitable for autonomous driving perception pipelines, with efficient GPU-based implementation.

title: ImplicitMemBench: Measuring Unconscious Behavioral Adaptation in Large Language Models
summary: Existing memory benchmarks for LLM agents evaluate explicit recall of facts, yet overlook implicit memory where experience becomes automated behavior without conscious retrieval. This gap is critical: effective assistants must automatically apply learned procedures or avoid failed actions without explicit reminders. We introduce ImplicitMemBench, the first systematic benchmark evaluating implicit memory through three cognitively grounded constructs drawn from standard cognitive-science accounts of non-declarative memory: Procedural Memory (one-shot skill acquisition after interference), Priming (theme-driven bias via paired experimental/control instances), and Classical Conditioning (Conditioned Stimulus--Unconditioned Stimulus (CS--US) associations shaping first decisions). Our 300-item suite employs a unified Learning/Priming-Interfere-Test protocol with first-attempt scoring. Evaluation of 17 models reveals severe limitations: no model exceeds 66% overall, with top performers DeepSeek-R1 (65.3%), Qwen3-32B (64.1%), and GPT-5 (63.0%) far below human baselines. Analysis uncovers dramatic asymmetries (inhibition 17.6% vs. preference 75.0%) and universal bottlenecks requiring architectural innovations beyond parameter scaling. ImplicitMemBench reframes evaluation from "what agents recall" to "what they automatically enact".

AI.tldr: A 300-item benchmark (ImplicitMemBench) assesses implicit memory in LLM agents via procedural memory, priming, and classical conditioning within a unified Learning/Priming-Interfere-Test protocol, revealing models perform far below human baselines and highlighting inherent bottlenecks that demand architectural innovations.
AI.motivation: To address the gap that existing memory benchmarks evaluate explicit recall only, by evaluating implicit memory—automatic, non-conscious procedural enactment—crucial for practical LLM assistants.
AI.method: A 300-item suite testing three cognitively grounded non-declarative memory constructs: Procedural memory (one-shot skill acquisition after interference), Priming (theme-driven bias via paired experimental/control instances), and Classical conditioning (CS-US associations shaping first decisions). Uses a unified Learning/Priming-Interfere-Test protocol with first-attempt scoring across 17 models.
AI.result: Models show limited implicit memory; none exceed 66% overall. Top performers: DeepSeek-R1 65.3%, Qwen3-32B 64.1%, GPT-5 63.0%, all far below human baselines. Notable asymmetries: inhibition 17.6% vs. preference 75.0%. Indicates universal bottlenecks not solvable by scaling; performance gaps indicate need for architecture-level innovations.
AI.conclusion: Shifts evaluation from declarative recall to automatic enactment; ImplicitMemBench exposes critical blind spots in current LLMs’ implicit memory capabilities and motivates architectural changes beyond parameter scaling.

title: PolicyLong: Towards On-Policy Context Extension
comment: Work in progress. Correspondence to ucaswu@tencent.com or wuxing@iie.ac.cn
summary: Extending LLM context windows is hindered by scarce high-quality long-context data. Recent methods synthesize data with genuine long-range dependencies via information-theoretic verification, selecting contexts that reduce a base model's predictive entropy.
