PAL performs closed-form affine color alignment using just covariance statistics and a small matrix inversion to suppress the nuisance photometric term while preserving task-relevant supervision.
AI.result: PAL consistently improves metrics and generalization across 6 tasks, 16 datasets, and 16 architectures, with negligible overhead (implementation details in the appendix).
AI.conclusion: PAL provides a flexible, covariance-based photometric alignment objective that reduces nuisance photometric discrepancy in supervised low-level vision, enhancing generalization across diverse tasks.

title: EgoEverything: A Benchmark for Human Behavior Inspired Long Context Egocentric Video Understanding in AR Environment
summary: Long context egocentric video understanding has recently attracted significant research attention, with augmented reality (AR) highlighted as one of its most important application domains. Nevertheless, the task remains highly challenging due to the need for reasoning over extended temporal contexts and diverse, unstructured activities. Although several benchmarks exist, most egocentric datasets rely on human worn cameras and focus mainly on visual content, with limited consideration of underlying user behavior when forming video-related queries. EgoEverything is a benchmark that explicitly considers human behavior by leveraging human attention signals, abstracted from gaze data, when generating questions. It comprises over 5,000 multiple choice question answer pairs, spanning more than 100 hours of video. By integrating human attention signals during question generation, it more faithfully captures natural human behavior and offers a realistic evaluation setting for long-context egocentric video understanding in AR.

AI.tldr: A long-context egocentric video benchmark (EgoEverything) uses gaze-derived attention signals to generate MCQ QA pairs, enabling realistic evaluation for AR in long-context settings; dataset includes 5k+ Q/A pairs across over 100 hours of video.
AI.motivation: Existing egocentric datasets emphasize visual content and neglect user behavior signals, which are crucial for long-context reasoning in AR. Incorporating human attention signals during question generation aims to produce more natural, behavior-aligned questions and a realistic evaluation setting.
AI.method: Collect gaze data to obtain attention signals, abstract these signals, and integrate them into the question-generation process to produce over 5,000 multiple-choice QA pairs from 100+ hours of egocentric video.
AI.result: Dataset release with over 5,000 MCQ QA pairs spanning more than 100 hours of video. Provides a realistic evaluation setting for long-context egocentric video understanding in AR by leveraging attention-informed question generation; the abstract does not report model performance results.
AI.conclusion: EgoEverything advances long-context egocentric video understanding for AR by explicitly modeling human attention in question generation, offering a more faithful benchmark that aligns with natural human behavior.

title: MedVR: Annotation-Free Medical Visual Reasoning via Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Medical Vision-Language Models (VLMs) hold immense promise for complex clinical tasks, but their reasoning capabilities are often constrained by text-only paradigms that fail to ground inferences in visual evidence. This limitation not only curtails performance on tasks requiring fine-grained visual analysis but also introduces risks of visual hallucination in safety-critical applications. Thus, we introduce MedVR, a novel reinforcement learning framework that enables annotation-free visual reasoning for medical VLMs. Its core innovation lies in two synergistic mechanisms: Entropy-guided Visual Regrounding (EVR) uses model uncertainty to direct exploration, while Consensus-based Credit Assignment (CCA) distills pseudo-supervision from rollout agreement. Without any human annotations for intermediate steps, MedVR achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse public medical VQA benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing models. By learning to reason directly with visual evidence, MedVR promotes the robustness and transparency essential for accelerating the clinical deployment of medical AI.

AI.tldr: MedVR is an annotation-free reinforcement learning framework for medical VLMs that grounds reasoning in visual evidence using Entropy-guided Visual Regrounding (EVR) and Consensus-based Credit Assignment (CCA), achieving state-of-the-art results on medical VQA without human intermediate supervision.
AI.motivation: Text-only grounding in medical VLMs limits fine-grained visual reasoning and can lead to visual hallucinations; there is a need for annotation-free methods that can learn visual reasoning directly from data.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Entropy-guided Visual Regrounding (EVR) uses model uncertainty to guide exploration of visual evidence; (2) Consensus-based Credit Assignment (CCA) distills pseudo-supervision from rollout agreement; the framework operates in a reinforcement learning setup without intermediate human annotations for reasoning steps.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse public medical VQA benchmarks and significantly outperforms existing models, with reported gains in grounding visual evidence and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Annotation-free visual reasoning via EVR and CCA enhances robustness and transparency of medical VLMs, facilitating safer and more reliable clinical deployment.

title: Bias-Constrained Diffusion Schedules for PDE Emulations: Reconstruction Error Minimization and Efficient Unrolled Training
summary: Conditional Diffusion Models are powerful surrogates for emulating complex spatiotemporal dynamics, yet they often fail to match the accuracy of deterministic neural emulators for high-precision tasks. In this work, we address two critical limitations of autoregressive PDE diffusion models: their sub-optimal single-step accuracy and the prohibitive computational cost of unrolled training. First, we characterize the relationship between the noise schedule, the reconstruction error reduction rate and the diffusion exposure bias, demonstrating that standard schedules lead to suboptimal reconstruction error. Leveraging this insight, we propose an \textit{Adaptive Noise Schedule} framework that minimizes inference reconstruction error by dynamically constraining the model's exposure bias. We further show that this optimized schedule enables a fast \textit{Proxy Unrolled Training} method to stabilize long-term rollouts without the cost of full Markov Chain sampling. Both proposed methods enable significant improvements in short-term accuracy and long-term stability over diffusion and deterministic baselines on diverse benchmarks, including forced Navier-Stokes, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Transonic Flow.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Noise Schedule and Proxy Unrolled Training improve short-term accuracy and long-term stability of autoregressive PDE diffusion models, outperforming baselines on Navier–Stokes, Kuramoto–Sivashinsky, and Transonic Flow benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive PDE diffusion models suffer from suboptimal single-step accuracy due to fixed noise schedules and incur high cost for unrolled training; a faster, more accurate framework is needed for reliable long-horizon emulation.
AI.method: Propose an Adaptive Noise Schedule that dynamically constrains exposure bias to minimize inference reconstruction error by exploiting the relationship between the noise schedule, error reduction, and exposure bias; derive and apply this to optimize scheduling. Introduce Proxy Unrolled Training to stabilize long-term rollouts without full Markov Chain sampling.
AI.result: Significant improvements in both short-term accuracy and long-term stability over diffusion and deterministic baselines across multiple PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The combination of an adaptive noise schedule with proxy unrolled training yields robust, efficient PDE emulation with diffusion models and suggests broad applicability to similar spatiotemporal tasks; prompts further exploration of dynamic schedules and lightweight training schemes.

title: OmniJigsaw: Enhancing Omni-Modal Reasoning via Modality-Orchestrated Reordering
summary: To extend the reinforcement learning post-training paradigm to omni-modal models for concurrently bolstering video-audio understanding and collaborative reasoning, we propose OmniJigsaw, a generic self-supervised framework built upon a temporal reordering proxy task. Centered on the chronological reconstruction of shuffled audio-visual clips, this paradigm strategically orchestrates visual and auditory signals to compel cross-modal integration through three distinct strategies: Joint Modality Integration, Sample-level Modality Selection, and Clip-level Modality Masking. Recognizing that the efficacy of such proxy tasks is fundamentally tied to puzzle quality, we design a two-stage coarse-to-fine data filtering pipeline, which facilitates the efficient adaptation of OmniJigsaw to massive unannotated omni-modal data. Our analysis reveals a ``bi-modal shortcut phenomenon'' in joint modality integration and demonstrates that fine-grained clip-level modality masking mitigates this issue while outperforming sample-level modality selection. Extensive evaluations on 15 benchmarks show substantial gains in video, audio, and collaborative reasoning, validating OmniJigsaw as a scalable paradigm for self-supervised omni-modal learning.

AI.tldr: OmniJigsaw introduces a self-supervised, cross-modal puzzle framework for omni-modal video-audio understanding, employing a two-stage data filtering pipeline and three modality strategies to achieve scalable gains across 15 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To extend reinforcement-learning post-training to omni-modal models and address the need for effective cross-modal integration with limited annotated data, while mitigating shortcut phenomena and enabling scalable self-supervised learning.
AI.method: Two-stage coarse-to-fine data filtering; temporal reordering proxy task on shuffled audio-visual clips; three strategies: Joint Modality Integration, Sample-level Modality Selection, Clip-level Modality Masking.
AI.result: Significant gains across video, audio, and collaborative reasoning on 15 benchmarks; mitigation of bi-modal shortcut; demonstration of scalability and effectiveness of the self-supervised omni-modal paradigm.
AI.conclusion: OmniJigsaw provides a scalable, effective self-supervised framework for omni-modal learning, enabling enhanced video-audio understanding and collaborative reasoning through well-designed puzzle tasks and data filtering.

title: Scaling-Aware Data Selection for End-to-End Autonomous Driving Systems
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026, 8 pages of main body and 10 pages of appendix
summary: Large-scale deep learning models for physical AI applications depend on diverse training data collection efforts. These models and correspondingly, the training data, must address different evaluation criteria necessary for the models to be deployable in real-world environments. Data selection policies can guide the development of the training set, but current frameworks do not account for the ambiguity in how data points affect different metrics. In this work, we propose Mixture Optimization via Scaling-Aware Iterative Collection (MOSAIC), a general data selection framework that operates by: (i) partitioning the dataset into domains; (ii) fitting neural scaling laws from each data domain to the evaluation metrics; and (iii) optimizing a data mixture by iteratively adding data from domains that maximize the change in metrics. We apply MOSAIC to autonomous driving (AD), where an End-to-End (E2E) planner model is evaluated on the Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS), an aggregate of driving rule compliance metrics. Here, MOSAIC outperforms a diverse set of baselines on EPDMS with up to 80\% less data.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a data-selection framework that partitions data into domains, fits scaling laws per domain for evaluation metrics, and optimizes a data mixture to maximize metric gains, achieving data-efficient performance in autonomous driving with an end-to-end planner.
AI.motivation: Addresses ambiguity in how individual data points affect multiple evaluation metrics in large-scale physical AI models, enabling principled, metric-aware data collection.
AI.method: 1) Partition dataset into domains; 2) fit neural scaling laws from each domain to the evaluation metrics; 3) iteratively add data from domains that maximize the change in metrics to optimize the data mixture.
AI.result: When applied to autonomous driving with an End-to-End planner evaluated via the Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS), MOSAIC outperforms baselines and uses up to 80% less data.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC provides a general, scaling-law-guided data selection framework that yields multi-metric data efficiency in physical AI and can be applied beyond autonomous driving.

title: SciFigDetect: A Benchmark for AI-Generated Scientific Figure Detection
summary: Modern multimodal generators can now produce scientific figures at near-publishable quality, creating a new challenge for visual forensics and research integrity. Unlike conventional AI-generated natural images, scientific figures are structured, text-dense, and tightly aligned with scholarly semantics, making them a distinct and difficult detection target. However, existing AI-generated image detection benchmarks and methods are almost entirely developed for open-domain imagery, leaving this setting largely unexplored. We present the first benchmark for AI-generated scientific figure detection. To construct it, we develop an agent-based data pipeline that retrieves licensed source papers, performs multimodal understanding of paper text and figures, builds structured prompts, synthesizes candidate figures, and filters them through a review-driven refinement loop. The resulting benchmark covers multiple figure categories, multiple generation sources and aligned real--synthetic pairs. We benchmark representative detectors under zero-shot, cross-generator, and degraded-image settings. Results show that current methods fail dramatically in zero-shot transfer, exhibit strong generator-specific overfitting, and remain fragile under common post-processing corruptions. These findings reveal a substantial gap between existing AIGI detection capabilities and the emerging distribution of high-quality scientific figures. We hope this benchmark can serve as a foundation for future research on robust and generalizable scientific-figure forensics. The dataset is available at https://github.com/Joyce-yoyo/SciFigDetect.

AI.tldr: Introduces SciFigDetect: a benchmark for AI-generated scientific figures built via an agent-based data pipeline; reveals detectors struggle in zero-shot, generator-specific overfitting, and vulnerability to post-processing.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in AI-generation detection for structured, text-rich scientific figures, which differ from open-domain images and challenge existing detectors and benchmarks.
AI.method: Develops agent-based pipeline: retrieve licensed papers, multimodal understanding of text and figures, construct structured prompts, synthesize candidate figures, and a review-driven refinement loop; creates diverse real-synthetic pairs across figure categories and sources; evaluates detectors under zero-shot, cross-generator, and degraded-image conditions.
AI.result: Current detectors exhibit poor zero-shot transfer, strong overfitting to generators, and fragility under common corruptions; significant performance gap between detectors and realistic high-quality scientific figures.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark lays groundwork for robust, generalizable forensic methods for scientific figures; release via GitHub.

title: SOLAR: Communication-Efficient Model Adaptation via Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparametrization
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as LoRA, enable scalable adaptation of foundation models by injecting low-rank adapters. However, their communication and storage costs remain a major bottleneck in resource-constrained settings. We propose SOLAR (Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparameterization), a post-training compression framework that substantially reduces the communication cost (i.e., the number of parameters to transmit or store) of PEFT adapters. SOLAR expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of basis vectors formed from the foundation model's singular vectors with controlled random perturbations. By exploiting the subspace similarity (the alignment of principal directions) between the foundation model and task-specific fine-tuned updates, SOLAR decouples the adapter size from PEFT structure and ensures compact yet expressive representations. It is model-agnostic and compatible with existing PEFT methods, including LoRA, AdaLoRA, and other adapter modules. We theoretically establish a bound on the reconstruction error. Experiments on language and vision tasks using LLaMA, GPT, and ViT models demonstrate that SOLAR preserves task performance while significantly reducing model representation sizes, offering an effective and communication-efficient solution for deployment in distributed systems and edge devices.

AI.tldr: SOLAR compresses PEFT adapters by representing updates as a subspace-based linear combination of the model's singular vectors with perturbations, yielding large communication/storage savings while preserving performance; model-agnostic and compatible with LoRA/AdaLoRA.
AI.motivation: PEFT methods improve parameter efficiency but still incur substantial communication/storage costs in constrained environments. A post-training compression that preserves expressiveness is needed, leveraging subspace alignment between the base model and task-specific updates.
AI.method: SOLAR forms a basis from the foundation model's singular vectors and expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of these basis vectors with controlled random perturbations. This decouples adapter size from PEFT structure by exploiting subspace similarity between the base model and fine-tuned updates. It is post-training, model-agnostic, and compatible with existing adapters such as LoRA and AdaLoRA.
AI.result: The paper provides a theoretical bound on reconstruction error. Empirical evaluations on language and vision tasks (LLaMA, GPT, ViT) show that SOLAR preserves task performance while substantially reducing adapter representation sizes, enabling deployment in distributed and edge environments.
AI.conclusion: SOLAR offers a general, communication-efficient compression framework for PEFT adapters that is compatible with existing methods and maintains performance, facilitating efficient deployment in constrained settings.

title: Vision-Language Foundation Models for Comprehensive Automated Pavement Condition Assessment
summary: General-purpose vision-language models demonstrate strong performance in everyday domains but struggle with specialized technical fields requiring precise terminology, structured reasoning, and adherence to engineering standards. This work addresses whether domain-specific instruction tuning can enable comprehensive pavement condition assessment through vision-language models. PaveInstruct, a dataset containing 278,889 image-instruction-response pairs spanning 32 task types, was created by unifying annotations from nine heterogeneous pavement datasets. PaveGPT, a pavement foundation model trained on this dataset, was evaluated against state-of-the-art vision-language models across perception, understanding, and reasoning tasks. Instruction tuning transformed model capabilities, achieving improvements exceeding 20% in spatial grounding, reasoning, and generation tasks while producing ASTM D6433-compliant outputs. These results enable transportation agencies to deploy unified conversational assessment tools that replace multiple specialized systems, simplifying workflows and reducing technical expertise requirements. The approach establishes a pathway for developing instruction-driven AI systems across infrastructure domains including bridge inspection, railway maintenance, and building condition assessment.

AI.tldr: Instruction-tuned vision-language models can handle specialized infrastructure tasks; PaveInstruct creates a large pavement-focused dataset to train PaveGPT, achieving significant gains over SOTA models and ASTM-compliant outputs, enabling unified assessment tools for agencies.
AI.motivation: General-purpose vision-language models underperform in specialized technical domains requiring precise terminology, structured reasoning, and adherence to engineering standards; there is a need to unify disparate pavement datasets and tools into a single, instruction-tuned model for practical deployment.
AI.method: Construct PaveInstruct by unifying annotations from nine pavement datasets into 278,889 image-instruction-response pairs across 32 task types; train PaveGPT on this dataset with instruction tuning; evaluate against state-of-the-art VLMs on perception, understanding, and reasoning tasks; assess ASTM D6433 compliance in outputs.
AI.result: Instruction tuning yields >20% gains in spatial grounding, reasoning, and generation tasks; outputs adhere to ASTM D6433 standards; performance surpasses competing vision-language models on pavement-specific tasks; enables unified, conversational assessment workflows for transportation agencies.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-driven AI can be extended to infrastructure domains beyond pavement (e.g., bridges, railways, buildings) to reduce tool fragmentation, simplify workflows, and lower expertise requirements, paving the way for domain-specific foundation models.

title: ADAPTive Input Training for Many-to-One Pre-Training on Time-Series Classification
summary: Recent work on time-series models has leveraged self-supervised training to learn meaningful features and patterns in order to improve performance on downstream tasks and generalize to unseen modalities. While these pretraining methods have shown great promise in one-to-many scenarios, where a model is pre-trained on one dataset and fine-tuned on a downstream dataset, they have struggled to generalize to new datasets when more datasets are added during pre-training. This is a fundamental challenge in building foundation models for time-series data, as it limits the ability to develop models that can learn from a large variety of diverse datasets available. To address this challenge, we present a new pre-training paradigm for time-series data called ADAPT, which can efficiently align the physical properties of data in the time-series domain, enabling mixed-batch pre-training despite the extreme discrepancies in the input sizes and channel dimensions of pre-training data. We trained on 162 time-series classification datasets and set new state-of-the-art performance for classification benchmarks. We successfully train a model within the time-series domain on a wide range of datasets simultaneously, which is a major building block for building generalist foundation models in time-series domains.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a self-supervised pretraining paradigm for time-series that aligns physical properties to enable mixed-batch pretraining across datasets with varying lengths and channel dimensions, achieving state-of-the-art results on 162 datasets and paving the way for generalist time-series foundation models.
AI.motivation: Current time-series pretraining methods excel in one-to-many settings but struggle to generalize when many datasets are added during pretraining, hindering the construction of broad, foundation-like models for diverse time-series data.
AI.method: ADAPT introduces an alignment-based pretraining framework that harmonizes the physical properties of time-series data (e.g., sampling rates, amplitudes, channel semantics) to support mixed-batch pretraining across datasets with large input-size and channel discrepancies.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on time-series classification benchmarks; successfully trains a model across 162 diverse datasets in a single pretraining stage, signaling feasibility of generalist time-series models.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT addresses cross-dataset heterogeneity and enables scalable, mixed-dataset pretraining, representing a key step toward generalist foundation models in the time-series domain.

title: EditCaption: Human-Aligned Instruction Synthesis for Image Editing via Supervised Fine-Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization
summary: High-quality training triplets (source-target image pairs with precise editing instructions) are a critical bottleneck for scaling instruction-guided image editing models. Vision-language models (VLMs) are widely used for automated instruction synthesis, but we identify three systematic failure modes in image-pair settings: orientation inconsistency (e.g., left/right confusion), viewpoint ambiguity, and insufficient fine-grained attribute description. Human evaluation shows that over 47% of instructions from strong baseline VLMs contain critical errors unusable for downstream training. We propose EditCaption, a scalable two-stage post-training pipeline for VLM-based instruction synthesis. Stage 1 builds a 100K supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset by combining GLM automatic annotation, EditScore-based filtering, and human refinement for spatial, directional, and attribute-level accuracy. Stage 2 collects 10K human preference pairs targeting the three failure modes and applies direct preference optimization (DPO) for alignment beyond SFT alone. On Eval-400, ByteMorph-Bench, and HQ-Edit, fine-tuned Qwen3-VL models outperform open-source baselines; the 235B model reaches 4.712 on Eval-400 (vs. Gemini-3-Pro 4.706, GPT-4.1 4.220, Kimi-K2.5 4.111) and 4.588 on ByteMorph-Bench (vs. Gemini-3-Pro 4.522, GPT-4.1 3.412). Human evaluation shows critical errors falling from 47.75% to 23% and correctness rising from 41.75% to 66%. The work offers a practical path to scalable, human-aligned instruction synthesis for image editing data.

AI.tldr: A scalable two-stage pipeline (EditCaption) improves VLM-synthesized image-editing instructions by addressing orientation, viewpoint, and fine-grained attribute errors, using SFT with automated and human refinement and DPO with preference data; achieves state-of-the-art alignment and reduced error rates on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-quality training triplets are a bottleneck for scaling instruction-guided image editing models. VLM-based instruction synthesis exhibits systematic failures (orientation, viewpoint, and insufficient attribute detail) that yield unusable instructions for downstream training.
AI.method: Stage 1 builds a 100k supervised fine-tuning dataset by combining GLM automatic annotation, EditScore-based filtering, and human refinement for spatial, directional, and attribute-level accuracy. Stage 2 collects 10k human preference pairs targeting the three failure modes and applies direct preference optimization (DPO) for alignment beyond SFT.
AI.result: On Eval-400, ByteMorph-Bench, and HQ-Edit, fine-tuned Qwen3-VL models outperform open-source baselines; 235B model scores 4.712 on Eval-400 (vs. Gemini-3-Pro 4.706, GPT-4.1 4.220, Kimi-K2.5 4.111) and 4.588 on ByteMorph-Bench (vs. Gemini-3-Pro 4.522, GPT-4.1 3.412). Human evaluation shows critical errors drop from 47.75% to 23% and correctness rise from 41.75% to 66%.
AI.conclusion: The workflow provides a practical, scalable approach to human-aligned instruction synthesis for image editing data and yields data of higher quality for training instruction-guided editing models.

title: Zero-shot Multivariate Time Series Forecasting Using Tabular Prior Fitted Networks
summary: Tabular foundation models, particularly Prior-data Fitted Networks like TabPFN have emerged as the leading contender in a myriad of tasks ranging from data imputation to label prediction on the tabular data format surpassing the historical successes of tree-based models. This has led to investigations on their applicability to forecasting time series data which can be formulated as a tabular problem. While recent work to this end has displayed positive results, most works have limited their treatment of multivariate time series problems to several independent univariate time series forecasting subproblems, thus ignoring any inter-channel interactions. Overcoming this limitation, we introduce a generally applicable framework for multivariate time series forecasting using tabular foundation models. We achieve this by recasting the multivariate time series forecasting problem as a series of scalar regression problems which can then be solved zero-shot by any tabular foundation model with regression capabilities. We present results of our method using the TabPFN-TS backbone and compare performance with the current state of the art tabular methods.

AI.tldr: A framework for multivariate time series forecasting using tabular foundation models by reframing the problem as multiple scalar regression tasks solvable zero-shot; demonstrates competitive results against state-of-the-art tabular methods using the TabPFN-TS backbone.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitation of treating multivariate time series as independent univariate forecasts, which ignores inter-channel interactions; leverages powerful tabular foundation models in a zero-shot regression setting to handle multivariate data.
AI.method: Recast multivariate forecasting into a series of scalar regression problems. Use a TabPFN-TS backbone to solve these tasks in a zero-shot fashion, enabling end-to-end forecasting without per-dataset training. Compare against current state-of-the-art tabular methods.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation using the TabPFN-TS backbone shows competitive performance relative to existing tabular methods, validating the scalar-regression decomposition and the zero-shot approach.
AI.conclusion: The framework generalizes tabular foundation models to multivariate time series forecasting, effectively modeling inter-channel interactions in a zero-shot regime and achieving competitive results with the current top tabular methods.

title: Generalization Under Scrutiny: Cross-Domain Detection Progresses, Pitfalls, and Persistent Challenges
summary: Object detection models trained on a source domain often exhibit significant performance degradation when deployed in unseen target domains, due to various kinds of variations, such as sensing conditions, environments and data distributions. Hence, regardless the recent breakthrough advances in deep learning-based detection technology, cross-domain object detection (CDOD) remains a critical research area. Moreover, the existing literature remains fragmented, lacking a unified perspective on the structural challenges underlying domain shift and the effectiveness of adaptation strategies. This survey provides a comprehensive and systematic analysis of CDOD. We start upon a problem formulation that highlights the multi-stage nature of object detection under domain shift. Then, we organize the existing methods through a conceptual taxonomy that categorizes approaches based on adaptation paradigms, modeling assumptions, and pipeline components. Furthermore, we analyze how domain shift propagates across detection stages and discuss why adaptation in object detection is inherently more complex than in classification. In addition, we review commonly used datasets, evaluation protocols, and benchmarking practices. Finally, we identify the key challenges and outline promising future research directions. Cohesively, this survey aims to provide a unified framework for understanding CDOD and to guide the development of more robust detection systems.

AI.tldr: A survey that unifies cross-domain object detection (CDOD) by formulating the problem as a multi-stage learning task, proposing a taxonomy of adaptation methods, analyzing domain-shift propagation across detector stages, and outlining datasets, benchmarks, challenges, and future directions to guide robust CDOD systems.
AI.motivation: CDOD suffers significant performance degradation when models are applied to unseen domains due to sensing variations, environments, and data distribution shifts. The literature is fragmented across methods and lacks a cohesive framework to understand domain shift and adaptation effectiveness in detection, motivating a unified survey.
AI.method: Define a multi-stage problem formulation for object detection under domain shift. Develop a conceptual taxonomy that groups approaches by adaptation paradigms, modeling assumptions, and pipeline components. Analyze how domain shift propagates through detection stages and compare complexity with classification. Review standard datasets, evaluation protocols, and benchmarking practices. Identify key challenges and propose future research directions.
AI.result: A cohesive framework for CDOD is presented, including a taxonomy and analysis that unify disparate methods, clarify where shifts occur in the detector pipeline, and provide guidance on datasets and benchmarks. The survey highlights practical challenges and directions to improve robustness in cross-domain detection.
AI.conclusion: The work aims to unify CDOD understanding and steer future research toward more robust detection systems by addressing multi-stage domain shifts, clarifying adaptation strategies, and improving benchmarking practices.

title: Adversarial Label Invariant Graph Data Augmentations for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
comment: 21 pages, 3 figures, accepted at ICML SCIS 2023
summary: Out-of-distribution (OoD) generalization occurs when representation learning encounters a distribution shift. This occurs frequently in practice when training and testing data come from different environments. Covariate shift is a type of distribution shift that occurs only in the input data, while the concept distribution stays invariant. We propose RIA - Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training, a new method for OoD generalization under convariate shift. Motivated by an analogy to $Q$-learning, it performs an adversarial exploration for training data environments. These new environments are induced by adversarial label invariant data augmentations that prevent a collapse to an in-distribution trained learner. It works with many existing OoD generalization methods for covariate shift that can be formulated as constrained optimization problems. We develop an alternating gradient descent-ascent algorithm to solve the problem, and perform extensive experiments on OoD graph classification for various kinds of synthetic and natural distribution shifts. We demonstrate that our method can achieve high accuracy compared with OoD baselines.

AI.tldr: RIA introduces adversarial data-augmentation-based regularization to improve OoD generalization under covariate shift by creating diverse training environments; solvable via alternating gradient descent-ascent and compatible with constraint-based OoD methods; validated on graph classification with strong performance.
AI.motivation: OoD generalization under covariate shift is common in practice; existing methods often overfit to in-distribution statistics; there is a need to explicitly generate and regularize against diverse environments to encourage invariant representations.
AI.method: RIA (Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training) performs adversarial exploration of training environments via label-invariant data augmentations to prevent collapse to in-distribution learners; operates as a constrained-optimization-compatible addition to many OoD methods; solved by an alternating gradient descent–ascent algorithm.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OoD graph classification with synthetic and natural distribution shifts show that RIA achieves high accuracy compared with OoD baselines.
AI.conclusion: RIA effectively enhances OoD generalization under covariate shift by cultivating adversarially generated invariant environments; it is compatible with existing constraint-based OoD methods and is solved efficiently via alternating gradient descent–ascent; results indicate robust performance across diverse shifts.

title: $\oslash$ Source Models Leak What They Shouldn't $\nrightarrow$: Unlearning Zero-Shot Transfer in Domain Adaptation Through Adversarial Optimization
summary: The increasing adaptation of vision models across domains, such as satellite imagery and medical scans, has raised an emerging privacy risk: models may inadvertently retain and leak sensitive source-domain specific information in the target domain. This creates a compelling use case for machine unlearning to protect the privacy of sensitive source-domain data. Among adaptation techniques, source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) calls for an urgent need for machine unlearning (MU), where the source data itself is protected, yet the source model exposed during adaptation encodes its influence. Our experiments reveal that existing SFDA methods exhibit strong zero-shot performance on source-exclusive classes in the target domain, indicating they inadvertently leak knowledge of these classes into the target domain, even when they are not represented in the target data. We identify and address this risk by proposing an MU setting called SCADA-UL: Unlearning Source-exclusive ClAsses in Domain Adaptation. Existing MU methods do not address this setting as they are not designed to handle data distribution shifts. We propose a new unlearning method, where an adversarially generated forget class sample is unlearned by the model during the domain adaptation process using a novel rescaled labeling strategy and adversarial optimization. We also extend our study to two variants: a continual version of this problem setting and to one where the specific source classes to be forgotten may be unknown. Alongside theoretical interpretations, our comprehensive empirical results show that our method consistently outperforms baselines in the proposed setting while achieving retraining-level unlearning performance on benchmark datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/D-Arnav/SCADA

AI.tldr: A study on privacy risks in source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) where source-domain information leaks into target domain; introduces SCADA-UL for unlearning source-exclusive classes, using adversarial forget samples with rescaled labeling and optimization; extends to continual cases and unknown forgotten classes; reports superior unlearning performance vs baselines and retraining-level results; code released.
AI.motivation: To mitigate privacy leakage of source-domain knowledge in SFDA when source data cannot be accessed during adaptation. Demonstrates that existing SFDA methods leak source-exclusive classes into the target domain and proposes a dedicated unlearning framework (SCADA-UL) to forget specific source classes while preserving adaptation performance.
AI.method: Introduce SCADA-UL: an unlearning mechanism that uses adversarially generated forget-class samples and a novel rescaled labeling strategy integrated into the domain adaptation process. Employ adversarial optimization to ensure the model forgets the influence of forgotten source classes. Explore continual and unknown-forgotten-class variants; provide theoretical interpretations and empirical validation on benchmark datasets; open-source code.
AI.result: SCADA-UL consistently outperforms baseline unlearning methods in the proposed setting, achieving retraining-level unlearning performance on benchmarks, while retaining domain adaptation capabilities. The method is robust across continual and unknown-forgotten-class extensions.
AI.conclusion: Source-exclusive class information can leak into the target domain under SFDA. A tailored unlearning approach (SCADA-UL) with adversarial forget samples and rescaled labeling effectively removes the influence of forgotten source classes without sacrificing adaptation, offering strong privacy protections in SFDA scenarios.

title: DBMF: A Dual-Branch Multimodal Framework for Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: The complex and dynamic real-world clinical environment demands reliable deep learning (DL) systems. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection plays a critical role in enhancing the reliability and generalizability of DL models when encountering data that deviate from the training distribution, such as unseen disease cases. However, existing OOD detection methods typically rely either on a single visual modality or solely on image-text matching, failing to fully leverage multimodal information. To overcome the challenge, we propose a novel dual-branch multimodal framework by introducing a text-image branch and a vision branch. Our framework fully exploits multimodal representations to identify OOD samples through these two complementary branches. After training, we compute scores from the text-image branch ($S_t$) and vision branch ($S_v$), and integrate them to obtain the final OOD score $S$ that is compared with a threshold for OOD detection. Comprehensive experiments on publicly available endoscopic image datasets demonstrate that our proposed framework is robust across diverse backbones and improves state-of-the-art performance in OOD detection by up to 24.84%

AI.tldr: A dual-branch multimodal OOD detector for endoscopic images that fuses text-image and vision cues to improve robustness, achieving up to 24.84% AUC/accuracy gain over state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: OOD detection in clinical DL is prone to distribution shifts; single-modality methods miss multimodal cues; leveraging text and image jointly can enhance reliability and generalization.
AI.method: A dual-branch architecture with a text-image branch and a vision branch. Each branch produces an OOD score (S_t and S_v). The final OOD score S is a fusion of S_t and S_v, used with a threshold for detection. Evaluated on public endoscopic image datasets across various backbones.
AI.result: Demonstrates robustness across backbones and achieves up to 24.84% improvement over state-of-the-art OOD detection on publicly available endoscopic datasets.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal fusion of text and image representations enhances OOD detection reliability and generalizability in medical imaging; framework shows strong empirical gains; future work could examine additional modalities, calibration, and real-time deployment.

title: Provably Adaptive Linear Approximation for the Shapley Value and Beyond
summary: The Shapley value, and its broader family of semi-values, has received much attention in various attribution problems. A fundamental and long-standing challenge is their efficient approximation, since exact computation generally requires an exponential number of utility queries in the number of players $n$. To meet the challenges of large-scale applications, we explore the limits of efficiently approximating semi-values under a $Θ(n)$ space constraint. Building upon a vector concentration inequality, we establish a theoretical framework that enables sharper query complexities for existing unbiased randomized algorithms. Within this framework, we systematically develop a linear-space algorithm that requires $O(\frac{n}{ε^{2}}\log\frac{1}δ)$ utility queries to ensure $P(\|\hat{\boldsymbolφ}-\boldsymbolφ\|_{2}\geqε)\leq δ$ for all commonly used semi-values. In particular, our framework naturally bridges OFA, unbiased kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ and the regression-adjusted approach, and definitively characterizes when paired sampling is beneficial. Moreover, our algorithm allows explicit minimization of the mean square error for each specific utility function. Accordingly, we introduce the first adaptive, linear-time, linear-space randomized algorithm, Adalina, that theoretically achieves improved mean square error. All of our theoretical findings are experimentally validated.

AI.tldr: Efficient linear-space framework for approximating Shapley-like semi-values with provable error guarantees; introduces Adalina and a framework achieving near-optimal query complexity under Θ(n) space, unifying several SHAP variants.
AI.motivation: Shapley values are computationally expensive for large-scale problems. There is a need for memory-efficient, provably accurate approximation methods under strict space constraints.
AI.method: Develop a vector concentration-based theoretical framework; derive sharper query complexities for unbiased randomized algorithms; construct a linear-space algorithm with O(n/ε^2 log(1/δ)) utility queries to bound the L2 error; unify OFA, kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ, and regression-adjusted approaches; characterize when paired sampling is beneficial; introduce Adalina, an adaptive, linear-time, linear-space randomized algorithm with improved mean square error; validate theoretically and experimentally.
AI.result: The framework yields sharper query complexity bounds for existing unbiased randomized SHAP-type estimators; provides a mechanism to minimize MSE for specific utility functions; Adalina achieves improved MSE in practice; empirical results support the theory.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a scalable, memory-efficient approach to approximate semi-values with strong theoretical guarantees, bridging multiple SHAP variants and enabling tailored error minimization; it demonstrates practical feasibility through experiments and introduces an adaptive algorithm for better accuracy.

title: Orion-Lite: Distilling LLM Reasoning into Efficient Vision-Only Driving Models
summary: Leveraging the general world knowledge of Large Language Models (LLMs) holds significant promise for improving the ability of autonomous driving systems to handle rare and complex scenarios. While integrating LLMs into Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models has yielded state-of-the-art performance, their massive parameter counts pose severe challenges for latency-sensitive and energy-efficient deployment. Distilling LLM knowledge into a compact driving model offers a compelling solution to retain these reasoning capabilities while maintaining a manageable computational footprint. Although previous works have demonstrated the efficacy of distillation, these efforts have primarily focused on relatively simple scenarios and open-loop evaluations. Therefore, in this work, we investigate LLM distillation in more complex, interactive scenarios under closed-loop evaluation. We demonstrate that through a combination of latent feature distillation and ground-truth trajectory supervision, an efficient vision-only student model \textbf{Orion-Lite} can even surpass the performance of its massive VLA teacher, ORION. Setting a new state-of-the-art on the rigorous Bench2Drive benchmark, with a Driving Score of 80.6. Ultimately, this reveals that vision-only architectures still possess significant, untapped potential for high-performance reactive planning.

AI.tldr: A compact vision-only student model Orion-Lite distills knowledge from a large VLA teacher (ORION) to retain LLM-driven reasoning in complex, closed-loop driving, achieving state-of-the-art Bench2Drive Driving Score 80.6 and surpassing the teacher.
AI.motivation: LLMs can provide rich world knowledge and reasoning for autonomous driving, but their size makes deployment expensive. Distilling LLM knowledge into a compact, vision-only model aims to preserve reasoning capabilities while reducing latency and energy usage, enabling robust, reactive planning in complex scenarios.
AI.method: Combine latent feature distillation from the VLA teacher with ground-truth trajectory supervision to train a vision-only student (Orion-Lite) in a closed-loop setting, evaluating against the VLA teacher (ORION) on Bench2Drive.
AI.result: Orion-Lite achieves a Driving Score of 80.6 on Bench2Drive, setting a new state-of-the-art and surpassing the teacher ORION, demonstrating that vision-only models can retain strong reactive planning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation from large LLM-enabled VLA models into compact vision-only architectures can retain high-level reasoning for interactive driving tasks, enabling efficient deployment without sacrificing performance; invites further exploration of distillation strategies and closed-loop evaluations.

title: Less Approximates More: Harmonizing Performance and Confidence Faithfulness via Hybrid Post-Training for High-Stakes Tasks
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes tasks, where confident yet incorrect inferences may cause severe real-world harm, bringing the previously overlooked issue of confidence faithfulness back to the forefront. A promising solution is to jointly optimize unsupervised Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) with reasoning-trace-guided Reasoning Distillation (RD), which may face three persistent challenges: scarcity of high-quality training corpora, factually unwarranted overconfidence and indiscriminate fusion that amplifies erroneous updates. Inspired by the human confidence accumulation from uncertainty to certainty, we propose Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to measure whether reasoning steps progressively strengthen support for the final answer. Furthermore, we introduce HyTuning, a hybrid post-training framework that adaptively reweights RD and RLIF via a PRG-style metric, using scarce supervised reasoning traces as a stable anchor while exploiting abundant unlabeled queries for scalability. Experiments on several domain-specific and general benchmarks demonstrate that HyTuning improves accuracy while achieving confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, supporting a practical "Less Approximates More" effect.

AI.tldr: Hybrid post-training framework HyTuning combines RLIF and RD with a PRG-based metric to improve confidence-faithful reasoning under scarce supervision.
AI.motivation: Confidence faithfulness in large language models for high-stakes tasks, tackling scarce high-quality training data, overconfidence, and risky fusion of updates.
AI.method: Introduce Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to track stepwise strengthening of reasoning for the final answer. Propose HyTuning, a hybrid post-training framework that reweights RD and RLIF using PRG-like signals, anchored on scarce supervised reasoning traces while leveraging abundant unlabeled queries for scalability.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on domain-specific and general benchmarks shows improved accuracy and confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, supporting a 'Less Approximates More' effect.
AI.conclusion: PRG-driven HyTuning provides a practical, scalable approach to jointly optimize internal feedback RLIF and reasoning distillation RD, achieving better performance and faithfulness with scarce supervision.

title: Preventing Overfitting in Deep Image Prior for Hyperspectral Image Denoising
summary: Deep image prior (DIP) is an unsupervised deep learning framework that has been successfully applied to a variety of inverse imaging problems. However, DIP-based methods are inherently prone to overfitting, which leads to performance degradation and necessitates early stopping. In this paper, we propose a method to mitigate overfitting in DIP-based hyperspectral image (HSI) denoising by jointly combining robust data fidelity and explicit sensitivity regularization. The proposed approach employs a Smooth $\ell_1$ data term together with a divergence-based regularization and input optimization during training. Experimental results on real HSIs corrupted by Gaussian, sparse, and stripe noise demonstrate that the proposed method effectively prevents overfitting and achieves superior denoising performance compared to state-of-the-art DIP-based HSI denoising methods.

AI.tldr: DIP-based HSI denoising with robust data fidelity and sensitivity regularization to curb overfitting, achieving improved denoising across various noise types.
AI.motivation: DIP-based unsupervised denoising tends to overfit, degrading performance; hyperspectral data require robust regularization to preserve spectral fidelity while removing noise.
AI.method: Combine a robust Smooth L1 data term with a divergence-based regularization term and perform input optimization during training to constrain the solution.
AI.result: On real hyperspectral images corrupted by Gaussian, sparse, and stripe noise, the approach mitigates overfitting and achieves superior denoising compared to state-of-the-art DIP-based HSI denoising methods.
AI.conclusion: Jointly enforcing robust data fidelity, divergence-based regularization, and input optimization improves DIP-based HSI denoising, reducing overfitting and enhancing denoising across diverse noise types.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Turbofan Health Estimation via Inverse Problem Formulation
summary: Estimating the health state of turbofan engines is a challenging ill-posed inverse problem, hindered by sparse sensing and complex nonlinear thermodynamics. Research in this area remains fragmented, with comparisons limited by the use of unrealistic datasets and insufficient exploration of the exploitation of temporal information. This work investigates how to recover component-level health indicators from operational sensor data under realistic degradation and maintenance patterns. To support this study, we introduce a new dataset that incorporates industry-oriented complexities such as maintenance events and usage changes. Using this dataset, we establish an initial benchmark that compares steady-state and nonstationary data-driven models, and Bayesian filters, classic families of methods used to solve this problem. In addition to this benchmark, we introduce self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches that learn latent representations without access to true health labels, a scenario reflective of real-world operational constraints. By comparing the downstream estimation performance of these unsupervised representations against the direct prediction baselines, we establish a practical lower bound on the difficulty of solving this inverse problem. Our results reveal that traditional filters remain strong baselines, while SSL methods reveal the intrinsic complexity of health estimation and highlight the need for more advanced and interpretable inference strategies. For reproducibility, both the generated dataset and the implementation used in this work are made accessible.

AI.tldr: New industry-grade turbofan health-estimation dataset with maintenance/usage changes; benchmarks show traditional Bayesian filters strong; SSL reveals problem complexity; reproducible data and code.
AI.motivation: Address ill-posed health-state estimation with sparse sensors under realistic degradation and maintenance patterns; bridge fragmented literature by providing fair baselines and exploiting temporal information; enable reproducibility.
AI.method: Create dataset with industry-oriented complexities (maintenance events, usage changes); benchmark steady-state and nonstationary data-driven models and Bayesian filters; introduce self-supervised learning (SSL) to learn latent representations without health labels; compare.ssl representations to direct prediction baselines.
AI.result: Traditional filters remain strong baselines; SSL methods reveal intrinsic complexity of health estimation and the need for more advanced and interpretable inference; establish a practical lower bound on problem difficulty; dataset and implementation are released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Highlights that further work is needed to develop interpretable, temporally-aware inference strategies; provides resources to support future research and fair comparisons.

title: Revisiting Radar Perception With Spectral Point Clouds
summary: Radar perception models are trained with different inputs, from range-Doppler spectra to sparse point clouds. Dense spectra are assumed to outperform sparse point clouds, yet they can vary considerably across sensors and configurations, which hinders transfer. In this paper, we provide alternatives for incorporating spectral information into radar point clouds and show that, point clouds need not underperform compared to spectra. We introduce the spectral point cloud paradigm, where point clouds are treated as sparse, compressed representations of the radar spectra, and argue that, when enriched with spectral information, they serve as strong candidates for a unified input representation that is more robust against sensor-specific differences. We develop an experimental framework that compares spectral point cloud (PC) models at varying densities against a dense range-Doppler (RD) benchmark, and report the density levels where the PC configurations meet the performance of the RD benchmark. Furthermore, we experiment with two basic spectral enrichment approaches, that inject additional target-relevant information into the point clouds. Contrary to the common belief that the dense RD approach is superior, we show that point clouds can do just as well, and can surpass the RD benchmark when enrichment is applied. Spectral point clouds can therefore serve as strong candidates for unified radar perception, paving the way for future radar foundation models.

AI.tldr: Spectral point clouds encode spectral info into sparse point clouds to create a robust, unified radar input; at sufficient density and with enrichment, they match or surpass dense RD performance.
AI.motivation: Dense spectra provide strong performance but vary across sensors, hindering transfer; a unified input representation robust to sensor differences is desired.
AI.method: Introduce spectral point cloud paradigm; treat point clouds as sparse, compressed representations of radar spectra; develop experimental framework comparing spectral PC models at varying densities to dense RD; implement two simple spectral enrichment approaches.
AI.result: PCs at certain densities can match the RD benchmark; enrichment can surpass RD; spectral PCs offer robust unified input; framework demonstrates density thresholds for parity.
AI.conclusion: Spectral point clouds are strong candidates for unified radar perception and future radar foundation models; enrichment enhances performance and transferability.

title: TTVS: Boosting Self-Exploring Reinforcement Learning via Test-time Variational Synthesis
summary: Despite significant advances in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) driven by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), this paradigm is fundamentally limited in specialized or novel domains where such supervision is prohibitively expensive or unavailable, posing a key challenge for test-time adaptation. While existing test-time methods offer a potential solution, they are constrained by learning from static query sets, risking overfitting to textual patterns. To address this gap, we introduce Test-Time Variational Synthesis (TTVS), a novel framework that enables LRMs to self-evolve by dynamically augmenting the training stream from unlabeled test queries. TTVS comprises two synergistic modules: (1) Online Variational Synthesis, which transforms static test queries into a dynamic stream of diverse, semantically-equivalent variations, enforcing the model to learn underlying problem logic rather than superficial patterns; (2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration, which balances accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across synthetic variants. Extensive experiments show TTVS yields superior performance across eight model architectures. Notably, using only unlabeled test-time data, TTVS not only surpasses other test-time adaptation methods but also outperforms state-of-the-art supervised RL-based techniques trained on vast, high-quality labeled data.

AI.tldr: TTVS introduces Online Variational Synthesis and Test-time Hybrid Exploration to let large reasoning models self-evolve using unlabeled test queries, by creating diverse, semantically-equivalent variants and balancing exploitation with exploration. It claims superior results across eight architectures, outperforming both static-test-time methods and supervised RL-based approaches trained on labeled data.
AI.motivation: RL-based large reasoning models with verifiable rewards struggle in domains with scarce or expensive supervision; test-time adaptation that learns from static query sets risks overfitting and fails to generalize to novel queries.
AI.method: Two-module framework: (1) Online Variational Synthesis transforms static test queries into a dynamic stream of diverse, semantically-equivalent variants to force the model to learn underlying problem logic; (2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration trades off accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across the synthetic variants to improve robustness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across eight model architectures shows that TTVS achieves superior performance using only unlabeled test-time data, surpassing other test-time adaptation methods and even beating state-of-the-art supervised RL-based methods trained on large labeled datasets.
AI.conclusion: TTVS enables LRMs to self-evolve at test time by leveraging unlabeled test data, achieving strong generalization across architectures and competitive performance with or without reliance on large labeled RL supervision; prompts potential broader applicability and indicates directions for further analysis of synthetic variation quality and computational considerations.

title: CAMotion: A High-Quality Benchmark for Camouflaged Moving Object Detection in the Wild
summary: Discovering camouflaged objects is a challenging task in computer vision due to the high similarity between camouflaged objects and their surroundings. While the problem of camouflaged object detection over sequential video frames has received increasing attention, the scale and diversity of existing video camouflaged object detection (VCOD) datasets are greatly limited, which hinders the deeper analysis and broader evaluation of recent deep learning-based algorithms with data-hungry training strategy. To break this bottleneck, in this paper, we construct CAMotion, a high-quality benchmark covers a wide range of species for camouflaged moving object detection in the wild. CAMotion comprises various sequences with multiple challenging attributes such as uncertain edge, occlusion, motion blur, and shape complexity, etc. The sequence annotation details and statistical distribution are presented from various perspectives, allowing CAMotion to provide in-depth analyses on the camouflaged object's motion characteristics in different challenging scenarios. Additionally, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of existing SOTA models on CAMotion, and discuss the major challenges in VCOD task. The benchmark is available at https://www.camotion.focuslab.net.cn, we hope that our CAMotion can lead to further advancements in the research community.

AI.tldr: CAMotion: a high-quality, diverse video benchmark for camouflaged moving object detection (VCOD) to enable robust evaluation and analysis of SOTA models.
AI.motivation: Current VCOD datasets are limited in scale and diversity, constraining evaluation and learning for data-hungry deep models.
AI.method: Build CAMotion with multi-species video sequences featuring challenging attributes (uncertain edges, occlusion, motion blur, shape complexity), provide detailed sequence annotations and statistics, and benchmark existing SOTA models.
AI.result: CAMotion offers richly annotated, diverse video data; comprehensive evaluation of SOTA methods highlighting key challenges and the dataset's analytical value.
AI.conclusion: CAMotion provides a valuable resource for advancing VCOD research and enabling in-depth motion-characteristic analyses; dataset available at the project site.

title: Persistence-Augmented Neural Networks
summary: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides tools to describe the shape of data, but integrating topological features into deep learning pipelines remains challenging, especially when preserving local geometric structure rather than summarizing it globally. We propose a persistence-based data augmentation framework that encodes local gradient flow regions and their hierarchical evolution using the Morse-Smale complex. This representation, compatible with both convolutional and graph neural networks, retains spatially localized topological information across multiple scales. Importantly, the augmentation procedure itself is efficient, with computational complexity $O(n \log n)$, making it practical for large datasets. We evaluate our method on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression, where it consistently outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors such as persistence images and landscapes. We also show that pruning the base level of the hierarchy reduces memory usage while maintaining competitive performance. These results highlight the potential of local, structured topological augmentation for scalable and interpretable learning across data modalities.

AI.tldr: A persistence-based data augmentation using Morse-Smale complexes encodes local gradient-flow regions across multiple scales, compatible with CNNs and GNNs, withO(n log n) complexity; it improves histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression over global TDA descriptors, and memory-efficient pruning preserves performance.
AI.motivation: Integrate localized topological information into deep learning pipelines while preserving local geometry and maintaining scalability; address limitations of global TDA summaries in preserving spatial structure.
AI.method: Construct Morse-Smale complexes to identify and encode local gradient-flow regions and their hierarchical evolution. Generate augmented representations that retain spatially localized topological features and are compatible with CNNs and GNNs. Achieve computational efficiency with O(n log n) complexity. Explore pruning of the base hierarchy to reduce memory while monitoring performance.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors (e.g., persistence images, landscapes) on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression. Pruning the base level reduces memory usage with competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: Local, structured topological augmentation provides scalable, interpretable learning across data modalities, effectively preserving local topology across scales and benefiting diverse tasks.

title: Can Vision Language Models Judge Action Quality? An Empirical Evaluation
summary: Action Quality Assessment (AQA) has broad applications in physical therapy, sports coaching, and competitive judging. Although Vision Language Models (VLMs) hold considerable promise for AQA, their actual performance in this domain remains largely uncharacterised. We present a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art VLMs across activity domains (e.g. fitness, figure skating, diving), tasks, representations, and prompting strategies. Baseline results reveal that Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-VL and InternVL3.5 models perform only marginally above random chance, and although strategies such as incorporation of skeleton information, grounding instructions, reasoning structures and in-context learning lead to isolated gains, none is consistently effective. Analysis of prediction distributions uncovers two systematic biases: a tendency to predict correct execution regardless of visual evidence, and a sensitivity to superficial linguistic framing. Reformulating tasks contrastively to mitigate these biases yields minimal improvement, suggesting that the models' limitations go beyond these biases, pointing to a fundamental difficulty with fine-grained movement quality assessment. Our findings establish a rigorous baseline for future VLM-based AQA research and provide an actionable outline for failure modes requiring mitigation prior to reliable real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: State-of-the-art vision-language models show limited ability for fine-grained action quality assessment across domains; performance near random; few strategies provide consistent gains; biases identified; reformulations offer little help; establishes a baseline and failure modes for future AQA research.
AI.motivation: Understand whether current VLMs can perform Action Quality Assessment reliably and safely, identify failure modes to guide future research and deployment decisions.
AI.method: Comprehensive evaluation of multiple top VLMs (e.g., Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-VL, InternVL3.5) across domains like fitness, figure skating, and diving; test different representations and prompting strategies (skeleton info, grounding instructions, reasoning structures, in-context learning); analyze output distributions and perform contrastive reformulations to mitigate biases.
AI.result: Models perform only marginally above random; isolated gains from certain strategies but no consistent improvement; two systematic prediction biases detected (over-predicting correct execution regardless of evidence; sensitivity to linguistic framing); contrastive reformulations yield minimal improvement; indicates a fundamental difficulty in fine-grained movement quality assessment.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous baseline for VLM-based AQA; outlines failure modes requiring mitigation before real-world deployment; directs future research toward addressing core limitations beyond surface biases.

title: Quantization Impact on the Accuracy and Communication Efficiency Trade-off in Federated Learning for Aerospace Predictive Maintenance
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving predictive maintenance across distributed aerospace fleets, but gradient communication overhead constrains deployment on bandwidth-limited IoT nodes. This paper investigates the impact of symmetric uniform quantization ($b \in \{32,8,4,2\}$ bits) on the accuracy--efficiency trade-off of a custom-designed lightweight 1-D convolutional model (AeroConv1D, 9\,697 parameters) trained via FL on the NASA C-MAPSS benchmark under a realistic Non-IID client partition. Using a rigorous multi-seed evaluation ($N=10$ seeds), we show that INT4 achieves accuracy \emph{statistically indistinguishable} from FP32 on both FD001 ($p=0.341$) and FD002 ($p=0.264$ MAE, $p=0.534$ NASA score) while delivering an $8\times$ reduction in gradient communication cost (37.88~KiB $\to$ 4.73~KiB per round). A key methodological finding is that naïve IID client partitioning artificially suppresses variance; correct Non-IID evaluation reveals the true operational instability of extreme quantization, demonstrated via a direct empirical IID vs.\ Non-IID comparison. INT2 is empirically characterized as unsuitable: while it achieves lower MAE on FD002 through extreme quantization-induced over-regularization, this apparent gain is accompanied by catastrophic NASA score instability (CV\,=\,45.8\% vs.\ 22.3\% for FP32), confirming non-reproducibility under heterogeneous operating conditions. Analytical FPGA resource projections on the Xilinx ZCU102 confirm that INT4 fits within hardware constraints (85.5\% DSP utilization), potentially enabling a complete FL pipeline on a single SoC. The full simulation codebase and FPGA estimation scripts are publicly available at https://github.com/therealdeadbeef/aerospace-fl-quantization.

AI.tldr: INT4 symmetric uniform quantization in federated learning for AeroConv1D on NASA C-MAPSS yields accuracy indistinguishable from FP32 with 8x gradient compression; INT2 is unstable; Non-IID evaluation is crucial; INT4 feasible on FPGA; code available.
AI.motivation: Reduce inter-client gradient communication in privacy-preserving FL for aerospace predictive maintenance while preserving accuracy under realistic non-IID heterogeneity.
AI.method: Quantize gradients with bit-widths {32,8,4,2} in FL using AeroConv1D (9,697 params) on NASA C-MAPSS FD001/FD002 with non-IID client partitions; multi-seed evaluation (N=10). Assess MAE and NASA score; compare IID vs Non-IID; perform FPGA resource estimation on Xilinx ZCU102; provide open-source code.
AI.result: INT4 matches FP32 accuracy on FD001/FD002 (p-values: 0.341 and 0.264 for MAE; 0.534 NASA score) and reduces gradient communication from 37.88 KiB to 4.73 KiB per round (~8x). Non-IID evaluation reveals true instability of extreme quantization; naïve IID partitioning underestimates variance. INT2 is not suitable: over-regularization yields lower MAE on FD002 but high NASA score instability (CV 45.8% vs 22.3% FP32). FPGA estimation for ZCU102 shows INT4 within hardware constraints (DSP utilization 85.5%). Codebase publicly available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Low-bit quantization, especially INT4, enables efficient FL for aerospace predictive maintenance without sacrificing accuracy under realistic non-IID conditions, with feasible FPGA deployment. Emphasizes the necessity of proper Non-IID evaluation to reveal quantization-induced instability; INT2 is unsuitable despite potential localized gains.

title: GroundingAnomaly: Spatially-Grounded Diffusion for Few-Shot Anomaly Synthesis
summary: The performance of visual anomaly inspection in industrial quality control is often constrained by the scarcity of real anomalous samples. Consequently, anomaly synthesis techniques have been developed to enlarge training sets and enhance downstream inspection. However, existing methods either suffer from poor integration caused by inpainting or fail to provide accurate masks. To address these limitations, we propose GroundingAnomaly, a novel few-shot anomaly image generation framework. Our framework introduces a Spatial Conditioning Module that leverages per-pixel semantic maps to enable precise spatial control over the synthesized anomalies. Furthermore, a Gated Self-Attention Module is designed to inject conditioning tokens into a frozen U-Net via gated attention layers. This carefully preserves pretrained priors while ensuring stable few-shot adaptation. Extensive evaluations on the MVTec AD and VisA datasets demonstrate that GroundingAnomaly generates high-quality anomalies and achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple downstream tasks, including anomaly detection, segmentation, and instance-level detection.

AI.tldr: GroundingAnomaly is a few-shot anomaly image generation framework with Spatial Conditioning and Gated Self-Attention, enabling precise per-pixel control and stable adaptation on a frozen U-Net; it achieves state-of-the-art results on MVTec AD and VisA across detection, segmentation, and instance-level tasks.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of real anomalous samples in industrial quality control leads to weak anomaly detectors. Existing synthesis methods struggle with integration (inpainting) or provide poor masks, limiting downstream performance.
AI.method: Introduce Spatial Conditioning Module that uses per-pixel semantic maps for precise spatial control over synthesized anomalies. Add a Gated Self-Attention Module to inject conditioning tokens into a frozen U-Net via gated attention, preserving pretrained priors and enabling stable few-shot adaptation.
AI.result: GroundingAnomaly yields high-quality synthetic anomalies and achieves state-of-the-art performance on downstream tasks—anomaly detection, segmentation, and instance-level detection—on the MVTec AD and VisA datasets.
AI.conclusion: GroundingAnomaly provides effective, controllable few-shot anomaly synthesis with preserved pretrained knowledge, improving downstream industrial defect inspection tasks.

title: The Impact of Dimensionality on the Stability of Node Embeddings
summary: Previous work has established that neural network-based node embeddings return different outcomes when trained with identical parameters on the same dataset, just from using different training seeds. Yet, it has not been thoroughly analyzed how key hyperparameters such as embedding dimension could impact this instability. In this work, we investigate how varying the dimensionality of node embeddings influences both their stability and downstream performance. We systematically evaluate five widely used methods -- ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, and VERSE -- across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions. We assess stability from both a representational perspective and a functional perspective, alongside performance evaluation. Our results show that embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality, but we observe different patterns across the methods we consider: while some approaches, such as node2vec and ASNE, tend to become more stable with higher dimensionality, other methods do not exhibit the same trend. Moreover, we find that maximum stability does not necessarily align with optimal task performance. These findings highlight the importance of carefully selecting embedding dimension, and provide new insights into the trade-offs between stability, performance, and computational effectiveness in graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: Embedding dimensionality affects stability and downstream performance; different graph embedding methods show distinct stability trends with dimension, and maximum stability does not always align with best task performance.
AI.motivation: To understand how the embedding dimension of node representations influences both stability (consistency across seeds) and downstream task performance in graph embedding methods.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of five popular graph embedding methods (ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, VERSE) across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions; assess stability from both representational and functional perspectives and measure downstream performance.
AI.result: Stability varies with dimensionality; some methods (node2vec, ASNE) become more stable at higher dimensions, others show no clear trend; maximum stability does not coincide with peak task performance; stability-performance trade-offs are method-dependent and dimension-sensitive.
AI.conclusion: Embedding dimension is a crucial hyperparameter that influences stability and performance in a non-uniform, method-dependent manner; practitioners should balance stability gains against potential performance loss and computational costs when selecting dimensionality.

title: Weakly-Supervised Lung Nodule Segmentation via Training-Free Guidance of 3D Rectified Flow
summary: Dense annotations, such as segmentation masks, are expensive and time-consuming to obtain, especially for 3D medical images where expert voxel-wise labeling is required. Weakly supervised approaches aim to address this limitation, but often rely on attribution-based methods that struggle to accurately capture small structures such as lung nodules. In this paper, we propose a weakly-supervised segmentation method for lung nodules by combining pretrained state-of-the-art rectified flow and predictor models in a plug-and-play manner. Our approach uses training-free guidance of a 3D rectified flow model, requiring only fine-tuning of the predictor using image-level labels and no retraining of the generative model. The proposed method produces improved-quality segmentations for two separate predictors, consistently detecting lung nodules of varying size and shapes. Experiments on LUNA16 demonstrate improvements over baseline methods, highlighting the potential of generative foundation models as tools for weakly supervised 3D medical image segmentation.
