AI.tldr: A weakly supervised 3D lung nodule segmentation method that fuses pretrained rectified flow with predictor models in a plug-and-play setup. It uses training-free guidance from the 3D rectified flow and only fine-tunes predictors with image-level labels, avoiding retraining the generative model.
AI.motivation: Dense voxel-wise annotations for 3D medical images are costly. Existing attribution-based weak supervision struggles with small structures like nodules; a generative foundation-model-driven approach could provide better weak supervision.
AI.method: Combine pretrained 3D rectified flow with predictor models in a plug-and-play fashion. Use training-free guidance from the rectified flow and fine-tune predictors with image-level labels; do not retrain the generative model. Validate with two predictors on LUNA16.
AI.result: Achieves improved segmentation quality compared to baselines for both predictors, consistently detecting nodules across varying sizes and shapes. Demonstrated on LUNA16 data.
AI.conclusion: Generative foundation models can serve as effective tools for weakly supervised 3D medical image segmentation, enabling improved segmentations without retraining the generative component.

title: What Drives Representation Steering? A Mechanistic Case Study on Steering Refusal
summary: Applying steering vectors to large language models (LLMs) is an efficient and effective model alignment technique, but we lack an interpretable explanation for how it works-- specifically, what internal mechanisms steering vectors affect and how this results in different model outputs. To investigate the causal mechanisms underlying the effectiveness of steering vectors, we conduct a comprehensive case study on refusal. We propose a multi-token activation patching framework and discover that different steering methodologies leverage functionally interchangeable circuits when applied at the same layer. These circuits reveal that steering vectors primarily interact with the attention mechanism through the OV circuit while largely ignoring the QK circuit-- freezing all attention scores during steering drops performance by only 8.75% across two model families. A mathematical decomposition of the steered OV circuit further reveals semantically interpretable concepts, even in cases where the steering vector itself does not. Leveraging the activation patching results, we show that steering vectors can be sparsified by up to 90-99% while retaining most performance, and that different steering methodologies agree on a subset of important dimensions.

AI.tldr: Steering vectors primarily manipulate the attention mechanism via the OV (Output Value) circuit, enabling interpretable, sparse activations and cross-method equivalence across layers; they can be heavily compressed with minimal performance loss while maintaining consistent important dimensions.
AI.motivation: To uncover the causal mechanisms by which steering vectors influence LLM outputs, addressing the interpretability gap in model alignment techniques.
AI.method: Multi-token activation patching to trace circuit-level effects, a case study on refusal; analysis of functionally interchangeable circuits across steering methods at the same layer; assessment of OV vs QK circuitry; a mathematical decomposition of the steered OV circuit; sparsification experiments (reducing activations by 90–99%); cross-method agreement on important dimensions.
AI.result: Steering vectors predominantly engage the attention OV circuit, largely ignoring the QK circuit; freezing attention scores reduces performance by only 8.75% across two model families; OV circuit decomposition yields semantically interpretable concepts even when steering vectors lack direct semantic content; activation patching enables sparsification by 90–99% while preserving most performance; different steering methods converge on a subset of important dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Steering vectors reveal interpretable, transferable circuit-level effects within the attention mechanism, enabling substantial activation sparsity and cross-method agreement, thereby supporting more efficient and explainable model alignment.

title: Fundus-R1: Training a Fundus-Reading MLLM with Knowledge-Aware Reasoning on Public Data
summary: Fundus imaging such as CFP, OCT and UWF is crucial for the early detection of retinal anomalies and diseases. Fundus image understanding, due to its knowledge-intensive nature, poses a challenging vision-language task. An emerging approach to addressing the task is to post-train a generic multimodal large language model (MLLM), either by supervised finetuning (SFT) or by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), on a considerable amount of in-house samples paired with high-quality clinical reports. However, these valuable samples are not publicly accessible, which not only hinders reproducibility but also practically limits research to few players. To overcome the barrier, we make a novel attempt to train a reasoning-enhanced fundus-reading MLLM, which we term Fundus-R1, using exclusively public datasets, wherein over 94\% of the data are annotated with only image-level labels. Our technical contributions are two-fold. First, we propose a RAG-based method for composing image-specific, knowledge-aware reasoning traces. Such auto-generated traces link visual findings identified by a generic MLLM to the image labels in terms of ophthalmic knowledge. Second, we enhance RLVR with a process reward that encourages self-consistency of the generated reasoning trace in each rollout. Extensive experiments on three fundus-reading benchmarks, i.e., FunBench, Omni-Fundus and GMAI-Fundus, show that Fundus-R1 clearly outperforms multiple baselines, including its generic counterpart (Qwen2.5-VL) and a stronger edition post-trained without using the generated traces. This work paves the way for training powerful fundus-reading MLLMs with publicly available data.

AI.tldr: A publicly trained, reasoning-enhanced fundus-reading MLLM (Fundus-R1) using RAG-based reasoning traces and process-reward RLVR, achieving state-of-the-art on three public benchmarks with image-level labels.
AI.motivation: Public fundus-vL model research is hindered by reliance on private in-house paired data; there is a need for reproducible, knowledge-intensive models trained on publicly available datasets.
AI.method: Two main components: (i) a RAG-based method to compose image-specific, knowledge-aware reasoning traces linking visual findings to image labels using ophthalmic knowledge; (ii) an RLVR framework augmented with a process reward to promote self-consistency of the generated reasoning traces during rollouts. Training uses public datasets with image-level labels and benchmarks FunBench, Omni-Fundus, GMAI-Fundus.
AI.result: Fundus-R1 outperforms multiple baselines, including the generic Qwen2.5-VL and a stronger edition post-trained without the generated traces, on three fundus-reading benchmarks (FunBench, Omni-Fundus, GMAI-Fundus).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of training powerful fundus-reading MLLMs with publicly available data, improves reproducibility, and provides a practical approach combining knowledge-aware traces with RLVR for ophthalmic vision-language tasks.

title: Meta-learning In-Context Enables Training-Free Cross Subject Brain Decoding
summary: Visual decoding from brain signals is a key challenge at the intersection of computer vision and neuroscience, requiring methods that bridge neural representations and computational models of vision. A field-wide goal is to achieve generalizable, cross-subject models. A major obstacle towards this goal is the substantial variability in neural representations across individuals, which has so far required training bespoke models or fine-tuning separately for each subject. To address this challenge, we introduce a meta-optimized approach for semantic visual decoding from fMRI that generalizes to novel subjects without any fine-tuning. By simply conditioning on a small set of image-brain activation examples from the new individual, our model rapidly infers their unique neural encoding patterns to facilitate robust and efficient visual decoding. Our approach is explicitly optimized for in-context learning of the new subject's encoding model and performs decoding by hierarchical inference, inverting the encoder. First, for multiple brain regions, we estimate the per-voxel visual response encoder parameters by constructing a context over multiple stimuli and responses. Second, we construct a context consisting of encoder parameters and response values over multiple voxels to perform aggregated functional inversion. We demonstrate strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization across diverse visual backbones without retraining or fine-tuning. Moreover, our approach requires neither anatomical alignment nor stimulus overlap. This work is a critical step towards a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding.

AI.tldr: Meta-optimized in-context learning approach enables cross-subject and cross-scanner visual decoding from fMRI without fine-tuning by quickly inferring subject-specific encoders from a small set of image-brain examples and performing hierarchical, inverted decoding.
AI.motivation: Cross-subject neural representation variability impedes generalizable brain decoding; existing solutions require subject-specific retraining or alignment, limiting scalability and robustness across scanners.
AI.method: Two-stage in-context learning: (1) estimate per-voxel encoder parameters across multiple stimuli to form a subject-specific encoding context across brain regions; (2) build a context of encoder parameters and voxel responses to perform aggregated functional inversion, decoding by inverting the encoder, without retraining or alignment. Demonstrates robustness across diverse visual backbones and scan conditions.
AI.result: Strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization without retraining or fine-tuning across diverse visual backbones; no anatomical alignment or stimulus overlap required; shows viability of a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding.
AI.conclusion: Indicates a feasible path toward a generalizable, foundation-model-like framework for fMRI-based visual decoding that reduces subject-specific customization and enables rapid adaptation to new individuals.

title: Lost in the Hype: Revealing and Dissecting the Performance Degradation of Medical Multimodal Large Language Models in Image Classification
summary: The rise of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has sparked an unprecedented wave of applications in the field of medical imaging analysis. However, as one of the earliest and most fundamental tasks integrated into this paradigm, medical image classification reveals a sobering reality: state-of-the-art medical MLLMs consistently underperform compared to traditional deep learning models, despite their overwhelming advantages in pre-training data and model parameters. This paradox prompts a critical rethinking: where exactly does the performance degradation originate? In this paper, we conduct extensive experiments on 14 open-source medical MLLMs across three representative image classification datasets. Moving beyond superficial performance benchmarking, we employ feature probing to track the information flow of visual features module-by-module and layer-by-layer throughout the entire MLLM pipeline, enabling explicit visualization of where and how classification signals are distorted, diluted, or overridden. As the first attempt to dissect classification performance degradation in medical MLLMs, our findings reveal four failure modes: 1) quality limitation in visual representation, 2) fidelity loss in connector projection, 3) comprehension deficit in LLM reasoning, and 4) misalignment of semantic mapping. Meanwhile, we introduce quantitative scores that characterize the healthiness of feature evolution, enabling principled comparisons across diverse MLLMs and datasets. Furthermore, we provide insightful discussions centered on the critical barriers that prevent current medical MLLMs from fulfilling their promised clinical potential. We hope that our work provokes rethinking within the community-highlighting that the road from high expectations to clinically deployable MLLMs remains long and winding.

AI.tldr: Four failure modes explain why medical MLLMs lag behind traditional models: (1) visual representation quality, (2) projection fidelity, (3) LLM reasoning comprehension, (4) semantic mapping misalignment; plus proposed quantitative 'healthiness' scores for feature evolution to enable cross-model/dataset comparisons; calls for rethinking deployment readiness.
AI.motivation: Despite larger pre-training data and larger models, state-of-the-art medical MLLMs underperform classical models in medical image classification. A granular, module- and layer-level analysis is needed to locate where information flow degrades and how it can be improved for clinical viability.
AI.method: Conducted experiments on 14 open-source medical MLLMs across three representative medical image classification datasets. Used feature probing to trace visual features module-by-module and layer-by-layer through the full MLLM pipeline, enabling visualization of how classification signals are distorted, diluted, or overridden. Introduced quantitative scores to characterize the 'healthiness' of feature evolution and facilitate principled cross-model/dataset comparisons.
AI.result: Identified four failure modes underlying performance degradation: (1) quality limitation in visual representation, (2) fidelity loss in connector projection, (3) comprehension deficit in LLM reasoning, (4) misalignment of semantic mapping. Also proposed healthiness scores for feature evolution, enabling systematic comparisons across MLLMs and datasets.
AI.conclusion: The gap between high expectations and clinical deployability persists. The study provides a diagnostic framework to locate bottlenecks in medical MLLMs and emphasizes the need to address visual representation quality, projection fidelity, reasoning comprehension, and semantic alignment to realize clinically viable multimodal models.

title: InstAP: Instance-Aware Vision-Language Pre-Train for Spatial-Temporal Understanding
summary: Current vision-language pre-training (VLP) paradigms excel at global scene understanding but struggle with instance-level reasoning due to global-only supervision. We introduce InstAP, an Instance-Aware Pre-training framework that jointly optimizes global vision-text alignment and fine-grained, instance-level contrastive alignment by grounding textual mentions to specific spatial-temporal regions. To support this, we present InstVL, a large-scale dataset (2 million images, 50,000 videos) with dual-granularity annotations: holistic scene captions and dense, grounded instance descriptions. On the InstVL benchmark, InstAP substantially outperforms existing VLP models on instance-level retrieval, and also surpasses a strong VLP baseline trained on the exact same data corpus, isolating the benefit of our instance-aware objective. Moreover, instance-centric pre-training improves global understanding: InstAP achieves competitive zero-shot performance on multiple video benchmarks, including MSR-VTT and DiDeMo. Qualitative visualizations further show that InstAP localizes textual mentions to the correct instances, while global-only models exhibit more diffuse, scene-level attention.

AI.tldr: InstAP introduces instance-aware pre-training to ground textual mentions to image/video regions, boosting instance-level retrieval and benefiting global understanding; InstVL provides dual-granularity annotations.
AI.motivation: Global-only supervision in vision-language pre-training limits instance-level reasoning; need to align text with specific regions temporally/spatially.
AI.method: Joint global vision-text alignment and fine-grained instance-level contrastive alignment via grounding textual mentions to spatial-temporal regions; uses InstVL with holistic captions and dense grounded instance descriptions.
AI.result: On InstVL, InstAP outperforms existing VLP models for instance-level retrieval and beats a strong baseline trained on the same data; induces competitive zero-shot video performance on MSR-VTT and DiDeMo; qualitative results show precise localization to correct instances.
AI.conclusion: Instance-aware pre-training yields benefits for both instance-level and global understanding, suggesting grounding textual mentions enhances VLP.

title: PokeGym: A Visually-Driven Long-Horizon Benchmark for Vision-Language Models
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in static visual understanding, their deployment in complex 3D embodied environments remains severely limited. Existing benchmarks suffer from four critical deficiencies: (1) passive perception tasks circumvent interactive dynamics; (2) simplified 2D environments fail to assess depth perception; (3) privileged state leakage bypasses genuine visual processing; and (4) human evaluation is prohibitively expensive and unscalable. We introduce PokeGym, a visually-driven long-horizon benchmark instantiated within Pokemon Legends: Z-A, a visually complex 3D open-world Role-Playing Game. PokeGym enforces strict code-level isolation: agents operate solely on raw RGB observations while an independent evaluator verifies success via memory scanning, ensuring pure vision-based decision-making and automated, scalable assessment. The benchmark comprises 30 tasks (30-220 steps) spanning navigation, interaction, and mixed scenarios, with three instruction granularities (Visual-Guided, Step-Guided, Goal-Only) to systematically deconstruct visual grounding, semantic reasoning, and autonomous exploration capabilities. Our evaluation reveals a key limitation of current VLMs: physical deadlock recovery, rather than high-level planning, constitutes the primary bottleneck, with deadlocks showing a strong negative correlation with task success. Furthermore, we uncover a metacognitive divergence: weaker models predominantly suffer from Unaware Deadlocks (oblivious to entrapment), whereas advanced models exhibit Aware Deadlocks (recognizing entrapment yet failing to recover). These findings highlight the need to integrate explicit spatial intuition into VLM architectures. The code and benchmark will be available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: PokeGym is a vision-only, long-horizon benchmark in Pokemon Legends: Z-A for evaluating VLMs in 3D embodied tasks; it reveals deadlock recovery as the main bottleneck and a metacognitive gap (Unaware vs. Aware Deadlocks), advocating explicit spatial intuition in VLMs.
AI.motivation: Address four core deficiencies of existing benchmarks: (1) passive perception tasks, (2) 2D environments lacking depth perception, (3) leakage of privileged state, and (4) costly, unscalable human evaluation; provide a scalable, vision-centric testbed for 3D embodied understanding.
AI.method: Instantiate with 30 tasks (each 30–220 steps) in a visually complex 3D RPG; enforce strict code-level isolation so agents use only raw RGB; an independent evaluator verifies success via memory scanning; three instruction granularities (Visual-Guided, Step-Guided, Goal-Only); tasks span navigation, interaction, and mixed scenarios; evaluation on GitHub.
AI.result: Findings show physical deadlock recovery, not high-level planning, as the primary bottleneck with deadlocks strongly correlating with task success; observe a metacognitive divergence: Unaware Deadlocks in weaker models vs Aware Deadlocks in stronger models; suggests need to imbue models with explicit spatial intuition.
AI.conclusion: To advance VLMs for 3D embodied tasks, integrate explicit spatial reasoning into architectures; leverage scalable, automated benchmarks like PokeGym; code and benchmark to be released on GitHub.

title: MegaStyle: Constructing Diverse and Scalable Style Dataset via Consistent Text-to-Image Style Mapping
summary: In this paper, we introduce MegaStyle, a novel and scalable data curation pipeline that constructs an intra-style consistent, inter-style diverse and high-quality style dataset. We achieve this by leveraging the consistent text-to-image style mapping capability of current large generative models, which can generate images in the same style from a given style description. Building on this foundation, we curate a diverse and balanced prompt gallery with 170K style prompts and 400K content prompts, and generate a large-scale style dataset MegaStyle-1.4M via content-style prompt combinations. With MegaStyle-1.4M, we propose style-supervised contrastive learning to fine-tune a style encoder MegaStyle-Encoder for extracting expressive, style-specific representations, and we also train a FLUX-based style transfer model MegaStyle-FLUX. Extensive experiments demonstrate the importance of maintaining intra-style consistency, inter-style diversity and high-quality for style dataset, as well as the effectiveness of the proposed MegaStyle-1.4M. Moreover, when trained on MegaStyle-1.4M, MegaStyle-Encoder and MegaStyle-FLUX provide reliable style similarity measurement and generalizable style transfer, making a significant contribution to the style transfer community. More results are available at our project website https://jeoyal.github.io/MegaStyle/.

AI.tldr: MegaStyle introduces a scalable data curation pipeline and MegaStyle-1.4M dataset for intra-style consistency and inter-style diversity, plus a style encoder and FLUX-based transfer model, achieving reliable style similarity and transfer.
AI.motivation: Address the need for large-scale, high-quality, style-diverse datasets to improve style transfer, ensuring intra-style consistency and inter-style diversity.
AI.method: Leverage consistent text-to-image style mapping from large generative models to generate images from style descriptions; curate a prompt gallery with 170K style prompts and 400K content prompts; synthesize MegaStyle-1.4M via content-style prompt combinations; apply style-supervised contrastive learning to fine-tune MegaStyle-Encoder; train a FLUX-based style transfer model MegaStyle-FLUX.
AI.result: Demonstrates that maintaining intra-style consistency, inter-style diversity, and high quality is crucial for style datasets; MegaStyle-1.4M is effective for training; MegaStyle-Encoder and MegaStyle-FLUX yield reliable style similarity measures and generalizable style transfer.
AI.conclusion: MegaStyle contributes to the style transfer community by providing a scalable, high-quality, diverse style dataset and associated models, enabling robust style similarity measurement and transfer; results are publicly available via project website.

title: SurfelSplat: Learning Efficient and Generalizable Gaussian Surfel Representations for Sparse-View Surface Reconstruction
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated impressive performance in 3D scene reconstruction. Beyond novel view synthesis, it shows great potential for multi-view surface reconstruction. Existing methods employ optimization-based reconstruction pipelines that achieve precise and complete surface extractions. However, these approaches typically require dense input views and high time consumption for per-scene optimization. To address these limitations, we propose SurfelSplat, a feed-forward framework that generates efficient and generalizable pixel-aligned Gaussian surfel representations from sparse-view images. We observe that conventional feed-forward structures struggle to recover accurate geometric attributes of Gaussian surfels because the spatial frequency of pixel-aligned primitives exceeds Nyquist sampling rates. Therefore, we propose a cross-view feature aggregation module based on the Nyquist sampling theorem. Specifically, we first adapt the geometric forms of Gaussian surfels with spatial sampling rate-guided low-pass filters. We then project the filtered surfels across all input views to obtain cross-view feature correlations. By processing these correlations through a specially designed feature fusion network, we can finally regress Gaussian surfels with precise geometry. Extensive experiments on DTU reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate that our model achieves comparable results with state-of-the-art methods, and predict Gaussian surfels within 1 second, offering a 100x speedup without costly per-scene training.

AI.tldr: SurfelSplat enables fast, feed-forward recovery of pixel-aligned Gaussian surfels from sparse views using Nyquist-guided cross-view aggregation, achieving DTU-level results with ~1s inference and 100x speedup over per-scene optimization pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D reconstruction with 3DGS requires dense input views and costly per-scene optimization. There is a need for efficient, generalizable surface reconstructions from sparse views.
AI.method: SurfelSplat is a feed-forward framework that produces pixel-aligned Gaussian surfels from sparse-view images. It introduces a cross-view feature aggregation module guided by Nyquist sampling to address high spatial-frequency content. Steps: (1) adapt Gaussian surfels with spatial sampling rate-guided low-pass filters; (2) project filtered surfels across all input views to obtain cross-view feature correlations; (3) fuse these correlations with a dedicated network to regress surfels with precise geometry.
AI.result: On DTU reconstruction benchmarks, SurfelSplat achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art methods while enabling surfel prediction within 1 second, offering ~100× speedup without per-scene training.
AI.conclusion: A fast, generalizable, feed-forward approach for Gaussian surfel reconstruction from sparse views is feasible, leveraging Nyquist-guided cross-view aggregation to retain geometric accuracy without dense inputs or expensive optimization.

title: Phantasia: Context-Adaptive Backdoors in Vision Language Models
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have greatly enhanced the integration of visual perception and linguistic reasoning, driving rapid progress in multimodal understanding. Despite these achievements, the security of VLMs, particularly their vulnerability to backdoor attacks, remains significantly underexplored. Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs are still in an early stage of development, with most current methods relying on generating poisoned responses that contain fixed, easily identifiable patterns. In this work, we make two key contributions. First, we demonstrate for the first time that the stealthiness of existing VLM backdoor attacks has been substantially overestimated. By adapting defense techniques originally designed for other domains (e.g., vision-only and text-only models), we show that several state-of-the-art attacks can be detected with surprising ease. Second, to address this gap, we introduce Phantasia, a context-adaptive backdoor attack that dynamically aligns its poisoned outputs with the semantics of each input. Instead of producing static poisoned patterns, Phantasia encourages models to generate contextually coherent yet malicious responses that remain plausible, thereby significantly improving stealth and adaptability. Extensive experiments across diverse VLM architectures reveal that Phantasia achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates while maintaining benign performance under various defensive settings.

AI.tldr: Phantasia is a context-adaptive backdoor attack on Vision-Language Models that evades static poisoning patterns by aligning malicious outputs with each input’s semantics, achieving state-of-the-art success while remaining robust under defenses; it also demonstrates that prior VLM backdoors were more detectable than claimed.
AI.motivation: VLM backdoor security is underexplored and prior attacks rely on fixed, easily detectable poisoned patterns. There is a need to reassess stealth claims and develop more resilient defenses that consider input semantics.
AI.method: First, adapt defense techniques from vision-only and text-only domains to evaluate the stealth of existing VLM backdoors. Then, design Phantasia, a context-adaptive attack that generates contextually coherent yet malicious outputs by dynamically aligning with input semantics, trained/validated across diverse VLM architectures under various defenses.
AI.result: Phantasia achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates while preserving benign accuracy under defensive settings, and exposes the vulnerability of static-poisoning backdoors to context-aware evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Context-adaptive backdoors significantly elevate threat levels for VLMs and reveal gaps in current defenses. The work underscores the necessity for semantics-aware defense mechanisms and suggests future work on generalizing context-adaptive attacks and improving detection across modalities.

title: SyncBreaker:Stage-Aware Multimodal Adversarial Attacks on Audio-Driven Talking Head Generation
summary: Diffusion-based audio-driven talking-head generation enables realistic portrait animation, but also introduces risks of misuse, such as fraud and misinformation. Existing protection methods are largely limited to a single modality, and neither image-only nor audio-only attacks can effectively suppress speech-driven facial dynamics. To address this gap, we propose SyncBreaker, a stage-aware multimodal protection framework that jointly perturbs portrait and audio inputs under modality-specific perceptual constraints. Our key contributions are twofold. First, for the image stream, we introduce nullifying supervision with Multi-Interval Sampling (MIS) across diffusion stages to steer the generation toward the static reference portrait by aggregating guidance from multiple denoising intervals. Second, for the audio stream, we propose Cross-Attention Fooling (CAF), which suppresses interval-specific audio-conditioned cross-attention responses. Both streams are optimized independently and combined at inference time to enable flexible deployment. We evaluate SyncBreaker in a white-box proactive protection setting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SyncBreaker more effectively degrades lip synchronization and facial dynamics than strong single-modality baselines, while preserving input perceptual quality and remaining robust under purification. Code: https://github.com/kitty384/SyncBreaker.

AI.tldr: SyncBreaker is a stage-aware multimodal defense against diffusion-based audio-driven talking-head generation, perturbing both image and audio inputs to disrupt lip-sync and facial motion while preserving perceptual quality; introduces Multi-Interval Sampling for images and Cross-Attention Fooling for audio, evaluated in white-box setting with favorable degradation to single-modality baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based talking-head synthesis enables realistic impersonation and misinformation; existing protections focus on single modalities and fail to suppress dynamics when both image and audio contribute. A multimodal, stage-aware defense is needed to robustly degrade synthesized dynamics.
AI.method: Image stream: nullifying supervision with Multi-Interval Sampling (MIS) across diffusion stages, aggregating guidance from multiple denoising intervals to push generation toward the static portrait. Audio stream: Cross-Attention Fooling (CAF) suppresses interval-specific audio-conditioned cross-attention responses. Independent optimization for each stream; combination at inference for deployment flexibility.
AI.result: White-box proactive protection evaluation shows SyncBreaker degrades lip synchronization and facial dynamics more effectively than strong single-modality baselines, while preserving perceptual input quality and remaining robust under purification.
AI.conclusion: A practical, modular defense against voice-driven portrait animation, enabling multimodal protection with competitive effectiveness and flexible deployment; code available for reproduction.

title: HST-HGN: Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Hypergraph Networks with Bidirectional State Space Models for Global Fatigue Assessment
summary: It remains challenging to assess driver fatigue from untrimmed videos under constrained computational budgets, due to the difficulty of modeling long-range temporal dependencies in subtle facial expressions. Some existing approaches rely on computationally heavy architectures, whereas others employ traditional lightweight pairwise graph networks, despite their limited capacity to model high-order synergies and global temporal context. Therefore, we propose HST-HGN, a novel Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Hypergraph Network driven by Bidirectional State Space Models. Spatially, we introduce a hierarchical hypergraph network to fuse pose-disentangled geometric topologies with multi-modal texture patches dynamically. This formulation encapsulates high-order synergistic facial deformations, effectively overcoming the limitations of conventional methods. In temporal terms, a Bi-Mamba module with linear complexity is applied to perform bidirectional sequence modeling. This explicit temporal-evolution filtering enables the network to distinguish highly ambiguous transient actions, such as yawning versus speaking, while encompassing their complete physiological lifecycles. Extensive evaluations across diverse fatigue benchmarks demonstrate that HST-HGN achieves state-of-the-art performance. In particular, our method strikes a balance between discriminative power and computational efficiency, making it well-suited for real-time in-cabin edge deployment.

AI.tldr: HST-HGN: AHierarchical Spatial-Temporal Hypergraph Network guided by Bidirectional State Space Models for real-time driver fatigue detection; achieves state-of-the-art performance with efficient computation via high-order spatio-temporal modeling.
AI.motivation: Assess driver fatigue from untrimmed videos under constrained compute; model long-range temporal dependencies in subtle facial expressions; overcome limitations of heavy architectures and lightweight pairwise graphs.
AI.method: Spatial: hierarchical hypergraph to fuse pose-disentangled geometry with multi-modal texture patches to capture high-order deformations. Temporal: Bi-Mamba module with linear complexity for bidirectional sequence modeling and explicit temporal-evolution filtering to distinguish subtle actions across lifecycles.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on diverse fatigue benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance; achieves a favorable discriminative-to-efficiency trade-off suitable for real-time in-cabin edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: HST-HGN effectively models high-order spatio-temporal patterns for fatigue detection while maintaining computational efficiency, enabling robust real-time deployment.

title: Entropy-Gradient Grounding: Training-Free Evidence Retrieval in Vision-Language Models
summary: Despite rapid progress, pretrained vision-language models still struggle when answers depend on tiny visual details or on combining clues spread across multiple regions, as in documents and compositional queries. We address this by framing grounding as test-time evidence retrieval: given a query, the model should actively identify where to look next to resolve ambiguity. To this end, we propose a training-free, model-intrinsic grounding method that uses uncertainty as supervision. Specifically, we compute the entropy of the model's next-token distribution and backpropagate it to the visual token embeddings to obtain an entropy-gradient relevance map, without auxiliary detectors or attention-map heuristics. We then extract and rank multiple coherent regions to support multi-evidence queries, and introduce an iterative zoom-and-reground procedure with a spatial-entropy stopping rule to avoid over-refinement. Experiments on seven benchmarks across four VLM architectures demonstrate consistent improvements over existing methods, with the largest gains on detail-critical and high-resolution settings, while also producing more interpretable evidence localizations.

AI.tldr: A training-free, model-intrinsic grounding method uses entropy-gradient signals from a vision-language model to actively retrieve and fuse multiple evidence regions for grounding, with an iterative zoom-reground process and a spatial-entropy stopping rule.
AI.motivation: Grounding answers that rely on tiny visual details or distributed clues is hard; existing approaches rely on external detectors or heuristics and require training. A model-centric, detector-free method using uncertainty can guide where the model should look next.
AI.method: Compute next-token distribution entropy during inference and backpropagate this entropy to visual token embeddings to generate an entropy-gradient relevance map. Extract and rank multiple coherent regions as evidence. Use an iterative zoom-and-reground procedure with a spatial-entropy stopping rule to prevent over-refinement. No auxiliary detectors or attention-map heuristics are used.
AI.result: Across seven benchmarks and four VLM architectures, the method yields consistent improvements over existing grounding methods, with the largest gains on detail-critical and high-resolution settings, and produces more interpretable evidence localizations.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, model-intrinsic grounding paradigm that leverages uncertainty signals enables effective multi-evidence retrieval and interpretable localization without external detectors, improving grounding performance across diverse architectures.

title: LAMP: Lift Image-Editing as General 3D Priors for Open-world Manipulation
summary: Human-like generalization in open-world remains a fundamental challenge for robotic manipulation. Existing learning-based methods, including reinforcement learning, imitation learning, and vision-language-action-models (VLAs), often struggle with novel tasks and unseen environments. Another promising direction is to explore generalizable representations that capture fine-grained spatial and geometric relations for open-world manipulation. While large-language-model (LLMs) and vision-language-model (VLMs) provide strong semantic reasoning based on language or annotated 2D representations, their limited 3D awareness restricts their applicability to fine-grained manipulation. To address this, we propose LAMP, which lifts image-editing as 3D priors to extract inter-object 3D transformations as continuous, geometry-aware representations. Our key insight is that image-editing inherently encodes rich 2D spatial cues, and lifting these implicit cues into 3D transformations provides fine-grained and accurate guidance for open-world manipulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \codename delivers precise 3D transformations and achieves strong zero-shot generalization in open-world manipulation. Project page: https://zju3dv.github.io/LAMP/.

AI.tldr: Proposes LAMP to lift image-editing cues into 3D priors, extracting inter-object 3D transformations for geometry-aware open-world manipulation, yielding precise 3D guidance and strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of current learning-based methods and VLAs in open-world robotic manipulation due to inadequate generalization and 3D awareness; leverage 2D spatial cues from image-editing to inform 3D transformations.
AI.method: Introduce LAMP; treat image-editing outputs as 3D priors; convert rich 2D cues into inter-object 3D transformations as continuous representations; geometry-aware.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show precise 3D transformations and strong zero-shot generalization in open-world manipulation.
AI.conclusion: LAMP demonstrates the viability of converting 2D image-editing signals into 3D priors for fine-grained, geometry-aware robotic manipulation, enabling better generalization; potential for broader integration with manipulation systems and real-world deployment.

title: Faithful GRPO: Improving Visual Spatial Reasoning in Multimodal Language Models via Constrained Policy Optimization
summary: Multimodal reasoning models (MRMs) trained with reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) show improved accuracy on visual reasoning benchmarks. However, we observe that accuracy gains often come at the cost of reasoning quality: generated Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces are frequently inconsistent with the final answer and poorly grounded in the visual evidence. We systematically study this phenomenon across seven challenging real-world spatial reasoning benchmarks and find that it affects contemporary MRMs such as ViGoRL-Spatial, TreeVGR as well as our own models trained with standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We characterize CoT reasoning quality along two complementary axes: "logical consistency" (does the CoT entail the final answer?) and "visual grounding" (does each reasoning step accurately describe objects, attributes, and spatial relationships in the image?). To address this, we propose Faithful GRPO (FGRPO), a variant of GRPO that enforces consistency and grounding as constraints via Lagrangian dual ascent. FGRPO incorporates batch-level consistency and grounding constraints into the advantage computation within a group, adaptively adjusting the relative importance of constraints during optimization. We evaluate FGRPO on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and 3B backbones across seven spatial datasets. Our results show that FGRPO substantially improves reasoning quality, reducing the inconsistency rate from 24.5% to 1.7% and improving visual grounding scores by +13%. It also improves final answer accuracy over simple GRPO, demonstrating that faithful reasoning enables better answers.

AI.tldr: RLVR-trained multimodal reasoning models improve accuracy but harm reasoning quality (coherent Chain-of-Thoughts and grounding in images). Faithful GRPO (FGRPO) uses Lagrangian dual ascent to enforce batch-level consistency and visual grounding within GRPO, adaptively weighting constraints. It improves reasoning faithfulness and final accuracy on seven spatial benchmarks, outperforming standard GRPO.
AI.motivation: There is a persistent mismatch between higher task accuracy and the quality of generated reasoning (CoT) in multimodal reasoning models. CoT traces often do not align with the final answer or with visual evidence. The study evaluates this phenomenon on seven real-world spatial benchmarks across models like ViGoRL-Spatial and TreeVGR, including GRPO-based training, to quantify and address faithfulness.
AI.method: Introduce Faithful GRPO (FGRPO), a GRPO variant that incorporates batch-level consistency and visual grounding constraints into the advantage computation via Lagrangian dual ascent. It adaptively tunes constraint importance during optimization. Evaluated on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and 3B backbones across seven spatial datasets.
AI.result: FGRPO reduces CoT inconsistency rate from 24.5% to 1.7% and increases visual grounding scores by 13 percentage points. It also improves final answer accuracy over simple GRPO, demonstrating that faithful reasoning enhances answers.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing faithfulness in reasoning via constrained GRPO yields substantial gains in both reasoning quality and task accuracy, suggesting broader applicability to multimodal instruction and reasoning systems and potential avenues for further refinement.

title: What They Saw, Not Just Where They Looked: Semantic Scanpath Similarity via VLMs and NLP metric
summary: Scanpath similarity metrics are central to eye-movement research, yet existing methods predominantly evaluate spatial and temporal alignment while neglecting semantic equivalence between attended image regions. We present a semantic scanpath similarity framework that integrates vision-language models (VLMs) into eye-tracking analysis. Each fixation is encoded under controlled visual context (patch-based and marker-based strategies) and transformed into concise textual descriptions, which are aggregated into scanpath-level representations. Semantic similarity is then computed using embedding-based and lexical NLP metrics and compared against established spatial measures, including MultiMatch and DTW. Experiments on free-viewing eye-tracking data demonstrate that semantic similarity captures partially independent variance from geometric alignment, revealing cases of high content agreement despite spatial divergence. We further analyze the impact of contextual encoding on description fidelity and metric stability. Our findings suggest that multimodal foundation models enable interpretable, content-aware extensions of classical scanpath analysis, providing a complementary dimension for gaze research within the ETRA community.

AI.tldr: Semantic scanpath similarity using vision-language models to encode fixations as text and assess content-level similarity, complementing spatial metrics like MultiMatch and DTW; reveals content-based agreement even when spatial alignment diverges.
AI.motivation: Existing scanpath metrics emphasize spatial/temporal alignment and neglect semantic equivalence of attended image regions; integrating vision-language models enables content-aware gaze analysis.
AI.method: Encode each fixation with patch-based or marker-based contextual cues, convert to concise textual descriptions, aggregate into scanpath representations, and compute semantic similarity using embedding-based and lexical NLP metrics; compare against spatial measures such as MultiMatch and DTW.
AI.result: Semantic similarity captures variance partially independent of geometry; identifies cases where content agreement is high despite spatial divergence; contextual encoding impacts description fidelity and metric stability.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal foundation models provide interpretable, content-aware extensions to classical scanpath analysis, offering a complementary dimension for gaze research within the ETRA community.

title: Novel View Synthesis as Video Completion
summary: We tackle the problem of sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) using video diffusion models; given $K$ ($\approx 5$) multi-view images of a scene and their camera poses, we predict the view from a target camera pose. Many prior approaches leverage generative image priors encoded via diffusion models. However, models trained on single images lack multi-view knowledge. We instead argue that video models already contain implicit multi-view knowledge and so should be easier to adapt for NVS. Our key insight is to formulate sparse NVS as a low frame-rate video completion task. However, one challenge is that sparse NVS is defined over an unordered set of inputs, often too sparse to admit a meaningful order, so the models should be $\textit{invariant}$ to permutations of that input set. To this end, we present FrameCrafter, which adapts video models (naturally trained with coherent frame orderings) to permutation-invariant NVS through several architectural modifications, including per-frame latent encodings and removal of temporal positional embeddings. Our results suggest that video models can be easily trained to "forget" about time with minimal supervision, producing competitive performance on sparse-view NVS benchmarks. Project page: https://frame-crafter.github.io/

AI.tldr: FrameCrafter adapts video diffusion models for sparse, permutation-invariant novel view synthesis by treating inputs as a low-frame-rate video completion task. It introduces per-frame latent encodings and removes temporal pos embeddings to enforce input permutation invariance, achieving competitive results with minimal supervision on sparse-view NVS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Single-image diffusion priors lack multi-view coherence. Video models contain implicit multi-view knowledge from sequence training, suggesting they can be repurposed for NVS with suitable modifications.
AI.method: Modify video diffusion models to handle unordered input sets: (1) use per-frame latent encodings to keep frame-specific information, (2) remove temporal positional embeddings to remove reliance on order, (3) frame the problem as low frame-rate video completion, ensuring permutation invariance across the K sparse views.
AI.result: FrameCrafter yields competitive performance on sparse-view NVS benchmarks, demonstrating that video priors can be adapted to forget time with minimal supervision and handle unordered input effectively.
AI.conclusion: Video diffusion models can be repurposed for sparse NVS by architectural adjustments that enforce permutation invariance; treating sparse NVS as low frame-rate video completion is a viable and effective approach.

title: Quantifying Explanation Consistency: The C-Score Metric for CAM-Based Explainability in Medical Image Classification
summary: Class Activation Mapping (CAM) methods are widely used to generate visual explanations for deep learning classifiers in medical imaging. However, existing evaluation frameworks assess whether explanations are correct, measured by localisation fidelity against radiologist annotations, rather than whether they are consistent: whether the model applies the same spatial reasoning strategy across different patients with the same pathology. We propose the C-Score (Consistency Score), a confidence-weighted, annotation-free metric that quantifies intra-class explanation reproducibility via intensity-emphasised pairwise soft IoU across correctly classified instances. We evaluate six CAM techniques: GradCAM, GradCAM++, LayerCAM, EigenCAM, ScoreCAM, and MS GradCAM++ across three CNN architectures (DenseNet201, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2) over thirty training epochs on the Kermany chest X-ray dataset, covering transfer learning and fine-tuning phases. We identify three distinct mechanisms of AUC-consistency dissociation, invisible to standard classification metrics: threshold-mediated gold list collapse, technique-specific attribution collapse at peak AUC, and class-level consistency masking in global aggregation. C-Score provides an early warning signal of impending model instability. ScoreCAM deterioration on ResNet50V2 is detectable one full checkpoint before catastrophic AUC collapse and yields architecture-specific clinical deployment recommendations grounded in explanation quality rather than predictive ranking alone.

AI.tldr: Introduces C-Score, a confidence-weighted, annotation-free metric to measure CAM consistency across instances; evaluates six CAM methods across architectures on Kermany X-ray, identifies AUC-consistency dissociation mechanisms, and shows C-Score can predict model instability and guide deployment.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of CAM explanations emphasizes localization accuracy against radiologist annotations, neglecting consistency of spatial reasoning across different patients with the same pathology. The authors propose a new annotation-free, early-warning metric to quantify intra-class explanation reproducibility.
AI.method: Compute intensity-emphasised pairwise soft IoU across correctly classified instances to quantify intra-class explanation reproducibility (C-Score). Evaluate six CAM techniques (GradCAM, GradCAM++, LayerCAM, EigenCAM, ScoreCAM, MS GradCAM++) across three CNN architectures (DenseNet201, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2) on the Kermany chest X-ray dataset, over 30 training epochs, covering transfer learning and fine-tuning. Analyze three AUC-consistency dissociation mechanisms and the relationship to predictive ranking; derive deployment recommendations.
AI.result: Identified three distinct mechanisms of AUC-consistency dissociation: threshold-mediated gold list collapse, technique-specific attribution collapse at peak AUC, and class-level consistency masking in global aggregation. C-Score provides an early warning signal of impending model instability. ScoreCAM deterioration on ResNet50V2 is detectable one checkpoint before catastrophic AUC collapse, yielding architecture-specific clinical deployment recommendations grounded in explanation quality rather than predictive ranking alone.
AI.conclusion: C-Score offers a practical, annotation-free monitor of CAM explainability robustness during training, enabling early detection of instability and informed deployment decisions that prioritize explanation quality over ranking metrics.

title: Phantom: Physics-Infused Video Generation via Joint Modeling of Visual and Latent Physical Dynamics
summary: Recent advances in generative video modeling, driven by large-scale datasets and powerful architectures, have yielded remarkable visual realism. However, emerging evidence suggests that simply scaling data and model size does not endow these systems with an understanding of the underlying physical laws that govern real-world dynamics. Existing approaches often fail to capture or enforce such physical consistency, resulting in unrealistic motion and dynamics. In his work, we investigate whether integrating the inference of latent physical properties directly into the video generation process can equip models with the ability to produce physically plausible videos. To this end, we propose Phantom, a Physics-Infused Video Generation model that jointly models the visual content and latent physical dynamics. Conditioned on observed video frames and inferred physical states, Phantom jointly predicts latent physical dynamics and generates future video frames. Phantom leverages a physics-aware video representation that serves as an abstract yet informaive embedding of the underlying physics, facilitating the joint prediction of physical dynamics alongside video content without requiring an explicit specification of a complex set of physical dynamics and properties. By integrating the inference of physical-aware video representation directly into the video generation process, Phantom produces video sequences that are both visually realistic and physically consistent. Quantitative and qualitative results on both standard video generation and physics-aware benchmarks demonstrate that Phantom not only outperforms existing methods in terms of adherence to physical dynamics but also delivers competitive perceptual fidelity.

AI.tldr: Phantom is a physics-infused video generation model that jointly infers latent physical dynamics and generates future frames, using a physics-aware representation to enforce physical consistency while maintaining competitive perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: Current large-scale video generation models achieve realism but often lack adherence to underlying physical laws. Merely scaling data and model size does not guarantee physically plausible dynamics, motivating a framework that explicitly reasons about latent physics during generation.
AI.method: Phantom conditionally models on observed frames and inferred physical states, jointly predicting latent physical dynamics and future video frames. It uses a physics-aware video representation as an abstract embedding of underlying physics, enabling joint prediction of dynamics and content without explicit specification of detailed physical laws.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative results on standard video generation and physics-aware benchmarks show Phantom outperforms existing methods in physical-dynamics adherence while delivering competitive perceptual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Integrating latent physical property inference into video generation yields sequences that are both visually realistic and physically consistent; a physics-aware representation can facilitate joint dynamics and content modeling without explicit physical modeling.

title: When Fine-Tuning Changes the Evidence: Architecture-Dependent Semantic Drift in Chest X-Ray Explanations
summary: Transfer learning followed by fine-tuning is widely adopted in medical image classification due to consistent gains in diagnostic performance. However, in multi-class settings with overlapping visual features, improvements in accuracy do not guarantee stability of the visual evidence used to support predictions. We define semantic drift as systematic changes in the attribution structure supporting a model's predictions between transfer learning and full fine-tuning, reflecting potential shifts in underlying visual reasoning despite stable classification performance. Using a five-class chest X-ray task, we evaluate DenseNet201, ResNet50V2, and InceptionV3 under a two-stage training protocol and quantify drift with reference-free metrics capturing spatial localization and structural consistency of attribution maps. Across architectures, coarse anatomical localization remains stable, while overlap IoU reveals pronounced architecture-dependent reorganization of evidential structure. Beyond single-method analysis, stability rankings can reverse across LayerCAM and GradCAM++ under converged predictive performance, establishing explanation stability as an interaction between architecture, optimization phase, and attribution objective.

AI.tldr: Semantic drift in attribution structure can occur during transfer learning vs. full fine-tuning in multi-class medical image classification, despite stable accuracy; explanation stability depends on architecture and attribution method.
AI.motivation: In medical imaging, high diagnostic accuracy alone does not guarantee trustworthy explanations. Understanding how attribution structures change with training strategies is essential to ensure reliable visual reasoning.
AI.method: Evaluate three CNN architectures (DenseNet201, ResNet50V2, InceptionV3) on a five-class chest X-ray task using a two-stage training protocol. Use reference-free attribution maps (LayerCAM, GradCAM++) and metrics for spatial localization and structural consistency (e.g., overlap IoU) to quantify semantic drift between transfer-learning and full fine-tuning.
AI.result: Coarse anatomical localization remains stable across architectures, but attribution structure reconfigures in an architecture-dependent way as shown by overlap IoU. Stability rankings of explanations can reverse between attribution methods under converged accuracy, indicating explanation stability arises from the interaction of architecture, optimization stage, and attribution objective.
AI.conclusion: Explanation stability is not guaranteed by accuracy; it is an emergent property of the interaction among model architecture, training regime, and the chosen attribution method. Cross-method and cross-stage analyses are needed for reliable explanations in medical imaging.

title: MolmoWeb: Open Visual Web Agent and Open Data for the Open Web
summary:
Web agents--autonomous systems that navigate and execute tasks on the web on behalf of users--have the potential to transform how people interact with the digital world. However, the most capable web agents today rely on proprietary models with undisclosed training data and recipes, limiting scientific understanding, reproducibility, and community-driven progress.
  We believe agents for the open web should be built in the open. To this end, we introduce (1) MolmoWebMix, a large and diverse mixture of browser task demonstrations and web-GUI perception data and (2) MolmoWeb, a family of fully open multimodal web agents. Specifically, MolmoWebMix combines over 100K synthetic task trajectories from multiple complementary generation pipelines with 30K+ human demonstrations, atomic web-skill trajectories, and GUI perception data, including referring expression grounding and screenshot question answering. MolmoWeb agents operate as instruction-conditioned visual-language action policies: given a task instruction and a webpage screenshot, they predict the next browser action, requiring no access to HTML, accessibility trees, or specialized APIs.
  Available in 4B and 8B size, on browser-use benchmarks like WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, and DeepShop, MolmoWeb agents achieve state-of-the-art results outperforming similar scale open-weight-only models such as Fara-7B, UI-Tars-1.5-7B, and Holo1-7B. MolmoWeb-8B also surpasses set-of-marks (SoM) agents built on much larger closed frontier models like GPT-4o. We further demonstrate consistent gains through test-time scaling via parallel rollouts with best-of-N selection, achieving 94.7% and 60.5% pass@4 (compared to 78.2% and 35.3% pass@1) on WebVoyager and Online-Mind2Web respectively. We will release model checkpoints, training data, code, and a unified evaluation harness to enable reproducibility and accelerate open research on web agents.

AI.tldr: Open, fully open-weight multimodal web agents (MolmoWeb/MolmoWebMix) built from large-scale synthetic and human data; claim state-of-the-art among open models; plan to release resources.
AI.motivation: Address opacity and reproducibility concerns surrounding proprietary web agents; advance open science by providing large, diverse, openly accessible data and models for web navigation tasks.
AI.method: MolmoWebMix combines 100k+ synthetic task trajectories, 30k+ human demonstrations, atomic web-skill trajectories, and GUI perception data (including referring expression grounding and screenshot QA). MolmoWeb is a family of instruction-conditioned multimodal web agents that operate on a webpage screenshot without relying on HTML, accessibility trees, or specialized APIs. Models sized at 4B and 8B parameters. Evaluation on WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, and DeepShop; test-time scaling via parallel rollouts with best-of-N selection. Plan to release checkpoints, data, code, and a unified evaluation harness.
AI.result: Open-weight 4B/8B MolmoWeb/MolmoWebMix achieve state-of-the-art results on web navigation benchmarks among open models; surpass SoM agents built on larger closed models like GPT-4o; reported pass@4 metrics (WebVoyager 94.7%, Online-Mind2Web 60.5%) compared to pass@1 baselines (78.2% and 35.3%), indicating strong performance and benefits from test-time scaling.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that open, multimodal web agents trained on diverse synthetic and human data can compete with and surpass larger closed models on established benchmarks, supporting reproducibility and community-driven progress in open web intelligence.

title: UniversalVTG: A Universal and Lightweight Foundation Model for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Video temporal grounding (VTG) is typically tackled with dataset-specific models that transfer poorly across domains and query styles. Recent efforts to overcome this limitation have adapted large multimodal language models (MLLMs) to VTG, but their high compute cost and limited video context still hinder long-video grounding. We instead scale unified supervision while keeping the model lightweight. We present UniversalVTG, a single VTG model trained with large-scale cross-dataset pretraining. An offline Query Unifier canonicalizes heterogeneous query formats into a shared declarative space, reducing linguistic mismatch and preventing the negative transfer observed under naïve joint training. Combined with an efficient grounding head, UniversalVTG scales to long, untrimmed videos. Across diverse benchmarks-GoalStep-StepGrounding, Ego4D-NLQ, TACoS, Charades-STA, and ActivityNet-Captions-one UniversalVTG checkpoint achieves state-of-the-art performance versus dedicated VTG models. Moreover, despite being $>100\times$ smaller than recent MLLM-based approaches, UniversalVTG matches or exceeds their accuracy on multiple benchmarks, offering a practical alternative to parameter-heavy MLLMs.

AI.tldr: UniversalVTG provides a unified, lightweight VTG model trained on cross-dataset pretraining, achieving state-of-the-art results across benchmarks with a significantly smaller footprint than MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Mitigate cross-domain and cross-query style transfer issues in VTG, enable long-video grounding, and reduce compute by avoiding large MLLMs while leveraging unified supervision.
AI.method: Introduce an offline Query Unifier that canonicalizes heterogeneous query formats into a shared declarative space, combined with an efficient grounding head and large-scale cross-dataset pretraining to support long, untrimmed videos.
AI.result: A single UniversalVTG checkpoint achieves state-of-the-art performance on GoalStep-StepGrounding, Ego4D-NLQ, TACoS, Charades-STA, and ActivityNet-Captions. Despite being over 100x smaller than recent MLLMs, it matches or exceeds their accuracy on multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: UniversalVTG offers a practical, scalable VTG solution that maintains high performance with a lightweight model and cross-dataset supervision, reducing reliance on parameter-heavy MLLMs.

title: FIT: A Large-Scale Dataset for Fit-Aware Virtual Try-On
summary:
Given a person and a garment image, virtual try-on (VTO) aims to synthesize a realistic image of the person wearing the garment, while preserving their original pose and identity. Although recent VTO methods excel at visualizing garment appearance, they largely overlook a crucial aspect of the try-on experience: the accuracy of garment fit -- for example, depicting how an extra-large shirt looks on an extra-small person. A key obstacle is the absence of datasets that provide precise garment and body size information, particularly for "ill-fit" cases, where garments are significantly too large or too small. Consequently, current VTO methods default to generating well-fitted results regardless of the garment or person size.
  In this paper, we take the first steps towards solving this open problem. We introduce FIT (Fit-Inclusive Try-on), a large-scale VTO dataset comprising over 1.13M try-on image triplets accompanied by precise body and garment measurements. We overcome the challenges of data collection via a scalable synthetic strategy: (1) We programmatically generate 3D garments using GarmentCode and drape them via physics simulation to capture realistic garment fit. (2) We employ a novel re-texturing framework to transform synthetic renderings into photorealistic images while strictly preserving geometry. (3) We introduce person identity preservation into our re-texturing model to generate paired person images (same person, different garments) for supervised training. Finally, we leverage our FIT dataset to train a baseline fit-aware virtual try-on model. Our data and results set the new state-of-the-art for fit-aware virtual try-on, as well as offer a robust benchmark for future research. We will make all data and code publicly available on our project page: https://johannakarras.github.io/FIT.

AI.tldr: Introduces FIT, a large-scale fit-inclusive virtual try-on dataset with 1.13M try-on triplets and precise body/garment measurements, plus a synthetic data pipeline (GarmentCode + physics drape) and a geometry-preserving, identity-aware re-texturing framework, enabling a baseline fit-aware VTO model and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of datasets providing precise garment and body size information, especially ill-fit cases, which current VTO methods ignore by default. A dataset and pipeline that capture garment-body size interactions is needed to study and improve fit realism.
AI.method: A scalable synthetic pipeline: (1) generate 3D garments with GarmentCode and drape them via physics simulation to capture realistic fit; (2) re-texture synthetic renderings into photorealistic images while strictly preserving geometry; (3) incorporate identity preservation to produce paired images (same person, different garments) for supervised training; dataset contains over 1.13 million triplets with precise measurements for training a baseline fit-aware VTO model.
AI.result: The FIT dataset enables fit-aware VTO research and achieves state-of-the-art performance on fit-aware benchmarks; provides a robust benchmark for future research; data and code will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: FIT provides a scalable, geometry-preserving, identity-aware synthetic data framework for studying garment-body fit interactions, enabling accurate ill-fit modeling in virtual try-on and laying a strong foundation for future research and benchmarks.

title: Self-Improving 4D Perception via Self-Distillation
summary: Large-scale multi-view reconstruction models have made remarkable progress, but most existing approaches still rely on fully supervised training with ground-truth 3D/4D annotations. Such annotations are expensive and particularly scarce for dynamic scenes, limiting scalability. We propose SelfEvo, a self-improving framework that continually improves pretrained multi-view reconstruction models using unlabeled videos. SelfEvo introduces a self-distillation scheme using spatiotemporal context asymmetry, enabling self-improvement for learning-based 4D perception without external annotations. We systematically study design choices that make self-improvement effective, including loss signals, forms of asymmetry, and other training strategies. Across eight benchmarks spanning diverse datasets and domains, SelfEvo consistently improves pretrained baselines and generalizes across base models (e.g. VGGT and $π^3$), with significant gains on dynamic scenes. Overall, SelfEvo achieves up to 36.5% relative improvement in video depth estimation and 20.1% in camera estimation, without using any labeled data. Project Page: https://self-evo.github.io/.

AI.tldr: SelfEvo is a self-distillation framework that leverages spatiotemporal context asymmetry to continually improve pretrained multi-view reconstruction models using unlabeled videos, achieving significant gains without any ground-truth 3D/4D annotations and generalizing across base models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-quality 3D/4D annotations for dynamic scenes are costly and scarce, hindering scalable learning of dynamic 4D perception from video alone.
AI.method: Introduce a self-distillation scheme exploiting spatiotemporal context asymmetry; systematically study loss signals, asymmetry forms, and training strategies; validate across eight benchmarks and multiple base models (e.g., VGGT and π^3).
AI.result: SelfEvo yields consistent improvements over pretrained baselines, with up to 36.5% relative improvement in video depth estimation and 20.1% in camera estimation, without labeled data; demonstrates generalization across base models and dynamic-scene gains.
AI.conclusion: Annotation-free self-improvement for 4D perception is feasible via context-asymmetric self-distillation; the approach is robust across datasets and base models and reduces dependence on costly ground-truth annotations.

title: RewardFlow: Generate Images by Optimizing What You Reward
summary: We introduce RewardFlow, an inversion-free framework that steers pretrained diffusion and flow-matching models at inference time through multi-reward Langevin dynamics. RewardFlow unifies complementary differentiable rewards for semantic alignment, perceptual fidelity, localized grounding, object consistency, and human preference, and further introduces a differentiable VQA-based reward that provides fine-grained semantic supervision through language-vision reasoning. To coordinate these heterogeneous objectives, we design a prompt-aware adaptive policy that extracts semantic primitives from the instruction, infers edit intent, and dynamically modulates reward weights and step sizes throughout sampling. Across several image editing and compositional generation benchmarks, RewardFlow delivers state-of-the-art edit fidelity and compositional alignment.

AI.tldr: RewardFlow is an inversion-free inference-time framework that steers pretrained diffusion/flow-matching models via multi-reward Langevin dynamics, integrating diverse differentiable rewards (semantic alignment, perceptual fidelity, grounding, object consistency, human preference) plus a differentiable VQA-based reward for language-vision supervision, with a prompt-aware adaptive policy to modulate rewards and step sizes. It achieves state-of-the-art edit fidelity and compositional alignment on image editing and compositional generation tasks.
AI.motivation: To coherently optimize and balance multiple, potentially competing objectives during image editing and generation, without requiring inversion or post-hoc editing, across semantic, perceptual, and linguistic supervision.
AI.method: Inference-time guidance using Langevin dynamics with a heterogeneous set of differentiable rewards (semantic alignment, perceptual fidelity, localized grounding, object consistency, human preference) and a differentiable VQA-based reward. A prompt-aware adaptive policy extracts semantic primitives from instructions, infers edit intent, and dynamically adjusts reward weights and step sizes during sampling.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks on image editing and compositional generation show state-of-the-art edit fidelity and compositional alignment, demonstrating effective multi-objective coordination at inference time.
AI.conclusion: RewardFlow provides a unified, inversion-free framework for aligning heterogeneous objectives in diffusion/flow-matching models, enabling fine-grained semantic control via multiple rewards and language-vision supervision, with adaptive weighting and step-size modulation.

title: ParseBench: A Document Parsing Benchmark for AI Agents
summary: AI agents are changing the requirements for document parsing. What matters is \emph{semantic correctness}: parsed output must preserve the structure and meaning needed for autonomous decisions, including correct table structure, precise chart data, semantically meaningful formatting, and visual grounding. Existing benchmarks do not fully capture this setting for enterprise automation, relying on narrow document distributions and text-similarity metrics that miss agent-critical failures. We introduce \textbf{ParseBench}, a benchmark of ${\sim}2{,}000$ human-verified pages from enterprise documents spanning insurance, finance, and government, organized around five capability dimensions: tables, charts, content faithfulness, semantic formatting, and visual grounding. Across 14 methods spanning vision-language models, specialized document parsers, and LlamaParse, the benchmark reveals a fragmented capability landscape: no method is consistently strong across all five dimensions. LlamaParse Agentic achieves the highest overall score at \agenticoverall\%, and the benchmark highlights the remaining capability gaps across current systems. Dataset and evaluation code are available on \href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/llamaindex/ParseBench}{HuggingFace} and \href{https://github.com/run-llama/ParseBench}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: ParseBench introduces a ~2,000-page enterprise document benchmark focused on semantic correctness across five capabilities (tables, charts, content faithfulness, semantic formatting, visual grounding); results show no method is consistently strong, with LlamaParse Agentic top-ranked.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks inadequately capture enterprise automation needs, relying on narrow document distributions and text-similarity metrics that miss agent-critical failures in semantic structure and meaning.
AI.method: Assembles ParseBench from ~2,000 human-verified pages across insurance, finance, and government; evaluates 14 methods spanning vision-language models, specialized document parsers, and LlamaParse across five capability dimensions.
AI.result: Benchmark reveals a fragmented capability landscape; no method consistently excels across all five dimensions; LlamaParse Agentic attains the highest overall score (reported as agenticoverall%).
AI.conclusion: ParseBench highlights persistent capability gaps in current systems for enterprise document parsing and provides dataset and evaluation code to drive focused improvements.

title: OpenVLThinkerV2: A Generalist Multimodal Reasoning Model for Multi-domain Visual Tasks
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as the de facto Reinforcement Learning (RL) objective driving recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models. However, extending this success to open-source multimodal generalist models remains heavily constrained by two primary challenges: the extreme variance in reward topologies across diverse visual tasks, and the inherent difficulty of balancing fine-grained perception with multi-step reasoning capabilities. To address these issues, we introduce Gaussian GRPO (G$^2$RPO), a novel RL training objective that replaces standard linear scaling with non-linear distributional matching. By mathematically forcing the advantage distribution of any given task to strictly converge to a standard normal distribution, $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, G$^2$RPO theoretically ensures inter-task gradient equity, mitigates vulnerabilities to heavy-tail outliers, and offers symmetric update for positive and negative rewards. Leveraging the enhanced training stability provided by G$^2$RPO, we introduce two task-level shaping mechanisms to seamlessly balance perception and reasoning. First, response length shaping dynamically elicits extended reasoning chains for complex queries while enforce direct outputs to bolster visual grounding. Second, entropy shaping tightly bounds the model's exploration zone, effectively preventing both entropy collapse and entropy explosion. Integrating these methodologies, we present OpenVLThinkerV2, a highly robust, general-purpose multimodal model. Extensive evaluations across 18 diverse benchmarks demonstrate its superior performance over strong open-source and leading proprietary frontier models.

AI.tldr: Gaussian GRPO (G^2RPO) replaces linear scaling in policy optimization with distributional matching to a standard normal, improving inter-task gradient equity, stability, and handling heavy-tailed rewards in open-source multimodal models. It introduces two shaping mechanisms—response length shaping and entropy shaping—to balance perception and reasoning, yielding OpenVLThinkerV2 with strong cross-benchmark performance.
AI.motivation: High variance in reward topologies across diverse visual tasks and the difficulty of balancing fine-grained perception with multi-step reasoning hinder open-source multimodal generalist models. A stable, equitable RL objective is needed to support robust training and generalization.
AI.method: Replace standard GRPO scaling with non-linear distributional matching that forces the task advantage distribution to converge to N(0,1). This provides inter-task gradient equity, reduces sensitivity to heavy-tail outliers, and yields symmetric updates for positive/negative rewards. Add task-level shaping: (1) response length shaping to promote longer reasoning chains for complex queries while maintaining grounded outputs; (2) entropy shaping to constrain exploration and avoid entropy collapse/explosion.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 18 diverse benchmarks shows OpenVLThinkerV2 achieves superior performance compared with strong open-source baselines and leading proprietary models, demonstrating robustness and generalizable multimodal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: G^2RPO, coupled with response length and entropy shaping, offers a stable, equitable RL framework for open multimodal generalist models and enables a robust, high-performing model (OpenVLThinkerV2) across broad benchmarks.

title: AVGen-Bench: A Task-Driven Benchmark for Multi-Granular Evaluation of Text-to-Audio-Video Generation
summary: Text-to-Audio-Video (T2AV) generation is rapidly becoming a core interface for media creation, yet its evaluation remains fragmented. Existing benchmarks largely assess audio and video in isolation or rely on coarse embedding similarity, failing to capture the fine-grained joint correctness required by realistic prompts.
