We introduce AVGen-Bench, a task-driven benchmark for T2AV generation featuring high-quality prompts across 11 real-world categories. To support comprehensive assessment, we propose a multi-granular evaluation framework that combines lightweight specialist models with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), enabling evaluation from perceptual quality to fine-grained semantic controllability. Our evaluation reveals a pronounced gap between strong audio-visual aesthetics and weak semantic reliability, including persistent failures in text rendering, speech coherence, physical reasoning, and a universal breakdown in musical pitch control. Code and benchmark resources are available at http://aka.ms/avgenbench.

AI.tldr: AVGen-Bench introduces a task-driven benchmark for T2AV generation with 11 real-world prompt categories and a multi-granular evaluation framework that combines lightweight models with Multimodal LLMs to assess perceptual quality and semantic controllability; results reveal a gap between audio-visual aesthetics and semantic reliability, with recurring failures in text rendering, speech coherence, physical reasoning, and pitch control. Resources released.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks either evaluate audio and video separately or rely on coarse embedding similarity, failing to capture fine-grained joint semantics required by realistic prompts; a joint, multi-granular evaluation is needed.
AI.method: Propose AVGen-Bench with high-quality prompts across 11 real-world categories. Develop a multi-granular evaluation framework that integrates lightweight specialist models and Multimodal LLMs to assess perceptual quality and semantic controllability from multiple angles.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation uncovers a pronounced gap: strong audiovisual aesthetics coexist with weak semantic reliability. Notable failure modes include text rendering, speech coherence, physical reasoning, and a universal breakdown in musical pitch control.
AI.conclusion: AVGen-Bench enables comprehensive, task-driven assessment of T2AV systems and highlights specific semantic and controllability weaknesses to address; code and benchmarks are publicly released.

title: Seeing but Not Thinking: Routing Distraction in Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have achieved remarkable performance on vision-language tasks. However, we identify a puzzling phenomenon termed Seeing but Not Thinking: models accurately perceive image content yet fail in subsequent reasoning, while correctly solving identical problems presented as pure text. Through systematic analysis, we first verify that cross-modal semantic sharing exists in MoE architectures, ruling out semantic alignment failure as the sole explanation. We then reveal that visual experts and domain experts exhibit layer-wise separation, with image inputs inducing significant routing divergence from text inputs in middle layers where domain experts concentrate. Based on these findings, we propose the Routing Distraction hypothesis: when processing visual inputs, the routing mechanism fails to adequately activate task-relevant reasoning experts. To validate this hypothesis, we design a routing-guided intervention method that enhances domain expert activation. Experiments on three multimodal MoE models across six benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements, with gains of up to 3.17% on complex visual reasoning tasks. Our analysis further reveals that domain expert identification locates cognitive functions rather than sample-specific solutions, enabling effective transfer across tasks with different information structures.

AI.tldr: Multimodal MoE models show 'Seeing but Not Thinking': they perceive images well but struggle to reason; routing divergence in middle layers separates visual and domain experts; a routing-distraction hypothesis explains this; routing-guided intervention activates domain experts, improving performance (up to 3.17%) and enabling cross-task transfer via domain-expert cognitive mapping.
AI.motivation: Understand why multimodal MoE models succeed at perception but fail at reasoning, despite cross-modal sharing, and identify the routing dynamics that segregate information processing across modalities.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of three multimodal MoE models over six benchmarks; examine layer-wise routing, cross-modal semantic sharing, and expert specialization; design a routing-guided intervention to boost domain-expert activation; evaluate improvements and transferability.
AI.result: Evidence of cross-modal semantic sharing; image inputs induce routing divergence in middle layers where domain experts concentrate; routing-guided intervention increases activation of domain experts, yielding consistent improvements (up to 3.17%) on complex visual reasoning tasks; domain-expert identification aligns with cognitive functions and transfers across tasks with different information structures.
AI.conclusion: Routing distraction limits multimodal reasoning; activating domain experts via routing-guided interventions enhances reasoning accuracy and enables cross-task transfer, suggesting design principles for better alignment between perceptual inputs and task-relevant reasoning in MoE architectures.

title: Scal3R: Scalable Test-Time Training for Large-Scale 3D Reconstruction
summary: This paper addresses the task of large-scale 3D scene reconstruction from long video sequences. Recent feed-forward reconstruction models have shown promising results by directly regressing 3D geometry from RGB images without explicit 3D priors or geometric constraints. However, these methods often struggle to maintain reconstruction accuracy and consistency over long sequences due to limited memory capacity and the inability to effectively capture global contextual cues. In contrast, humans can naturally exploit the global understanding of the scene to inform local perception. Motivated by this, we propose a novel neural global context representation that efficiently compresses and retains long-range scene information, enabling the model to leverage extensive contextual cues for enhanced reconstruction accuracy and consistency. The context representation is realized through a set of lightweight neural sub-networks that are rapidly adapted during test time via self-supervised objectives, which substantially increases memory capacity without incurring significant computational overhead. The experiments on multiple large-scale benchmarks, including the KITTI Odometry~\cite{Geiger2012CVPR} and Oxford Spires~\cite{tao2025spires} datasets, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in handling ultra-large scenes, achieving leading pose accuracy and state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction accuracy while maintaining efficiency. Code is available at https://zju3dv.github.io/scal3r.

AI.tldr: A neural global context representation enables scalable long-range 3D scene reconstruction from long videos by using test-time-adapted lightweight sub-networks, boosting accuracy and memory efficiency; achieves state-of-the-art results on KITTI Odometry and Oxford Spires; code released.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction models struggle to maintain accuracy and consistency over long sequences due to limited memory and weak global cues. Humans leverage global scene understanding to inform local perception. A global context mechanism is needed to capture long-range information efficiently.
AI.method: Propose a neural global context representation composed of lightweight sub-networks that compress and retain long-range scene information. These sub-networks are rapidly adapted at test time via self-supervised objectives, boosting memory capacity with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness on ultra-large scenes, achieving leading pose accuracy and state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction accuracy on benchmarks such as KITTI Odometry and Oxford Spires, while maintaining efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The approach validates that compact, adaptable global context representations can enhance long-range 3D reconstruction, offering scalability and practical performance. Code is released.

title: Act Wisely: Cultivating Meta-Cognitive Tool Use in Agentic Multimodal Models
summary: The advent of agentic multimodal models has empowered systems to actively interact with external environments. However, current agents suffer from a profound meta-cognitive deficit: they struggle to arbitrate between leveraging internal knowledge and querying external utilities. Consequently, they frequently fall prey to blind tool invocation, resorting to reflexive tool execution even when queries are resolvable from the raw visual context. This pathological behavior precipitates severe latency bottlenecks and injects extraneous noise that derails sound reasoning. Existing reinforcement learning protocols attempt to mitigate this via a scalarized reward that penalizes tool usage. Yet, this coupled formulation creates an irreconcilable optimization dilemma: an aggressive penalty suppresses essential tool use, whereas a mild penalty is entirely subsumed by the variance of the accuracy reward during advantage normalization, rendering it impotent against tool overuse. To transcend this bottleneck, we propose HDPO, a framework that reframes tool efficiency from a competing scalar objective to a strictly conditional one. By eschewing reward scalarization, HDPO maintains two orthogonal optimization channels: an accuracy channel that maximizes task correctness, and an efficiency channel that enforces execution economy exclusively within accurate trajectories via conditional advantage estimation. This decoupled architecture naturally induces a cognitive curriculum-compelling the agent to first master task resolution before refining its self-reliance. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our resulting model, Metis, reduces tool invocations by orders of magnitude while simultaneously elevating reasoning accuracy.

AI.tldr: HDPO reframes tool-use as a conditional efficiency objective aligned with accurate trajectories, yielding Metis that dramatically reduces tool invocations while improving reasoning accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current agents suffer from a meta-cognitive deficit: scalarized penalties push excessive tool use, harming efficiency and performance. A decoupled, conditional optimization aims to resolve this by separating task accuracy from tool-use economy.
AI.method: Introduce HDPO with two orthogonal channels: (1) an accuracy channel that maximizes task correctness and (2) an efficiency channel that enforces economy only within accurate trajectories via conditional advantage estimation, no reward scalarization. This creates a cognitive curriculum that prioritizes task mastery before self-reliant tool use.
AI.result: Metis achieves orders-of-magnitude reduction in tool invocations and improves reasoning accuracy across extensive evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled, conditional optimization overcomes scalarized-RL bottlenecks, enabling more efficient and accurate agentic systems and suggesting a robust framework for future large-scale multimodal agents.

title: When Numbers Speak: Aligning Textual Numerals and Visual Instances in Text-to-Video Diffusion Models
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://h-embodvis.github.io/NUMINA
summary: Text-to-video diffusion models have enabled open-ended video synthesis, but often struggle with generating the correct number of objects specified in a prompt. We introduce NUMINA , a training-free identify-then-guide framework for improved numerical alignment. NUMINA identifies prompt-layout inconsistencies by selecting discriminative self- and cross-attention heads to derive a countable latent layout. It then refines this layout conservatively and modulates cross-attention to guide regeneration. On the introduced CountBench, NUMINA improves counting accuracy by up to 7.4% on Wan2.1-1.3B, and by 4.9% and 5.5% on 5B and 14B models, respectively. Furthermore, CLIP alignment is improved while maintaining temporal consistency. These results demonstrate that structural guidance complements seed search and prompt enhancement, offering a practical path toward count-accurate text-to-video diffusion. The code is available at https://github.com/H-EmbodVis/NUMINA.

AI.tldr: NUMINA is a training-free identify-then-guide framework that improves numerical alignment in text-to-video diffusion by selecting discriminative attention heads to build a latent layout, refining it, and modulating cross-attention to guide regeneration; it yields better object-count accuracy and CLIP alignment without sacrificing temporal consistency, and is released with code.
AI.motivation: Text-to-video diffusion often miscounts objects specified in prompts; a training-free method is needed to enforce correct numerosity while preserving quality and temporal consistency.
AI.method: Identify discriminative self- and cross-attention heads to derive a countable latent layout; conservatively refine the layout; modulate cross-attention to steer generation; evaluate on CountBench across Wan2.1-1.3B, 5B, and 14B models; training-free.
AI.result: Counting accuracy gains up to 7.4% on Wan2.1-1.3B; 4.9% (5B); 5.5% (14B); improved CLIP alignment; temporal consistency maintained.
AI.conclusion: Structural guidance complements seed search and prompt enhancement; presents a practical path toward count-accurate text-to-video diffusion; code available at GitHub.

title: GaussiAnimate: Reconstruct and Rig Animatable Categories with Level of Dynamics
summary: Free-form bones, that conform closely to the surface, can effectively capture non-rigid deformations, but lack a kinematic structure necessary for intuitive control. Thus, we propose a Scaffold-Skin Rigging System, termed "Skelebones", with three key steps: (1) Bones: compress temporally-consistent deformable Gaussians into free-form bones, approximating non-rigid surface deformations; (2) Skeleton: extract a Mean Curvature Skeleton from canonical Gaussians and refine it temporally, ensuring a category-agnostic, motion-adaptive, and topology-correct kinematic structure; (3) Binding: bind the skeleton and bones via non-parametric partwise motion matching (PartMM), synthesizing novel bone motions by matching, retrieving, and blending existing ones. Collectively, these three steps enable us to compress the Level of Dynamics of 4D shapes into compact skelebones that are both controllable and expressive. We validate our approach on both synthetic and real-world datasets, achieving significant improvements in reanimation performance across unseen poses-with 17.3% PSNR gains over Linear Blend Skinning (LBS) and 21.7% over Bag-of-Bones (BoB)-while maintaining excellent reconstruction fidelity, particularly for characters exhibiting complex non-rigid surface dynamics. Our Partwise Motion Matching algorithm demonstrates strong generalization to both Gaussian and mesh representations, especially under low-data regime (~1000 frames), achieving 48.4% RMSE improvement over robust LBS and outperforming GRU- and MLP-based learning methods by >20%. Code will be made publicly available for research purposes at cookmaker.cn/gaussianimate.

AI.tldr: A three-step Skelebones framework that converts non-rigid 4D shapes into controllable bones and a category-agnostic skeleton, enabling novel motion synthesis via Partwise Motion Matching; shows significant PSNR and RMSE gains over LBS and BoB and strong low-data generalization; code release.
AI.motivation: Non-rigid surface deformations require a kinematic, controllable representation. Free-form bones capture deformations but lack structure; a category-agnostic skeleton is needed for intuitive control and topology-correct motion; binding via Partwise Motion Matching aims to synthesize new motions from existing ones.
AI.method: 1) Bones: compress temporally-consistent deformable Gaussians into free-form bones to approximate non-rigid surface dynamics. 2) Skeleton: extract a Mean Curvature Skeleton from canonical Gaussians and temporally refine it to produce a topology-correct, motion-adaptive kinematic structure. 3) Binding: bind skeleton and bones with non-parametric Partwise Motion Matching to synthesize novel bone motions by matching, retrieving, and blending existing poses.
AI.result: The approach achieves 17.3% PSNR gains over Linear Blend Skinning (LBS) and 21.7% over Bag-of-Bones (BoB); maintains high reconstruction fidelity for complex non-rigid dynamics. PartMM generalizes well to Gaussian and mesh representations, especially in low-data regimes (~1000 frames), yielding 48.4% RMSE improvement over robust LBS and outperforms GRU- and MLP-based methods by over 20%.
AI.conclusion: Skelebones compress the Level of Dynamics of 4D shapes into compact, controllable skelebones that are expressive and adaptable across representations and data regimes. The PartMM binding enables effective motion synthesis by leveraging existing motions, and code will be publicly released for research.
